ayoreo: Ore acade cucha jiei ga ore chijnóngaja údi cucha pibosode.
english: When they had a lot of produce from their gardens, they would put some of it at the foot of the Totem.

ayoreo: Ore chijócha darujna gai ga ore chijna údi. Ujeta pero ga gajnareque. Cho na aja jnumi. Jnanione ore chijnója jnumi uje ojnane gu. Ore erásoningo u guebéode nanique uje ore chijno. Ore jno údi aja guidai ajã́ro ga ore chijóchame iji guidai gatócoro.
english: They would dig around the cactus that was chosen for the Totem,  and kept it whole.  Bad things would happen if it broke. (Cactus’ break apart easily.) The men would chop it down because of the thorns. They used metal tools for chopping it down.

ayoreo: Ga chi ore doi guidai ñane ome dapesudi namacade. Que ore chuseque que.
english: The cactus was taken to the middle of camp and let down into a hole that they had dug for it. When it was upright in the hole, everyone gathered around the Totem.

ayoreo: Ore cho cheme uje que ore narare go. Guidai uñai ore pógu re ome namacade ijnimisórone ga guidai uñai que chunina ijnimisórone. Ga ore chijocase dacãra tiji. Ga ore ipesusórone ore churúsãre dádode jnese ome curudé. Ore churúsãre darídade carubode. Ore churúsãre namacadé údode. Irarãre aja gaté.
english: The men would wear all of their adornment paraphernalia. When they came before the Totem no one talked or made noise. People from other villages might sneak up on the Totem participants, that is the reason they kept quiet, they didn’t want the people from other villages to know they had a Totem.

ayoreo: Jnuruminone – pajei carubode
english: They would shake their heads and make their tied-back hair move back and forth. They would paint themselves with the powder that came off the red stone called*carodé*. The strings on their sandals were also painted red.

ayoreo: Picanerane –pajojode Cutamurajane – pedobicaidie Dosapéode – yajogaroa Posorajane – gogogoi carigode, chugúpẽredatéi Étacõrone –suarecari Ajinereminie ore chica ome cucarani carobé ga ca toc jeta chise cojñotic jeta pero. Ore tãrei ome aja namacadé yui: jnacarode, jnanione ore. Ore chijñecáraja. Ore chutai. Ore chadia cóbipise jé aja ore ayode. Ore chipocásu dajmainie ga ore cho ‘juiiiiiii’ aja jnumi. Ga ore chojninga: “Codé a, yiñongóquedatei, cá chisa dejóringome yu jne.” Ore canare iji dejai, dirode. A iyajmaque ore iji. Ore chijna dacáranie jnese.
english: Designs were painted on the Totem. They would paint the marks pertaining to the various clans on the Totem—some that*Cajoide*mentioned were the following: *Jnuruminone –  pajei carubode*(strings worn around the waist) *Picanerane –   pajojode (?)* *Cutamurajane –  pedobicaidie (?)* *Dosapéode –  yajogaroi*(skin of the anteater) *Posorajane –  gogogoi carigode*(big bird’s tail feathers) *Étacõrone –  suarecari*(parrot tails) Small axes were used to make cuts on the red stone called*carodé*, and if the stones broke while cutting them, this meant they wouldn’t be able to kill ‘non-ayoré’ victims. So they would cut the stones very carefully. (The killing of the civilized was very desirable to an Ayoré man at that time.)

ayoreo: Ore tibidi guidai uñane. Que ore chimitingare. Ujetiga ore chimitingare enga ore chachẽre cucha urõsode ga cucha urõsode chimate ore.
english: The young men and older men would all dance in front of the Totem, using the same steps of the dance used in the ceremony to free them from the guilt of having shed blood called*ore tãrei ome*.

ayoreo: Uje ore abayode toi ga ore doi carichade ga ore chinguíra uñane iji ore ójo.
english: They would sing to the Totem and petition it to give them things. They would wear their neck feathers and their jaguar headdresses.

ayoreo: Ore na abai aja acoté ujúbei ajei uje ore chijóchame ore.
english: Folding their hands, they would chant loudly:*who-eeeeeee, who-eeeeeee*,*who-eeeeeee.*They treated the Totem as though it were their grandmother and would say, “Grandmother, you washed me and initiated me into your clan. I know that you will not deny me, but will give me what I ask for.”

ayoreo: Ore cho i iji gu uje ajnorane chujé ga ore chojninga: “Dujuábidé uruode.” *Cajóidé – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988.*
english: They would keep this up night and day for a long time. They would shake their gourd rattles and chant. They would rub the palms of their hands together as they mentioned other groups. (*Cajoide*pantomimed this.) Nothing was thrown away. If they discarded anything, that meant that harmful things would latch onto them and kill them.

ayoreo: *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Cajóidé atatagode udojo.*
english: When their husbands would die they would grab snail shells and hit each other on the head with them. They would put the dead husband in his wife’s large round bag to bury him.

ayoreo: Ore chiraja chugúpẽre ijnangórone ome guedoside; u uje deyade déji gatócoro ga chugúpẽre poringai u. U to uje: (*Cajoide*cho quique: “Yocajminone u udore, cuchade udore; uje tocade ga yirajágo uje chugúpẽre ijnangórone u. Ore tocade bajac.”)
english: *Chugúpẽre Gajneone Cucha* **When to celebrate the Bird God:**The signs that indicate it is time to celebrate the Bird Festival. When the following things come to pass (pop out) they bring the Bird. But first we must see*all*the signs. When we do, then it is time to prepare for the yearly festival when we worship the Bird. The Ayoreos know the Bird is near by the moon and the constellation called ‘*deyade”*in Ayore.

ayoreo: (*Cajoide*cho quique: “Gatomini chisa ti ga jé uñieque.”)
english: (Cajoidé:  When the wasps attach their hives to the twig, then it sprouts leaves.)

ayoreo: Jnanione ore dayode u iyatade. Iyatade ore narare ore gai uje ore chajna guesi iji taningapísi. Ore chojninga: “Ãrijíño ga ajnáño uaté aja guesi ga amácho to jne,  ga yoco paraque to jne.”
english: The men who make preparations for the Bird Festival are called ‘*Iyatade’;*only grown men and their fathers participate in being ‘*Iyatade.’*It’s the “Iyatade” that speak over the young men participants and get them going in the very beginning of the Festival. They say: “Wake up! Follow her! Chase after her! And when you do it, we’ll be very quiet.”

ayoreo: Uyatágode que ore taguiji erámi. Yoquitódi u jeta ayorérique cho paraque ga chipese dotique cutepec ga chujé ayorérique.
english: The participants don’t eat when entering the jungle. What we fear is that someone will make a mistake and start sucking honey and it will kill someone.

ayoreo: Iyatade ore charo doridie iji degúi. Ore chácariji mu uje dirípise ga quene ore jno to ome dabatigai ga ore chipese dódode iji erámi. Ore chóji chicórone ga subudé ore iji erámi, iyatade.
english: The “*Iyatade*” take handfuls of wild pineapple fruit from camp as they begin the preparations. They sit down, but when it’s light then they go to the place where they’ll prepare and they suck ‘*dódode*’ (a type of honey) in the woods. The ‘*Iyatade’*drink from the water plant until they’re full there in the woods.

ayoreo: Jnacarode uyatac ore ga que ore tac iji deréjnai tudé. Ga ore di iji gatócoro. Mu uñane jĩraque ore chijno cutérone ga ore di bu. Ore chijna re aja eramúni.
english: The young men fast and they don’t eat that day. They come back at noon (when the sun is in the middle). But some find honey quickly and they hurry back before the others. They take turns going out into the woods so they get the honey more quickly.

ayoreo: Ga iyatade chaquesu ore ome dajũrururite. Yoquerásõri tu dajũrururite. Uje iyatai chaquesu gosi ga uté e chóji yodi iji cucoi ajei. Mu ore chójne ome dabogoro bajai, uyapídi gajnei u gu. Ga e ore chójipíse.
english: The “*Iyatade*” whip them with their whips. What we used for whips was wild pineapple string.

ayoreo: Iyatade ore pésu cucoi ga chicháji dapácadi, dajapídi. Ga e omé ore uje ore chójipis. Ga quigade enga chijmo doridie to.
english: After one of the “*Iyatade*” whips someone, the person he whips can now drink water from the hollowed out log troughs that the ‘*Iyatade’*prepared ahead of time. But he must spit out his first mouthful, for it belongs to the ground prepared for the trough. After that he can drink all he wants.

ayoreo: Mu cáma ore tõra dajũrurui. Qué ore sase gu uyatade. Iyatai chicháji dádi ome di ejode. Ore chujé ore chaqui*jnac, jnac, jnac*ome dajũrururui.
english: The “*Iyatade*” hollow out the log and put it where they decide to place it.

ayoreo: Ga chequedíe ore charo dameranie ome dabai. Chequedíe ore chojningame dabode: “Bayé gajnenie de diji. Asi ome.”
english: After a young man’s thirst is quenched they say he is ‘good’ (pure).  Afterwards the young men can eat wild pineapple, which has been roasted ahead of time – they eat the meal from the leaves  much like eating artichoke leaves by scraping the meal from the leaves off on their lower teeth.

ayoreo: Ayoré uña charo dameranie ome dabai ga tagu pósiode. Ga abai di, a daté chojningame dabi: “Be yipósiode ga bayé tagu jne.”
english: But the ‘*Iyatade’*do not throw away their whips yet. They have to wait for the other fasting young men to return. The “*Iyatade*” set their whips aside for the next ones coming back. They whip the young men by hitting their bottoms*‘knock, knock, knock’*with their whips.

ayoreo: Iji taningapísi ga ore chijna pucuguedie aja guesi ga ore chiqueta piogode iji guedé cacadi yui ga ore turúa. Ore chojninga: “Ca yiborárique ca pirágome yu, Chugúpẽre Cuchabéraque.” Ore chijócha chugúpẽre ajei údi uje ore turúa gueguerade.
english: The women take handfuls of the twisted off*‘doridie fruit’*for their husbands to eat. The women give them to their children to take to their fathers and they say: “These are for your father. Give them to him.”

ayoreo: Ore chicáji. Ga ore tujúnapo danochade rĩ, jé aja ore ayode, jé aja potayedie, jé aja ore cóbidie, ore ibotadie. Uje iyataque cho potírique ga qué turuapo dabai te rĩ. Ore chapúsaja.
english: An Ayoré woman might take the fruit herself to her husband and he eats what she offers to him.

ayoreo: Uje cutérone sué yoquibai ga iyatade ore chijmare. Ore nája catójaraque uñáraque. Ga e ore tõra detepídode, ga chugúpẽre iborade u. Asojna ibora sué yoquibai. Uñane suse guio, jé uñane que suse guio.
english: When a husband comes, the mother will say to her child: “Get some of what I’ve cooked so your father can eat it.”

ayoreo: Ga iyatade ore cha chaquesu uyatade ga jé u uje ore tõra dajũrurururit. Ore tṍraja guedo (guedógosode) gai, acorãcabi (este-noreste).  Ga e yibotáco dabũrururit jnese.
english: At the very start of the Festival the young men pull half-burnt sticks out of the fire that they have started west of the camp, and when the sticks are cool, they blacken their bodies with the carbon.

ayoreo: (Mu chequedíe ore cho paraque uje ore todo gu.)
english: And as they do so, they say: “Great Bird, may finding honey not be hard for me.”

ayoreo: Mu ore botãre ga ore cho ¡*jei-jei-jei*! Ore chejna dire chojmarai guesi. Guedé doi ti.
english: (Cajoidé pantomimes rubbing the carbon between the palms of his hands and then rubbing his arms; I asked him how I could say this in Ayoré; he says he doesn’t know.)

ayoreo: Yucuegoi gajñonie uje yirajágo uje gué tagu erámio ga gajñonie déji erámi. “Arocho yui,” ore chojninga.
english: After they petition the Bird, and have finished blackening their bodies they leave to hunt honey.

ayoreo: Ga yejnángo guesi jne, enga dimane yogome to jeta yimáco ga yoco paraque to jne.
english: They also wear their headbands, jaguar headdresses, neck adornments made of parrot feathers woven to look like a tail and long white feathers that stand up around their necks and other types of feather neck pieces.

ayoreo: Ga arei ga yapúcoja jogadi uje yirajágo uje que yotique déji erámi ga yijóchago chicórone ga yimáco gu chicórone.
english: The “*Iyatade”*who prepare things for the Festival also paint their bodies black with carbon. They do it in different ways.

ayoreo: *Cajoide – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1985.*
english: It they bring back too much honey, the “*Iyatade*” store it. They put it in honey gourds. But they throw away what doesn’t please them, and what they discard  is for Asojna, the Bird God.

ayoreo: *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Cajoide atatagode udojo nanique.*
english: The surplus honey is for*Asojna*– the name of the little night owl they worship, but often refer to only as*The Bird*.

ayoreo: Ore chubúchu uje durasé enga ore chipotingára. Ore chujé cucha urõsode enga ore chigójna gai. Ore chipotingára (potadie) uje ore doi tabóidie. Cucha urõsode chimate date urõsode ga ayoréode u uñane, jé aja caratade, aquirájnane, cojñone ore to. Ore doi taboidie uje ore jnusina iyode. Uje guedé e déji jnumi enga ore chipotingare. Ore cho jóno-jóno.  Chojmára ayorei uje chipotingare. Ore chujé degode ica mu ore jnusina ga ore doi tabóidie. Jnacarode, jnanione ore pésu taboidie jnese. Ga ore jno uje guedé jé paráta. Ga ore chipese cutepéode aja dajesúode uje que ore taque to. Jnanipísode uje ore cuta ga ore chácariji degúi. Mu uje uyatac ore enga ore doi jnese. Gare ore catójadie uje ore chijnoque. Ore gajneque uñai, jé basa ga ore acotei gajneque uñai. Ore di cutepéode údi enga ore pésu taboidie iji degúi. Ore tibidi dajorane enga ore chojninga: “Beyoi iyoi te jne a.” Ga ore turua gueguerade, ore chiporigase dabai ome gueguerade. Ore baque ga ore doi cutepéode. Ore chijnime dagatója iji depei. Ore jno údi ome dacotei. Ore chisi ome ñane. Jeoga yoca, ga ore chisi ome ñane to. Ore cãra ore gatójadie. Ore tẽra daborade ome dacotéode. Picaneraque gosi ga Cutamúrajane u uté gosi acotéode. Étacõrone que toc jeta ore tagu daborárique uje uerate ore gu. Ga ore chisi ome ore jnese.  Que Étacõrone tagu pocáidode uje uerate ore gu. Dutué utiedie ga Étacõrone uerate ga que ore tagu dutué utiedie. Ore ininaquéo iji dire chojmãra.
english: The Ayoreos blow the whistles (*potadíe)*early in the morning and hold them up for all to see. When they have killed harmful animals or people, they sing a victory song about the killing. But they blow the large wooden whistle when they remember the blood that was shed. When they thought about how dangerous their victims had been in life and how much harm they had done, they feared retaliation from their victims for having shed their blood. The ones they feared most among their victims were Ayoreos, or jaguars, or cattle, or*cojñone*(civilized people). There was only one thing they could do now to free themselves of the blood guiltiness.  They must do the Blood Exchange Ceremony, called*taboidie,*to confuse their victims concerning who was responsible for the shedding of their blood. When the sun comes up they blow the whistle. It sounds like this,*jóno-jóno.*Just one Ayoré blows it. They may have killed their victims in the past, but when they remember them, they must do the*taboidie*ceremony. Young men and grown men all do the*taboidie*ceremony. They leave camp while the sun it still tender. It is so early that they haven’t eaten yet, so they suck honey as they go to hunt for more honey. The older men who get tired easily stay in the camp. But they fast as well as those who go for honey. They each take two gourds full of honey with them to use in the ceremony. One is for them, the other for their male mates, called*wives*. When those who have gone for honey come back, they may now begin the*taboidie*ceremony in the camp. They call all their friends and they say: “Come, do the ceremony to remove the guiltiness for having shed blood.” In preparation for the ceremony, they paint their bodies, rubbing them with coals from the fire, until their whole body is black. They have hunted honey, and brought it back to camp, and now they take the gourds of honey out of their bags. They take them to their male mates and begin to exchange over and over again the honey among themselves. Maybe they have turtles, if so, they give turtles to each other also. They keep on exchanging honey gourds. They offer what they’ve brought to their male mates.

ayoreo: Uje ore chisi ome dacotéode uje ore acade jiei ga ore pésu*pocáidode*ga ore chisi sequeredie gajnéone cucha ome dacotéode. Ore chojningame dacotei: “Yipésu yajóningarique ome yacotei.” Ore acotéode ore poso ore ipesudi pocai. Ore chojninga: “Yicopoi dirome rĩ, que yajo yoquibotique iji bajai,” ga ore dopoi rĩ.  Ore tujuapója rĩ. Ga ore chécãre dagaidi cuchai iyoi, jeoga ayoré iyoi ga cojñoi iyoi ejoi, jeoga caratai tu. Chequedie ore todo cucha urõsode iyode ga que ore tagu. Jé basa ga jeta ore tagu ga tagu ore ajéode. *Cajoidé – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1985.* *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Cajoidé atatagode udojo.*
english: (*Cajoidé*didn’t explain the rest of the choosing of mates among other Clans.) The*Étacorone*can’t eat the honey they bring back because they are*uerate*(pretty- royal). So they give it to all the others. To be ‘*uerate’*you must have no hair on your arms. And it appears that the ones who are considered ‘uerate’ are a sort of nobility. The*Étacorone*don’t eat ‘beans’ because they are ‘pretty.’ And the*Étacorone*who are “pretty” do not eat the first fruits of the harvest. *Chiquejnójna*is what they call a man who is not a true*Chiquenoi*because he has arm hair. *Picanerajnai*is what they call a man who is not a true*Picanerai*because he has hair on his arms. There is a celebration for the harvest of beans that lasts for one day. On that day they give gifts to their male wives from the ‘rainy season crops,’ which assures them of having a plentiful harvest. They prepare ‘*pocáidode*’ (a type of food made from beans) in celebration of the plentiful harvest they will have because they have given food to their male wives. They say to their male mates: “I’m making ‘a living’ for my male mate.” They cook beans for their ‘male mates’. They say: “We’ll do it tomorrow, also, we didn’t eat all of our food on the first day,” and so they do it again. And in this way they exchange the blood of the one they’ve killed, whether an Ayoré’s blood, a*cojñoi’s*(civilized person’s) blood, or a jaguar’s. This exchange makes it impossible for the victim to know who is actually to blame for his death, because so many have exchanged his blood. The women fear the blood of the harmful victims so they don’t eat any of the exchanges of honey. But if they do, their stomachs hurt.

ayoreo: Ore chujé cucha urõsoi ga jé ditac ore ga ore chise gapudíe, ga chisi ñane cucha pibosode, cutepéode, ga quigade u uje ore chimate ditai ga ore chacája ñane.
english: When a jaguar is killed, the one that killed it draws a rectangular figure on the ground to represent the jaguar:

ayoreo: E ore doi dabayode ejode.
english: When they kill a wild animal they are contaminated and have guilt. So they go to young girls, and they exchange food and honey with them, and afterwards when they’ve finished the exchange they move about.  And then the girls lay down with their husbands (sex implied).

ayoreo: Chugúpẽri adode u. Uje chujé ayoréode, mu ayoréode yiquetágopíse yocurasade to, chugúpẽri yugode ayoré iji móntedie. Ga erásõri jec u de ica ga adode u uje jé cho dajñáre iji udore:
english: *Chugúpẽre Poringai Adode*

ayoreo: Doi uje yobéco erámi, uje yobéco erámi iji ti uje yicoi dejábi atíamone. Ga yipotingáco iji ti. Uje cho dajñáre. Yijnángo ti guesi uje cho dajñáre. Ga jé cho dajñáriji, ga yicagoi ta.
english: When we’d stop some place and eat honey, that would be where she would kill us. She would curse us and finish us off. Or when an Ayoré would eat food and then sharpen something, they would die.

ayoreo: Jé cho dajñáriji uje yacágo ducayode. Yacágo ichade, ducayode, cóbidie ore. Jé cho dajñáriji to. Cho dajñáriji udore uje yurúgome udaque yoquibáyode ga adi tu ome gueguerade. Cho dajñáriji to gueguerade. Ga yejnángo guesi ga yicoi udore uje yejnángo yocate ichádaja, utébeode. Uje yacágo cuchárigaja to. Ga chojninga: “Cuchárigo a chichaga raja yu é. Cuchárigo a chichaga raja yu é.”
english: The old men would say: “Wake up and follow Asojna away from camp into the jungle.” We’d go out of camp toward the west. We’d blow the whistle when it was morning but still dark. And we’d wear our long white feather neck-pieces, our jaguar headdresses, and whistle necklaces. We’d wear our whistles as we left camp, the Bird’s whistles. We’d finish adorning ourselves and the little boys would place our things before us: our honey gourds, our ropes, our bags, our axes. And we’d take our things along with us as we left to hunt honey. But we’d spread out and we would be fasting.

ayoreo: Uje yijnójo yocutaidode, yocate omone u. Uje cho dajñariji.
english: We’d hunt honey and then, when we returned with honey, the men would whip us. Then we’d drink water from wooden bowls, balsam wood troughs, and gourds. We’d drink, but spit out the first mouthful that would wet our dry mouths. We’d quickly spit out the first water, but drink the next. And we’d eat ‘doridie’ fruit first (scraping the leaves off on our lower front teeth).  The women would get up very early to go for ‘doridie fruit’ and then it was that they’d tell their children: “These are for your father.”  And they’d scrape out the mealy white parts.

ayoreo: Jé cho dajñariji uje que yiborárique pirac. Uje que yiborárique pirac. Ga chisi ome yoque to. (Jé adi turásu Dupade adi. Mu chisíome daborade ome yoque to uje que pirac.) Uje cho dajñáriji. Uje tẽrája ayoréode to. (Adi jé urásu Dupade adi to. Uje adi urásu.)
english: We’d eat honey afterwards. We’d eat other food afterwards, as well. We’d reach the camp when the sun was past the middle, and we’d make crosses to put before us as we entered camp.  (They put the crosses in front of them and then the Bird couldn’t get past them and harm the women.) When we reached camp we would bathe and get all cleaned up. And the women would make crosses and place them between them and the men. The men that lived long ago claimed that “Asojna” feared crosses. We’d reach camp and a singer would quickly start singing with his rattle. But at midnight the singer would stop and we’d all go to sleep. The good singers sang with rattles for it was claimed the ‘rattle’ belonged to Asojna according to the men who lived long ago. The women would say to their children: “Don’t look at them. They’re holy (taboo) and it’s forbidden to look at them.” And both the women and the children would strictly avoid looking at the men.

ayoreo: *Íquede –  Puesto Paz, Bolivia – 1976**Timoteo Wyma churúsãre Íquede atatagode udojo*
english: Some of the little boys would be where the men were, but the women were not permitted to be among them. *Dejabi – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988.* *Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie.*

ayoreo: *(Los nombres están en cursiva)*
english: *Jnani Bajade Anguretigade*

ayoreo: **chatataSopauruode uje uté chatata dai o quigode ome re nanique:**
english: ‘I am*Sopa*’s father.’ I am commenting about what he is saying. He says,  ‘*Sopa*, don’t say anything bad about yourself,’ he says. ‘*Dupade*is everywhere around us,’ he says. ‘He is*our words*,’ he says. ‘What you say has the power to make bad things happen to you,’ he says. ‘It will make bad things happen if you say something bad might happen,’ he says. ‘So only mention the good things that will happen,’ he says, ‘when you go hunting for honey.’

ayoreo: Chojninga:*Sopa*uje chĩ́care. “*Sopa*dai yu, yĩcarai.*Sopa*dai yu,” chojninga. “*Sopa*, ga ca barúrique ca gajnaréngaja ua to,” chojninga. “Dupade déji yoquibai,” chojninga. “Ga u yocuruode,” chojninga. “A achaga cucha gajnarétique aja baquei,” chojninga, “a chicha cucha gajnarétique aja baquei te jne,” chojninga. “Jé a chicha cucha omone iji baquéode,” chojninga, “ujetiga abaque.”
english: ‘Don’t say anything negative,’ he says. ‘Don’t mention death in connection with yourself. Only say good words concerning yourself,’ he says. ‘*Dupade*is in*our words*,’ he says. ‘He is all around us; our forefathers told us this.’

ayoreo: “Ca barúrique gajnaréngaja ua,” chojninga. “Ca ẽ́care pitoringai tome ua. Gusu jeta barúrigo omone ua,” chojninga. “Dupade u yocuruode,” chojninga. “Déji yoquibai,” chojninga yocai casicaite.
english: ‘He is*Dupade*,’ our ancestors said, referring to the sun. It is believed that*Dupade*is the sun according to our forefathers. They claimed he is the sun,’ my father told me.

ayoreo: Jnani bajade ore chojninga: “Dupade u guedé uaté,” chojninga. Mu chiese Dupade u guedé ome yocai casicaite. Chiese u guedé. Chojninga: “Ca achaga ua aja cucha gajnarétique jetiga cucha urõsótique chujé ua. Gusu jeta barúrigo omone ua ga a ca cuchárique chosi ti iji ua,” chojninga. “Ca acha cucha gajnarétique iji baquénique, cucha sijnátique. Gusu jeta barúrigo omone ua ga a ca cuchárique chosi ti iji ua,” chojninga. “Gusu jeta acha bacuegode cucha aja baquei. Cutepéode u. Dotipéode ore, ga acha baquéode ga a chicha baquei,” chi chojninga, yocai casicaite. Que chirajarase cuchárique to nanique, Dupade adode. Mu cáma chududipise to nanique. Chugúji ga chacãra Dupade aja cuchade. Chi yoquedópasa, ga chugúji ga chacãra aja Dupade. Chi Étacorone yoquedópasa. Chacãrája, ore chugúji. Ta tibidi nanique ga – ẽjé.
english: He said, ‘Don’t be thinking that bad things will happen to you or that some harmful animal will kill you. Only think and say positive things and then nothing will happen to you,’ he said. ‘Don’t say that bad things are going to happen, or that evil will be coming. Only mention good things and then you will be safe,’ he said. ‘Only mention survival and the life ahead of you. Mention honey being obtainable, dripping out of the hive, and keep those thoughts about what will happen, and then*Dupade*will put them before you; this is what our forefathers said.’

ayoreo: Ore tibidi Dupade ga jé chicha cuchade iji ore iquéode to uje ore chutáji to. Ẽjé. Tangári uje chicha cuchade iji daquéode. Chi tibidi ga chi – ẽjé. Ore tibidipísi. Ore tibidipísi ga pijnapíso.
english: They didn’t know anything about the things of*Dupade*long ago. But then they really hadn’t heard about them. They chanted and likened*Dupade*to the things around them. Even our clan possessions, they would chant the names of our clan possessions and liken them to*Dupade*.*Dupade*was the clan possession of us*Étacorone*clan members. An*Étacori*would liken*Dupade*to the things they chanted about. That is how they called them into being long ago – yes.

ayoreo: Que ore catecã́ri to uje ore pijnapíso uje ore tibidi ga chicha anire, tajique, paroco, paoi. Paoi u uje yuchígoi nanique iji móntedie. Chi chichai iji iquei tenga dacódejna. Ga chi chica dachiédode iji. Uje chi Dupade chisi ome paoi tome. Uje chi uchiédode ga chachopise ga jé chi tibidi Dupade ga chicha paoi taja iquei. Ga chi chicai erámi pao, dachiédode iji.
english: They would call upon*Dupade*to give them things and he would put the things in front of them that they asked for. Yes.  He would listen as they would think of things being before them. They would call out to him – Yes. They would really call out. They would call loudly.

ayoreo: Chi tibidápoi rĩ, tibidi sibíjnai. Ga chi guéode urásu ore ojninga mu a ca chiraja ga chicha caratai taja iquei. Ore chujé caratai ga chisiome caratai  ga chujé. Uje chicha cuchade iji iquéode to uje tibidi nanique.
english: They wouldn’t just talk calmly to him, they shouted for him to place before them what they asked him for, such as*paoi*(the tapir’s hide). The*paoi*were what we wore for shoes in the jungle.*Dupade*would put huge tapirs in front of us to kill. And they would cut shoes from their hides. And it was*Dupade*that provided hides for them. When they were in great need of shoes, they would call out to*Dupade*to place hides in front of them. And they went into the jungle with shoes on their feet.

ayoreo: **chatata uje jnani bajade que ore chiraja uje Dupade u uje déji yoquibai:**
english: He’d call out also for pools of water. And instead of rain, without knowing why,*Dupade*would place a jaguar in their path. They would kill the jaguar, for that is what*Dupade*provided, so that is what they killed. For*Dupade*was always placing things in front of them because they called upon him long ago.

ayoreo: Mu que chiraja uje Dupade déji yoquibai. A changuretaráse to, yocai casicaite, jetiga e chudute to nanique iji uacacajnaquéode. Uje que changureta nanique. Ẽjé. Changureta ga catecã́ri ome dejnaréngone uje chejna iquei. Ẽjé. Catecã́ri ome. Que changureta to iji móntedie nanique. A changureta to jeta e chudute to nanique, ujnomei.
english: But we didn’t know when*Dupade*was nearby us. Our forefathers would really have believed if they had heard the things you [missionaries] teach. For they didn’t believe then. Yes. They did believe some things, and they prayed to*Dupade*about their sickness that he would take it away. Yes. They prayed for that. They didn’t believe in Jesus in the jungle, but had they heard about him, they would have believed the good news*about him.*

ayoreo: **chatata uje chi jnani bajade ore changureta uje ayoréode ore gãrapíode u cuchisode:**
english: **shares the beliefs his people had about their origins:***Timi*, (his name for Tim Wyma) they say that the Ayoreos originated from the jungle animals long ago, according to the first men. Yes. From all kinds of animals. We were originally animals according to those who lived long ago. But they didn’t know all about it. They claimed we came from all kinds of animals, though, those first men. Yes. All of the different animals.

ayoreo: Tími, chi ayoréode chi yocãrapíode chi jec u cuchisode udore iji erámi to nanique ome jnani bajade. Ẽjé. Cuchiso ponáyone. Chi yocãrapíode u to ome jnacare bajade. Mu que ore chiraja gu. Chiese yocãrapíode cuchiso  ponáyone iji erámi to iji uté, jnacare bajade. Ẽjé. Cuchisode jnese. Chiese yocãrapíode jé u to ome jnacare bajade.
english: They told about the tapir taking an Ayoré man away with him, one of the first men told about this. The Ayoré crawled upon him one day. And the tapir provided all the different kinds of food for him. He carried the Ayoré. And he gave the Ayoré food things. The Ayoré got hungry and that is when he gave him honey to eat. And told him how to get honey. And how to chop for it. And how to suck it. The tapir said, “Why, honey is really delicious.’ And the honey gourd was filled with honey. He said he took it out of the honeycomb.

ayoreo: **chatata uje chiese cucha pibosode ipesungore u cabayui nanique:**
english: The tapir, said: ‘Here’s the honey called*ajidábia*. Take it, and suck it,’ they say the tapir said. ‘It’s true that other kinds of honey are vengeful.’ And he gave this kind to him, he got the kind called vengeful honey. It’s said he gave it to the Ayoré that he carried on his back. And then he took off with the Ayoré again. He took him far away, and the Ayoré kept sucking honey.

ayoreo: Chi cabayui jno ayorérique údi, ome jnacare bajade. Chi choru to iji duruñeque. Ga chi jé chisi cucha pibosode iji erámone. Chi chijnoque ayoré. Ga chi chisi cucha pibosode iji  erámone to. A chi chaguhei chejna ga chi chisíome cutepéode ome. Chi chisi  cuterĩ to. Chi aquesucade iji. Ga chi chipese. Cabayui chi chojninga: “Ũrénga ajidape unerai te jõ.” Ga chi cachi chijnime ajidábia ducúpie ga chi chaji ne. Chi chijnime udojo iji darúdode.
english: After a long time the Ayoré got hungry again. He said: ‘Why, there’s some more honey. The honey is coming out. I’d like to suck some,’  this is what they say he said. ‘There’s some honey here,’ they say he said. He took the honey out. The kind called*ducúpie*is there. And so he placed some in the container and sucked it. For they had gone far away, the tapir and the Ayoré.

ayoreo: Cabayui, dajusui chi chojninga: “Déji de, ajidábia. Be, apese jne,” chi chojninga. “Ũré a jnutai unejaque u jõ.” Ga chi chisíome, chi chijnime jnutai ducúpiedie iji udojo darúdode. Chi chisíome doidi ayoré. Chi chijnoque ayoré iji ta. Chorẽ́i iji tõ, doidi ayoré gotique. Chi chipese.
english: He got hungry and the tapir gave him things from the jungle to eat. And honey also came from the tapir according to the first men of long ago. The tapir brought out all the food things. But he kept coming up with more things. For they were a long time in the jungles. And it’s like what you tell about*Dupade*creating things. They believed that the tapir kept bringing forth more and more. Yes, from everywhere. They say that they stopped listening for the return of the Ayoré that the tapir had carried off, but then he brought their countryman back to them.

ayoreo: *Yocáqui Jnupabéjnaringui*
english: *Yocáqui Jnupabéjnaringui*

ayoreo: “Iapá, ¿ṍrañoji rique nanique a?” “Yocaranone u yocáqui jnupabéjnaringui.”
english: “Father, where did we come from long ago?*“*asked a little boy. “We came from our ancient fathers,” answered his father. And he began to tell him the ancient stories. Long ago they thought the sun was God, but they referred to it as “her”;  they would  talk to her and say: “You who are*The One Above Us*.”

ayoreo: Mu guedé i tu*Yoquimamito*, ga ore cho Dupade iji guedé nanique. Ore pijnane aja guedé: ‘¡*Yoquimamito*é, asi ome yoque to é!’ Ore tibite durasé, jeoga gatócoro, jeoga guedé jnoringai. Guedé guẽrate cuchade ome ore.” Cabayudie chajna dajeque uñai ujetiga ore dirase bajaque ga ayoré togode nijiapo rĩ. Guedoside chajna dajeque uñai. Bajai tu guedoside ga chequésãre Ayoréode ga ore topísi.
english: They would call out to the sun long ago and pray to it, saying, “*One Above Us*, give things to us!”  They would call to her in the early morning, or at noon, or at the going down of the sun. And the sun would give things to them. Tapir went down a different road so he could arrive first. If Tapir arrived first, then dead people would wake up again after they died.

ayoreo: Cojñócho ayoréode. Chi ayoré Aramato, cho otadábode. Ore chujé Yamochai ga chijaro ajnoquéode ga chisite ajnoquéode. Ga pésu ayoréode adode tiji jnese, Aramato. Pésu ayoré; chijaro ajnoquéode, Yamochai ajnoquéode. Chimate ga chibote ujñari aja ajei. Chimate ga ayoré. Chi chicháji ti. Chiese cojñone yocarãpi tu Yamochai ajnoquéode.
english: Moon went down another road. Moon was first to arrive, and he caused the Ayoré people to stay dead. “Ayoré people were like the non-Ayoré people at first,” the father told his little boy. “They killed *Yamochai (Monkey) and they ground his bones and sifted them into powder. “Lizard was an Ayoré before, and had legs. And Lizard took the powder made from the ground-up Monkey’s bones and made body parts from it. She finished and then she blew breath into them. When she finished doing this, it was a person. “It’s said that she put the first man to one side, and started to make the rest. “They say that non-Ayoré people and Ayoreos all were made from the Monkey’s bones.” The father said to his little son, “Animals originated from a very large woman. Bigger than anything.”

ayoreo: Cuchisode garané tu chequé cuchábia. Sué cuchade. Yocade sué cuchisode ore iji erámi. Chi chijire dejode ga chejna cuchade. Chi disírique chirote gajño. ‘*Codé á*, yi ga yujé jeta yirote.’ Chijna doji tome. Ga dacode chojninga: ‘Ajuse bero ome.’ Chejna dojode cucha. Chicho gajño, chimochẽre ógoro. Ga ajnangói u. Jecute yocade que chayo; chinoningase gu. *Oidábiadé – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988.* *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Oidábiadé atatagode udojo.*
english: The turtles were the largest animals in the world at that time. It’s said that,  just by looking at things around them they could destroy them. It’s said a little boy looked for Huge Turtle near the lake. He said: “*Codé a,*(Grandmother), I am going to kill Huge Turtle. I’ll wait for it by the lake.” He took his bow and arrows with him. The grandmother said: “You be careful.” He took all his arrows. He shot all of them them at the lake, and he broke Huge Turtle’s hip. And that is how it was wounded. That’s why turtles can’t run; when the boy wounded Huge Turtle, he changed it, and to this day turtles can’t run. *Related by Oidábiadé in Campo Loro, Paraguay (1988)**Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie.*

ayoreo: Ga jé ore cho derasṍri iji corãcha jnungór*i*. Chi corã́cha jnungóri u jochacai*(tatu cadeta)*iji quicujatígui uje ayoré nanique. Uje Chiquenone ore cho dedópasa iji jochacai ga ore chacãra raja.  A jé yo jochacai ome gajnéone corã́chac. Ayoré dacasutedie ore chugúji nanique ga ore chacãra raja uje chi corã́cha jnungóri tu jochacai iji quicujatígui uje chijócha jnumi. Chi ore edópasa iji quicujatígui enga ore chacãra raja. Ore chacãra raja ome úringai corã́cha jnungóri mu chi ca ayoré gajnarétique chacãra raja, uje dacasuté gu. Chi ayoré dacasutedie ore edopása u uje degu gajnengore ore.  Ga chi ore chacãra raja uje chi corã́cha jnungóri gotic iji quicujatígui. Mu chi ore chachẽre degúi enga gosi ca chajire rome iji dajnei degu ga gajnei corãchaque dépoji rĩ uje ore chimate gosi údode ga jé u uje ore chacãra raja. Uje ore igojnone (yumajosórone) – caratade, cojñone, cucha urosode – ore chimate ore ga jé u uje ore chacãra raja. Ore chacãra raja gu udojo. Chiquenone. Ore chacãrapo raja úringai corã́cha jnungóri iji darugúringane, uje ore chugúa cucha urósode yúode. Ga jé u uje ore chacãra raja, mu cuchade udore jiei ome yocáquiode. Ga jé ore chopo derásõri iji dedópasade rĩ. Ga ore cho derásõri iji ore ujode edópasade.  Ore chojninga: “Yujoi corã́cha jnungore.” Ore edopasade udojo jé u ore erásorone nanique iji guesode. Uje ore edopasa ga jé ore cho derásõri iji rĩ. Uje Chiquenone ore erásõri corãcha jnungóri ome dojode ayoré ore. Enga ore chacãra raja rĩ iji darunguringane ga ore chojninga: “Yacãra yu aja gajiné ome yajnetique corã́cha gajiné.” Ore chijnime dedópasai jmané – jé yo jne. Enga ayoréode ore datedie edópasa ga ujnacarode ore chacãra raja. Jé yacãco yógaja yocatedie ore erásorone, ore edopasade to nanique.
english: They consider the*weapon carrier*as their tool. The large armadillo was a*weapon carrier*in long ago times when animals were human. Since the*Chiquenone*claimed the large armadillo as a clan possession they follow it’s example.

ayoreo: Ore erásõre udé corã́cha jnungore adode ga u uje ore cho derásõri iji Chiquenone ore edópasa uqué ga ore edópasa queséode udore iji dati, jé aja guebéode. Ajiinerenie ore. Chiquenone edópasa guebéode udore; ome yocáquiode, ore edópasade jiei. Ore edópasa piguirané. Uje ayoréode ore chimochere gai nanique ga ore edópasa. Chequé bajedie nanique ore chojninga: piguirané. Ore edópasa guebéode rĩ udore iji dati. Ajinerenie jé aja guebéode udore jnese. Picanerane uyoque ga yoquedópasa udaji uacaguíjnane udáji, cochocoidie udaji; cochocoidie udiji ójo. Picanerane ore edópasa udiji ome yocáquiode ore. Ore chojninga: “Picanerane edópasa cochocoidie.” Uje chi Picanerane ore imané ójo nanique ga chi ore edópasa ójo.
english: They say: ‘I’m going to be like the armadillo and carry weapons.’

ayoreo: Ore edópasa*pajojo.*Picanerane ore edópasa tu*sioti*te rĩ. Uje ore ayo nanique uje chósi te iji ore enochai enga Picanerane edópasa. Picanerane edópasa*cuquébi*(un tipo de pájaro). (Oidabiadé cho quique: Ayoré nanique ga jeta baro*Patricia*potarása*Ayoré Gajnui*ga uyu ga yiji – mu*Noemia*abai tu ga yãratayipis ome*Juan*uhuaque enga dire uñai enga*Patricia*cha. Mu chugúpẽre uté u iji datí. Boquere chugúpẽre. Datedie ore chojningame dabode: “Aya dara, “*cuquébi a!*” Ujetiga abi que cha go enga daté chojninga: “¡*Cuquep*!”) Yoquedópasa*coyidé*(un tipo de pájaro) uje chojninga: “*Coyi-coyi-coyi*!” Agoyé u. Que yiraja ore edópasade, cucherane jnoji yáyipie gu. Jnuriminone ore edópasa pitoringai uje yitogoi. Chi ore edópasa pitoringai. Mu que yiraja Jnuriminone edopasade to. Ujeta gosi acoté toi ga abai chojninga: “Yimope cochocoi abubie sogójma yacoté uaqué to que.” Gaidi u uje chijnorapise dacoté ga chijna edópasa ingoningájnai taja. *Oidabiade – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988.* *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Oidabiadé atatagode udojo.*
english: Ayoré warriors when they worked themselves up to kill long ago would liken themselves to the armadillos, that’s because the armadillo was a*weapon carrier*in far off times and would dig into the ground to protect himself. They say, it was the*Chiquenone*clan possession in far off times and that’s why they would liken themselves to it.

ayoreo: *(Los nombres están en cursiva)*
english: *Pedópasade*

ayoreo: *Ajatiguei Étacõri*,*Jicai Chiquenoi*, ga ore guiyé cuchade iji erámone. Ga ore chicai ti, ga ore chise diri eruei. Mu chi Chiquenoi chacája. Doi guesi, ga chacája. Ga ore chise diri. Étacorone ore chise dejai eruei. Étacorone,*Ajatiguei*chajuque dejai. *Jicai*chajuque ijnangunua.*Ajatiguei*ga chise diri tudé iji uéchai. Étacõri chejna direcaringuei aja re ga edópasade u.
english: *Ajatiguei Étacõri*and*Jesai Chiquenoi*discovered the things in the world. And they went to far away places before they came to the edge of ‘day.’ But it was*Jesai Chiquenoi*that went ahead and entered ‘day.’ He went out and entered it. And so it was the*Chiquenoi**Clan*that discovered ‘day.’ The*Étacorone*reached the edge of night.*Ajatiguei*, who was a member of the*Étacori Clan*, crossed through the night*. Jicai*crossed through the darkness, also. But*Ajatiguei*kept on going through the darkness until he reached the ‘light of day’ on the other side of it, and that is why the*Étacõri*Clan took ‘light of day’ for its ‘clan possession.’

ayoreo: Chiquenone ore chise guebéode ga ore chejna aja re ga edópasai u.
english: The*Chiquenone*came upon iron, and that is why it belongs to them as a ‘clan possession.’

ayoreo: Chi chequé charẽi dutuedie. Ga tṍra dacaranone aja, ga uneripise. Mu jorane que chiraja garãtique cucha ga uneripise ãraquegode. Ájnamone chojninga: “Yiroco*Codé*. Ga yajico isocáringome jne.” Ájnamone ore chajire mu tṍra dacaranone aja daraquegué dutuei. Ga uniripise ichagája ga quene ore chiraja isocai. Ga chi uaté toi enga echoi garané tu uaté. Dire ejoi jnani bajade ga*Ducarajámené*,*Sugácadé*chucué ga ore chise. Ga di údi ga chojninga: “Yise cuchábi ipeya echujna uaté.”
english: It’s said a woman cooked squash. And she threw*‘snot’*into it, and it tasted very good. But her friends didn’t know what made her food so good. Her grandchildren said, “We will watch Codé (Grandma). We will see what she does to make her food taste good.” While her grandchildren watched, she threw*‘snot’*into the squash she was boiling. The food she prepared was really good but only they knew what she did to prepare it. They say that after the grandmother died and was buried, it was she who brought salt into existence in the area where she was buried. The next day some of the old timers named*Ducarajámené*and*Sugácadé*went looking for salt and they found it. They brought it back with them and said, “We have found something to suck on or to use for cooking. One of their wives cooked squash and seasoned it with the salt and it was very good. And their sisters said: “*Ducarajámedaté*, give some to us and we’ll use it to season the food we cook, too.” That is how the*Picanerai Clan*made salt their ‘clan possession’ for it was a*Picaneré*woman who discovered it. They really liked salt.

ayoreo: Ga acoté chãrei dutuedie ga chumute gai ga uneripise. Ga ore neronie chojninga: “*Ducarajámedaté*, asi yóguiji to ga yumuco yocãraqueguedie gai.” Jé u uje Picanerane cho dedópasade iji uje Picaneré diyaque. Ore chajmárapúsu.
english: It was in the town of*Igábi*where the Bolivian and the Paraguayan Ayoreos lived together; but then they fought each other. That’s when they separated and some went to Paraguay. And when the Ayoreos tell their descendants about what happened, they say to them: “But your grandfathers were all together as relatives at Igábi.”

ayoreo: Igabi u uje Bolivia, Paraguay ore chujé ñane ga ore chijna aja re Paraguay gosode. Ga uacáquiode ore chopise darása ñane iji Igábi. Bolivia eduguéjna cuchábi u*Ajugorócuchátique*. Paraguay eduguéjnane u:*Sugácadé Picanerai*.*Presidente*yuminóri tu.*Ducarajámené*ga guidoboi charisṍri u*Yacamaque*. Chiqueno*Yacamaque*.
english: The name of the big chief in Bolivia was*Ajúgorocuchátique*. The leader of the Ayoreos in Paraguay was*Sugácadé Picanerai*. He was almost like a president. The one who came after*Ducarajámené*was*Yacamaque*.*Yacamaque*was of the*Chiquenoi Clan*. (In Ayoré they say*Yacamaque*sat on the back of*Ducarajámené;*another way to say idiomatically*the one who followed him*was*Yacamaque.*) What was discovered by a clan or a clan member becomes the clan’s*edopasai*(it’s clan possession).

ayoreo: Ore chãraque dedopasade ome ñane.
english: Clan members may prohibit others the use of what they consider possessions of their clan. *pedópasade –*general term for ‘clan possessions’*edópasai –*clan possession (m)*edópasa –*clan possession (f)

ayoreo: *Oidábiadé – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988.* *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Oidábiadé atatagode udojo nanique.*
english: *Oidábiadé*–*Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988**Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie.*

ayoreo: Yocayode, yocatedie ore ga ore todo jnese rĩ. (Yuruode u udojo uje querénga yiraja yocayode tódode cucha to.) Enga yocayode ore chiquetame ore tódode cucha nanique. Ga jiei yocayode tódode cucha nanique. Jiepísi yocayode tódode cucha nanique. Ga disi ejode ica ga jé yajnángo ore quigade ome ore tódode cucha. Mu dire ejoi dicháca tudé que yitodógopo cuchade rĩ. Ga yirajágo udore ome cojñone uje que cucha puyátique cus ome Dupade. Ga cuchade choqui guesode ome yoque nanique ga je yitodógo cuchade nanique. Yitodógo nanique ga jé jno dádi ome yoque úringane pitócho uje yocáquiode chĩ́cara cuchade ga doi ore.
english: Both our fathers and our mothers had fears. (This is just what I say, though,  I don’t really know much about what our fathers used to fear.) What I do know is that our fathers tried not to sleep in the direction of the things they feared, and they would burn the things they feared. Very many were the things our fathers feared long ago. Very many were the fears of our grandfathers. For a long time we who were their descendants had the same fears.

ayoreo: Dejabi ore tagu yacorenie arione nanique iji Campo Loro. Enga tibibi dajorane enga chingo ore uje io jmainie, enga chojninga: “A ca yuru yijmanie jne. A yimo jne enga a ca yĩ́cara ga a ca yacorenie doi yu jne enga ca jno yúdi to jne. A ca cuchárique chosi ti iji yu jne.” Uruode chajuque dojode ayoréo agutedie. E ore mo jnese. Mu uje e dire enga ore chojninga: “Dejabí, ¿jé areque ua ya?” Enga Dejabí chajnai enga chojninga: “Jé areguipíse yu ãríe.” Mu unojo quigade enga jmainie dorite. Enga jorane jnanio ore chojninga: “Yacorenie a u jõ.” Mu Dejabi chojninga: “Que u go. A yocáquiode jmainie dorite nanique ga ore pésu daruode ujetiga dojórigo ayoré ore todo enga ore churu dajmainie uje ore tagu yacorenie arione.” Yocáquiode ore todopo*cabayúode*to rĩ, dajesúode, paode quiguebéode ijode uje chi doi ore ga chi ore tãre ome. Uricaitade uje ore chĩ́cara ore chuchi jnumone põ-põ-põ. Ore todo umutade (quiguebei) ga ore tajía ti yui ome arodie qui rĩ, uchade ore. Enga qué ore tódode uñéngo cuse uje jiépisi ore tódode iji montedie gu. Ore todopo anírenie rĩ uacachidedie*aquirájnanie*. Que ore cási ati uchátique déji ore gaterasúrique. Que ore casi ati ore cho degúji umutátic. Chi vácadie doi ore to uje yocáquiode gachidé u to nanique, ga chi ore chĩ́cara ga chiese ore chimo gaitique cucha uje ore gachidé u to nanique. Que ore casi ati pajiripite umatátique iji udore yocunirone udore. Enga yimóngo to nanique uje cojñone a ore chujé ñane iji yocunirone udojo nanique ga ore umutájna jiei. Ga yimóngo ore umutájnane ga jnacare bajaque chi qué ore chujé iji. Que yiraja ore to jnani bajade uñengo. *Oidábiadé – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988.* *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Oidábiadé atatagode udojo nanique.*
english: But in these days we no longer fear these things. That’s because we have observed the non-ayoreos who do not fear these things, because they believe in God. And now we see more clearly so we don’t fear things. But before that,  we continued passing on to other generations the fears of our grandfathers, because we believed what they told us about the animals coming to carry us off.

ayoreo: StartFragment
english: *Chugúperi Cho Dajñare*

ayoreo:
Chugúpẽri adode
u. Uje chujé ayoréode, mu ayoréode yiquetágopíse yocurasade to, chugúpẽri
yugode ayoré iji móntedie. Ga erásõri jec u de ica ga adode u uje jé cho
dajñáre iji udore:Doi uje yobéco erámi, uje yobéco erámi iji ti uje yicoi dejábi atíamone. Ga yipotingáco iji ti. Uje cho dajñáre. Yijnángo ti guesi uje cho dajñáre. Ga jé cho dajñáriji, ga yicagoi ta.
english: I will tell how I blowed on people when we were in the woods long ago to heal them. And it is taboo. It has to do with the Bird God. It could kill people, but we Ayoreos could heal our people who were victims of the Bird God in the woods. This was the way we could pacify the Bird and cause her to rest:

ayoreo: Cho dajñáriji uje yacágo ducayode. Yacágo ichade, ducayode, cóbidie ore.  Jé cho dajñáriji to.
english: It would happen when we’d rise early to go far into the woods. We’d rise early while it was still dark.   We’d file into the jungle while she was still resting. We’d go in chanting:*joño, joño, tíngui, tíngui, tíngui.*That would keep her calm as we went farther into the woods.

ayoreo: Cho dajñáriji udore uje yurúgome udaque yoquibáyode ga adi tu ome gueguerade. Cho dajñáriji to gueguerade.
english: In the same way, she could be pacified when we would wear our jaguar headdresses. We’d put on new headdresses, and long white neck feather adornments. Doing that would cause her to be pacified.

ayoreo: Ga yejnángo guesi ga yicoi udore uje yejnángo yocate ichádaja, utébeode. Uje yacágo cuchárigaja to. Ga chojninga: “Cuchárigo a chichaga raja yu é.  Cuchárigo a chichaga raja yu é.”
english: She would be pacified when we would wash our bodies and then blacken them by rubbing them with charcoal.

ayoreo: Uje yijnójo yocutaidode, yocate omone u. Uje cho dajñariji. Jé cho dajñariji uje que yiborárique pirac. Uje que yiborárique pirac. Ga chisi ome yoque to. (Jé adi turásu Dupade adi.) Mu chisíome daborade ome yoque to uje que pirac.)  Uje cho dajñáriji. Uje tẽrája ayoréode to. (Adi jé urásu Dupade adi to. Uje adi urásu.)
english: When we finished doing these things then we would fill round bags with new, clean things. As we put something in them, we would say: “Things will be promised to me! Things will be promised to me.” When we carry the things we ask for, they represent our good deeds. And the Bird is pacified. She is pacified when we have honey and other foods from the jungle that are not hard to recognize. When our crops aren’t hard to recognize, then she will give them to us.

ayoreo: *Íquide –  Puesto Paz, Bolivia – 1976**Timoteo Wyma churúsãre Íquide atatagode udojo*
english: *Íquede – Puesto Paz, Bolivia – 1970.*

ayoreo: Yicháji yuruode to. Anire, Marina gajnecho yurúrigo jne uje yicha yuruode aja máquina to iji de Dupade iguíjnai.
english: I’m going to add my words. My words are for Marina, that is why I’m putting them on the recorder here in God’s house.

ayoreo: Iyamaque uje ore chaca yedodie iji yodi nanique uje jé gapu yu nanique uje anire, ore tṍra yu aja nanique iji yodi. Ga yiraja uje anire, ore tṍra yu aja yodi nanique mu que yicháji yuruode aja to go jeta yatata yitestimonio.
english: It was a long time ago that I was baptized. When I was a young girl they threw me into the water. I have never yet told on the recorder about when they baptized me.

ayoreo: Mu yiraja uje yangureta Dupade ga yicháji yuruode aja to iji de iguíjnai. Iyamaque uje gapu yu nanique uje yangureta Jesus nanique, uje ore chaca yedodie iji to nanique uje gapu yu nanique. Mu e catadape yu.
english: But I know that I believed in God and now I’m putting my words on the recorder here in his house. It’s been a long time ago that I believed in Jesus when I was a young girl and when they baptized me as a youth. But now I’m*very short*in my older age.

ayoreo: Mu gusu uje jé que yanguretique cucha uñeque cuse go, mu gusu Dupade uje yangureta. Yiraja gu uje yangureta Dupade abi to ga e yicháji yuruode to iji de Dupade iguíjnai jeta Marina chudute to.
english: But I only want to tell you that I have never believed in anything else, only in God have I trusted. I know that I believe in God’s Son also, and I want to record that so that Marina can hear what I say here in God’s house.

ayoreo: Yirajápo rĩ uje anire, jecucha yabode toguebécho. Yabode jecucha toguebécho. A gadioque totígo yabode, mu que yiya go Dupade uruode. Que yiya Dupade uruode go uje yiraja gu uje yangureta Dupade nanique uje ore chaca yu iji yodi nanique. Ga yirajápo rĩ uje yiraja yu iji Dupade.
english: I realize that I have had trials, and my children always die. They always died. I have lost three children, but not even that has caused me to stop listening to God’s word. I’ll never stop listening to his word, because I am a believer and I have been baptized. And I also know that I’m sure of my relationship with God.

ayoreo: Jecucha ore naráraja maquina mu que yicháji yuruode aja to go iji de máquina iji iguíjnai iji Dupade iguíjnai. Mu yicháji to iji de. Yiraja yu gu ga jé yicháji to yuruode. Anire, jecucha yojninga: “¿Jé gotique gu a uje que yicháji yurúrigo to?”
english: People are always recording their words, but I never have until now put my words on the machine here in God’s house. Right now, I’m putting them on it. I am sure of my faith and that is why I’m doing it. I’m always telling myself: “Why is it I never have told about my faith and put it on the machine?”

ayoreo: Yaropie u uje iyamaque uje ore chicháji yedodie aja yodi nanique. Luis tṍraja yu aja yodi, ga anire, yojninga anire, iyamaque rĩ uje yicatecã́ri Dupade nanique iji de iguíjnai. Ore cana yibai, mu que yangári ore go. Que yangári uaque go. Jetiga yangaránenie jé u uaque uje uacaganáño yibai uje yatecã́ri Dupade. Uacaganáño yibai, mu gusu Dupade uje yangári, que yangári ore go.
english: It’s so very long ago that they put my face under the water. Luis (Louis Small) baptized me, and now, I can say that it’s also been a long, long time that I have been praying to God in the church. I’ve been laughed at, but I don’t pay attention. When you laugh at me, I don’t listen to you. As if you are my respected leaders, you who laugh when I pray. Go ahead and laugh at me, for God alone hears me. I ignore those who laugh at me.

ayoreo: Iyamaque rĩ uje jecucha yatoi ũroso rĩ ica ga yatoi ũroso ga, mu que yangári go. Satanas u gu uje pota jetiga yiya Dupade, mu que yiya go. Yojninga anire, yojninga Satanas su uje pota jeta yiya Dupade. Satanas su uje déji yu mu que chacája yajei go. Yojninga anire, a pota gu ujetiga yiya Dupade adode, mu que yipota go jetiga yiya.
english: It’s also been a long time that I have suffered headaches, but I ignore the pain. Satan wants to use the pain to make me stop following God, but I won’t stop. I tell myself, Satan is who wants to make me leave God. Satan wants to be in me, but he can’t get in. I say, it’s because he wants me to stop serving God that he gives me trials, but I do not want in any way to stop.

ayoreo: Yipéro nanique rĩ mu iyamácho uje que yipota go ujetiga yiya Dupade. Enga yitécu jnumone. Mu que toque go ujetiga cuchárique jeta chátaja yu. Gusu Dupade uje chátaja yu. Mu a Satanas pota gu jetiga yiya Dupade adode, mu que yiya go. Gusu Dupade gu uje anire, uje yangári, uje Dupade u uje yirajápo gatuaque rĩ.
english: I was crippled a while back and couldn’t walk, but that didn’t make me want to give up on God. I had to crawl on the ground to get around. Nothing would help me. Only God could help me. That was another trial Satan brought my way to make me stop following God, but I didn’t stop at all. Only God is there for me, and he hears me, and he helps me, and I am getting to know him better through my trials.

ayoreo: Uje anire, que yicadígui go uje ore tṍra yu aja yodi to nanique. Ga que yiya go. Gusu uje anire, que yugúsiji udojo iji ujade to Dupade iguíjnai, ga yajnai data ga, a gajnui tu. Ise ga Dupade gajnui tu aniri, disi dacabi udojo uje yajnángoji data, ga jé ugúsi de iguíjnai to, que yicadígui go. A gajnúone u disi dacabode Dupade gotic.
english: And so, I do not forget when I was baptized to show that I believed. Since then I have continued following Jesus. The only thing is, I sometimes have not been well enough to come to God’s house for meetings, and had to stay home, but that’s the way it is. I think that God can be compared to a child’s friend that goes with him wherever he goes, and when I’m here or not, he is with me. This God of ours that we have never seen is our father and our friend.

ayoreo: Yirasique yu uje ore chujé tãrara ga cho ujetiga cho disiode uje yoquirasique yocabode yui uje yajnángoji data. Yirasique yui uje ore chujé tãrara. Yisárase cuchade mu yojninga, ‘E yiya jne.’ Yiya ga yajnai to jne Dupade iguíjnai udé. Chãje yirasique yui gu. Gajnui u disiode uje yajnángoji datá ga yoquirasique udé Dupade iguíjnai.
english: I’m happy when they ring the bell for church, and it’s like the kind of happiness we felt when we were children and went about with our friends. I look forward to them ringing the bell. I am about to start doing something, but when I hear the bell, I say, I’m going to do this later. I stop what I’m doing and take off for God’s house. It’s because I’ve been happily looking forward to it. I’m like a child getting together with my friends, that’s how happy we are to be going to church.

ayoreo: Jé yiya yuruode. E yiya.
english: This is all my words. I will stop.

ayoreo: *Pininaquei irágu yu uje ore chojningame yu: “Bo, ga yicagóji Pangarádejnai Dupade iguíjnai.” (Salmo 122:1)*
english: *I was glad when they said to me,**“Let us go to the**Lord**‘s house.” (Psalm 122:1)*

ayoreo: *Betidaté – Tobité, Bolivia –**08/23/75.* *Marina de Morarie churúsãre uruode nanique enga cháta Dupade uruode aja to.*
english: *Betidaté’s – Tobité, Bolivia – 08/23/75**Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie**Scripture portion added.*

ayoreo:
Yipota jetiga yisi ome yu ome
Dupade to. E yẽra yu to. (*Romanos 12:1)*
english: *“Offer yourselves as a living sacrifice to God, dedicated to his service and pleasing to him. This is the true worship that you should offer.” (Romans 12:1)* I wanted to give myself to Dupade. To give myself to Him and hold nothing back.

ayoreo:
Que
yicadígui go uje don Juan*(Howard
Morarie)*, Germán (*Sherman Miller)*ore
chaca yedodie iji yodi nanique. Yirasique Dupade Uruode to.
english: I will never forget when don Juan and German*(Sherman Miller)*planted my eyes in the water long ago.

ayoreo:
Mu
yetei déji yu uje que yicatecã́ri Dupade to go. Yirasique Dupade Uruode to. Mu que yicatecã́ripísi
Dupade to go, mu yirasique, mu ejoi udé u uje e yẽra yu to ome Dupade. Yipota
jetiga e guesája yui to. Yipota jetiga e guesája Dupade yui ome yu to. Uje e
yẽra yu. E yẽra yu ome Dupade yuruode to. Yirasique Dupade uruode to.
english: I loved Dupade’s Word. But I had something I didn’t like and that was that I never prayed, even though I loved his Word. But for some reason, I never prayed to Dupade, even though I loved him. But just recently I “sold” myself to Dupade. I wanted access to Dupade and to know he was available to me. I wanted Dupade to open a way for me to reach him after I’d given myself over to him.

ayoreo:
Uje yetaque gu uje que
yicatecã́ri Dupade to, mu ejoi udé u uje e yẽra yu to ome Dupade. Jecucha
yicatecãcoi Dupade nanique, mu ejoi udé diríca ga, que yicatecãrápoi Dupade to
rĩ. Mu ejoi upo rĩ ga e yẽra yu. Yẽra yu ome Dupade Uruode to iji de ejoi.
Ipota gu jeta guesája gadoi to ome Dupade. Yipota jetiga jé Dupade sué iji
yajei to jne.
english: I didn’t like myself very much because I wasn’t talking to Dupade, but maybe, now that I’ve surrendered to him, I will pray. I want to draw near to Dupade. I want Dupade to grow inside of me.

ayoreo:
*Ujai*jecucha
chetaque yu ome ica uje yijo*conferencia*,
mu yojninga: “Que uyu ua go, uje unejángue yu, ga yijo.”
english: *Ujai*didn’t like me going to meetings, but I would say: “You’re not me and I am different from you, so I go.”

ayoreo:
Mu*Ujai*potarása
ica ga chicáji*conferencia*to. Que
pota to iji ejode udojo mu a déji to jne. Déji dínique to jeta pota Dupade Uruode
to. E pota to gu. A irasé to jne. E pota iglésia gu jetiga e chise ajei.
Chetaque ica mu e pota to.
english: But then,*Ujai*would want to go to meetings with me. But lately he hasn’t been going. One day he’ll want Dupade’s Word also. At least he’s open to it and likes it now. He used to be very opposed to it.

ayoreo:
Que yetétique cuse go iji
Dupade. Yirasiguipise. Chamáñaje e yicatecã́ri gu.*(Santiago
4:8)*
english: There is nothing I don’t like about Dupade. I love him. That is because I now talk to him.

ayoreo: *Cótade – Tobité, Bolivia – 1975**Marina de Morarie churúsãre Cótade uruode udojo nanique*
english: *“Draw near to God, and he will draw near to you.”**(James 4:8)*

ayoreo: Imo diríca enga e dejaque. Enga icatecã́ri Dupade bajac uje imóngo uje e dejac enga que imo to iji dejai údode. Yúdode urõso, enga dejnínga ga cuchárique a chiqueta raja yu. Basája ne, yibai tiji ne, enga chojninga: “Jec ũré yu.”
english: *Dejnínga ga cuchárique a chiqueta raja yu*

ayoreo:
Tõria yibai.
Chojninga: “Ayoré á, ¿araja yu a?” chojninga.
english: I went to bed last night and it was dark.

ayoreo:
Enga ojninga:
“Iraja ua.”
english: I prayed to*Dupade*first before we went to sleep because it was dark. But I couldn’t sleep all night long. My body hurt and at midnight something appeared to me. It came down upon me and said: “I am the truth.”

ayoreo:
Enga
chojninga: “Uyu ¿gosi te e?” chojninga. Ojninga:
“Iraja ua enga ísenga Jesus gotique u uámia.”
english: Uninvited, it approached me. It said: “Ayoré Woman, do you know me?” That is what it said.And I said: “I know who you are.”

ayoreo:
Enga
chojninga: “Baraja yu. ¿Jé ca araja Yapade to a?” chojninga. Enga ojninga:
“¡Ẽje!”
english: And it said: “Who am I?” That’s what it said.

ayoreo:
Mu chojninga:
“Acatecã́ri Yapade ome, jeta araja Yapade,” chojninga. Enga icatecã́ri enga ojninga iji dejai tiji yurigode: “Dupade é, qué icatecã́ri ua iji ne. Yacaranguipise uje babi Jesus chiqueta raja yu, enga catecã́ri yu ome, enga qué icatecã́ri ua ome yu to uje urõsoringuéone uásejna iji yu ujeta átajabá aja yu to,” chiese yojningame Dupade iji yurigode. “Átajape yejnaréngone aja yu,” ojninga iji yurigode ome Dupade. “Yacaranguipise uje babi Jesus chiqueta raja yu,” ojninga.
english: I said: “I know who you are. Who I think you are, dear one, is Jesus, although I’ve never seen you.”

ayoreo:
Enga gajnerĩ enga*Ichajúidé*e diósi. Mu Jesus chojninga: (chaquĩ́rasi tiji garani) “A yajúrãi ta, yajúrãi te, ajúrãi, ajúrãi,” chojningame*Ichajúidé*. Enga chiese,
aníri, gajnerĩ, enga chojninga: “¡*Ichajúidé*é!”
english: And he said: “You do know me. Do you know my Father, also?” he asked me.

ayoreo:
E chirõrosi
enga chojninga:*“¡Venid! ¡Venid! ¡Venid!”*chi, Jesus chojningame*Ichajúidé*. *Ichajúidé*tibágui enga Jesus chigarúsu*Ichajúidé*enga chigarúpo yu to rĩ enga
chojninga: “Yacaranguipise ayorábi á, ¿ja araja yu to a?” chojninga.
english: And I said: “Yes!”

ayoreo:
Enga*Ichajúidé*chojninga: “Iraja ua enga
Dupade ap ua enga Jesus u ua,” chi Ichajúidé chojninga. Chi, uté
chojninga uje ore cãra darúo. Jec ore cãra daruode.
english: But he said: “Pray to my Father, if you know my Father.” This is what he said.

ayoreo:
“Jec ũré enga uacarajáyo Yapade,” chiese
Jesus chojninga. Chujé yógaja jnumone ome darúo.
english: And, in my dream that night, I prayed, and I said: “*Dupade*! I’m talking to you right now. I’m very thankful that your Son Jesus appeared to me and talked to me about you, and that is why I am talking to you about myself, because I’m in lots of pain and I ask you to help me.” This is what I seemed to say to*Dupade*in my dream.  “Do something about my sickness, please,” I said in my dream, praying to*Dupade*. “I’m so thankful that your Son Jesus appeared to me,” I said to him.

ayoreo:
Ore cháji
gaté yui, que ore cháji jnumi gotique. Enga o ta ta
ta ta. Ujnusína uje ayoré yu mu Jesus chiqueta raja yu uje chiese, anire,
chojninga: “Uacarajáyopise Yapade. Acatecãchobi enga yangarámi uagome uacaru
ojningai.”
english: And right then, dear*Ichajúidé*arrived. But Jesus spoke to him, counseling him as he was coming. He said: “A yajúrãi ta, yajúrái te, ajúrãi, ajúrãi,” he said to*Ichajúidé*.*(Note: This seems to be a chant, words I do not know.)*

ayoreo:
Icatecãcoi iji yurigode enga icatecã́ri enga ojninga: “Dupade, átajape yurigode aja yejnaréngone aja yu,” chiese ojninga. “Uásejna yejnaréngone. Yacaranguipise uje diyajmánguipíse uje ipésu badode to rĩ, diyajmángapo yejnaréngone rĩ,” chiese ojninga. Chojninga:
“Yacaranguipise. E iraja baruode. Gome,” chiese chojninga.
english: And then as*Ichajúidé*came nearer, Jesus called his name: “¡*Ichajúidé*á!”:

ayoreo:
Chiese*Ichajúidé*catecãrápoi to rĩ, mu que
yiraja*Ichajúidé*uruode. (Ore cãra
darúo. Jec ore cãra daruode.) E gusu yuruode, Señorítabia. Ta jé yuricho diríca. Qué déji yu mu jé cho yojñane uje uásejna.
english: And*Ichajúidé*now went toward him and Jesus called to him: “Come. Come. Come.” This is what Jesus said to*Ichajúidé*.

ayoreo:
Atatáji to
uje chojninga, chiese Jesus chojninga: “¿Ja ayorérique déji siñeque to a?
ujetiga ore chiraja yu,” chiese chojninga. Enga ojninga:
“Ore déji ta.”
english: When*Ichajúidé*came up to him, Jesus embraced him, and he put his arms around me also. And he said to*Ichajúidé*: “I’m so thankful for you, dear Ayoré Person; do you know who I am?” he asked.

ayoreo:
Mu
chojningame yu: “Atata gotodie ayoredie.” Mu chiese
ojninga: “Que yatata ore uje idája ore iji yu. Jé cachi ca araja ore íode.”
english: And*Ichajúidé*said: “I know you, you are*Dupade*’*s*Son, you are Jesus.”

ayoreo:
Mu Jesus
chojninga: “Yiraja. Acatecã́ri Yapade enga atata ore íode rĩ ome Yapade, enga
yiraja ore íode,” chiese chojninga.
english: This is what*Ichajúidé*seemed to say to him.

ayoreo:
Enga yo yojñane diríca. Uje qué yiya iji te, enga o ta ta ta, enga*Ichajúidé*chaguesi te, enga chojninga: “Acatecã́ri Dupade to iji yocachabie tuté enga icatecãcoi Dupade iji yocurúrigo.” Enga
chojninga: “Irajapise uaque. Ca acadíchoi yu jne,” chiese chojninga.
english: And that was what*Ichajúidé*said to Jesus and then they spoke privately.

ayoreo: E gome, Señorita. Señorítabía, ango*Daniélné*ujeta ese Zapoco uje daté qué eca to,*Pajéidé*acoté.*Danielnacode*iji ta.*Guidaigosode*to enga chi ojninga: “Yujnusiétigade uásejnapise ome ore iji datá,*Enuei*ore.*Yacamai*que ore jnoji yajei,” yojninga. Ojningame ore uje*Daniélnacode*,*Pajeidé*acoté uruode u udojo. E gusu. *Ucuetódacóde – Zapaco, Bolivia – 1970**Janet Briggs churúsãre Ucuetódacóde atatagode udojo nanique*
english: And then, Jesus said to us: “Truly, you both do know my Father.”

ayoreo: A yicháji yimoringare cuchade aja: Yico nanique ga yijoranie ore jno. Ga*Toqué*ore, ga ore chojninga: “Ajína ore to jne.” Ga yiji ga yijína ore. Enga que yajire yijõra*Juse*gaté enga yajni. Yajni ga anire, jnacarode bajade tṍrai yoque iji ta.
english: We were going to leave and my friends went first.*Toqué*was among them and others said to me: “Go with them.” So I went with them. But I didn’t see my friend*Jusé*so I decided to go back. And as I was going back some of the young men met up with us. When they saw I was determined to go back they left me.

ayoreo: Enga yajni ga ore chijíñome yu uje yajníngo. Yajníngo. Ducosi tagu yóguiji ta ga yitoi. A yo sẽre mu a yiquecapo rĩ. Enga yicṍraja ta. Ga yijosa yedodie aja jnumi. Yĩriji enga yóji yiyojnai iji ti.
english: I got a fever and I passed out. I probably fainted, because when I came to I was flat on my face on the ground laying in my own blood.

ayoreo: Yijócha iji poria tajei. Yicha iji poria tajei enga yóji ga, ga yiji. Yijína yodábi ga chicha poria tajei ga yiya. Ga yiji yimochádi ga yimóji. Mu dire ga yiji. Yicai de ga ayoré do yu. Yajna yurásade iji ti, móntedie. Yise ore iji ti uje godogape yu. Ga yĩri mu godogué yu. Yĩri iji yurásadie.
english: I knew I needed water, so I dug around inside a tree. I found water accumulated between the branches and I drank, and then I left. I took some water that was in the tree with me.

ayoreo: Yajire yiquetai mu a toi dúrasede. Ga yajire mu a íjnoque pioi iji dojo. Yajire mu a toi dúrasede. Cachi yiquetaque pioi. Ga yiji. Yedu erámone uje gusu yu. Que yurásaraque erámoro cus. Gusu yu iji erámone ga yedu erámone.
english: But I was so weak that  I made a bed and slept. When it was day I left again but I soon realized I was lost. I didn’t know where I was. I tried to follow my friends. I found where we had camped. By this time I was very weak and had lost a lot of weight. I knew I was going in the right direction, though, because I recognized that my friends had been there.

ayoreo: Mu ore eruguéjnane iji erámone. Jé gusu yu uje yitoi diríca gu, mu yiqueta yu. Yajna yurásadie iji erámone. Yĩri ji toniñeque uje godogape yu.
english: I looked at the fire I had lit, but it was completely dead. So I looked around but there was no other fire. All of the fires had been extinguished.  I made myself a fire. I rested a while, and then I left in search of my friends.  It was only me and the jungle. I was all alone. There wasn’t even a deer or any other animal in sight.  I was alone in the woods, and I only had the woods around me.

ayoreo: Yujé yocai mu sĩri (*español: cojo*) mu jé yagu. Yiraja uje sĩri mu jé yagu. Godogué yu gu. Yise ayoredie gajnerĩ ga yijíñome yajuránone iji (*español: tapeque*).
english: But there were some big holes someone had dug there in the woods. I was all alone. This was after I had passed out, and brought myself around again. So I tried to follow the tracks of my friends again. I had nothing to eat and was very skinny from hunger.

ayoreo: Ga yojninga: “A yĩri jne iji ayoredie. Ga yĩri. Yise ore irábode cutérone iji te ga yumuña, mu e ore déji yejoi. Ga ore déji yejoi ga yimo ore iji ta, ga ore chimo yu. Yĩríji uque ga yatata uje yimo iji ti oródi  mu ore déji ejoi.”
english: I found a lame turtle, which I killed and ate it in spite of it being lame. I knew I shouldn’t have because it was crippled, but I ate it anyway. I was starved. So I made a lunch of the turtle, but when I reached the women I threw my lunch away.

ayoreo: *Umaridaté*chojninga: “¡*Jocha*casicaite de a tí!”
english: I said to myself, “I’ll soon reach the other women.” And I did. I found where they had gotten honey, and I found some also and carried it with me. I saw them at a distance. I saw them and right away I saw that they had seen me too. When I arrived I told them I had slept where someone had made a grave, and at that time I had no idea how close they were.

ayoreo: Mu oródi déji ejoi. Yibo uje*Jusé*poji yejode uje edoparé. Yuáre iji yejode. Ga yibo to. Yujnusina yapade enga yibo nanique.*Jusé*po yejode enga yuáre ga yibo to. Chosõre yu.
english: *Umaridaté*announced my arrival and said: “**Jocha*who was lost, there she is!”

ayoreo: Yipísirásiji yiramai. Yĩri*Asomedái*mu ore chãraque jogadi. Ga*Jusé*chiroque yu aja dajogadi. Chojninga: “Ajni, ga be baterama jogadi a.” Ga yuare. Ga yibo to.
english: But there was a grave near by. I cried when*Jusé*mourned for me for she was blind. And I mourned for her. And I cried. I remembered my father and I cried. Jusé cried for me and I cried and mourned. She felt sorry for me. I thought I could stay with my uncle, but when I got there my uncle,*Asomedái*, wouldn’t let me stay with them. So*Jusé*sent me to where she was staying and I stayed with her.

ayoreo: Mu chojninga: “Aya. Godogué ua. Ca abo,” chojninga*Jusé*.
english: She said to me, “Go back and stay with your aunt.”

ayoreo: *Enuedaté – Tobité, Bolivia – 1970.* *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Enuedaté atatagode udojo nanique.*
english: That made me cry. And I was so sad. But Jusé said: “Stop crying. You are in bad shape. Don’t cry.” And then she tried to comfort me.

ayoreo: *Enueidaté bijna nanique enga chise yodi iji poridie:*
english: *Jocha – Enuedaté’s name before she had children *Enuedaté – Tobité, Bolivia – 1960**Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie*

ayoreo: Ore chaquĩ́ra nanique ga*Ajópedaté*chi chaquĩ́ra. Ga chicai ti degúi uéchai. Chajire*Gutódaté*ujñarone. Chi chojninga: “Bajñarone jiei. Cho*Gusúdaté*ujñarone.” Ga chejnáreque enga ore pojna ñane. Chejnáreque darejna*(hermana mayor)*ujñarone enga po. Enga ore pojna ñane.*Ajópedai*chejnáreque enga chirota dacotedie*(sus cuñados)*. Pojna*Guiéjnanái*to.
english: In those days they would counsel one another long ago,  and on one occasion it was*Ajópedaté*who counseled a fellow woman. She went to the other side of camp. She looked at*Gutódaté*and saw how she was breathing. She said: “You’re really breathing hard. It’s like the way Gusúdaté breaths.”

ayoreo: *Guejnane*jé déji dabatigai. Enga ore chojninga, chequedíe ore chojninga, urátadie: “Achai bayé jne iji gaté. Enga ojninga jne, ‘*Ajópedé*pojna daté ore.’”
english: And*Gutodaté*was offended and they started bawling each other out. She was offended by her older sister’s breathing and she began to cry. And they began to fight.

ayoreo: Enga*Guiéjnané*pojna*Ajópedái*. Chi ore chujérase mu que ore chujé go. Chi ore pojna ñane bisidec.
english: *Ajópedái*was offended and he began defending his sisters-in-law. He scolded*Guiejnanai.*

ayoreo: Mu yicorási, yicagorási iji dore. Mu ore chajna yoc ga ore chujé yoque jne iji ta.
english: *Guejnane*was honey hunting. And they said, the women (their wives) said: “Go to your father.” And tell him: “*Ajópedé*is bawling out his mother.”

ayoreo: *Uayé*chi chojninga: “*Ajocha*ica tagúji diríca ga a qué ore déji úbireraque*(un nido).”*
english: And*Guiejnane*scolded*Ajópedái.*And they almost came to blows, but didn’t hit each other. They just fought with words.

ayoreo: Mu ãrapatague*Uayé*. Jarie*(mentira)*. Yajníngo uje ãrapatague ome pocaningai iji ta. Chi chojninga: “*Ajópedé*pojna uaque diríca. Ga ore chajni gai*Ajópedái*enga ore chujérase.”
english: But we were about ready to leave and go somewhere else. But they followed us and began to hit us.*Uayé*said: “*Ajocha*’s stomach hurt and they are probably now ‘laying low.’

ayoreo: *Duábidácabi*chi chĩ́cara ore iji dajei. Chi chojninga: “Yĩ́cara ga cho daijnai*Ajópedé*.” Enga ore chedu ujetiga ore chujé. Chi ore déji yoquiquigade*(dialecto diferente: queyiquigade).*Jé*Ajópedái*chiraja uje ore déji yoquiquigade enga ore chujé yoque dirome. Mu chaca*Ajódedái*iji ta. Chajna cutérone iji degújnai*(el campamento que tenía ayer)*. Chicai te. E yajnángo dajei. Jé chaca. Chicai te. Mu yico degúi enga yicoi doridie.
english: But U*ayé*was lying. She was a liar. We returned because she was lying about the fight going on. And she said: “*Ajópedé*bawled you guys out, and now they are turning on*Ajópedái*and about to come to blows.

ayoreo: Gusu disiode iji degúi. Yugúcoi ta doridie. enga ore pijnane chijinasía yocácabi iji. Enga ore jos eti yugúcoi ti. Ore chinguíra dabijnanone aja yocácabi. Yugúcoi doridie. Yimóngo iji ta. Yudúco ore pijnane. enga yiquejnángome ore. Yiquejnágome ore ga –
english: *Duábidácabi*dreamed about them. He said: “I had a dream about*Ajópedé*that he was a medicine man. And they talked about killing him.”

ayoreo: *Dujuráidacode*chajni ore gai. Yajníngo jocaratade gai iji degúi.*Ajópedatéa*(la ‘a’ final significa que ya está muerta) oré. Mu yiquejnángo. Yiquejnángo jnese.*Ijnerainatea*, gata, jé chijína yoc, aja*Ojo*uqué casecaite yocungaro*(hermano menor)*. Ga yiquejnángo ome jocaratade. Aja*Jésedaté*, aja*Sigaidaté*.
english: And they were following behind us. And that is how*Ajópedái*knew that they were behind us and they would try to kill us the next day. And then he entered the jungle*.*He went after honey at the camp that he’d made before. He went toward it. And we followed him and he went farther into the jungle. But we went to the camp to get the*doridie**fruit*.

ayoreo: Jé garedie chajni ore gai iji degúi. Ga yico ga yicagoi tõõ. Yiquejnángome jocaratade ga yicagóji ¡tĩĩĩĩ! Mu*Ajópedé*chaca que. Tṍraji te ga yṍrangopísiji. Yijoco jocaratade aja. Jé*Ajópedé*a u. Mu yṍrangopísiji. Ga ore chaquĩ́ra. Ore chojninga,*Ajópedái*chojninga: “A yi ore iji degúi jne. A yingome*Guiéjnané*ore jne.” Uje pojna*Garáijnai*ore diríca.*Garáijnai*aja*Tocoidé*ore. Pojna ore údode diríca. A yibágui ore. Enga a yingome ore jne uje ore dorase yu ga ore chujérase yu. Mu a yipatíora yitique jne.*(*Mi víctima y yo, perezeremos juntos.*)”*
english: Only the children were in the camp. And we kept getting*doridie fruit*and then we heard people calling to our grandpa. And they thought we were there. They kept calling loudly to our grandpa. We were off getting*doridie*. We slept out there. We heard their calls and ran away. We were running and then*Dujuráidacóde*turned toward them. We returned to the fighters in the camp.  **Ajópedatéa*and them were there. But we kept running, all of us.*Ijnerainatéa*, who was an old lady at the time, came with us with*Ojo,*our little sister. And we kept running from the warriors.*Jésedaté, Sigaidaté*were with us.

ayoreo: Yiquejnángome jocaratade ga*Ajópedái*chise yóguiji ta. Mu jé gaidirase ore iji degúi. Mu*Oguéa*,*Usigaidatéa*ore chaquĩ́ra. Ore chojninga: “Ácari te enga bo.”
english: Two returned to the camp. And we went far away. We kept running from the warriors and ran far away.

ayoreo: Mu*Jnatújnai*chijinarása ore. Ore chicarásiji te. Ore chicarásiji udojo. Yocurasade, Jnupedógosóde ore chicarásiji darengúi aníri gotique.*Cuyadai*, chi: “A yi jne. Yicáji ore jne ga yicobi ore to jne. Yujégo ore to jne.”
english: But*Ajópedé*entered and came out and we were frightened. We thought he was the warriors! But it was only*Ajópedé*. But it really frightened us. But they comforted us and said,*Ajópedé*said: “I’m going back to camp and I’ll tell*Guiéjnane*and them. For they were the ones that had the altercation with*Garáijnai*and them yesterday. With*Garáijnai*and*Tocoidé*and them. He really bawled them out. I’ll go to them and tell them that they almost caught me and killed me, but that I’m ready to die with my victim, if it comes to that.”

ayoreo: Yiquejnángome ore. Mu yajníngo to enga yico jocaratade iji ta.*Bajébiadái*ore u.*Bajébiadái*chojninga: “Ca abo,” chojninga, “*Jochá*, ca abo. Anire, que chugué uaque gotique. Que chugué uaque gotique,” chojninga.
english: We ran from the warriors and*Ajópedái*caught up with us. But he was on his way to the camp. But*Oguéa*and*Usigaidatéa*counseled him not to go. They said: “Stay here and run away from them.”

ayoreo: Mu taque. Mu yojninga: “¿Ja ca ore chujé*Jécude*uaqué que a?”
english: But*Jnatújnai*was about to go with them, and would have gone. Our group the*Jnupedógosóde*were about to go to their camp.*Cuyadai*said: “I’m taking off. I’m going to them and I’m going to kill them.”

ayoreo: Mu chojninga: “A ca ore chujé que.” Mu a ore chujé durasede, enga ore chaquesu etabi. A*Popi*chujé ga chaquesu etabi. Ga doi ti. Doi dai iboca údi. Ore chujé. Ga yibo. Yáyipie déji iapai ga yibo. Yocanique a déji ore iji nojo, Jnupedógosóde. Enga yibo uje ore chujé yoque. Ore chujé*Ajópedé*rase. Mu que ore chujé. Que ore chujé codé, yáquiode uñeque gotique uje ore todo yocai yúode enga que ore chujé gotique.
english: We ran from them. And as we were returning the way we had come, we ran into the warriors.*Bajébiadái*and them.*Bajébiadái*said: “Don’t cry,” he said, “**Jochá*, don’t cry. You won’t be killed. No one’s going to kill you,” he said.
