ayoreo: Enga*Pajéidaté*ore noñáji erámi*(*se quedaron*).*Enga ore jnoji erámi.*Tujnungue*,*Jusigai*ore. Ore déji dire uñai enga ore chujérase*Tujnungue*. Iguiosode chujérase. Chiese ore chipese*Ajópedé*iborárigo iji sañeque mu que ore chise go. A noñáji erámi uje ore chijócãre. Yiríngo iji degúi. Mu yajico ti uque mu ore chujé*Jécudé*enga déji ti. Ore chaquesu etabi. Ore chejna yocugúchade. Ore pogurásu yugúchade uje ore chijna uje ore todo yocai yui.
english: But he was lying. But I said: “Didn’t they fight with*Jecude*and you guys?”

ayoreo: Ore chojninga: “Ajnáño orugúchade enga apóchopúsu jne jeta ajnáño orugúcharigo.”
english: But he said: “They didn’t kill him.” But they had already killed him and they had cut off his head. It was*Popi*who killed him and cut off his head. He was out there and he had taken his father’s gun. And they killed him. And I cried. I kept thinking about my father and I cried for my father.  They almost killed*Ajópedé.*But they didn’t kill him. They didn’t kill grandma and grandpa either for they were afraid what our father would do to them and so they didn’t kill them.

ayoreo: Chejna yocugúchade. Yocácabi*(nuestro abuelo)*chojninga: “Yijnime yúringáringo jnacare jõ ome ore uje ore cho dajócaningame uyu. Que picasite yu diríca uje jnacare yu.”
english: And*Pajéidaté*and them stayed in the jungle.*Tujnungue*and*Jusigai*and them went into the jungle. They were planning to kill*Tujnungue*. Her relatives wanted to kill her. They said that they had gotten honey from*Ajópedé*somewhere else, but they weren’t even there. They stayed in the jungles.

ayoreo: Ore chejna yocugúchade enga ore chijna*Usigáidatéa*ujúbei ore.*Jusédacóde*dóji ore gari ga chumuña. Mu chijíñome ga cho parac enga ore chijnapo rĩ, jocaratade.
english: We arrived back in camp. But when we looked around we saw that they had killed*Jecude*and he was laying over there. They had beheaded him. They had robbed all of our things. They hid my things that they took because they were afraid of our father.

ayoreo: *Enuedaté – Tobité, Bolivia – 1970.*
english: They said: “Take their things but hide them well.”

ayoreo: *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Enuedaté atatagode udojo nanique.*
english: They took everything we had. Our grandpa said: “I wish I could have been a young man again when they came against me. No one could overcome me when I was a young man.”

ayoreo: Yachigo chugúpẽre nanique ga icoji degúi. Ga*Acensiadái*: “A yi jne. Yibágui echoi casicaite jne. (Ome dabia*Yaride*.) Uje yosõre*Yaride*uje chipese yotué.” Ga chi: “Yi ga yibágui echoi jne.”
english: We had just finished worshiping the bird god when we left our camp. And*Acénsiadái*said: “I’m going to leave. I’m going to the salt beds that we frequent.

ayoreo: Ga yico ga yibájoi echoi. Ico dajere casicaite. Dajere casicaite. Yimóngo yojñane echoi yui mu yimóngoji. Mu dire ga*Acensiadái*casicai chojninga: “A yi jne. Yibágui echoi jne. A guérique basosi jne ga a gajnárengapo jne rĩ, echoi.”
english: He said this because of his daughter*Yaride*. “I feel sorry for*Yaride*because all she has to suck is pure water.” And he said: “I’m going to get some salt for her from the salt beds.”

ayoreo: Yico echoi mu*Guidaigosode*ore icóbidie iji. Ore icóbidie ga itodógopise. Ore tujúna*Guidaigosode*icóbidie. Enga ijnójo echoi. Upo rĩ*Guidaigosode*. Ichajója yoquipedie echoi.
english: And later we left and went to the salt beds. We reached the road we always went on. We saw the salt beds that I referred to above, but we slept first. At first light*Acénsiadái*said: “I’m leaving now. I’m going to the salt beds. It’s going to rain and the salt will be ruined, that salt way over there.”

ayoreo: Mu icagoi ga imóngo iji ta. Mu dire ga yico ga anírami chojningasia,*Pongora*dáyabí: “Boyo ga abásoi sañeque jne. Mu yocatigai u ejórique gajnerĩ.”
english: We reached the salt beds but the*Guidaigosode*had already been there; we found a feather necklace they had left behind. It was a long white feather necklace, and we were really afraid. They started putting it around their necks.  We began carrying the salt we had filled our bags with; and the*Guidaigosode*had taken salt too.

ayoreo: E ico ga ibácoi ta. Enga icopo rĩ ga yocatigai uné. Ga e yicoi cuchade iji dati, chicorone. Mu imo cutẽri iji ebédua enga*Garáijnaidái*chijno. Ga yipéco.
english: And then we left and slept again. At daylight we left again and*what’s-his-name*said,*Pongora dáyabí*said: “Come on, we’ll stop later. And then the next time we stop, we’ll eat.”

ayoreo: E yiríngopo to rĩ iji ta yoquiyaridi. E ore íjnongaja erámi ome doridie. Uñane chi chimo orojno*(un tipo de abeja*) ga ore dopoi rĩ. Anire casicaite ore chojninga: “Qué jedóji que. Yicoi jeta guedé doi ti enga a degúi u ejórique jne. Yijnojorase echoi to.”
english: So we took off and stopped farther off. And then we left again,  and when we stopped again we ate. And we dug up some water plants. And then we saw there was honey in the tree called*ebédua*and*Garáijnaidái*chopped into the trunk of the tree for it. And then we sucked honey.

ayoreo: E ore jno ga ore basiji ta, mu e yicopo rĩ. E ore chise degúi iji te bajade. Enga*Garáijnainái*chachai yu rĩ, ga yisíome yi ome ga chijnoc. Degu te. Jé u gajnerĩ uje yijíñongome yoquigode gajnerĩ iji diri.
english: And then we returned to our campsite.  And everyone was out getting wild pineapple food called*doridie.*Some of us saw the*orojno*bee. They said: “Wait for us to get the honey, and then we’ll leave when the sun is over there and the next place we come to will be our camp. And we’ll be carrying our salt.”

ayoreo: E yico.*Pongora*dayabi chojningasia: “Boyo ga abasoi sañeque jne. Mu yocatigai  a u ejórique gajnerĩ. E yico. Yibácoi ta, mu e yicopo rĩ. Yocatigai urásu uné gajnerĩ mu ijíñongome echode. E isõcóji gai mu a e disorãque uñengo.
english: And so they stopped for honey, and then we took off again. And they reached camp, the first ones. And*Garáijnainái*came out to meet me, and I gave him the salt I was carrying and he carried it in for me until we reached the camp. And then we were able to take a break from carrying the salt for a day.

ayoreo: Mu ore ichorade iji ti yoquiquigade. Jodeti Guidaigosode chise ore iji. Jé a cosárei (avispa) chise ore iji ti (garapiedie u, Guidaigosode di). Yicoi ore iji ti nanique. Ore chojninga: “Cosárei que. Yore yoquibai. Chajna dajere uaté aja yoque.” Ore chojninga, “Ajíñoñóme enga yico,”*Pongara*dayabi chojningasia.
english: Then we left again.*Pongora*’s dear father said: “Let’s go and stop later on. But we’ll eat the next time after that when we stop. So we left. We stopped for a while, and then left again. We should have eaten on the next stop, but we were leaving the salt beds and wanting to get far away from the*Guidaigosode*. Our group had gotten through the salt beds, but others were travelling behind and hadn’t caught up with us yet.

ayoreo: Uñane chijnóra jóroque echode. Ore chojninga: “Guidaigosode idája arĩe,” ore chojninga.
english: But then we heard some noise behind us. Perhaps it was the*Guidaigosode*and they had reached those behind us and were attacking them. Or maybe those behind us had seen the wasps called*cosárei,*a sign (*garapiedie)*of the*Guidaigosode*coming. So we went to them, and they said: “¡*Cosárei qui!*There were ‘wasps’ and we were startled. The wasps were coming down the same path we were on, right at us.”

ayoreo: “Ajíñoñóme enga yico,”*Pongara*dayabi chojningasia.
english: They said: “Let’s get out of here, let’s leave,” that is what*Pongora*’s dear father said.

ayoreo: Ico yoquiyaridi ga chojningasia: “Jnacarode uñéngo doi yodi enga yórangopísi jne iji ta,” chojningasia,*Pongora*dayabi.
english: Some of them, however, wanted to go back for more salt. They said: “Don’t worry, the*Guidaigosode*are far away,” that’s what they said.

ayoreo: E yico yodi tiógare nanique. Ga yicoi mu iríngopo rĩ. Yórangopísiji ore iji ti. Jnanione ore dé ti to. Jé ijíñongóme. Dire nanique ga yico. Mu e yirõco dajéredie udire. Ga e yico dajéredie mu jé yitodógopísa cojñone rĩ ome yoquigode echo jeta yiríngo bisideque jne. Ico dajere mu ajnésacoja usodi. Uñane a jé chaquesu bajade. A e iquíjnongosi to ome yi echo. Yojninga: “Yijíñome to gajnerĩ. Yiquíjnongosi gu. Umuñási utébetabi jogonórami,” ojninga, “A yiríringuiámi a su jne.”
english: “Leave the salt beds behind and let’s go!” That’s what*Pongora*’s dear father said.

ayoreo: E udúco ore pijnane. E ibájoi to. Jec u uje ico uéchai. Yimóngoji ta nanique. Tejnuringárique jé ujuyapise yoque.
english: We left the place where we were and the dear one said: “Some younger men should go get water and then they’ll catch up with us and share it with us,” the dear one,*Pongora’s*father said.

ayoreo: Ore chojninga: “¡Jo! ¿Yico rique gajnerĩ a? uje que yajo yoquibotique.”
english: So we went off to get water.  And we got it and then returned. And we met up with them farther along the trail. The grown men were there too. Now we left them. The next morning we left.

ayoreo: Mu*Jnatua*dayabi casicaite chojningasia: “Qué apéso páyipierigui ga yiyáji siñeque gajnerĩ. A yácariji yoquiquigade ga jé abáchoi gajnerĩ.”
english: We were approaching the trail. We found the trail, but were afraid we’d meet up with non-Ayoreos and have to abandon our load of salt and arrive home empty handed. We found the trail but went off of it. Some of us crossed paths with the first ones who had left. I was growing very tired because of having to carry my load of salt. I said: “I’m going to leave some of it. I’m just too tired. I’ll fill a little bag and carry it.”  I said: “It will be the little thing I arrive with.”

ayoreo: Ga dire nanique ga e yico.*Jnatua*dayabi chási ti yoquiquigade. Cojñone a e ore chisósi ti. E irocorase iji yoquiyaridi. E jnupo (bulla de andar) chayo. Cojñone e ore diapo rĩ.
english: And then we heard their calls. So we stopped, and turned off the trail and slept there. It was really cold and we couldn’t get warm.

ayoreo: *Beúidái*casicaite chojninga: “*Bariguéjná*, ¡asa yocugúchade jne a!” Enga jno. A ore chayo.
english: They said: “*¡Jo!*(Whew!) What are we going to do? We haven’t eaten anything!”

ayoreo: Jec qué yiyaridirase iji echoi iji ti ore yui. Mu yajire ne mu*Garáijnaidái*casicaite iji ne. Chojninga: “¡Boyo yico to jne a! ¡A yejna yocugúchade iji ti uque que.”
english: But*Jnatua*’s dear father said: “Start thinking of something and we’ll stop farther on and I’ll stay behind you.”

ayoreo: E yico. Edo yijmané. Ṍrangóji yodi iji ti. Ãréraque jé ujuyapise yoque.
english: So at daylight we took off.*Jnatua*’s dear father stayed behind us. Non-Ayoreos must have found him there.  We waited for him. And then we heard people running.  It was the non-Ayoreos running after us.

ayoreo: *Guibéjnané – Tobité, Bolivia – 1970.*
english: *Beúidái*(now deceased) said: “*Bariguéjná,*take our stuff!” And he left. And they all started running.

ayoreo: *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Guibéjnané atatagode udojo.*
english: That was the place I would have been at the salt beds, right where they were coming. But I looked and saw*Garáijnaidái*over there. He said: “Let’s go! I’ll leave our stuff over there.”

ayoreo: Sijnaque yu ga ore pojna yu ga que yibásiji ore yúode. Ga jiei yugode. Dacasuté yu ga yajneque degúi. Yaranguipise ome ore. Ga yisagode u yarengone ore.
english: I was very fierce, and if they bawled me out, I wasn’t afraid of them.  I killed many victims. I was the first warrior to go into a battle. I didn’t want anyone ahead of me. I captured people and wouldn’t let others kill them.

ayoreo: Pocaningadatei u uje ore chujé ñane iji Rincón tiógarei.
english: The biggest war was the one they fought in the direction of Rincón.

ayoreo: Garaigosode ore dacasuté u Jmanenáquidé.  Jnupedogosode ore dacasuté u Rincóngosi*Ingoiné*nanique, enga udore ore uaguedie ore chujé ore urodie nanique.
english: *Jmanenáquidé*was a warrior of the*Garaigosode*.*Ingoiné*was a warrior of the*Jnupedogosode*long ago; he was from Rincón. These warriors would kill the daughters of their enemies.

ayoreo: Yarengo yoquigode ore gai. Chi ore cṍraja daragapídi ga ore toi, jeoga ore ñona bisidec.
english: After a battle we would do the ceremony called*‘ore tãre gai’*; this was what we did to get rid of being guilty after shedding blood. We drew a likeness of what we killed in the dirt. When someone would fall on the drawing of the victim (*daragapídi)*it was a very serious matter: he could die or disappear.

ayoreo: Dejai chojmára ga ore pésu daragapídi. Cojñone yungórone u uje ore tãre gai. Ga dajíroméone que ore doi to. Ayoré dacasutedie yuque cuchade nanique ga ayoré gajnaréngone que ore cási ati ore chujé to. Uje dacasutéraque gajneque degúi ga ayoré gajnaréngone iji*cho buru, buru*iji ore quigade. Uje yicai pocaningai ga bajáguipísi dacasutedie ga ayoré gajnaréngone ore chácariji yoquiquigade.
english: It’s generally when their victims were white people that they do ‘the blood guiltiness ceremony’ (*ore tãre gai*). Those who didn’t participate in the killing (*dajíroméone)*do not do ‘the blood guiltiness ceremony’. It was the warriors of the tribe that killed people, but the common Ayoreos aren’t courageous enough to kill. It’s the real warriors who attack a camp first, and the common people run in after them. So when I went to war, I was with the warriors who went in first, and the common ones stayed behind us.

ayoreo: Yigode gagájni ore iji Guidaigosode asutedie.
english: I killed nine of the Guidaigosode warriors.

ayoreo: Uje dago yoque ga yacágoja Boliviagosode.
english: When we were looking for girls, we’d go down to Bolivia.

ayoreo: Yibatigai jogadi Engábi u guidábi nanique. Iapade i tu*Jomainé*. Ité i tu*Podé*. Direquedéjnagose yacoté dai ga i tu*Igoi*. Daté i tu*Jumuné*.
english: The place where I was born was called*Engábi;*that was the name of the little village. My father’s name was*Jomaine.*My mother’s name was*Podé*. My wife’s father was a*Direquedéjnagosi*, and his name was*Igoi*. Her mother’s name was*Jumuné.*

ayoreo: *Oidábiadé – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988.* *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Oidábiadé atatagode udojo nanique.*
english: *Oidábiadé – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988**Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie*

ayoreo:
Yirasé yujodie chequei ore uje
ore chatata da-testimonio iji yoquiquei. Uje que yimo ujetiga yujodie Dupade
anguretatoi uaque, uacarasé ore to. Uje yirasé uje ore chatata dadode. Yirasé
yoque uje yõco yógaja Dupade uruode.
english: *Yirasique yujodie chequei ojñane*

ayoreo:
Enga arajáyo gotique, yujodie
chequei uaque, uje yirasé yu, mu que yirasepise yu go. Arajayope gotique,
yujodie chequei, uje yirasé yu mu que yirasepise yu go. Ujeta jec ũré ga
arajáyo Dupade uruode. Yirasé yu gu anire, yirasé yu mu que yirasepise yu
gotique; a jé ca déji uaque to. Uje yirasé yu mu uje yangureta Dupade ome
yajéode. Mu aníre, u yabai uje cáma changureta Dupade to, ga u uje a que
yirasepise yu go. Uerate jetiga yejnángo yógaja Dupade Uruode – yabai, yabode uyoque.
english: I love it when my fellow women stand before us and give their testimonies. I don’t know if you who are my fellow believers enjoy this also, but I do. I like to hear the stories they tell about themselves. I’m also happy that we are increasing in number in our meetings.

ayoreo:
Jecucha yojninga ica: ‘Yachopise
Pujnúcabi uje tṍra yoquiplátadie ome *sigásodie. A yingome yujodie Dupade
anguretatoi ga yimátagope uñane ujetiga yatecãcoi Dupade ome uté, ga cha dorijnane
jne,’ yojninga.
english: **husband doesn’t yet believe in****Dupade****:**But, my fellow women, I want you to know that, while I’m happy, there are other things that I am not happy about. Guess why that is, my fellow women, that I am happy, but, at the same time, not happy? If you truly know Dupade’s Word, then you probably know why I am not happy. I am both happy and not so happy; is this how it is with you also?

ayoreo:
A jé ca yujóraque chequé oyo yu
gotique, uje yetaguipise sigásodie uje ore chijna. Yitodopísa borácho yugode
rĩ. Que yipota jetiga uñeque idógosi iji yu.
english: I’m happy, because I believe in Dupade with all my heart. But this is what I am not happy about, my husband doesn’t believe in Dupade. It would be so much better if all my family were sincerely following Dupade – my husband, my children, and myself.

ayoreo:
Uerate uje uacatatáyo uaca-testimónio
iji de. Mu uerate ujetiga anire, ca ajéchome. Uacamóño uje Satanas u uje pésu
ga ajéchome cuchade. Pésu ga yocáyipiedie cho quiyíguiyí. Chãraque Dupade
uruode yui ome yoque gu.
english: I used to always say: ‘I’m so tired of Pujnúcabi throwing away our money on cigarettes. I’m asking you, my fellow women who believe in Dupade, to join me in praying for my husband to stop smoking.’

ayoreo:
E yatata diríca iji ucacasé
semána bajei casé uje udute uacajnomei diríca iji de guidai uje chi uacatatayo
uaca-testimónio, ga yirasique uacaruode.
english: I don’t know if any of you, my companions, feel like I do about cigarettes – but I hate them. I’m afraid of people when they get drunk, also. I don’t like them to get near me.

ayoreo:
Ugúcoi erámone diríca ga unere
conferencia yui ome yu iji ujade. Yujnusina jetiga ugúsi to mu que ugúsi go, mu
idája yu, mu Dupade u uje déji yajei.
english: It is a good thing when you give your testimonies, and a good thing not to be bashful about it. Know that it is Satan that makes you feel shy, and to waver in your faith. He tries to keep us away from Dupade’s Word. I’ve already told you how happy I was a few weeks ago, when I heard the news that you were going to be giving your testimonies, and I was so happy with what you had to say.

ayoreo:
A jé ca cho uaque to uje Dupade
gajnúone u yocugúcha ichade jeta yicoi yocugúcharique ga yoquirasique
yocugúcha ichai.
english: We were out in the woods, and I kept thinking about getting back, so I could attend church. I would think about being among you but was too far away to attend. But Dupade was in my heart.

ayoreo:
E gusu yuruode mu yipota jetiga yicatecã́ri Dupade ome uaque. Amóño iji
gaté ga yicatecã́ri Dupade ome yoque jne iji conferencia. E gusu yuruode,
Elena, mu yicatecã́ri Dupade jne.
english: **want:**I don’t know if you ever think about this, but sometimes we make “gods” of the things we want. When we get something new we get so excited and happy. We should be excited and happy about Dupade, but instead, our things take Dupade’s place in our hearts.

ayoreo:
Yoque Dupade, yicatecãcoi ua iji
de viérnes uje yugúcoji conferencia ajei. Uje yacaranguipísome ua jeta dínique
gajneri ga unere baruode yui tome yoque gajneri, yoque Dupade. Yoque Dupade,
yocacaranguipis ome ua gajneri jeta átaja yoque gajneri, ga jé ca yajéjome baruode
iji banguretasórone ore iquei jeta jé ũrepise yocanguretigai iji de, yoque
Dupade. Jé ca yajéjome udore gajneri uje ore cana yoquibáyode, yoque Dupade.
english: That is all I have to say, but I would like to pray for us first. Bow your heads and I will pray for us here in our meeting. Elena, this is the end of my words, and now I will pray to*Dupade*.

ayoreo:
Yoque Dupade, yacaranguipis ome
ua uje barote yóguiji erámone diríca iji yocajesúode erámio. Yugúcopóji de rĩ
jnese, jnanione ore uyoque. Yoque Dupade, jeta goto déji siñeque, jetiga cáma
unere baruode yui tome to gajneri, ga asíome gajneri baruode, ga unere yui ome
to gajneri, yoque Dupade. Gusu ua uje baruode cho cucha uneréjnane ome yoque,
Dupade.
english: Our Dupade, we pray to you today at our Friday meeting. I thank you, and we are happy that we are going to hear your Word today. Our Dupade, I am not ashamed to share your Word with my companions who are true believers. I am not ashamed of your Word even when others laugh at us.

ayoreo:
Yoque Dupade, yacaranguipis ome
ua gajneri jetiga átaja yoque gajneri jeta baruode quẽra iji yocáyipiedie
gajneri. Ga ca yicadíjoi yocacadisórone acaditigade ome yoque gajneri, yoque
Dupade. Yoque Dupade bojninga: “Ujetiga uaté goto chajéngome yu, ute gosi
chajéngome yu, ga yajégome to jetiga yise jnumi.” Chi bojninga nanique iji
taningane, yoque Dupade.
english: Our Dupade, I am grateful that you take care of us here on earth wherever we go. We are gathered together once again, we women as well as the men. But, our Dupade, we pray for the women who are not here with us, those who are not yet interested in your Word. Give them a hunger for your Word and a desire to hear it. Only you and your Word are what we want, because it is sweet to us, our Dupade.

ayoreo:
Dupade, átaja yujodie chequei,
uyoque to gajneri, ga uneripise baruode yui ome yoque gajneri, yoque Dupade.
Yirajágo uje jiei yoque nanique uje yoco cucha bisideque iji baruode, mu que
yirajágo gotique gu, mu e yirajágo uje que cucha bisideque ua gotique, yoque
Dupade. Yiraja uje baqueta togode nanique iji taningane, yoque Dupade. Que
uñeque cuse gotique jeta chiqueta totique. Gusu ua uje baqueta togode nanique,
yoque Dupade.
english: Our Dupade, I thank you for helping us to grow in our understanding of your Word. Help us not to forget the things our teachers have taught us about you. Our Dupade, you said, “Whoever is ashamed of me, I will also be ashamed of that person when I return to earth.” This is what you said a long time ago when you were here on earth.

ayoreo:
Yoque Dupade, yacaranguipis ome
ua uje e baqueta Teresa, yoque Dupade. Bamo uje ejnareté dirica, mu baqueta,
yoque Dupade. Que uñeque cuse ujetiga chiqueta ejnaretátique; gusu ua uje
baqueta yoque. Yacaranguipis ome ua uje udúco baruode iji dirode jnese,
jnanione uyoque.
english: Dupade, help my fellow women and myself to love your Word, our Dupade. We know that there are many of us who do not pay attention to your Word, but I do not know why, because I know that your Word is not something unimportant. Instead, it is powerful. When you were on earth, you even raised the dead with your Word, our Dupade. There is no other person who has raised a dead person, only you can do this.

ayoreo: Dupade, yicatecã́ri ua ome bajnacari Jesucristo i. Amen.
english: Our Dupade, thank you for healing Teresa. You saw that she was ill, and you cured her. There is no one else who can cure the sick; you alone can heal us.

ayoreo:
*sigásodie –
cigarillos
english: Thank you that we hear your Word every day, both men and women.

ayoreo: *Dajuñangue – Tobité, Bolivia –  agosto de 1975**Marina de Morarie churúsãre Dajuñangue uruode nanique*
english: Dupade, we pray for these things in the name of your Son Jesus Christ. Amen.

ayoreo:
*Yo soy Jómone*uje
yacate ua iji aníri Tobité taningane.
english: I am*Jomone*who taught you in Tobité in the beginning.

ayoreo:
Señora Marina, bajneque
yuruode, uje yipota ujetiga araja yipesudode cucha gajnarécho iji aníri, yuniri
udé. Iyajmánguipíse uje ujnusina anírone, yipesudode cucha gajnárecho. Ga
yipota ujetiga yibo, mu ujnusina cuchade uje gajnárecho iji yuniri, ga yipota
ujetiga yibo. Mu Dupade Uruode u ujetiga yibópise uje yacate ore ome Dupade
uruode nanique enga yátaja ua to uje bajnerame udore ome yocuruode.
english: *Señora Marina (Maxine Morarie)*, these words of mine are for you, because I want you to know the ugly things I have done here where I now live. For a long time I’ve been thinking about all the things I’ve done. I am very sad when I think about how bad things are here.

ayoreo:
Mu que yiraja cuchárique gu, uje
yipota jetiga yujé cojñotique. Jeoga yo yiba iji yacotéraque, mu gusu Dupade
aníri, pajei sereringai, uje déji enga uté u uje acadique ayoréode uyóguiji
erámone jnese ujetiga yujnusínango ipesudode cucha ueracho ome yoque. Uje
irajágo gu uje uté u uje imanique ayoréode ore uyógaja pajei sereringai.*(San
Juan 14:27)*
english: I want to cry, but it’s*Dupade*’s*Word*that I really cry about, because I’ve taught my people about it and I’ve helped you to translate it into our language.

ayoreo:
Ga yipota ujetiga angári yuruode
unojo, Señóra Marina. Uje deréjnai u uje yicoi*arrepentido*ome aníri, yocáyipiedie. Enga yajnángo Jesucrísto uje
gusu uté u gu ujeta chĩro yoquisijnaringuéone, uje chi doi*resucitado*ome danguresórone ore ujetiga
chisi ome pucuecaringué ichárique ome danguresórone. Gaidi u gu ujetiga ore chachai
iji garãtique ujeta diapo rĩ iji jnumi ome yoque.
english: I even wanted to kill a*cojñoi*. I’ve sinned against my wife. But it’s only*Dupade*that can give me peace, for he is the one who has peace and he is the one that teaches all of us about the beautiful things he’s done for us. And we know that it is he that brings peace to us.*(John 14:27)*

ayoreo:
Mu gusu udore uje que ore changureta
ga ore todo yui, uje ore todo yui ujetiga pujuya ore ome pioi, jeoga pujuyaquéo
uña.  Mu Dupade ujuyaquei tu uje tṍraji
gaté jne. Que yirajágo ujetiga yodi upo rĩ, ujeta pioque. Mu yirajágopo rĩ
uje pioque a u jne, ejórique, jeta cho dajuñame jnumi jogosórone uyoque.
english: **nly****Jesus Christ****can forgive our sins****:**I wanted you to hear these words of mine,*Señora Marina*, because today we have repented with all out hearts. And we’re following Jesus for only he can forgive our sins, and it is he that rose again to give new life to his believers. He did that so that when he returns to earth for us, we will go up to meet him in the air.

ayoreo: *Jómone – Tobité, Bolivia – 1970**Marina de Morarie churúsãre Jómone uruode*
english: It’s only those that don’t believe in him that are afraid for him to come back, because they fear the fire and other kinds of punishment they will receive. But*Dupade*’s punishment will also come from the sky. We don’t know if it will be water again. But what we do know is that the next great punishment will be fire when he punishes the people on this earth.

ayoreo:
*“Yajéode
u ujetiga pimeserãcho yuruode udore uje tṍraji yibijnani, jé aja yáyipie
quigode udore ore iji baquei, Pangarádejná. Ua u Yãreto cucarané jé aja
Yiramesõre.” (Salmo 19:14)* Yipota jetiga yatata yadode. Gapape yu ica ga que yiraja cuchárique go. Mu ejode ga yiraja. Jéodeti ca cucha ueratique cuse nanique. Que yipésu cuchade. Qué ueracho cuchade ome ñu jnese uje gapape yu. Mu ejode ga yiraja uje cucha omi cuse, cucha ueradi cuse. Déji dirode, diri udojo uje yẽra yu ome Dupade iji.
english: *(Psalm 19:14)*When I was a young girl I didn’t know anything at all. But now I do. I don’t think there was anything beautiful about my past. I didn’t do anything. But when I was a girl everything seemed beautiful to me. But later I found out what was truly good, what was truly beautiful.

ayoreo:
Ga que toque gotique jetiga yajnésãra
yiquigade uje e yẽra yu. Que cucha bisideque go ujetiga yiyáguego iji ore
iquei. Que cucha bisideque go. Yiraja yu ga yiyágue iji de. Uje yiraja yu.
Yiraja yajei ga yipota jeta yingo yuruode aja ore.  Que cucha bisideque go, jé ca yiyáguego ore
iquei jetiga yatatago yocadode. Jé areque jetiga yirajágo yóguiji ga yatatágo
queyadode. Yiraja yu. Que yiraja cuchárique ica ga
sijnangue yu, sijnanguepise yu. Yipota jetiga jĩraque udute yajnéode darade.
Mu ejode gajneri ga ise ga ca yudúdapo daráringo rĩ. Yiraja yu iji, yiraja yajei.
Ise ga ca yátapo yu aja puágueraque rĩ gu uje edote cuchábei ayoredie gu enga
jé ore chotigase Dupade. Ise ga ca yátapo yu aja ore rĩ.
Yiraja gu. Yiraja uje Dijaide chingome yu uje gajnarengue puáguedie uje edote
cuchabei ayoredie. Déji uje uñanie chotigase Dupade. Uje yajéngome ore. Ore
edotigade ome Dupade. Que yiraja cuchárique bisideque
go. Yiraja gu Dijaide uje chingome yu. Dijaide chiraja Dupade ga chingo yoque
Dupade adode.
english: I found out what was truly beautiful on the day that I gave myself to*Dupade*. And there is now no possibility of turning away from*Dupade,*because I have given myself to Him. Standing up and giving our testimonies before others is no small thing. It is not an insignificant step. I know myself now and so I stand here before you. I know myself. I know what is in me and I want people to know what I have to say. And what I have to say isn’t unimportant, for we generally don’t stand before others to tell our testimonies. It’s a good thing for us to search our hearts and to share what is in our hearts with others. I have searched my heart.*(**Romans 10:9,10**)*

ayoreo: Dijaide chingo Señora Marina uruode aja yu ga unere Marina uruode. Dupade chiraja yu ga chise yajei. Chiraja yu ga chimo yu. A ore cháta raja ñane ome yu dirome. E idógosi jeta ore cháta raja uñane ome yu uje ore chimo yu gu. Uje ore chiraja yejnarei.
english: **when they gossip:**I was full of sin and didn’t know anything, I sinned a lot. I couldn’t wait to hear the gossip. But how different it is now, I don’t like to join in the talk that is going on around me. I know myself, I know my heart. I think I will probably not be in the gatherings of the women any longer for they are always talking about others, and even expressing doubts about*Dupade*. So I don’t think I will join with them anymore, because I know what goes on. I know that*Dijaide*has told me that it is not a good thing for women to get together to gossip and talk bad about others. And not only that, for them to speak bad things about*Dupade*, things that make me ashamed of them. To listen when they talk bad about*Dupade*hurts me.

ayoreo:
Yico Cochabamba ica. Yudute Timi
uruode ome yangoninguinone. Uje yicagóji daijnai uje yimatarane ũroso. Mu
íjnoque yibijnani ga que yingo Timi yuruode mu a Dijaide chingo.
english: **Dupade****and he tells us about him****:***Dijaide*and*Josué*went to help*Señora Marina (Maxine Morarie)*a while back.

ayoreo:
Timi chojninga: “Dijai yá, ¿ga
Gayujna changureta Dupade to a? Uje Gayujna e chise daijnane jnese iji erámi.”
english: When*Dijaide*returned he told me what*Señora Marina*wanted him to tell me, and it was so good to hear from her.*Dupade*knows me, and he’s found a place in my heart. He knows me, and he sees me.

ayoreo:
Mu íjnoque yibijnani ga que yimo
go.
english: A group of doctors will examine me soon. They want to find out what to do about my illness.

ayoreo:
Ayorérique que chimo yajei go; gusu uje ore chimo yiguidé iji yibai. Ore chiraja yiguidé enochai, mu que ore chimo yajei go. Yise yajei. Yiraja yu. Yiraja
uje Dupade chimo yu ga chátaja yu ome cuchade. Yutai ga chátaja yu. Yirasique
uje eduguéjnane ore chiraja yu iji erámi. Chiese ore chiraja yajei gu. Ore chimo
yu. Ore chátaja yu. Yirasique rĩ uje Señora Marina chiraja yu rĩ, ga pota yu.
Chiese chiraja yu rĩ. Chiese chimo yajei.
english: When we went to Cochabamba once, I heard*Tim Wyma*speak. We went to the dentist while we were there. I didn’t get to talk to*Tim,*but*Dijaide*must have told him about me.

ayoreo:
Mu Josué e pota Dupade uruode.
Pota jetiga chacate yoque iji conferenciadie. Que disírique cho Josué go.
Yirasique diríca Señora Marina uruode uje chi ore catecãri ome Josué uje disi
mu chiraja cuchade.
english: *Tim*asked him: “*Dijaide*, has*Gayujna*put her faith in*Dupade*? Since she is ill and seeing so many doctors, that is important for her.”

ayoreo:
Don Pablo chiese chiraja yajei
rĩ. Mu ise ga ca ore chiraja yajéode go. Ise ga gusu Dupade jetiga chiraja
yajéo. Que toque gajneri ujetiga jé yajnésãra yiquigade, que toguipise go. E
yiya jetiga yipésu cucha gajnarétic. Ise ga jé ca yudúdapo darárique rĩ iji
de ejoi tudé. Que udute darárique go. Déji puáguedie gu uje ayoredie ore chatata
cucha bisidégode iji puáguedie. Ipota jetiga e Dijaide chimate
yiguíjnai ga e gusu yu iji ta ga jé ca yudúdapo cuchárique rĩ. Edoite cuchabei
ayoredie gu ome Dupade anguretatodie uyoque. .Ga chi chojningame Dijaidé: “Jé ca adúcho uje queyate uje yanguretágo Dupade ga pumajodaté yoque uje yugúcoji dirode udojo. Chi Dupade Abi jé ate nanique. Chojninga: ‘Que yiraja yibárique mu jé ore chujé yu jne, ga a ore chajo yu aja curesei.’ Chi chojninga, ‘Tamuré uje ayoré uaque iji jnumone udé ga pumajódaté uaque to rĩ.’”
english: But I didn’t say anything, because, at that time I didn’t know what to say. It was after that when I got right with*Dupade*. I decided to believe. No human knows my heart; they can only can see the clothes I wear. They know what color my dress is, but they can’t see my heart. I have now found my heart. Now I know myself. I know that*Dupade*sees me, and he is helping me.

ayoreo:
Yirasique uté uje yatata
yitestimonio ome Señora Marina ga ore chudute ga tamuré uje ore chudute yuruode
gajneri iji erámi. Que cuchárique cuse go jetiga cuchárique qué déji erámi uje
chiese uerate, uje gusu Dupade uje uerate ome yoque.
english: When I ask him to help me, he does help me. I’m happy that the leaders know me, and say they believe in me. They’ve observed me. They’ve helped me. I’m happy that*Señora Marina*knows me and feels close to me. She says she believes in me. She says she knows me.

ayoreo:
Yurúo rĩ ome yujodie chequei
uaque, achẽcho uacangoninguinone gáyode uje que cucha gajnareque Dupade go. Mu
que ore chise dajéode gu. Yico yoque ga yico yocajéode. Yudute ica rĩ uje ore
chojninga: ‘Ise ga tague ga a jé ca chatata Dupade uruode ome.’ Que tague yu
go. Yirasique uje Dijaide chingo yoque Dupade uruode.
english: There is no child in the world that knows*Dupade*’s*Word.*But*Josué*is already interested in*Dupade*’s*Word*. He wants to begin teaching us in our meetings. There aren’t many children like*Josué*. I was pleased when*Señora Marina*said that they were praying for*Josué*who even as a child was understanding things.

ayoreo:
Ise ga jé ca yajniapo aja
yiquigade iji de ejoi rĩ. Yise yu ga que yajni yiquigade go. Jetapure gajneri
iji dirode udojo gajneri uje yẽra yu ome Dupade iji. Jetapure rĩ gajneri uje
yocujnomei iji erámone déji diri udé. Déji uje uñanie uje ore poja Dupade
uruode mu que yibója go. Yadode gajnaréngome garósi ica.
Ore pésu cuchade ica ga gapape yu, mu yipesudode que chise yajei go. Yiraja
yajei ga je chise gu. Jetapure iji ejode ga yuruode e jno. Ise ga ca yududapo
darárique ga que yudute go. Yise yu gu.
english: *Don Pablo (Paul Gess)*says he knows me, also, but I think nobody really knows my heart or what I think. I think only*Dupade*knows what is in my heart. And he knows that it isn’t possible for me to turn away from him, not now, not at all. I no longer want to do the things I used to do. I am no longer interested in what is being said by people around me. I don’t pay any attention to it. The things that are said when the women get together are just idle words.

ayoreo:
A yujóraque ayoré narari jóroque
gajneri: ‘Gayujna todo uati ga que chajnai urúrique go.’
english: I want for*Dijaide*to build a house where we could live alone. Then I wouldn’t have to listen to all the things the women say. They tend to gossip most about those of us women who are believers.

ayoreo: Yitodódi u Dupade ujuyaquei cuchábi mu que toque go jetiga jé yise.  Ga que yise yiquigade ga que yáta yu aja puágueraque go gajneri. Satanas ipesutique puáguerac. Jiei iji erámi uje ayoredie ore chotigase. Yajéngome ore uruode.
english: *Señora Marina*talked to*Dijaide*about this and told him: “Haven’t you heard that we who believe and follow*Dupade*will be persecuted? And that is what happened to*Dupade*’s Son Jesus also. And he said: “I have done nothing wrong and yet they will kill me and nail me to a cross,” and he said, “how much more will they persecute you who are my disciples.”

ayoreo:
Yirajágo Dupade gu. Yajéngome.
Yiraja yu iji Dupade uruode ga jé yatata yadode, yate bajade. Que cucha
bisideque go. Que cuchárique, que cuse iji erámi jogonónique cuchárique uje
uerate, gusu Dupade uje uerate. Gusu uté uje chátaja yóguiji erámi.
english: I’m so happy to be telling my testimony to*Señora Marina*and how much more because others will hear it in other places in the world. There is nothing in the world that we find beautiful any more, only*Dupade*is beautiful to us.

ayoreo:
Dupade átajapie yu ga jiei uje
daijnane ore chátaja yu. Dupade cháta raja daijnane ome yu uje idógosi jeta ore
cháta raja uñane ome yu. Que ayoréraque chimo yu, mu e
uté u uje chimo yu. Idógosi uje ore cháta raja uñane ome yu, ore chiraja yu gu.
Chiraja yajei.
english: And this is what I have to say to my fellow women, all of you, open your ears and know that there is nothing ugly about*Dupade*. But it’s because they haven’t discovered themselves yet that they have a low opinion of*Dupade*. Let’s find ourselves and then we’ll know our hearts.

ayoreo: Chi chojninga: ‘Ga yudute Gayujna ica uje chatata datestimónio ica ga chijnime yeducadode.’ Mu yiraja chiese chiraja yajei. Que yimóngo jetiga chiraja yajei. Yirasique uje anire, yangureta Dupade ga chátaja yu ga yise erámi.
english: I am so happy with*Dijaide*for it is he that tells us what is in*Dupade*’s*Word*. I heard that some were saying: “*Gayujna*is lying.*Dijaide*doesn’t teach her about*Dupade*’s*Word*.”

ayoreo:
A yisapo Rincón dirome rĩ jeta
e Dijaide doi enga chicáji ome congreso. 
A yicopo Señora Marina dirome rĩ. Yirasique uje ore chudute yuruode. Que
toque go jetiga jé yajnésãra yiquigade. Que toque go jetiga yudute ayoréraque
urúrique ga que toguipise gu.
english: But, I’m not lying about that. It makes me so happy because he does teach our family about*Dupade*’s*Word.*And that is why I never want to turn back from where I now am. I have found myself at last, and I don’t ever want to live the way I did. Especially now, in these days since I have given myself to*Dupade*. And especially now that we have a testimony to maintain before others. There are some women who complain about*Dupade*’s*Word*, but I don’t complain about anything to do with*Dupade*.

ayoreo:
Sijnangue yu ica ga jĩraque
yudute yujodie chequei uruode. Jĩraque chacája yatoi ga unere jetiga yipojna
ore. Mu ejode udojo ga yudute uje jiei yududigode, mu jé jĩraque yicadígui.
english: I did a few bad things before. I joined in and did what others did when I was a young girl and thought nothing about it. When I did think bout it, it didn’t seem important. Later it bothered me, however. Especially afterwards and I didn’t know what to say. I think I stopped listening to the opinions of others. And that was when I found myself.

ayoreo:
*“Ca uacarúrique gajnareque aja uacojórique ayoré. Mu
ueradi u ujetiga uacarúrigo omío ome ñane. Ga ajneráñome uacajorane, ga ore
quérã iji Dupade adode. Ga a Dupade chátaja ore ome bojñane.” (Efesio 4:29)*
english: Probably my fellow women talk about me, saying, “*Gayujna*is afraid and that’s why she doesn’t answer back when we scold her.”

ayoreo: *Gayujna – Tobité, Bolivia – 1975**Marina de Morarie churúsãre uruode enga cháta versículode aja to*
english: I’m not afraid of anything at all. I only fear*Dupade*’s punishment, but I won’t have to suffer it. And that is why I cannot ever go back to how I was, and why I won’t join the women when they get together. Satan is the one who gets them together to accuse and cast doubts on so many things in the world. They say things that embarrass me.

ayoreo:
Jecucha don
Juan Krunklis chingo Dupade Uruode aja yoque iji dirode jnese. Chacate yoque
enga e yiraja Dupade uruode to. Chi jeta angoninguite guesátique ore tenga, ore
tangári to Dupade Uruode. Uje que araja yu mu e que yipésu yate gajnarétique
gotique to. Uje yanguretapise Oroné, Yiguijnamúiné, Juan ore acajnaquéode ome
yu, ga jé que yipésu yate gajnarétique to.
english: I want to share*Dupade’s Word*with you, things I have learned from messages that*Juan (Juan Krunklis)*gives us every day. He taught us that the ones who are obedient, and who have open ears, are those who hear*Dupade’s Word*. You don’t know me, but I am not doing things that are wrong. And that is because I really believe the things*Oroné, Yiguíjnamúiné,*and*Juan*teach me and so I try not to do the things that are wrong.

ayoreo:
Yacaranguipise
uje Dupade pésu yabi ga iyoqui, ga jé tiógara jeta yicatecã́ri Dupade iji
deréjnane udojo ome suté.
english: I thank*Dupade*that he gave us a little boy, and I want to set an example for him and pray for him these days as his father.

ayoreo:
Enga
yicatecã́ri Dupade ome uaque to, yurasade uaque á. Yutai Dupade ujetiga uaque
uñeque changureta to enga angoninguesã́tigo déji uaque.
english: And for you, my countrymen, I hope that you believe also, and have obedient hearts and open ears.

ayoreo:
Uje yicai
monte diríca mu Dupade que jnoji yajénique gotique diríca, ga yatecã́ri iji dirode
jnese. Yacaranguipise uje guéode beque ga chuta cucha pacade, ga a yajo ade
jne. Jecucha ga Dupade urúo jetiga yatecã́ri iji dirode jnese.
english: Recently when I was out in the woods I couldn’t stop thinking about*Dupade*, the one I talk to every day. I am thankful for the rain he gives to water our crops so that we will have sufficient food. I am always reading in*Dupade’sWord*that I should pray faithfully every day.

ayoreo:
Jecucha ga
ejnaretaque yu mu yatecã́ri Dupade ome yu, yurásade ore uyoque jnese. E
yirasiguipise Dupade Uruode.
english: It seems I am sick often, and when I am, I pray to*Dupade*for myself as I pray for all my countrymen. I am really pleased with*Dupade’sWord*and its guidance!

ayoreo:
Disidie
uaque, disiode uaque, angáchopísi yuruode to. Uacamóño yu uje que yiraja
cuchárique, mu e yangureta Dupade ga Dupade chiraja uje yangureta abi Jesus.
english: Children, all of you, pay attention to my words. You know that I don’t know very much, but I believe in*Dupade*, and*Dupade*knows that I believe in his Son Jesus.

ayoreo:
Yacaranguipise
uje yangácoi don Juan acaditigade iji deréjnane jnese. E gusu yuruode.
english: I am especially thankful for*Juan’s*daily teachings.

ayoreo: *Lídiadé – Rincón de Tigre, Bolivia – 1970**Juan Kruklis churúsãre Lídiadé uruode udojo ome Pujnoméone*
english: This is all my words.

ayoreo: Iyamaque uje*Rosadé*chaca yedodie iji yodi to nanique. Jé disi yu nanique uje*Rosadé*chaca yedodie iji yodi, ga que yiraja cuchade go. Jéodeti ore chaca ore edodie bisideque iji ujade. Mu ejode ga yiraja. Yiraja uje chi Dupade u uje anire, uje ore chaca ore edodie iji yodi uje ore changureta Dupade ujnacari.
english: It’s been a long time since*Rosadé*put my face under water. I was a little girl then and I didn’t really understand what was happening. Sometimes, I think baptism can be done without the person fully understanding its significance. It wasn’t until afterwards that I understood. I understand now that baptism has to do with*Dupade*and having faith in his son Jesus.

ayoreo: Ga yipota to rĩ jetiga yajni to rĩ. Yipotapísa jeta yo ore uje ore pésu date gajnaréngone, mu ore doi ore iquei iji de ujetiga ore chatata dadode. Mu que yi ore iquei to go. ¡Yise mu que ore u yu jõ! Uje querénga yi Satanas iroquéode to. Jecute que yingo yu aja Dupade anguretatodie to jne.Yipota jetiga yajni Dupade uruode to rĩ. Uje ité,*Omé*ore chetaguipise Dupade Uruode nanique, mu ore pota to rĩ iji ejode. Uje chi jé ũré Dupade gu ome ore. Ore pota to Dupade Uruode iji ejode,*Omé*, ité ore.
english: And I wanted to go back and start over again. I wanted to be like the people that had sinned and were repenting of their sin in front of the believers. I had sinned too but had never repented publicly. I thought, ‘These people are not like me. I haven’t sinned as much as they have.’ So, I didn’t go up to confess my sin.

ayoreo:
Uyu u uje ise ga catague yu to
jõ aja Dupade, uje anire, yicota Dupade uje yojninga, ise mu cataque to Dupade
uje chi ca jnoji yari. Jã́rape yijnime yu iji to go.*(Hebreos 13:5)*
english: I wanted to start going back to meetings again, but my mother, and my sister*Omé*hated going to church at that time, but later they did want to go back. They said, ‘There is a*Dupade*.’ And then my mother and*Omé*had a desire to hear*Dupade**’s Word*.

ayoreo: Uje chequedíe ore chojningame yu: ‘Mu tague*Sama*; doi Satanas iroquéode.’ Jecute que yingo yu aja Dupade to jetiga yajniapo to rĩ uje chequedíe ore chotigase yu gu.
english: But I think I’m ‘stuck’ with*Dupade*. I praise him and say to myself, ‘I think*Dupade*didn’t lie to us when he said he would never leave us, and that is why I haven’t been able to leave him.’ *“For God has said, “I will never leave you; I will never abandon you.” (Hebrews 13:5)*

ayoreo:
Yiyárasu Dupade Uruode nanique
gu uje yabai cha yu.
english: But the women didn’t believe me. They said, “*Sama*is lying; she’s a sinner.”

ayoreo:
Yabai cha yu nanique ga yiya to Dupade
Uruode. Jé u uje yipotarása Satanas iroquéode to. Mu que yipotapísa go.
english: Because the women doubted me, I didn’t come before*Dupade*and didn’t go back to serving him. I almost stopped going to meetings at all after my husband left me.

ayoreo: Chequedíe ore chojninga: ‘Jnani acoté cuse mu jé*Sama*pota,’ mu que yipota go. Mu acoté chicáji Santa Cruz ga tṍra dabi. Ga iyamaque ga sequeré chojmára, ga jé u uje yipota to uje abi e toi.
english: My husband left me, and I stopped going to church. It was then that I fell into sin, even though I really didn’t want to.

ayoreo: Enga udojo u uje jé yingo yadode aja ore to. Uje jé gusu udojo yabayode uje gare. Que yiyarásu yabai bajai nanique go. A jé ore chiraja to, chequedíe ore, uje pota gapudíe jiedie nanique. Ga yiya to uje potapo uña rĩ. Pota gapudíe jiei. Ga yojninga: ‘A yiya uté to jne uje dopísi Satanás iroquéode gu. Jeta e potapo gapu uegue iji yejoi rĩ ga jé u uje yiya to.’
english: The women said, ‘*Jnani*has a wife but*Sama*wants him,’ but I didn’t want him at that time. But his wife left him and went to Santa Cruz and wanted nothing more to do with her child. It was a long time later, one year or so, that I went to him after his child had died.

ayoreo:
Yiya to ga anire, yijína Jnani.
Ga e gusúapo rĩ. E gusu. Que toque ujetiga jé yijína jnacare uñeque iji
ejode.
english: Finally, I went before the church, and this is what I told about myself. I told them I had only had two husbands. I would not have left my first husband, as the women well knew, but he wanted many other women. And when he wanted yet another one, that’s when I left him. He was with many other young women. And I said to myself, ‘I’m going to leave him, because he does not resist Satan’s temptations. If he is unfaithful again, I will leave him.’

ayoreo: Chequedíe ore cho tague iji yu, mu ise mu ca tague yu jõ; ise mu jé ũré yu.
english: I left him and after some time, went to*Jnani.*And he was the last one. There were no more. I would never go to another young man after that.

ayoreo:
A Jesuse dirase ga chijna
danguretatodie. Ga ise mu a jé yugúsirásiji Dupade idai to iji ore ujade jõ.
Uje que tague yu to go, ome yu.
english: The women called me a liar, but I don’t think I am a liar; I think I’ve been truthful about all of this.

ayoreo:
Uje jerénga (querénga) yiji
Satanas iroquéode to go.
english: If Jesus were to arrive to take his believers, I think I would be in God’s town among them. For I have not lied about myself.

ayoreo: Yijírasi to nanique uje anire yabai cha yu. Yojninga: “A yi to jne ga yicáji ti ga yóji*borácho*to jne.” Ga jé yicárasi Santa Cruz to. Yicai Santa Cruz ga yijína yijoranie ore. Uje anire, que yiraja i.*Joré*u, uje e toi nanique. Yijína ore ga jé yugúsiji Santa Cruz to. Mu anire, borácho yugué yu, chujerase yu. Ga yojninga: ‘A noñaque jõ borácho yugode.’
english: I only sinned like this for a little while, and I never went back to it.

ayoreo: Yojninga: ‘Yajni to ga yangureta Dupade to jne.’ Yajni to ga anire, que yipotapo borácho to rĩ go.
english: I took off when my husband left me. I said to myself, ‘I am going to leave and go up there and get drunk.’ And I got ready to go to Santa Cruz. I went to Santa Cruz with my friends. I went with her, but I’ve forgotten her name. Oh, yes, it was Joré, but she has died since then. I accompanied my friends and we lived in Santa Cruz. But then I got drunk, really drunk. Afterwards I said to myself, ‘Getting drunk is awful.’

ayoreo:
E gusu. Gusu yadode anínique,
jecute jé yingo yadode aja ore iji oriquei to.
english: I said to myself, ‘I’m returning and believing in*Dupade*once more. I’m turning back to him, and I don’t ever want to get drunk again. I’ve had it! There will be no more of me living like that because I’m going to confess what I have done in front of the church.’

ayoreo:
Jé yajéo gu, uje ise mu ca tague
yu go ome yadode udojo. E gusu udojo yadode uje yicárasiji Santa Cruz to
nanique. Anire, ise mu ca tague yu go uje yabayode gare. Yiya nanique gu uje
anire pota gapudíe jiedie. Yojninga: ‘Yiya to jne.’ Ga yijína Jnani ga e gusu.
Que toque jeta yijína jnacare uñeque.
english: That’s what I wanted to do, and I don’t think I lied about myself or about what I did. The only thing I did was to go to Santa Cruz. And I think I’ve not lied about having had only two husbands. I only left my first husband because he’d been unfaithful with many young women. And I told myself, ‘I will leave him.’ And then, I went to*Jnani*and only him. There is no way I would go to any other man.

ayoreo: Ore chichaga yu aja cuchade, mu ise mu ca tague ore jó. A Jesuse dirase ga chijna danguretatodie ga ise mu chijnarase yu to. Uje jé gusu udore gu yate gajnaréngone.
english: They accused me of things that I didn’t do, but I don’t think they meant to lie about me. Were Jesus to arrive to take his believers, I think he would take me, also. Because those are the only ugly things I’ve done.

ayoreo:
Yingo yu aja ore to gu yajéode u
jeta yajniapo rĩ. Uje yangári ore ga ore doi Dupade
anguretatodie iquei mu ore tangári ore. U mu que ore cha date gajnaréngone ome yu go.
english: I went before them with the intention of getting right with them again. I listened as others spoke, and how they accepted them, but they still wouldn’t stop their ugly treatment of me.

ayoreo:
Jecute que yo taque iji Dupade
to ga yojninga: ‘Jé ũré ga chiraja uje ũré yu rĩ.’
english: For this reason, I do not consider*Dupade*a liar and I say to myself, ‘*Dupade*knows the truth.’ But I ask myself, ‘Why don’t I go to meetings any more?’ But I know why. It’s because I went away from*Dupade*long ago and started doing bad things. But I would never want to do those things again. I know I told the truth when I said I’ve only had two husbands.

ayoreo:
¿Mu jé gotique gu a uje que
yijiapo conferénciadie to rĩ? Mu yiraja. Yiraja gu uje yiya Dupade nanique gu
uje yipésurásu cucha gajnaréngone. Mu ejode ga que toque jeta jé yipotapo yate
gajnaréngone rĩ. Mu ise ga je ũré yu uje yabayode gare.
english: And this is all I have to say.*(John 6:37-39)*

ayoreo: E gusu jne yuruode. *Sama – Tobité, Bolivia – agosto de 1975**Marina de Morarie churúsãre Sama uruode udore nanique*
english: Dupade – GodDupade’s word – Bible, ScripturePut someone’s face under water – Baptise *Sama – Tobité, Bolivia – August of 1975**Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie**Scripture portions are added.*

ayoreo:
Que yadode déji móntedie to
nanique; yadode jogadi udé Tobité.
english: I will tell you about myself. I won’t tell about when we were in the jungles; I will tell about after we came to a place called Tobité.

ayoreo:
Yetaque Dupade nanique, yetaguipis. Que yo yujodie
chequei to go uje jĩrique ore irasique Dupade. Yetaque nanique, yetaguipis.
english: I was not like the other women at all that so quickly loved Dupade. I hated him, I despised him.

ayoreo:
Yujé yabode. Gare yabode uje
yujé ore.
english: I killed my babies. I killed two of them.

ayoreo:
Eva chojningame yu: “Dapuré á,
¿adute Dupade Uruode a?”
english: Evie*(Evelyn Morrill)*said to me: “Dupuré, have you heard about Dupade’s Word?”

ayoreo:
Mu yojninga: “Eva, que yudute
Dupade urúrique go. Íjnoque Dupade.”
english: I said: “Evie, I haven’t heard any of Dupade’s Words at all. There is no Dupade.”

ayoreo:
Eva narárome yu. Chojninga:
“Anire, Dapuré, ¿arajá ya uje Dupade cus, ga déji gaté?
english: Evie spoke to me. She said: “Well, Dapuré, did you know there is a Dupade, and he is in heaven?”

ayoreo:
Mu yojninga: “Eva, yiraja ãrie,
mu que Dupárique cuse go. Íjnoque gaté; idája. Idája gaté ga que Dupárique cuse
go.”
english: But I said: “Evie, of course I know, but there is no Dupade at all. There is no heaven; if there is, it’s far away. The sky is a long way up, and there is no Dupade up there.”

ayoreo:
Mu jé ore chiraja to, chequedíe, yujodie
chequei ore, uje jé upo uje yangureta Dupade to rĩ ica. Uje que urénga déji
yajei. Ore tṍra yu aja yodi. Yojninga: “Yi jne ga yangureta Dupade to jne.”
Ga e yangureta Dupade Abi ore. **ã́ri Dupade:**
english: But now they know, the women, my fellow women, that only recently I have come to believe in Dupade. Though I almost didn’t. They have*thrown me in the water.*I said to them: “I want to be baptized and now I believe in Dupade.” And I believe in Dupade’s Son.

ayoreo:
Señora Marina chojningame yu:
“Dapuré yá, ¿jé areque a ujetiga acatecã́ri Dupade?”
english: Señora Marina said to me: “Dapuré, wouldn’t it be good if you would talk to Dupade?”

ayoreo:
Yojninga: “Señora Marina, jé
areque, yicatecã́ri jne.” Ga e yicatecã́ri.
english: I said: “Señora Marina, that’s right, I’ll talk to him.” And I now pray to Dupade.

ayoreo:
Chojningame yu: “Ueradipis ome
yu uje bacatecã́ri Dupade. Aya badode to jne. Ca ajepo babode jne rĩ.”
english: She said to me: “It’s wonderful that you now pray to Dupade. Now you need to stop doing the things you did before you believed in him. Don’t kill your babies, like you used to do.”

ayoreo:
Ga yiya. Yiya yadode. Yiya
yadode ga yangureta Dupade; yanguretapise. Yangureta. Ga unere yui ome yu. Que
yiya go Dupade Uruode go.
english: And I stopped. I stopped doing the bad things I used to do. I stopped, because I believed in Dupade. I really do believe in him. I have faith in him. And it feels good to me. I always go to meetings to hear Dupade’s Word.

ayoreo:
A Señora Marina chudute jne
udojo yuruode uje que yiya go. Señora Marina ujetiga a cho dáyugu re jne
ujetiga yiyárasa. Mu a irasé re uje chudute uje que yiya go Dupade urúrique
go. Jecucha yicoi iji domíngo jne. Mu ejo iji te uje e yicopoi rĩ iji de
sábado.
english: Señora Marina will probably hear these words of mine, that I’ve not stopped going to meetings. She would be very sad if I had. But she’ll be happy that we never miss going to hear Dupade’s Word. We always go on Sundays. And along with that, we go on Saturdays also.

ayoreo:
Ejode ga Yúni chojningame yu
ica: “Ité yá, arajayorase Dupade urúrique. Jecucha uacapojnáño chequedíe. Gusu
Davidaté uje que pojna chequedíe to go.”*(2 Corintios 5:17)*
english: *Yuni*talked to me recently and said: “Mother, as if you didn’t know what Dupade’s Word says. You women are always scolding and criticizing each other. Only Davidaté doesn’t scold people.”

ayoreo: E gusu yuruode.
english: This is all I have to say.

ayoreo: *Dapuré – Tobité, Bolivia – agosto de 1975**Marina de Morarie churúsãre Dapuré uruode nanique*
english: *Dapuré – Tobité, Bolivia – August – 1975**Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie**Scripture portions are added.*

ayoreo: Atata uje ĩri iji Tobitéraque nanique. Yetingarape uje*Yójidé*chijnárase yabode iji yari. Yicagóji de nanique iji Tobité yúode. Taningane ga yicagóji dojo nanique Tobité yúode iji ta. Ga yico cojñone ore iji ta.
english: I’ll tell about arriving in Tobité long ago. I’ll try to remember about*Yójidé*trying to take my children from me. We were on our way to Tobité. It was when we first were going to Tobité in the beginning. And we ran into some people on the way.

ayoreo: Mu ayoré déji jnoringai ga chatata. Chojninga: “A yicode chijna uacabidie jne, disi játigo iji uacari,” chojninga*Chané*. “A codé chijna uacabode jne, disi játigo,” chojninga.
english: But an Ayoré was going somewhere and he relayed some news to us. He said: “My grandmother wants to take your children, all of the orphans among you,” said the man named*Chané.*“Grandma will take your children, the orphan children,” he said.

ayoreo: Ga yitodógo ore yúode mu ca yitodógo ore go uje ore chijna disiode, disi jádode.
english: And we were scared to meet them, really scared of them because they were saying they were going to take the children, the orphans.

ayoreo: Ore chaco itarapérique*Dequerai*gai, mu cachi ore acógai. Yicoi uje dóji ti guedé. Guedé doi te uje idája uje yico. Mu chayóna ore,*Chané*, ga chijna poca.
english: They hid*Daquerai*under a blanket, but just hid him. We left when the sun was ‘there’. The sun was far down when we left. But*Chané*ran after them, carrying his gun.

ayoreo: Dacode chi chojninga: “Acho jne á jeta quejna om ua.”
english: His grandma said: “Eat something before running off.”

ayoreo: Yibidi. Yojninga: “¡De ti é! Chicho uaque,*Dequerainaté*é!” yojninga.
english: I yelled. I said: “He’s over there! He’s going to shoot you,*Dequerainaté!*” I said.

ayoreo: Ga yico. Yicagóji dojo. Ga anire, yicoji ta, degúrase ta. Mu cho ta degúrase ji de.
english: We took off. We went over ‘there’. And then, we went another direction toward the camp nearby.

ayoreo: “¡*Chané*e diapo rĩ é!” ore chojninga. “Ajíñome yoquiga casodicaite gáyode ome eduguéjnai.” Ga yico.
english: “¡*Chané is coming back again,*” they said. “Let’s go!”

ayoreo: Yisago yocugúchade. Yico garai ga yimóngo iji ta uje guedé jno. E dejac uje yimóngo iji yoquirengúi. Chaguhei chejnapise yoque. Jnanione chachẽre dati múñi que yajo cuchárique. Yico garai údi mu ore chojninga: “*Burai*ducoré chacose á.”
english: We grabbed our things. We went toward the plains and saw that the sun was going down. By the time we caught sight of our camp it was night. We were hungry. Even though the men had been scouting for food we hadn’t eaten anything. We crossed the plains, and they told us: “*Burai*and them are hiding.”

ayoreo: Yidogorodíe põ rĩ. Mu ore chacose que uje ore pógu iji te yiquigade.
english: My ankles were swollen. But I had to keep going because I knew that they were hiding, but following us.

ayoreo: “Angoño yu ga yijína ore que á,” yojninga. Ga ore chacáji ta. Mu cojñone iji ta ga ore chojninga, “¡Boyo!”
english: “Tell me what to do and I’ll go with them,” I said. And they entered the jungle. But the non-Ayoré person said: “Run!”

ayoreo: “Ore chinguirarása Ijmai yoque que a. Ore chinguirarása poca iji*Querúidé*,” ore chojninga.
english: “They are going to shoot*Ijmai*and us. They’re going to shoot their guns at*Querúidé,”*they said.

ayoreo: Ga yacágoji ti. Yajéngome cojñone. Mu uñai chise yoque iji toniñeque iji tie.  Chajna tie tiji jnumi iji ti yoquiquei.
english: And we went way off ‘there’. I was afraid of the non-Ayoreos. But one of them came to us farther away by the river. He was following the river ahead of us.

ayoreo: Ga chojninga: “Asi óriji echode jne, barásade,” chojningame eduguéjnai ome*Jnaniné*. “Mu asi ome queséode ome. Mu asi ome barásade echoi jne.”   E gusu.
english: He said: “Give your countrymen some salt,” he said to the chief, to*Jnaniné.*“But give them some knives, too, when you give your countrymen salt.”

ayoreo: *Enueidaté – Tobité, Bolivia – 1970.*
english: That’s all.

ayoreo: *Marina de Morarie**churúsãre Enueidaté atatagode udojo.*
english: *Enuedaté – Tobité, Bolivia – 1960**Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie.*

ayoreo: *Pasédaté*ipísi u*Tojúrua Picaneré*. Uñaria i tu*Toqué*. Dajábi i tu*Chicópode*. Isoguié i tu*Usúa*.
english: *First of all Pasédaté wanted to tell about her family:*Pasédaté’s real name is*Tojúrua*of the*Picaneré Clan*. Her daughter-in-law’s name is*Toqué*. Her younger brother’s name is*Chicópode*.*Isoguié’s*other name is*Usúa*.

ayoreo: Ducodégoto u*Pasédaté*.  Abai bajai i tu*Imocarãi*ga ducosi chequésãre. Ducosi pos.
english: Pasédaté is from the*Ducodégosode*(People of the Graves), so she, as a woman, is called*Ducodégoto*.

ayoreo: Daté ipísi u*Ocojnamené Jnuruminé*. Daté ica põ ga ajénique chequésãre. Dai ipísi u*Yurané Picanerai*. Choquíjnape dai nanique ga tosi ome.*Pasédaté*dajábi uñai qué eca ga déji Pai Idai ga gusúpúsu uté. Ipísi u*Iringai*nanique.
english: Her first husband’s name was*Imocarãi*and he died from fever. In describing his illness she said, “He was “food” for the fever.”

ayoreo: Abai iji deréjnane unojo i tu*Dorojoi Chiquenori*,*Ojicureidé*. (Apodo:*Presidente*.)
english: Her mother’s real name was*Ocojnámene*, of the*Jnuruminé Clan*. Her mother’s stomach became swollen and she died from intestinal complications. Her father’s real name was*Yurané*of the*Picanerai*Clan. He lived to be very old and died of old age. Pasédaté’s other brother is still living and lives at the Priest’s Camp and he is her only living relative. Long ago his real name was*Iringai*.

ayoreo: *Pasédaté*ipísi u*Tojúrua*ga i tṍraji unojo: Ujeta jnanírique tṍra dacoté ga chijnoque dagúchade ga chiretási depé ga chósi ‘tojúruru’ aja dajesua.
english: Her present day husband’s name is*Dorojoi*of the*Chiquenóri Clan*, father of*Ojicurei*. (He is affectionately called*Presidente*.}

ayoreo: Yiméco doridie ica ga yijmóco. Yicaruaco  dajudíe.  Yipéco cutérone iji monte. Yibájo ga yijnójo cutérone. Ga yijínango yocabayode uje ore bac.
english: Pasédaté’s real name is Tojúrua and this is where that name came from: When a man throws his wife out, he takes his things and fills his bag with them, and takes off “helter-skelter” along his way. So I guess you could call Pasédaté ‘helter-skelter”.

ayoreo: Ité ore chimere doridie to. Yijóchago chicórone to.
english: We twisted off*doridie*fruit from the plant, and scraped the pulp off the leaves with our bottom teeth. We harvested the*dajudíe*plant to make string from it’s leaves.

ayoreo: Yijárogo damini aja pucutadé iji mimiré ajñunie, jé aja dutuedie ajñunie rĩ. Paraquedie uje yijárogo cucha ajñunie. Yipéco echoi.
english: We sucked honey when we lived in the jungles. We hunted honey and chopped into trees to get the honey. We accompanied our husbands on the honey hunts.

ayoreo: Uneréjna yacorenie arione, yajóguedie. Ore chibore yajóguedie. Yijóchagome. Ore chiqueta ejo tome. Ore chedu chanúone aja ejo, pidode. Yocade, yoca ajámone u apiejnane ga yipéco.
english: My mother also twisted off the*doridie*fruit in her day. We also dug up water plants.

ayoreo: Uje uajácho dosipéode ga yarengoi. Cobéode, mu jé yajo.
english: We ground up the seeds of watermelon and squash with our mortars and pestles. We cooked the seeds that we would grind. We sucked salt as we ate that food.

ayoreo: Dachabeque piracadie nanique. Cojnanie ore chiro dopéode ga ore chátaja peroguedie.
english: The meat of the wild pig (*yacorenie*) is very delicious, as well as anteater. We roasted anteater by digging holes for it. We lit fires in the hole. We only used coals from wood in our underground ovens. We roasted turtles, and whole families of small turtles all went in and we sucked on their meat.

ayoreo: *Pasédaté – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988.*
english: When the wild meat was tough we boiled it. It gets white when we boil it, but we go ahead and eat it.

ayoreo: *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Pasédaté atatagode udojo nanique.*
english: The pots we used were made of clay long ago. When they made their pots and jars they added the broken pieces of pottery to the clay mass.

ayoreo: Ore chingo ñane cureséode, ga jnanione chijna uñai, ga jnacarode chijna uñai. Ujetiga ore curesei charãja ore oidi ga ore yucho. Ore isíode u dacháyode.
english: The game is played like a relay, with the more mature men forming one team, and the younger men another.

ayoreo: *Cajoide – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 11 de octubre de 1985.*
english: *Cajoide – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1985.*

ayoreo: *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Cajoide atatagode udojo nanique.*
english: *Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie.*

ayoreo: Jnanione ore chojningane ñane nanique: “¡Apéso uacamichiracudie ga yimichirecaco jne!”
english: The invitation to play is:  “Make your forked sticks for throwing and we’ll throw them and see who’s stick goes farthest!”

ayoreo: *Cajoide – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 11 de octubre de 1985.*
english: *Cajoide – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1985.*

ayoreo: *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Cajoide atatagode udojo nanique.*
english: *Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie.*

ayoreo: Ore chojninga: “¡Beyoi te á, ga yoco cojñúgu ore jne!”
english: *Pajñaminone – Cojñopite ñane* Invitation to wrestle: “Go over there, and we’ll wrestle with them.”

ayoreo: Uje gosi chirajapise enga jorane ore chojninga: “¡Que pinguíraja jnumi gotique!”
english: If a person is especially good at wrestling, this is said of him:  “He’s invincible.”

ayoreo: *Cajoidé – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 11 de octubre de 1985.*
english: *Cajoide – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1985.*

ayoreo: *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Cajoide atatagode udojo nanique.*
english: *Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie.*

ayoreo: A gosi tibidi dajorane enga chojningame ore: “¡Abasója ajã́ro ga yurugúco ñane!”
english: The invitation to play is: “Go to the center of the village and we’ll arm wrestle each other.”

ayoreo: *Cajoide – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 11 de octubre de 1985.*
english: *Cajoide – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1985.*

ayoreo: *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Cajoide atatagode udojo nanique.*
english: *Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie.*

ayoreo: “¡Beyoi ga yicoi*jobedayé*á!” Ore pésu dagué taja ga jé ore chijñecare aja jnese, ga ore chisa ñane jmanáyone. Ga jnacarode, gapudíe ore chapúse ñane aja. Abechugode ore doi to uje dago ore, mu chequedie que ore doi go.
english: The invitation to play is:  “Come on, let’s play*jobedayé*!” They group up and then they all join hands and sing. The young boys and young girls keep changing  partners.

ayoreo: Orichorájna ga cho uje jnacarode ore doi ‘pelota’ ga orichorájna to.
english: There’s lots of noise like the noise when young men play soccer. They can play ‘*jobedayé*’ in the dry or rainy season.

ayoreo: Ore chapúsaja esode, sequeredie ore jnese uje ore pésu*jobedayé*. Ore doi*jobedayé*ga ore pésu quigode. Ore pésu iji dejai uje cho diri. Ueradé ajárone ome, ore ajñamite gu, enga ore cãra daradedie ome ñane.
english: When they play ‘jobedayé’ they follow certain rules. They play on nights when it stays light out. A good place to play it is in the center of the village because it’s a game the people like to watch.

ayoreo: Ore pésu gu uje, yocajñamicho gu uje cucha oméjna ome yoque.
english: They play it because it’s a fun game to play, a sort of celebration for when things are going well.

ayoreo: *Cajoide – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 11 de octubre de 1985.* *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Cajoide atatagode udojo nanique.*
english: *Cajoide – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1985**Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie*

ayoreo: “Abasója ajã́ro ga yicoi ‘pachiapídi.”
english: The invitation to play is:  “Go to the center of the village and we’ll play*touch*.”

ayoreo:
“Yocacháidode jé
pijoque.”
english: When the teams are formed there are two young men for each older man.**Acháidode*means teams. When they have formed their teams they say: “Now that we have our teams we can play.”

ayoreo: “¡E chijna!”
english: When a person is ‘touched’ they say,*He got him!*

ayoreo:
Ore chisi ome ore ugúchade ome ore: enuéone, duchúbiedie,
paode.
english: The object of the game, besides winning prizes, is to keep in fit condition. The losers give the winners from their possessions, or they hunt honey for the winners. Among the things given would be:  bags, gourds, rope, honey gourds, tapir hide shoes.

ayoreo: “¡Ba casé de daque ja!”
english: If they bring in honey for the winners this is what they say when they give it to him: “Here’s the honey, you earned it.”

ayoreo: *Cajoide – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 11 de octubre de 1985**Marina de Morarie churúsãre Cajoide atatagode udojo nanique*
english: *Cajoide – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1985.*

ayoreo: *Achinguirai*chi inóningáyató*erapurujnánguenie*. Jõra tu*gaco*uje inoninga*achinguirane*. Jõrai tu*yodi*;*achinguirai*inóningáyasórone u*yodi*.
english: *Achinguirai Inóningáyatódie Cucha*

ayoreo: Ore chinguíra posode aja ayoré ga ore chinguíra*gaco*ujñarone ga que chequésãre gotique. Ore chinguíra*erapujnángueníe*ujñarone ga que chequésãre gotique.*Achinguirane*inóningáyató u gu.
english: The antidote for rattlesnake venom is the fox. Another antidote for rattlesnake bite is the tall, white, stork-like bird called*gaco*. Another is water.

ayoreo: Ayoré irajatade ore chiraja inóningáyasórone cucha ga ore chiqueta posode ayoré ore.
english: If the snake throws its venom at a person and the medicine man uses the stork-like bird’s breath it won’t kill a person. Or if they use the breath of the fox, it won’t kill them. That’s because it is the antidote for snake venom.

ayoreo: Ujñarone u ore uruode ga chubúchu ga tṍraji. Daijnane ore chigase ga chijnímiiji posode ayoré. Daijna cuchábode ore. Ore iquetaque posode ayoré; ore chijnímiji ore gu. Pujopie garani dajnu ditáji ome jnacare bajade uje daijna ore. Ga ore tocade ga pujopie chiqueta raja ore iji dajnu ditáji ga daijna ore ga chijnímiji ore.
english: Ayoré persons who understand witchcraft know what the antidotes are and they can heal people who have been bitten by snakes. Breath, that is their word for blowing, and medicine comes out of them when they blow.

ayoreo: Chi daijnane ore chujé dajnu ditáji ga pujopie tṍraji ore ome ore enga jé daijna ore.
english: The medicine men bite to pull venom out of the one who has been bitten by a rattlesnake. The medicine men are powerful. To heal people who have been bitten they pull the venom out of them. According to the medicine men who lived long ago, power comes to the medicine men from the twisted wild pineapple plant’s fibers. They open themselves up and power presents itself to them from the twisted wild pineapple plant. By using the twisted wild pineapple plant, those who are already medicine men are able to bestow power to heal on other men.

ayoreo: *Sidode*chijnime ore ga daijna ore. Ore chóji*sidode*otadi ga jé u uje chijnime ore ga daijna ore.
english: We are told the medicine men use the twisted wild pineapple fibers like a whip, and when they hit a person with the fibers power comes out of them and enters the person they whip. In this way the person that was whipped becomes a medicine man, also.

ayoreo: Uje daijnárique ujuya ga cho dajuñame ayoréode ga chequésãre ore ga ore chujé daijna ujuyarane.
english: Tobacco is able to bring power to people, also, so that they can become medicine men. The tobacco is steeped in water, and when the tobacco brew is drunk it overcomes the person who has drunk it, and they go into a trance. After that they become medicine men.

ayoreo: *Cajoidé –  Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1985.*
english: The medicine man has the power to curse as well as heal. When a medicine man mistreats or curses people and causes them to die, however, the people kill him for he has become a danger to his people.

ayoreo: *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Cajoidé atatagode udojo nanique.*
english: *Cajoidé – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988**Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie*

ayoreo: Ore erane u*Dosapéode,*ga ore tujúa dáringai deyácho.
english: When the tunes pertained to the **Dosapéode Clan*, they would announce this when they sang.

ayoreo: *Picanerane*ore erane u*Tarajane, Umarajane*ore ga ore tujua dáringane deyácho.
english: Two of the **Picanerãi Clan*tunes were **Tarajane*and**Umarajane*, and they would announce them when they sang.

ayoreo: *Chiquenone*ore erane u*Ugájnane,*ga ore tujua dáringane deyacho –*Chiquenone*ore erãcho*Jucaramenie*to.
english: One of the **Chiquenoi Clan*tunes was **Ugájnane*, and they would announce this when they sang – another of the*Chiquenoi Clan*tunes that singers used was the **Jucaramenie*.

ayoreo: *Étacorone*erane*Étacorojnane*, ga ore tujua dáringane deyácho.
english: The*Étacori Clan*tunes were called **Étacorojnane*, and singers would announce this when they sang.

ayoreo: Ore terãchu ga ore chisa dejai gatócoro ome.
english: When they sang with rattles they would sing till the middle of the night.

ayoreo: Yoco jiame piradedie, perane.
english: We would*tune up*our voices before singing by trying out the tune and the words of the songs until we were sure we had them right.

ayoreo: Deyaquechoquíode ore tujua*Tarajane*iji dejaque uñai, dejaque uñane ga ore tujua*Umarajane,*dejaque ejoi tujua*Ugájnane.*
english: The really good singers would announce the tunes they were going to use. One night, a singer might say, I’m going to use the tune called **Tarajane*, and on another night he might announce that he was going to use the tune called **Umarajane*or perhaps the one called  **Ugájnane.*

ayoreo: Ga deyaquechoquíode chojninga: ‘Yujua perane jnese uje deyaquechoqui yu gu.’  Dejaque ejoi ga tujua*Jucaramenie*, dejaque ejoi ga tujua*Dosapéode.*Dejaque ejoi tujua*Étacorojnane*.
english: And really good singers would know all of the tunes. A talented singer might say: ‘I know all the tunes, because as a good singer I’ve had a lot of practice.’ And so one night he would use the tune called **Jucaramenie,*and the next night might choose to sing the tunes of the **Dosapéode*. Or perhaps he might announce on another night that he’d be using the tunes called **Étacorojnane.*

ayoreo: Chequedie ore nijína deyaquechoquíode úringai deyaque ga diyajmenie ga ore chucãre dajudie; ga ore chiro dorájnui; ga ore chiro dora.
english: The women would stay awake to listen to the performances of the really good singers, and those that did, would spin thread or work on their weavings and bags while they listen.

ayoreo: *Jicai*chojninga: ‘Yuguhese yerámone gai ga yisa peraque unejángone aja yerane.’
english: **Jicai*once said: ‘Sometimes I get mixed up on the tunes and while I’m using one tune, I’ll mix another tune into it.”

ayoreo: Uje deroco ga que pirijína ore. Mu ayoré iñeca unérone ore chijócãre moi tiji yoquedodie.
english: When ‘the singing’ is not so good (tasteless) people don’t stay up to listen. But a really good Ayoré singer throws sleep right out of our eyes.

ayoreo: *Icadiguédé*, ore iroguebeque, ore chajmára iradedie.
english: The songs  of **Icadiguédé*are some that we really look forward to hearing! People are always asking him to sing.

ayoreo: *Chojmárané*, ore chajmára iradedie uje uneréjna.
english: **Chojmaráné’*s songs are really sweet and we love hearing him sing.

ayoreo: *Uchaidé*, ore chajmára ga ore chiroque ome iradedie. Ore chiroque*Mariadé*to.
english: People find **Ucháidé*’s songs very entertaining and they often ask him for his songs.

ayoreo: *Carodidé,*ore chiroque uje ore chajmára iradedie.
english: They ask **Mariadé*to sing, as well.

ayoreo: *Ajidi*, ore chiroque uje ore chajmára iradedie.
english: **Carodidé*was and is still someone who is always asked to sing, because they really liked his songs.

ayoreo: *Imeseráné,*ore chajmára iradedie ga ore chiroque*Imeseráné*.
english: **Ajidi*, was also someone who was always asked to sing, because they really liked his songs.

ayoreo: Gapudie ore chedo dabayode uje ore jnusi ore gu, ga ore chijñecare ga ore chedo dabayode, ore jnusi ore gu.
english: They are always asking **Imeserané*to sing, because they really like the way he sings.

ayoreo: Jnacarode ore chijñecare to uje ore jnusi dacotedie, ga ore chedo ore to uje ore jnusi ore gu.
english: When the young women sing about their husbands, it’s generally because they miss them, and their songs are about missing their husbands.

ayoreo: Abechugué, ga cha ijñecacai, abechugué. Puyágome chequedie gu. Ore chijñecare ga ore todo jeta dago ore.
english: The young men miss their wives when they are away from them, and they sing about them and about how much they miss them.

ayoreo: Uje chequéraque tṍra dabi nanique, ga chijñecárapo rĩ uje cana gaté gu.
english: Married women with children quit singing. It’s taboo for women to sing. They’re afraid if they do sing, others will think they’re flirting.

ayoreo: Disiode ore chijñecare aja dasoránenie, mu que ore chijñecare iji dejai.
english: Sometimes a woman would throw her child out, and she would start singing again to show that she wanted to attract men and play around again.

ayoreo: *Oidábiadé – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988.*
english: Children sing when they’re out playing, but they don’t sing at night.

ayoreo: *Marina de Morarie churúsãre udojo nanique.*
english: ImeseranéAjidiCarodidéMariadéUcháidéChojmaránéIcadiguédé

ayoreo: **chedo dajesudie diyequé quedéjna:**
english: *Alíciadé Iradedie Quigode*

ayoreo: *Aliciadé*chojninga: “Yiabi yaquesugode unojo enga yijñecáraja. Yedobu yajesudie diyequé quedéjna.”
english: Alíciadé: “I am going to sing. I will be mentioning one of the women I loved on my travels.”

ayoreo: Alíciadé chojninga: Yo jóroque cucha pajugúguiji yu, yajesudie diyequé ore jõ. Yipetique nóna ore jnese jõ. Yipetique nónarása yijnamía (mi hermanita –  yĩcarai u ome yacoté). Yuhécha uñeque nónarása yimochade. Yipeque uñeque canárase iji gaté yui.
english: Alíciadé: “In vain, I would like to divide myself up among the women I have taken during my travels, leaving a little part of me with each one of them. And a part of me with my “little sister” (Note: an endearing name he uses in reference to his wife). But another part of me is with those I slept with.  Another part of me looks forward to playing around in the future.”

ayoreo: Alíciadé chojninga: Yopié chugúpẽre chóguiji yu ga yajna. Enga yimochadé ore iji quigode erãpe jõra.
english: Alíciadé: “In my thoughts, I’m like a bird that takes flight to follow my lovers. I pursue one of my lovers in my thoughts.

ayoreo: A yimochadé chajiriráse dajei gajnerĩ mu chimo yu iji. Chojningame daté: “Ité  á,*Ocoroi*u. Déji dajei iji ti.” (Nota:******Aliciadé i u Ocoroi uje jnacare nanique, enga cáma abode cuse.)*
english: My lover is looking down the road to see if I’m coming. She says to her mother, ‘Mother, it’s Ocoroi. He’s on the road coming this way.’ (Note: Ocoroi was Alíciadé’s name before he had children.)

ayoreo: Mu yimochadé a narariráse enga chojninga: ‘*Ocoroi*á, ¿gotique bãri? Uje que acasipísi rique uñéngome bacoté casodica ore a?”
english: She speaks to me. She says, ‘Ocoroi, why have you come? Don’t you have the nerve to approach any of your other wives?’

ayoreo: Yichajnai ga yojninga: “Mu bamo uje acho yiyaridedie edoi enga yajna ua.”
english: I answer her, ‘But can’t you see that I’m tired of the others that you mention and I think only about you?’”

ayoreo: Alíciadé chojninga: Yopié chugúpẽre chóguiji yu. Yiyója garai casodicai jõ. A yiyo ga yibasirásaja*yijnamía*.
english: Alíciadé: “I become a flying bird again. And I fly back to the plains where I was when my thoughts took me away. I fly till I land close to my wife, ‘my little sister!’”

ayoreo: *Guebéjnané – Tobité, Bolivia – 1960.* *Joyce Davis Buchegger churúsare Guebéjnane atatagode udojo nanique.*
english: *Guebéjnané – Tobité, Bolivia – 1960**Transcribed by Joyce Davis Buchegger**Translated to English by: Maxine Morarie*

ayoreo: *(**Igarubidaté*achachagúgode u. Jé basa ga*Jnurumini*u júdi.)
english: *Igarubídaté Igoriguéode***Praise chant for a grandson that killed a Jaguar**(*Words of the chant by *Igarubídaté – her tune is a Jnurumini Clan tune.)*

ayoreo: E yirasiguipise yájnami **Ijniminayé*uqué que’.
english: I am so happy about what my grandson **Ijnimínayé*did just now:

ayoreo: “¿Jé gotíguipie, que yagu que ga que yugúsi yebiédatérigui?”
english: “Now why didn’t I just eat him at the start, then I wouldn’t be slobbering right now?” (Slobbering is another way to refer to crying/lamenting)

ayoreo: Jé yirasiguipise yájnatigo ore ome carataque-chagúode-daté uaqué.
english: I am so happy about what my grandsons did to that very fierce, man-eating jaguar.

ayoreo: ¡Carataquechaguá! Ãriji ga apésu yujñarinie yoquijmátaringuejnái! Chi babose yocai nanique, mu bapesute gajnarenguipísi uté u uje e que yujnusínangopo yocai uté rĩ. Uhua u uje bapésu yoquijmátaringuéone nanique.
