english: O, man-eating Jaguar, rise up from the ground! Make my sisters and me poor orphans all over again if you can. They tell me that long ago you consumed our father, who we can’t remember any more. It was you who caused my sisters and me to suffer such a great poverty.

ayoreo: ¿Jé gotíguipie uje yujo casidé gata ore cho jõro ome caratai? ¿Jé gotíguipie uje yujo casidé gata ore cháguena*cucha urõsódatéi*uñai gai?
english: O, why did so many old women like myself become paralyzed with fear because of **the harmful creature*? Why do we old ladies have to stand silently by before*one of these harmful creatures*?

ayoreo: Jé yirasiguipise yocurásai cojñoque imocarãque*Luis*uqué uje nóna*Acácomi*diríca, enga u uje chinguíra*Acácomi*yui*cucha urõsodaté*. Chinguíra*Acácomi*yui*cucha urõsódatéi*aja jnumi ome daboca.
english: I am very happy about our countryman, the non-Ayoré man “who carries a gun,” that one called Luis, because it was he who went with**Acácomi*and brought down*the harmful creature*that Acácomi killed. He brought Acácomi’s victim down for him,*the harmful creature*.

ayoreo: Yirasiguipísi upo yape uñai*Dejabi*uqué rĩ uje chinguíra*cucha urõsódatéi**¡túgaja!*jnumi durasede. Uñeque chinguíra cucha urõsodatedie uña aja jnumi ome dajorane ore que.”
english: I am also happy with that nephew of mine,**Dejabi*, because on that very day he just threw*that harmful creature*down with a thud! Another one of*those harmful creatures*has been thrown to the ground by someone who loved his friends.

ayoreo: Jé upo rĩ ga “dutué a pita,”*Ijniminaté*uaqué, doi ga irasiguipise dabai, yájnape*Ijniminé*uqué.
english: May that “*Tender New Squash*”**Ijnimínaté,*appreciate what her husband, my grandson,*Ijnimínayé*has done. (Note: One of*Ijniminaté’s*clan possessions is the*tender new squash;*that is why Igarubídaté refers to her grandson’s wife with this term – which is very complimentary.)

ayoreo: Jé upo ga qué bajéo jeta yugúsiji yebiédatérigui rĩ ome ‘jnu upo’ dequedójnari.
english: You’re probably wishing that I were slobbering right now for that greatly esteemed*Ripe Green Hot Pepper*! (Note: One of her grandson’s clan-possessions is*‘the**green hot pepper,’*so she is using this term as a complement to her grandson when speaking of him to the jaguar.)

ayoreo: Yájnape*Acácomi*uqué átedaté uqué ome*cucha urõsodedaté uaqué.*
english: What my grandson*Acácomi*did to*that harmful creature*was a marvelous deed.

ayoreo: “¡Dirábiá! aguitóji gadoi iji duruñeque ga chugúpẽre-chagúode jĩraque tagu uté arione ga ore chijnime pujnúone ga ore chisi ñane iji uje chajningarásaja dejode.
english: *Little Daylight*, hurry, come from wherever you are to us so that the vultures can see to quickly devour*the harmful creature’s flesh*, tearing it apart, pulling out its lungs and dividing them up among themselves.

ayoreo: “E yininapúsu tupo*‘pojnátique’*yape rĩ,*Dejabi*ore. ¡Urénga, jé ore pésupúsu yininaquéode! Uje ore chinguíra*cucha urõsodaté*aja jnumi.”
english: I’m so happy right now about my son,*Dejabi*, blooming like a flower, and the others. I’m so moved! They have made me so happy. They have thrown down*that harmful creature*to the ground!

ayoreo: “Jé yajire yu ome yájnatigo jnacare ore que, uje yirasiguipise yájnami*Daturájnai*,*Luís*ore gachidode tamoco ome cutejmane ore oráso uje ore chujé carataque-chagui uqué.”
english: Now, I’ve worked hard, praising my grandsons, the young men, and I will now share how happy I am about the dogs of my grandson **Daturájnai,*and those of*Luis*; because of the speed of these dogs in chasing the jaguar, the young men could kill*that harmful creature*.

ayoreo: *Igarubidate – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988.*
english: cojñoi*– white person*corẽ́*– the woman that chants for those who have killed a jaguar*corãi*–the man who chants for those who have killed a jaguar*imocarãi*– the one who has a weapon*Acácomi*– the person in the chant who fired the first shot – thus he is the one who get’s the praise*orasoi*–fast runner (pl. orasode)*oraserãi m*. –he who runs fast*oraserẽ  f.*– she who runs fast*coriguei*–praise chant for the person who kills a jaguar or another important victim. Many times it is someone who has had family members killed by a jaguar who chants the praises of a jaguar killer.  (possessive form: igoriguei)*The harmful creature – a way to refer to a jaguar*

ayoreo: *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Igarubidaté igoriguéode nanique.*
english: *Ijnimi – name of child**Ijnimínaté – mother of Ijnimi**Ijnimínayé – father of Ijnimi**Ijnimináquide – grandfather of Ijnimi**Ijnimidacode – grandmother of Ijnimi**Acácomí – Ijnimínayé’s name before his child Ijnimi was born

ayoreo: (Catecã́ri Caratai)
english: *Acacomi Yui Caratac*

ayoreo: “*Cucha Urõsódatéi*á, jé badi írique durasede uje que asururase paicarupe tiẽchui yirabai uqué durasede, ga qué yugúsiji yebiédatérigui. Ga uñeque datéraque qué yugúcoji yoquebiédatérigui iji uñeque quigáningo.
english: *You who caused such awful harm in the past*, why didn’t you react more quickly, then you could have thrown my grandson down, the one who has the beautiful line-markings as one of his **edopasade*(clan possessions). Then you would have seen me slobbering (Note: another way to refer to crying/lamenting) right now, along with the mother of another victim. We would both be lamenting the absence of our loved ones if you had been quicker – they would have become your victims, instead.

ayoreo: “*Cucha Urõsódatéi*uripiecojná, basururase yájnape iyiejnai tuqué durasede. Mu ujóriguidatei ore cho cucha pimotomáji ua iji bagachodi.
english: O,*you who caused such awful harm in the past*, you did destroy one of ‘my awesome grandsons’, tearing him apart. (Note: he is referring to Mateo’s dog who the jaguar killed.) But now his great companions consider you a ‘worthless thing’ there on the ground where you met your end.

ayoreo: “*Cucha Urõsódatéi*á, bamatate yájnatigo, yatígo ore. Gojñáringo a dobi yájnatigo, yatígo ore iji Filadélfia casé gajneri enga gojñáringo a narare gai jne enga chojningame: ‘Yirabai ica*Pojórone*, yirabai ica*Imeserané*ore gajneri: Chi*Ijnimínayé*baque diríca. Mu*Cucha Urõsódatéi*chóji ñane. Ga noñómiji gaté ga jnutaque degumito ome. Ga ore doi*Ijnimínayé*uje chi noñome caratai tiji gaté. E mu jnutaque degumito ga ore chujé.’ A gojñáringo chojninga dirome ome yirabai ica*Pojórone, Imeserané*ore.
english: Oh,*you who caused such awful harm**in the past*, you tried to belittled my grandsons and my sons. But now the news about my grandsons and sons will have reached faraway Filadelphia where my other grandsons live, those who live far away from me. My grandsons whose names are*Pojórone*and*Imeserané*will hear about what happened, and the news will go like this: ‘They say*Ijnimínayé*was hunting honey the other day, but*he that causes such awful harm*fought with him.*Ijnimínayé*left him (the jaguar) in the trees and went to call others in the village to take vengeance on*the one who causes such awful harm.*And they went with*Ijnimínayé*to where he left the jaguar. And the ones from the village took vengeance upon him and killed him.’ (That is the news that will be broadcast in Filadelfia.)

ayoreo: “*Cucha Urõsódatéi*á, bamataté yájnatigo, yatígo ore ome cucha dacadode yúode uje yirãrare ome*Dosadídacóde*iji yejode. Ga yojningame*Dosadídacóde*iji yejode: ‘Jé cachi que ducunire maringa, jé ca yo yinamáji jnacarode ore iji yejode, yujnacarode, yatígo ore iji yirosadode udore.’
english: *Oh, you who caused such awful harm in the past*, you who tried to belittle my grandsons and my sons by threatening their futures. I spoke to*Dosadi’s*grandmother by my side. I said to*Dosadi’s*grandmother by my side: ‘Even though we aren’t*cojñone (*white people), I consider the young men about me as strong as ‘brush fortresses,’ my young sons and older sons for they are all around me, protecting me.’

ayoreo: “*Cucha Urõsódatéi á*, a angureta jóroque ua ome yajnatígo, yatígo ore to durasedie. ¿Que ajna gójni a? babárique iji ore. Urénga cuchiyíode jõrai tupúsu dajeque toguéodedatéi, dajeque tuguréodedatéi, yajnamenie ore dai tuqué,*Dejabi*uqué gajneri. Yacãra agoyé uneréjnaja tudaque ome*Cucha Urõsódatéi*uqué.
english: *Oh, you who caused such awful harm*, in vain was your confidence in being able to kill my grandsons and my sons. How is it that you haven’t, as you planned, been able to take one of them as your victim?

ayoreo: “*Cucha Urõsódatéi*á, ararãre iji jóroque que ome yirabai*Yacamai*tuqué que: ‘Ga ore ojningai*Yacamai*gotique casirase yu iji yurugúringui tudé.’
english: *You who caused such awful harm*, when you roared, you talked in vain about my grandson. You said: ‘As if the one they call*Yacamai*, that insignificant one, has enough courage to fight against me when I am angry!’

ayoreo: “Urénga, bamatate yirabai*Yacamai*uqué durasede. Chise pujnomei taningai uqué que ga gojñáringo chojninga durasasede: ‘Chi*Yacamai*e jnome caratai.’
english: Truly, for so long you belittled my grandson*Yacamai*, the one you referred to. But when the news first came this is what was said: ‘They say*Yacamai*has gone after the jaguar!’ And I said: ‘My grandson*Yacamai*is very important.*Iporide*is important also. And they are the ones who will*kill you who has caused so much harm*.’

ayoreo: “Ga yojninga: ‘Cuchiyíode jõrai tu yirabai*Yacamai*uté. Cuchiyíode jõrai tupo*Iboride*uqué rĩ ome*Cucha Urõsódatéi*uqué.’
english: Now why haven’t you killed one of them as you bragged you would?*O, you who caused so very much harm in the past*, why haven’t you, as you customarily do, torn to pieces my grandson*Ijnimínayé*? If you had, I would now be lamenting the demise of one of my grandsons. O, how you tried to kill my younger brother*Ugaréjnaidái*long ago, or my brother*Maríadayé*. Surely you could have killed one of them! Why didn’t you put forth more effort to kill them? It’s obvious you didn’t try hard enough, or you would have.

ayoreo: “¿Que ajna gójni a? babáriguiji udore ore to.*Cucha Urõsodaté*á, ¿Jé badi tírique que, que asururase yirabai*Ijnimínayé*tuqué durasede? Ga qué yugúsi yebiédatérigui iji uñeque quigade. Cachi bajuyapise ua ome isa*Uragaréjnaidayé*uñeque durasede, aja*Maríadayé*uñeque. Que asa gójni bejóringome ore durasede.
english: Truly, I am very content with my grandsons and sons, because of what they now have done*to you, the one that causes so much harm*.*The one that causes so much harm*, that insignificant one, they tore to pieces and left scattered all over the ground.

ayoreo: “Urénga jé yirasiguipise yájnatígo, yamaníngo ore durasede ome*Cucha Urõsódatéi*uqué.*Cucha Urõsódatéi*tuqué gotique, uje ore chajnangarase*Cucha Urõsódatéi*tuqué aja darosadode durasede.
english: And they found you easy to kill and didn’t give you any importance. You belittled my children and my grandchildren.*O, you who caused so much harm in the past*, very few of my forefathers had courage to fight you. But, truly, my grandsons and my children found you very easy to conquer in these days. And that is how they tore you to pieces just now and threw your body parts all over the ground.

ayoreo: “Ore chopise cucha iyiéguiji ua durasede ga que ore chiraja tijóringome ua. Cachi bajuyapise ua ome ore. Bamatate yatígo, yajnatígo ore.*Cucha Urõsódatéi*á, chise yáqui arójnaringo nanique ga chi jé ca picasite ua nanique ga chi ca bacasingónique ujnájna nanique. Urénga yájnatígo, yatígo ore chopise cucha iyiéguiji ua durasede. Ga jé u uje ore chajnangarasipise ua aja dejode durasede.”
english: I’ll stop now.

ayoreo: *Oidábiadé – Campo Loro, Paraguay**– 1988*.
english: *Oidábiadé – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988**Transcribed and translated into English by: Maxine Morarie*

ayoreo: Yedobu*Carayoi*todótigade iji ité, yoquejode. Yiji jnacarode jnese iji data mu ore todo jnese. Yãrei*Carayoi*posode ica mu que pota posode. Chujérase yu. Jé chaquesu poría. Yujnusina yapade ga yipojna*Carayoi*.
english: I am going to sing about*Carayoi’s*fear of my mother when we were together. All of the young men I was ever with feared her. I cooked for*Carayoi*but he didn’t want to eat it. He was going to hit me. He was cutting a stick from a tree.  I remembered my father and I bawled*Carayoi*out.

ayoreo: Yojninga: “*Carayoi*, ca ajé yapade ejode. Jé ca ajepise yu uje batodo yapade.*Carayoi,*yiya ua jne. Que yujnúsi ua go. Yujnusiétigade u*Iagábide*. A yajire guedé aja jnumone uje yujnusipísi uje que di. Nóña iji erámi. Chácare dacoté uña gari. Gusupúsu ore.”
english: I said: “*Carayoi*, who hits me, hits my father. Don’t hit me at all for you do fear my father.*Carayoi*, I’m going to leave you. I won’t miss you at all. Who I miss is*Iagábidé.*I watch for him all day long but he doesn’t arrive. He’s lost in the jungle. He’s with another wife. They’re out there alone.”

ayoreo: Yiji ga chaguhei chejna yu iji dajéode. Yáyipie déji ité ore ga yagu ore posode. Yicagoi idai jne, Bella Vista. Yácariji ité edopasai, cute ajidi piminingane ga jnacarode chisa yu. (Uje jnacarode ore timíninga aja quedéjnane.)
english: I left, and I was hungry on the road.  I kept thinking about my mother and that I would be eating their food. So I went to the town of Bella Vista. I sat down by my mother’s clan possession, the honey called*cute ajidi piminingane,*and young men wanted me. (Young men were always looking at her whereever she went.)

ayoreo: *Ajoté – Tobité, Bolivia – 1960.* *Joyce Davis Buchegger churúsãre unojo.*
english: *Ajoté – Tobité, Bolivia – 1965**Transcribed by: Joyce Davis Buchegger**Translated to English by: Maxine Morarie*

ayoreo: Yijñecárosibíji de. Yedobu. Yosõripise ayoré yocurẽja,*Áquidé*. Chaguhei chejna. “*Dejabi*, aguinéru gadoi ga yibágui jnacarode.”
english: I will sing a bit. I will sing about others. I am sorry for Áquidé, our older sister. She is hungry.

ayoreo: Yijñecárosibíji de. Yedobu. Yosõripise ayoré yocurẽja,*Áquidé*. Chaguhei chejna. “*Dejabi*, aguinéru gadoi ga yibágui jnacarode.”
english: “*Dejabi*, hurry and come to me and I will go to the young men.”

ayoreo: Yimóji*Chícode*gari mu yajéngopis ome*Chícode*uje que catecã́ri yu. A yiyápo*Chícode*rĩ uje que catecã́ri yu ga yibágui*Ecarai*. Jé yimesẽre*Ecarai*uje jĩrique catecã́ri yu, mu chi*Chícode*chijnóra yu diríca. Que catecã́ri yu diríca mu chisapo yu rĩ ga chojninga: “Qué yajéngome ua diríca gu.”
english: I slept with*Chícode,*but I was bashful around him because he didn’t talk to me. So I broke up with him, because he didn’t talk to me, and I went to*Ecarai.*I liked*Ecarai*because he quickly started talking to me, but he told me that*Chícode*loved me.

ayoreo: *Bariguéjnai*chojninga: “Yijínarása ua,*Dapuré*á, jeta ese yu.”
english: *Bariguéjnai*hadn’t talked to me, but he came to me again, and said: “I was shy when I was with you.”

ayoreo: Mu*Bariguéjnai*chãra yu. Yiji mu que nóna yu go uje jnusi*Uré*.
english: *Bariguéjnai*said: “I would like to be with you,*Dapuré,*if you’d have me.”

ayoreo: Yáyipie déji*Ecarai*uje que chãra yu.*Bariguéjnai*u uje chãra yu. Yáyipie déji*Ecarai*ome ayoré docosõra ome ayoré yurẽja uje que tagu dabotic iji deréjnane. Yijosa yiya*Ecarai*. Yiya gu uje ore chojninga: “Que trabajándo*Ecarai*gu. Jé trabajándo uyu. Ajína yu.” Mu ore chãra yu. Que ore nóna yu.
english: But*Bariguéjnai*deceived me. I went to him, but he didn’t want anything to do with me, because he was missing*Uré*.

ayoreo: *Dapuré – Tobité, Bolivia – 1960.*
english: So I started thinking about*Ecarai*again, because he didn’t deceive me, as Bariguéjnai had. I thought about*Ecarai*and how he was kind to people, to my older sister that had nothing to eat. I thought about leaving*Ecarai*. And other men told me: “*Ecarai*doesn’t work like I do. Stick with me.” But the men that said these things were just fooling around, and didn’t stay with me.

ayoreo: *Joyce Davis Buchegger churúsãre unojo.*
english: *Dapuré – Tobité, Bolivia – 1965**Transcribed by: Joyce Davis Buchegger**Translated to English by: Maxine Morarie*

ayoreo: Yijñecarósi iji de. Yedo*Pijnagámia, Áquidaté*. Yiji ome trabajando diríca mu e déji dasijnaringuei enga chojne daraqueguei café taja yibai. Jnacare urungap uyu. (*Lloré)*Yiya gapu pijoranie. Ca yijínapo ore jne, gapudíe to jne.
english: *Ecábi chedo Pijnigámia*

ayoreo: *Yocaoi,*yicagoi Rincón ica, mu*Yocaoi*jnusina Tobité ga chojninga: “*Ecábi*, yajníngo to. Yujnusipísi Tobité. Yajire cucaranone ga yujnusipísi Tobité.”
english: I will sing a bit. I will sing about*Pijnagámia (Áquidaté)*. I was about to leave for work but she was up to no good and spilled the coffee she was boiling on me. I was a very disgruntled young man!  I was through with girl friends! I decided to stop going with girls.

ayoreo: “Jé cho yu. Yajire cucaranone ga yujnusiétigade ome Tobité to, uje yoc-ucucaranone cachi u udore. Yise Tobité ga ca yicapo siñeque rĩ. Yujnusipísi gu.”
english: *Yocaoi*and I went to Rincón, but*Yocaoi*missed Tobité and he said: “*Ecábi,*let’s go back to Tobité.  I really miss Tobité. When I look at the mountains it makes me miss Tobité.’

ayoreo: *Yocaoi*chojninga: “Jé cho yu,*Ecábi*á, ga ca yicapóji ti to jne rĩ.”
english: “Me, too,” I said. I look at the hills and a longing for Tobité comes over me, and I think*those are our mountains, too.*When I get to Tobité, I’ll think, ‘I’ll never leave here again.’ I’m so homesick for Tobité.”

ayoreo: Yojninga: “Yujnusipísi Tobité. Cachi ca yimo pachamite quedéjnane. Deroco erámi iji pachamite quedéjnane. Yise Tobité ga unéri erámi jne jeta yajire cucarani. Yimate yu aja Tobité ga que yicapóji ti rĩ.”
english: *Yocaoi*said: “Me, too,*Ecábi*. Why, I don’t ever want to go anywhere else again.”

ayoreo: *Yocaoi*chojninga: “*Ecábi*á, jé cho yu. A ca yicapóji ti to. Yejna sequeredie jiei iji Tobité.”
english: I said: “I really miss Tobité. I don’t want to see what it’s like in other places.  Every other place is ‘blah.’ When I reach Tobité the whole land will seem good as I see the mountain. When I get myself to Tobité, I’m not going to leave again.”

ayoreo: *Ecábi – Tobité, Bolivia – 1960.*
english: *Yocaoi*said: “*Ecábi,*I feel the same way. I won’t ever go anywhere else. I’m going to stay in Tobité for many years.”

ayoreo: *Joyce Davis Buchegger churúsãre udaji nanique.*
english: *Ecábi – Tobité, Bolivia – 1965**Transcribed by: Joyce Davis Buchegger**Translated to English by: Maxine Morarie*

ayoreo: *Gajnémiadái*i u*Garajade*nanique uje jnacare, enga*Yocaoi*dai uñai u uje dacasute nanique. Chijñecare ga chósi te:
english: *Gajnémiadái Chijñecare*

ayoreo: Yijiabi enuei iji de jeta jec ũré yu diríca ga yujé uñeque gajnerĩ, cojñone. Quigade jeta cojñone uñane chujé yu to. A yujé pujñeque bajaque gajnerĩ. Quigade ga a pujñeque chujé yu to.
english: I’ll get a rope and be like I used to be, and go out and kill someone, a non-Ayoré. And then the people will kill me too. But I’ll kill someone first. And then someone will kill me.

ayoreo: Yedobu*Gajnémiadaté*. Dojnóngaja yu diríca mu cáma yise cojñone. Cáma uñéngo chujé yu to. Yise ore iji putugútoi yungóri casé mu ore chisa daboca taja yírique ga ore chinguíraja yu. Que yatígo pijoc ome ga que yujé uñeque.
english: I will sing about*Gajnémiadaté*. Before we were with the non-Ayoreos she made fun of me. That was before anyone fought with me.

ayoreo: Ayoréode ore chaquĩ́ra yu iji dejode ga que yatígo pijoc ome ore. Yujé uñeque gajnerĩ ga yajneque yujóraque*Chiquéjnoro*piya.  A yucué guesode gajnerĩ. Yijnapo aja yu gajnerĩ. Cachi cucha pimosoringuei ore ojningámia*Erábia*.
english: I came upon some people killing a tiger, but they pointed their gun at me and shot at me instead. I didn’t know what to do and I didn’t retaliate or try to kill anyone.

ayoreo: Yãraque yejode ome jeta moji yari. Que déji yajénic. Que yipotasía. “Besi siñec ga amóji. Que yipota ua.” Mu angoneparé ga moji yari. Yijosa yujórac*Chiquejnorodaté.*Yoquenocháric bijna ome ñane.
english: The Ayoréos tried to stop me from doing something, because they didn’t know what I would do. If I killed someone, then I could get a*Chiquejnoro*woman of my same clan. But normally I should get someone from an outside clan.

ayoreo: *Gajnémiadái – Tobité, Bolivia – 1960.*
english: Dear*Erábia*was someone I had known about. But I wouldn’t let her sleep with me. I didn’t desire her. I didn’t want the little one. “Go somewhere else to sleep. I don’t want you,” I told her. But she was stubborn and slept with me. I mistakenly thought she was a*Chiquejnorodaté*, from my same clan, and that’s why I refused her. But once I slept with her, being different didn’t matter (our color was lost on each other).

ayoreo: *Joyce Davis Buchegger churúsãre Gajnémiadái iradedie udaji nanique.*
english: *Gajnémiadái – Tobité, Bolivia – 1965*

ayoreo: Posorajã*Jonoine*.Yo yáyugu yu uje Posorajã́daté,*Jonoiné,*cha yu. Yeteque jnacare gatode uje que chimo yucanongonie urupi bisidétique. Uje ore tujúna gueguerade bisidec uje que ore chujé ayoréric, cojñotic ore. Jnusina*Jonoiné*uje*Jonoiné*chujépise caratade, cojñone, ayoréode ore, ga jé ũrepise uruode. Jé chopise uje cucha umusode. Jiei u jnese yugode.
english: *Jonoiné*was of the Posorajãi Clan. I feel sorry for myself that*Jonoiné*left me.  I don’t like younger men for they have no experience. They paint themselves up with carbon from the fire but for nothing, for they don’t ever kill non-Ayoréos nor their fellow Ayoreos.

ayoreo: Jnusina dajai uje Ayoré. Uásejna ore tagu yacorenie ga chosõripise dajai mu que iguiotique cuse ga ore gajnepíode garósi iji yacore. Chajire dajai. Chojninga: “Yajire bora póroro mu que gajnepítique cuse ga yosõripis.”
english: She remembers*Jonoiné*for*Jonoiné*really killed jaguars, non-Ayoreos, and Ayoreos, and he told the truth. He was like the “south winds!” He had many, many victims.

ayoreo: Áyipie déji*Jonoiné*uje abai ica mu e cha.
english: She remembered his brother who was an Ayoré. They ate a lot of wild pig meat and he was generous in giving to his brother because they didn’t have many relatives and were given only a little meat from the wild pigs. He always took care of his brother. He used to say: “I looked at some white cloth, but when my brother didn’t get any of it, I felt sorry for him.”

ayoreo: *Guié – Tobité, Bolivia – 1960.*
english: Guiei thought about*Jonoiné*her former husband who had broken up with her.

ayoreo: *Joyce Davis Buchegger churúsãre Guié iradedie udaji nanique.*
english: *Guié – Tobité, Bolivia – 1965.*

ayoreo: Yedobu yujode Étacõre,*Jupi*. Yachopise yujode Étacõre*Jupi*ujosipiedie. Yojninga: “Que*Jupi*chuse guio dajéningo ome Chiquejnone urodie ga chicha yibijnatígoningai ome date ejode. (Canirépise daté mu.)
english: *Ijnamene Chedo Jupi*

ayoreo: Disidie ore chachai yu ga ore cho dojña ome yu. Ore chojninga: “*Ijnamene*, bãri  mu*Jupi*chajnarase ua, ga chijinarase ua aja degúi.”
english: I’m going to sing about*Jupi*who is also from the Étacõre Clan. I’m very tired of my fellow Étacõre Clan member*Jupi*and his abuses.  I said: “*Jupi*isn’t careful with what he thinks about the daughters of the Chiquejnoi Clan; and he claims I say good things about his mother. (His mother is very nice, but I didn’t say good things about her.)”

ayoreo: Mu*Ijnamene*chojninga: “Cachi ayipieraque uje yujo gapua déji ejoi mu jé chujñieta yu gu. Yiya. Yiabi yujo Dosapeque ome yabai,*Oregajnúi*.”
english: Some girls came out to meet me and told me things. They said: “*Ijnamene*, you’ve arrived, but*Jup*i is following you, and wants you to go with him into the camp.”

ayoreo: Mu yiyápo*Oregajnui*rĩ uje*Oregajnui*chajire ome*Jupi*, abai bajai.
english: But*Ijnamene*said: “That’s just what you think, he’s already got another girl and is just making fun of me. I’m through with him. I’m going to find someone from the Dosapeque clan, like myself, to be my husband, I’m referring to*Orégajnúi*.”

ayoreo: Mu*Jupi*chojninga: “Be*Iyai*gari ga amóji.”
english: But I left*Orégajnúi*because he was jealous of*Jupi*, my former husband.

ayoreo: Jĩrique yiyapo*Iyai*rĩ. Jĩrique yisapo yujode Etacõre*Jupi*rĩ. *Ijnamene – Tobité, Bolivia – 1960.*
english: But*Jupi*said: “Go to*Iyai*and sleep with him.” I did, but quickly left*Iyai*also. And I went back to my fellow Étacõre clan member*Jupi*again.

ayoreo: *Joyce Davis Buchegger churúsãre Ijnamene iradedie udaji nanique.*
english: *Ijnamene – Tobité, Bolivia – 1965*

ayoreo: Cho cuyuque dejai gatócoro mu ayorei chisa yu. Chojninga: “Yimóji bari jne.” Yo cuyuc mu ayorei chisa yu. Yajire mu yajei u ayoré quedéjna mu yiguios. Mu yojninga: “Bo, ajni to. Que yipota ua.”
english: It was the middle of the night when someone grabbed me. He said: “I’m going to sleep with you.” I awoke and someone grabbed me. I looked and thought it was a stranger but it was a relative. But I said: “Go away. I don’t want you.”

ayoreo: “Yiraja ga yiguioto ua mu yipotapísa jeta yacoté ua.”
english: He said: “I know you are my relative but I really want you for a wife.”

ayoreo: Mu que yipota jeta yabai iguiotique. (Etacõre*Jadia,*Etacõre jnacari*Ejémi*.) “Bo, ajni to. Bacoté déji guidai.”
english: But I didn’t want a relative for a husband. (Both Jadia and Ejémi were of the Étacõre Clan.) “Go, get away from me. Your wife is in the village.”

ayoreo: “Mu yachopise*Ajotá*josipiedie ga yibágui ua jeta yacoté ua.”
english: He said: “I’m tired of how bossy*Ajota*is and so I’ve come to have you as a wife.”

ayoreo: Jeti ũré yu nanique, ca yiji yiguiotique nanique. “A ca yiguiotique,” yojninga, mu tágape yu. Yiji*Ejémi*ga chisa yu uje potapísa yu. Yetaque mu chijína yu.
english: I had said long ago that I wouldn’t be with a relative and I meant it at the time. “He’s probably not your relative,” I said to myself, lying to myself. So I went to*Ejémi*and he took, me for he really wanted me. I didn’t like it but he went with me.

ayoreo: *Jadia – Tobité, Bolivia – 1960.*
english: *Jadia – Tobité, Bolivia – 1965.*

ayoreo: *Joyce Davis Buchegger churúsãre Jadia iradedie udaji nanique.*
english: *Transcribed by: Joyce Davis Buchegger.*

ayoreo: Qué yedobu jnacarode acoté*Chequé Dago*. Chojninga: Ẽ́ yisapié yacoté gapu.*Pujnucode*déji sañeque ga tangári yu ga chirate yu. Ca chajéngome yu jne. Chiradirase yu iji dejode gajnerĩ, yacoté casé,*Átapide*.
english: I’m going to sing about*Chequé Dago*who was the wife of young men. Supposedly, I had relations with a young girl. When*Pujnúcode*was away he heard me sing and imitated me. He didn’t respect me. He tried to imitate what I sang about a former wife called Átapide.

ayoreo: Jé yijñecare iji de. Yedóbu jnacarode acoté,*Chequé Dago*.*Chequé Dago*chise yoquirengúi, ga*Chequé Dago*chijna yajnenie purúcho. Chãrágome.
english: I’ll sing now. I’m going to sing about*Cheque Dago*who was the wife of several young men.

ayoreo: *Jnaniné*uyu yojninga*:*“Ẽ́*Chequé Dago*, ãrame yu. Yijina yurasade ore to. Deroco yui jeta tṍra yugúchade, yacoté. Babe yu gu,” yojningame*Chequé Dago*. “Be bajogadi gotique ga yimóji yijogadi. Jnani yu. Yabode déji degúi.”
english: *Cheque Dago*came to our village, and*Cheque Dago*took shelter where I was in the shade. (But*Jnaniné*wouldn’t let her in.)

ayoreo: Mu angoneparé*Cheque Dago*ga jé móji yari.*Cheque Dago*chojninga: “*Jnaníné,*ayague ga yico yijogadi ga yimóngo iji.”
english: He said to her: ‘*Cheque Dago,*let me go, I’m going with my relatives. I don’t look forward to my wife throwing my things out. She would too, because you’ve come to me,’ I said to*Cheque Dago. ‘S*o, go wherever it is you live and I’ll sleep at my place. I’m a family man. I have children here in the village.”

ayoreo: *Chequé Dago*ajéode jno yúdi aja darásade ga ore urejanie ore chicha yu aja darásade uje jnacare pariguéjna yu. Que yijína aja urásade. Deroco yugúchade dacaranone yui jeta yijína*Chequé Dago*.
english: But*Cheque Dago*ignored me and she slept with me.

ayoreo: *Jnaniné – Tobité, Bolivia – 1960.*
english: *Cheque Dago*said, “Jnaníné, get up, let’s go to my place and we’ll sleep there.”

ayoreo: *Joyce Davis Buchegger churúsãre piradedie udaji nanique.*
english: *Cheque Dago*wanted to take me to her relatives where their older sisters were. They would have put me with the male relatives because I was a young male.

ayoreo: Yijñecari iji de. Yedobu*Guidabía (Osõrádaté).*Yajnemia u disia iji de. Yijõra, angári yu ga arate yu gajnerĩ ome yoquijõrai ore ga ore chirate yu  gajnerĩ. Ca ajéchome yu gajnerĩ. Yuságaja uñeque gajnerĩ uje yijñecaco, yedobu*Tajerogue*iji de gajnerĩ. Yisapie iji siñeque ga tangári yu ome dajnéone, dachachagúcho ga cho dáyugu gajnerĩ  jõ iji siñec. Yacoté chojninga: “Uacoyo dicasei iji uaque uje uacátayo uagaja ore iji degu quedéjnane.  Jĩrique uaté tṍraja dabáyode iji degu quedéjnane.”
english: I’ll sing. I’ll sing about*Guidabía (Osõrádaté)*. The one I wanted was a young girl. My friend, listen to me and imitate me along with our friend and them who imitate me.  Don’t be ashamed of me.  I will include another as we sing, I will sing about*Tajérogue*at this time. Supposedly, I had relations with her  when I was away, and she listened to me about her things, and her relatives, but then she probably regretted it later when we were far away.

ayoreo: Yajni yeronie ore gari. Yo yáyugu uje chicha urõsode. Cojñone chujé ore ga jĩrique yajni yeronie ore gari. Yo yibaya ore ire garasórone cucha.
english: My wife said: “You consider yourselves so brave when you’re in other camps. And she has always thrown away her husbands in other camps.

ayoreo: *Joyce Davis Buchegger churúsãre Josedé iradedie udaji nanique iji Tobité, Bolivia iji 1960.*
english: So, I went back to my sisters. I felt bad about how she made them suffer. The white people fought with them and killed them, so I quickly went back to my sisters. And I will suffer with them in the remaining things they will have to suffer.”

ayoreo: Yijñecarósi ji de.
english: *Pejnócode chedo dajnusiétigade ome dacotedie*

ayoreo: Yedobu yujnusiétigade. Yiji cucarani iji Tobité garané ome yujnusiétigade.
english: I will sing a bit. I will sing about my sorrow.

ayoreo: Dire gajnerĩ ga yajni. Yiji yesade. Yujnusipísi ore gu, yesade. Yugúsi Santa Cruz, mu yáyipie déji yesade. Dire gajnerĩ yaca*máquina*to gajnerĩ. Ugoi yu ome yujnusiétigade. Yiji*estación*jeta dire ga yirote*máquina.*Yijñecare iji de mu jé yacadique yu. Cho jeta ayoréric yacadique mu yacadique yu. Cho jeta ayoréric gajnecho yijñecade, mu jé yajnecho jeta jé yiradique yu. Yujnusi mu yiya yujnusiétigade uje jnacare boquere yu. Cachi jé yujnusi mu que yujnusi. Boquere ga yacho. Yijiape to. Yĩriapo jne rĩ jeta amanique cusapo rĩ,*Dapuré*.
english: I’m going to the mountain I left behind in Tobité because of my sorrow. Today I’m heading back. I’ll go back to my younger brothers. I really miss my younger brothers. While I was in Santa Cruz, all I could think about was my brothers. One day I got on the train. I was mourning, because of my sorrow. I got to the station as soon as it was light and waited for the train.

ayoreo: Yĩri diríca ga que yiguísãre. A jose ca yujnusíric. Yujnusi mu ayoré boquere yu ga yijñecáraja yajesúode ga cachi que yujnusi. Gapudíe doi yu mu que yijína uñárac. Cachi a yujnusi uñárac, cojñone gachidé. Cachi ore pesapo yujnusiétigade rĩ. Yiya gapu pijõranie, mu yijína uñárac jeta iyajmaque. Yigana iji cojñone ujade udojo. Yáyipie déji jnupepisai mu yujnusina uje que yacotéraque cus ga cachi yáyipie. A yipesapo  yi*cháco*to jne jeta yijínapo uñáraque jne rĩ. Jeta yijínapo cojñone gachidé uñáraque to jne rĩ.
english: I’m singing, but I’m teaching myself as I sing. It’s as though I’m teaching someone else, but I’m really teaching myself. I was lonely, but I stopped being lonely when I started flirting. I was still lonely, but not as lonely. I was playing around, but soon got tired of it. So I’m leaving.

ayoreo: *Joyce Davis Buchegger churúsãre Pejnocode iradedie udaji nanique iji Tobité, Bolivia iji 1960.*
english: When I get there, back to Tobité,  will I find that*Dapuré*has another husband?

ayoreo: *(Chi ore changacára daguiosode uje ore chijñecare.)*
english: *Piajoi Acoté u Jutira* *(It’s said, when they sing about their relatives, it blesses them.)*

ayoreo: Yedobu ayorábi*Piajoi*uje gapua uña chisa. A*Paré u*. Chiese*Ichajúidé*iguioto yu. Yipógu yachachagútic ome*Piajoi*.
english: I’m going to sing about*Piajoi*who a young woman made love to. It was probably*Paré.*(They claim I am a relative of*Ichajúidé*. But I’m hiding the words of my song from*Piajoi.)*

ayoreo: Chequé uña chojninga: “Aya babai. Gajnárec uje acotedie gare.”
english: One of the women told her: “Abandon your husband. It’s wrong for a husband to have two wives.”

ayoreo: Chequé uña chojninga: “Jé ajína babai uje gajnareque uje gapudie abayode jiei.”
english: Another women told her: “Stay with your husband for it’s wrong for young girls to have many husbands.”

ayoreo: Jnacari chingome yu uje pota*Maria*. Chojninga: “*Manuelné*, ango*Maria*ga yacoté jne.”
english: There was a young man who wanted to make love to*Maria*. He said to*Manuelné*: “*Manuelné,*tell*Maria*to be my wife.”

ayoreo: Mu*Maria*chojninga*: “Uneredaté,*ca apojna yu. Que yipota babai.”
english: But*Maria*said to his wife: “*Unéredaté,*don’t scold me. I don’t want your husband.”

ayoreo: *Manuelné – Tobité, Bolivia -1960*
english: *Manuelné – Tobité, Bolivia – 1965*

ayoreo: *Joyce Davis Buchegger churúsãre piradedie udaji nanique.*
english: *Transcribed by: Joyce Davis Buchegger*

ayoreo: Yijñecárosi iji de. Yisapie ayoré yocuyábi*Pejnócode*iji siñeque. ¿Tangári yu jõ a? Yise Rincón ga yajire cucarani ga yujnusipise*Pejnócode, Pédrode*ore. Dire gajnerĩ ga yajni to gajnerĩ aja Tobité. Cho jeta urugu yu iji jnutígo jeta ca yĩri ga yejna guedosírigo aja Tobité yui. Uneripise guidai yui, Tobité. Chaguhei chejna yu mu yagu ore potígo gajnerĩ. Chi íjnoque jnusiétigade ome. Chise Portón ga unere guidai yui. Jé uneripise guidai yui jeta yagu ayoréode yoquesai*Cucójnai*, ayoréode dacuyábi*Pejnócode*ore posode.
english: I will sing a bit.

ayoreo: Yijñecárosi iji de. Yisapie ayoré yocuyábi*Pejnócode*iji siñeque. ¿Tangári yu jõ a? Yise Rincón ga yajire cucarani ga yujnusipise*Pejnócode, Pédrode*ore. Dire gajnerĩ ga yajni to gajnerĩ aja Tobité. Cho jeta urugu yu iji jnutígo jeta ca yĩri ga yejna guedosírigo aja Tobité yui. Uneripise guidai yui, Tobité. Chaguhei chejna yu mu yagu ore potígo gajnerĩ. Chi íjnoque jnusiétigade ome. Chise Portón ga unere guidai yui. Jé uneripise guidai yui jeta yagu ayoréode yoquesai*Cucójnai*, ayoréode dacuyábi*Pejnócode*ore posode.
english: I went to find our older brother*Pejnócode*. Maybe he would listen to me. I got to Rincón and whenever I’d look at the mountain it would make me lonesome for*Pejnócode*and*Pédrode*. The next day I took off for Tobité. It was as though, even if I had to go on foot and walk for a month, I would keep going till I got to Tobité. I could hardly wait to get to the village of Tobité. I was hungry along the way, and ate the food that others gave me. And that made me feel better, less lonesome. When the train reached Portón I was excited to reach our town. Oh, how I longed for our town, being there and eating the food that*Cucójnai cooked*and our dear older brother*Pejnócode*.

ayoreo: *Pujnúcabi – Tobité, Bolivia – 1960*
english: *Pujnúcabi – Tobité, Bolivia – 1965.*

ayoreo: *Joyce Davis Buchegger churúsãre Pujnúcabi iradedie udaji nanique.*
english: *Transcribed by: Joyce Davis Buchegger.*

ayoreo: Yijñecarósiabíji de. Chiese*Jnaníné*desaque yu. Uyu marínga yipógu yachachagúgode ome. Yujnusipísi*Ojo*mu que yajna. Jé yajnemirase diríca*Ojo*. Yijosa yujóric jnacare uje cha ucaningongoningajnai. Pijnate gonigai. Pijnáte quite uje que pojna. Yáyipie déji*Ojo*ome ore acoté*. Pujnuidaté*pojna yu. Jé yujnusiétigáde. Yibaque diríca. Yiji yocade. Yáyipie chachẽ́re*Ojo*. Cachi déji gari iji degúi. Teráji gu ga chisi ome dajoranie urejnanie ore uje caniré enga ore chirajúraja.
english: I’m going to sing a bit.

ayoreo: Yijñecarósiabíji de. Chiese*Jnaníné*desaque yu. Uyu marínga yipógu yachachagúgode ome. Yujnusipísi*Ojo*mu que yajna. Jé yajnemirase diríca*Ojo*. Yijosa yujóric jnacare uje cha ucaningongoningajnai. Pijnate gonigai. Pijnáte quite uje que pojna. Yáyipie déji*Ojo*ome ore acoté*. Pujnuidaté*pojna yu. Jé yujnusiétigáde. Yibaque diríca. Yiji yocade. Yáyipie chachẽ́re*Ojo*. Cachi déji gari iji degúi. Teráji gu ga chisi ome dajoranie urejnanie ore uje caniré enga ore chirajúraja.
english: They say I’m*Jnaníné’s*little brother. Even though I am, I’m going to hide the words of my song from him. I really miss*Ojo*but I’m not going to follow her.*Ojo*was almost my fiancee. I thought about my fellow young men and how she hadn’t stopped attracting them with the cute things she would say and her direct, but kind, talk. I  kept thinking they would want her for a wife. Then*Pujnuidaté*scolded me. And it made me very sad. So I went off to hunt honey and look for turtle. But I kept thinking about*Ojo.*As though I were already with her in her camp. She rewarded her older sisters and gave them gifts because she is so kind, and they would cook for her.

ayoreo: *Pujnuiné – Tobité,Bolivia – 1960*
english: *Pujnuiné– Tobité, Bolivia – 1965.*

ayoreo: *Joyce Davis Buchegger churúsãre Pujnuine iradedie udaji nanique.*
english: *Transcribed by: Joyce Davis Buchegger.*

ayoreo: *Chijñecare:***
english: *Suaréjnacaríde Chijñecare*

ayoreo: Yo yáyugu uje yijñecare to. Yijñecárosi iji yucuegode yoca. Yijíñome yúringai ayoré dacasuté mu yijñecare iji de. Íjnoque yúringai panguréjna. A jé yijñecare iji de jne.
english: I feel sad when I sing. I sing when I hunt turtles.  I’ll put to one side my being a warrior, and sing now. I have no confidence, but here goes, I’m going to sing.

ayoreo: Yijñecare iji yajesúode erámi. Yijñecare iji yibatigade tiji de. Jé panguréjna yu to mu*Cojñoi*chejna yúringai panguréjna uje yujnusi*Cojñoi*ore,*Ipesudidaté.*Yujnusipise ga yijñecare iji yibatigai erámi. Yijíñome yúringai pidosobade charisórone ome*Cojñoi*uaque.*Ijnamía*chejna yájningarã́i. Que yájningai cusapo rĩ. Jé yujnúsi. Qué yujnúsisi disidíe ore datábia. Yujnusi*Ijnamía*. Yijóji*Yijnamía*taja yejode ga que yujnusiapo disidíe ore datábia rĩ. Yurúo ica ga a yujé*Cojñoi*uñeque gajnerí ga yijna*Ijnamía*aja yu gajnerĩ. E yiji yuruode. Yujé uñai diríca cojñoque iji jnanione ga e yijna Inamía taja yu jne. Jé yiji yuru casodé. Yijna*Ijnamía*aja yu.
english: I sing wherever I go. And I sing here on my honey hunts. I was once confident but*Cojñoi*took away my confidence when I started missing*Cojñoi*and the others, and*Ipesudidaté.*I really miss her, so, I’m singing about her as I go about hunting for honey. I put aside my state of being one to fear because of*Cojñoi*and the rest of you.

ayoreo: Yijñecare iji yajesúode erámi. Yijñecare iji yibatigade tiji de. Jé panguréjna yu to mu*Cojñoi*chejna yúringai panguréjna uje yujnusi*Cojñoi*ore,*Ipesudidaté.*Yujnusipise ga yijñecare iji yibatigai erámi. Yijíñome yúringai pidosobade charisórone ome*Cojñoi*uaque.*Ijnamía*chejna yájningarã́i. Que yájningai cusapo rĩ. Jé yujnúsi. Qué yujnúsisi disidíe ore datábia. Yujnusi*Ijnamía*. Yijóji*Yijnamía*taja yejode ga que yujnusiapo disidíe ore datábia rĩ. Yurúo ica ga a yujé*Cojñoi*uñeque gajnerí ga yijna*Ijnamía*aja yu gajnerĩ. E yiji yuruode. Yujé uñai diríca cojñoque iji jnanione ga e yijna Inamía taja yu jne. Jé yiji yuru casodé. Yijna*Ijnamía*aja yu.
english: (*Inamía*means*little sister.*It is an endearing name that a man uses for the woman he loves.)*Inamia*has taken away all my anger. I have no more fierceness. I just miss her. I just miss the little girls’ mother. I miss*my little sister*. I keep thinking about*my little sister*and holding her close and then I don’t miss the mother of the little girls. I said long ago that I would kill a*white person*to have*Inamía*back. And I did what I said I would do. I killed a white man who was a full grown man and  I now I will take*Inamía*back. I kept my word. I took*Inamía*back.

ayoreo: *Suaréjnacaríde – Tobité, Bolivia – 1960.*
english: *Suaréjnacaríde – Tobité, Bolivia – 1965.*

ayoreo: *Joyce Davis Buchegger churúsãre udaji nanique.*
english: *Transcribed by: Joyce Davis Buchegger.*

ayoreo: Yijñecarósi iji de. Chiese*Ipeyai*nero yu nanic. Gajnei marínga yipógu yachachagúgode quitic ome*Uchiedie*ejoi.
english: *Tajerogue Chijñecare*

ayoreo: Yiyárási ua diríca mu yujnusina beronie uje ate goingane ome yu ga yijinapo ua rĩ. Yosirási yijíñome ua mu babepo yu iji gari rĩ. Que pujnusiétigarási ome yu. Yajire ua ga yosõre ua uje educadode tamuta jnumone ga yajniapója ua rĩ. Que pujnusiétigade déji yu. Jeta gusu jnacare ua ga yujnusirási ua. Jnacarode cachi uacaruábio ome gapúraque uyoque. Chiese ca bajnusi yu diríca mu bagarupo yu rĩ. Ũré uyu uje que yujnusirási ua. Bagota jõroque bãringai cojñoque dequejnangore pujnusiete ome yu. Ca yugúsi penochai nanique uje uacajuso yu. Que yujnusi uraque to.
english: I will sing a bit. They say I was*Ipeyai’s*sister. Even though it’s true I will hide the words of my song from*Uchiedie*.

ayoreo: *Tajérogué – Tobité, Bolivia – 1960.*
english: I almost left you, but then I remembered your sisters and how kind they were to me, so I went back to you. I was almost with another man, but you came and took me away from him. I wasn’t sad at all. I looked at you and when I saw tears falling from your eyes I felt sorry for you, and I went back to you. But I wasn’t sad. Even if you were the only young man there was – as if I would miss you! The things you young men tell us girls are just your little words. You claim you didn’t miss me, but here you are hugging me again. Truthfully, though, I wouldn’t miss you at all. You keep repeating how much you suffered and sorrowed for me.  I wasn’t thinking of you back thenlike you guys thought I was. And I don’t miss you guys at all.

ayoreo: *Joyce Davis Buchegger churúsãre Tajerogué iradedie udaji nanique.*
english: *Tajerogue – Tobité, Bolivia – 1965.*

ayoreo: ***chedo Cojñoi:***
english: *Ubuchúa Chedo Cojñoi*

ayoreo: *Cojñoi*tibágui yu iji dejai. Chojninga: “*Ubuchua,*amóji yari.”
english: *Cojñoi*came to me in the night. He said: “*Ubuchúa,*sleep with me.”

ayoreo: Mu yetac. Yojninga: “Ajni to. Que yimo ji bari.”
english: But I didn’t want to. I said: “Go away. I won’t sleep with you.”

ayoreo: *Cojñoi*chojninga: “Jé amóji yari. A jé be yujórique jnacanic, ga ca ajína yu.”
english: *Cojñoi*said: “Come on, sleep with me. You’ll go to another young men like myself, but you won’t come with me.”

ayoreo: “Que yijína ua. Cojñoc ua gu. A ore chojninga, ‘Ẽ́, cojñocho quedejnane uje chigaru yu.’”
english: “I won’t sleep with you because you are a white person. People will say that white people embrace me.”

ayoreo: Chigaru yu mu yetac. Yujnusipísi ayoré piguiosode. Yojninga: “Yiji ore iji Cochocoigosode irenguóne.” *Cajérode*chise yu ga chojninga: “*Ubuchua*, ajína yu aja degúi ga yacoté ua jne.”
english: But he went ahead and embraced me, but I refused him. I really missed my Ayoré relatives. I said: “I’m going to go to my friends in the Cochocoigosode camps.”

ayoreo: Mu yetaque ga yibo. Chijíñome yu. Yojninga: “Que yipota jnacare abechugode uje qué batique cus. A yijínarase ua jeta ca batique cus.”
english: *Cajérode*came to me and said: “*Ubuchúa*, come with me to the camp and be my wife.”

ayoreo: *Joyce Davis Buchegger churúsãre Ubuchúa iradedie udaji nanique iji Tobité, Bolivia iji 1960.*
english: But I didn’t want to be with him and I cried. So, he left me. I said: “You have children, and I don’t want someone that has children. I would have gone with you if you didn’t have children.”

ayoreo: Yijñecare iji dejai uné.
english: I am going to sing:

ayoreo: Yequésãre. Yipesapo ore dai ejo rĩ, ore dai uñeque, mu tágape yuruode.
english: I’m going to curse someone and make another father for them. But I’m not telling the truth.

ayoreo: Ipesupo uñai rĩ. Jnanione uje ore baque que ore jno uje ore jno sona. Ore di dire uñane uje chajire ore, ga chaca dabai uje toi  Ayoré uñai u; ueca uñane. Ga jnusipísi dabai uje toi ga chose dedodie iji jnumi. Dejaque ga tibidi*Guidábi.*Chojningame dabode: “Ayáyo iji ne. Yibágui*Guidábi*. Itiróguiji yajesui. A jnacanique chetaque yu.
english: I’ll make another one. When the men go to  hunt honey, when they are way over there.  They will return on another day and will see them. One of them will have intestinal gas and will die. The others will live.

ayoreo: *Guidábi*chojningame: “*Uneredaté*, bamo uje yacoté déji idai uñai, mu amóji yari iji dejai udé.”
english: And she is very sad because her husband has died and she looks down (in her sorrow).

ayoreo: “Yiya ome*Uneredaté*uje que yipota yacotérac.”*Edoguesaide*chacho ome*Uneredaté*.
english: When it was night she called to the young man named*Guidábi*.

ayoreo: *Uneredaté*chojninga: “*Edoguesaide*, tágape ua. Que jnacanique etácho yu gotique.”
english: She said to her children: “Stay here. I’m going to*Guidábi*. I’m going directly there. ¿Could a young man refuse me?”

ayoreo: *Uneredaté*chajire dabode uje chijñecare. Chaguhei chejna ore ga*Uneredaté*baque. Abode chácare degúi. Baque ga chujé yocade. Chijnoque cutérone. Jnusi dabai ga poji dabode yui, uje que chequedie baque to. Que ore chicáji erámi. Jé po.
english: *Guidábi*said to her: “*Uneredaté*, you know that I have a wife in the village, but you can sleep with me tonight.”

ayoreo: *Uneredaté – Tobité, Bolivia – 1960.*
english: Another says: “I don’t want anything to do with*Uneredaté,*because I don’t want a wife.”*Edoguesaide*was tired of*Uneredaté*.

ayoreo: *Joyce Davis Buchegger churúsãre Uneredaté iradedie udaji nanique.*
english: *Uneredaté*said: “*Edoguesaide*, you’re lying. There are none of you young men who can refuse me.”

ayoreo: *Borade – Asõrenie Ore*
english: *Borade – Asõrenie Ore*

ayoreo: Ga chi ore jnome dajocaningai, jnani bajátigo, ga ore chigaru arurã́tadie Picanerai gai. Ga ore chimánaja, ga ore chigaru aja Chiquenoi gai, ga arurã́tadie déji Chiquenoi gai. Ga ore doi Dosapé ga ore chigaru arurã́tadie Dosapé gai. Ga ore doi Étacõri gai ga gusu ore – gagájni asutedie ome dajocaningai gai uje ore chimánaja arurã́tadie ore gai.
english: And they say the men who lived long ago left for war, and they tied loincloth string on a*Picanerai*clansman. And when they finished tying string on him, they tied a loincloth around a*Chiquenoi*clansman, and the*Chiquenoi*then had a loin cloth. And they brought the*Dosapé*clansman and tied a loincloth around him. And they brought the*Étacõri*clansman and tied string on him, but just those four brave leaders were ready for war because they had finished putting loincloths on them.

ayoreo: Ore chimate ore ga chetingãre asõrenie uje bajai u Picanerai, ga ore chisi ome Chiquenoi ga chetingãre asõrenie Chiquenoi gai. Dosapei u uje chetingare dasõrenie, ga chetingare Étacõri gai.
english: They finished them and now they gave out lances; first to the*Picanerai*, and then to the*Chiquenoi*. Then they had them try out the lances together. The*Dosapei*tried out the lances, and he had the*Étacori*try them out.

ayoreo: Que picasite Picanerane, jé aja Chiquenone, Dosapéode, jé aja Étacorone. Cojñone, ore chejna. Ore sué diri gai, que ayoréode chujñieta ore uje dequejnangore ore gu.
english: The*Picanerane*could not be conquered, neither could the*Chiquenone*, the*Dosapéode*, or the*Étacorone*. They finished off the*cojñone (*white men).

ayoreo: Darujna – ga ore churúsãre gai; ore chicha cucha pibosode iji údi.
english: For the Totem Pole they used the tall cactus called*Darujna*– they wrote on it; they put food before it.

ayoreo: Cutamuraja edópasa namacade.
english: The*Cutamuraja Clan*claims the*namacade*ceremony as their ‘clan possession.’

ayoreo: Ore chutai ore ome cojñone jeta ore chujé.
english: They would ask the Totem Pole to give them*cojñone (white people)*to kill.

ayoreo: Ore chãraque ejode ome disiode. Gusu jnanione iji údi. (Umaripise namacádedie.)
english: They wouldn’t let little children get near the Totem Pole. Only grown men could look upon it from underneath. (The Totem Poles were very tall.)

ayoreo: *Oidábiadé – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988.*
english: *Oidábiadé – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988.*

ayoreo: *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Oidábiadé atatagode udojo nanique.*
english: *Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie.*

ayoreo: Ga uñane chojninga: “¡Acatecãchoi ore jne é!  ¡Acatecãchoi ore jne é!”
english: *Ore chujé Cornelio Isaac*

ayoreo: Ga jno boradie údi aja Tudujnaté. Chi Menonitane ore eduguéjnai tu nanique ga jno boradie údi aja Tudujnaté ga yicóji Madrejón iquei. Enga chi yicoi ga uñane chojninga: “Yicoi ga yicatecãcoi cojñone.” Uñane uje ajéqueséo, jé uñane ájningarac ore. Ga yutúãjóa gachidé tãi iji Madrejón iquei.
english: This Mennonite Missionary (*Cornelio Isaac*) was taking clothes to the Ayoreos who were near a small river called*Tudujnaté*. It’s said he was taking clothes to a place near the small river long ago, and we (Ayoreos) came upon him this side of*Madrejón*.

ayoreo: Mu uñane que chamurase doráchade to, ajningarane. Que uñane base yungórone gajiné.
english: When we reached him, some of our group said: “Let’s go talk to that person who is white.”

ayoreo: Ga eduguéjnai chisi boradie ome ore. Chijnime boradie ga chisi ome ore. Bajade u uje ore eduguéjnai chisi ore, jõrai dopoi guesi to iji tãia rĩ. Jõrai doi guesi ga*Echogai*chisa jmanegatadé.
english: Some in our group were peaceful, others were angry. We met his truck on the road this side of Madrejón.

ayoreo: Ore eduguéjnai qué chisi ore iji boradie. Chisi ore iji boradie, mu*Jonoine*chachẽre iji úringai jnéca gai uje qué tẽríji borade. (Uje jnani bajade chojninga nanique: jnéca.) Qué chisírase ore iji boradie mu*Jonoine*chichagu iji gai. Jonoiné tõria chichagu uje qué chisi ore iji boradie.
english: And some among us said: “Talk to them! Talk to them!” But others didn’t put down their weapons, those that were angry. They kept going with intent to kill him.

ayoreo: Cojñoi cho ¡*eee*! ome*Jonoiné*asõre. Enga cojñoi ejominóri chisárase poca. Jĩraque chãraque yóguiji darosadode. Ga yocurásade chojninga: “¡Boyo, boyo, boyo! ¡Ore chichagu ore! ¡Ore chichagu ore! ¡Ore chichagu ore!”
english: The missionary gave clothing to them. He’d pull out clothing and give it to them. First it was the chief who gave things to them, and then his companion got out of the truck also. His friend got out, and*Echogai*grabbed his friend’s wrist. The missionary just kept giving clothes to them.

ayoreo: Yayógome ore ga yibaco iji ore ejode iji datí. Mu que yirajágo urúrique – cho ¡*ié, ié, ié*¡
english: He was giving out clothing, but*Jonoiné*grabbed him as he was giving clothing to them. He was about to give them some more clothing when*Jonoiné*pierced him with a lance.*Jonoiné*hid his intentions to pierce him and did it suddenly while he was giving out the clothing.

ayoreo: Ga jõrai chichorase yoque ome poca, mu jõrai chãraque yoque, mu chãraque yoque. Uje chi chojninga nanique: “Ca acho ore jne, ga ore chujé yu, ga jé ore chujé yu jne.” Chãraque yoque; chojninga, ore yuque uté eduguéjna. Chãraque yoque enga chijna poca iji dajõrai gari, ga que ore chinguíra aja yoque.
english: The man went like this:*“Eee”*as*Jonoiné’s*lance pierced him. And the man’s companion went for his gun. He wouldn’t let us get any closer – he held the gun on us.

ayoreo: *Carodidái*casicaite que ore jno to, enga jé ore dépoji ejoi te rĩ, enga ore chãrapoi potayedie iji to rĩ.
english: And our people said: “Let’s go! Let’s go! Let’s go! They’ve pierced them! They’ve pierced them! They’ve pierced them!”

ayoreo: Ga e ore chacája tãia ga e ore jno. Ore jno ga ore chayója idai iji Madrejón. Jno ga ayoréode jé ore chayo aja ñane to ga ore chojninga: “Boyo ga yajnángo ore ga yachẽco ore jne.  A ore déji ore idai ga yico ga yucajningacome ore.”
english: We ran from them and then we stopped while  still within sight of them. But we  couldn’t understand his words – he said, “*ee-aye, ee-aye, ee-aye*!”

ayoreo: “Yucajningacome ore mu ore idai cho te.” Enga ore uñane, chaquĩ́ra ñane ga a ore chojninga: “Abaso ga yicatecãcoi ore jne.  Jachi ajéquesé cojñone que, ga yicoi ore iji ore idai.” “Ore ichajui uqué a déji ore idai.”
english: His  friend was going to shoot us with his gun, but his companion, the one that was pierced, kept him from it; he protected us. They say he said: “Don’t shoot them; if they kill me, let them kill me.” He protected us; the missionary who was their victim said this.  He protected us and took the gun away from his friend, and they didn’t shoot at us.

ayoreo: “Yajico ga chécãre asõrepie enga chicha dajmanecai.”
english: *Carodidái*, who is now deceased, and those with him stayed and didn’t run, and they were by them, and they offered them some long, braided neck feathers to show they were still friendly.

ayoreo: Yicatecãcoi ore mu chi chojninga: “Ca acho ayoréode jne.”
english: But then the men got into their truck and drove away. They left and went as fast as they could to Madrejón.

ayoreo: Chojninga: “Asi ore iji boradie jne,” chi chojninga. Chi chojninga: “Ore chichagu yu que, mu a gusu ore yuque yu jne.” Chãraque yoque, jecute ore doi deduguéjnai uruode ga ore chisi ome boradie ome yoque. Ore tẽrája yoque ga yocujuya ore ugúchade, boradie. Yocujuya camísadie, pónchodie ore. Sábanane ore.
english: They left, and then the Ayoreos got together and they said: “Let’s go, let’s follow them and attack them. When they get to their town we’ll overtake them there.” We could overtake them because their town wasn’t far away.

ayoreo: Udojo u nanique uje yicatecãcoi ore iji. Dire ga yicopo ore rĩ uje yimóngo ta uje qué yocajneque ore gu. A jno diríca ore eduguéjnai, ore ichajui, ga gusu irógode iji de. Mu que toc jeta ore pojna yoque uje a jé ore eduguéjnai urúo gu.
english: Some of the Ayoreos counseled together and they said: “Stop those plans! Let’s talk to them. Probably the men are peaceful, and we can approach them in their town. Probably that one that was pierced is in the town.”

ayoreo: (*Cornelio Isaac era el hombre al que llamaban el jefe de los Menonitas.) *Patataque nanique – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1985.* *Marina de Morarie churúsãre patatagode unojo nanique.*
english: We who saw it happen thought maybe he was just pierced in his arm rather than his side, and that he might live. And we began to talk about what had happened. We recalled how he’d said to them: “Don’t shoot the Ayoreos.” And how he said: “Give them clothes.”  We remembered that he’d said: “They pierced me just now, but probably I’ll be their only victim.”

ayoreo: *Oidábiadé – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988.*
english: *Oidábiadé – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988.*

ayoreo: *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Oidábiadé atatagode nanique.*
english: *Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie.*

ayoreo: – ore uniri tu*Ducode*, ga jiei ore nanique.
english: *Ducodégosode –*“People of the graves” Their land was where there were many graves; there were many of the*Ducodégosode*long ago.

ayoreo: Ore asutedie u: Idaidé Etacõre, Asidáquide ore.
english: Their chiefs were:*Idaidé Etacõre*, and*Asidáquide*.

ayoreo: – ore uniri tu*C**ucarani Cerro Leon*. Garósi ore nanique.
english: *Totobíegosode*–  “People of the wild pig” A mountainous area called Cerro Leon was their land. There were few*Totobíegosode*long ago.

ayoreo: Ore asutedie u: Jochadáquide, Pajnidáquide Chiqueno.
english: Their chiefs were:*Jochadáquide,*and*Pajnidáquide Chiqueno*.

ayoreo: – ore uniri tu*T**ié*, ga jiei ore nanique.
english: *Tiégosode***–**“People of the river” Their country was composed of lands by the river;  there were many*Tiégosode*long ago.

ayoreo: Ore asutedie u: Beuidé, Noainé ore.
english: Their chiefs were:*Beuidé,*and*Noainé*.

ayoreo: – ore uniri tu*I**dáyabi*, ga jiei ore to.
english: *Guidaigosode*– “People of the village” They lived in a large village; there were many*Guidaigosode*long ago.

ayoreo: Ore asutedie u: Uruode (Degúyade yuc), Cuteridáquide, Eodedáquide, Jnumone ore.
english: Their chiefs were:*Uruode (Degúyade’s*victim),*Cuteridáquide, Eodedáquide,*and*Jnumone.*

ayoreo: – ore uniri tu*G**uerácadie*, ga jiei ore to.
english: *Cuterepajógosode*or*Cuterepaogorónegosode –*“People of honey” (called by two names) Their land was referred to as*‘guerácadie’*; there were many*Cutérepajógosode*.

ayoreo: Ore asuté u: Uniné.
english: Their chief was:*Uniné.*

ayoreo: Ore i uñai u:**Cutérepaogóronegosóde**
english: *Chabotodiegosode –*“People of the bats.”

ayoreo: – Ore uniri tu D*eyaidatedie*,*Puredai ore*.
english: Their land was land of the ‘great singers’ (deyaidatedie); ‘*Puredai*’ was one of the great singers, he was the now dead father of*Puré*. Their chiefs were*Cutadedé,*and*Tocoidie*.

ayoreo: Ore asutedie u: Cutadedé, Tocoidie.
english: *Dajeguéogosode*–  “People of ‘dajegueo’.”

ayoreo: – ore uniri tu*D**ajeguéo*.
english: Their land was ‘*dajegueo*’. Their chiefs were:*Guebei,*and*Cajodáquide*.

ayoreo: Ore asutedie u: Guebei, Cajodáquide.
english: *Erã́peparígosode –*“People of the dense forest.”
*Amomégosode***–**“People of*amomé*.”
*Tiequedéjnagosode –*“People of the strange (large) river.”

ayoreo: – ore uniri tu*D**orojobié*, ga jiei ore to.
english: Their land was called ‘dorojobíe’; there were many*Tiéquedéjnaigosode*long ago. Their chiefs were:*Ichaguedáquide, Ichabiade Dosapeque,*and*Pachade.*

ayoreo: Ore asutedie u: Ichaguedáquide, Ichabiadé Dosapeque, Pachade.
english: *Dorojobiegosode*–

ayoreo: – ore uniri tu*Tie Quedéjnai*, ga jiei ore.
english: *Direquedéjnagosipísode (Unirequedéjnagosode) –*“Original people of the strange day (land).”

ayoreo: *–*ore uniri tu*D**ía*Q*uedéjnai*jeoga*U**niri**Quedéjnai*,*ga jiei ore to.*
english: Their land was by the large river; there were many*Direquedéjnagosipísode*long ago. Their chiefs were*Capichádedé,*and*Guijnane*.

ayoreo: Ore asutedie u: Capichádedé, Guijnane ore.
english: *Picadebúigosode –*“People of the “picade” palm grove.”

ayoreo: – ore uniri tu*P**icadebui,*ga jiei ore.
english: Their land had groves of “picade palms;” there were many*Picadebúigosode*long ago. Their chiefs were:*Pochade,*and*Jororode.*

ayoreo: Ore asutedie u: Pochade, Jororode.
english: *Cochocóigosode –*“People of the large houses.”

ayoreo: – ore uniri tu*C**ochocoidie; Tie Jochaque**Cochocoi;*ga jiei ore.
english: Their land was where there were once “large white houses”; there were many*Cochocóigosode*long ago. The river knocked down the houses long ago. Their chiefs were:*Ingoinie,*and*Guiejnane*.

ayoreo: Ore asutedie u: Ingoinie, Guiejnane.
english: *Pajógosode –*“People of the clearing.”

ayoreo: – garósi ore iji ore uniri*Garai*nanique.
english: There were few*Pajógosode*in their land on the plains long ago. Their chiefs were:*Aitede,*and*Utatade*.

ayoreo: Ore asutedie u: Aitede, Utatade.
english: *Tuijnáigosode –*“People of land with streams flowing through.”

ayoreo: – ore uniri tu*T**uijnai.*
english: Their land was where there were streams. Their chiefs were:*Arobede,*an*d Etoguede*.

ayoreo: Ore asutedie u: Arobede, Etoguede.
english: *Cucójnaigosode –*“People of the large bottle trees (cucójnane).”

ayoreo: – ore uniri u*G**arai*nanique. (ore i ejoi tu) – ore uniri i tu*C**ucójna*bajaque, ejoi i tu*J**nupedo**Cucarãte**Cuchábode*ga jnupedo déji.
english: Their land was in the plains where there were many*bottle trees*. *Jnupedógosode –*“People of the caves.

ayoreo: Ore asutedie u: Jnohainé Cuchábi, Idaide ore.
english: Their land had lots of tall mountains where there were caves.  The*Cucójnaigosode*were later on called*Jnupedógosode.*In the beginning, when they were still in Paraguay, they were called*People of the star.*Their chiefs were:*Jnohainecuchábi,*and*Idaide*.

ayoreo: (Ore i uñai u*Guédogosode*to iji taningai uje qué ore déji Paraguay nanique.)
english: *Uechamitógosode*–  “People of the other side.” The name of their land was “Forests.”

ayoreo: – ore uniri i tu*Uguíjnamúone*.
english: Their chiefs were:*Tununguené, Eduguedé,*and*Moiné.*

ayoreo: Ore asutedie u: Tununguene, Eduguede, Moine ore.
english: *Tiquinachúigosode***–**“People who kill jaguar.” Their land was the same as the land of “The People of the Other Side” and the “Forest People” – (the three groups shared the same land).

ayoreo: –Uechamitogosode uniripise to aja*Uguíjnamuone*.
english: *Jachaigosode*–  “People of the ‘jachai’.”

ayoreo: –ore uniri tu*C**ucójnai*.
english: There were large*cucoi*trees in their land, but they left there and began to live in the ‘*jachai*’  and in the plains. Their chiefs were:*Jonanine,*and*Acui*.

ayoreo: Ore asutedie u: Jonanine, Acui.
english: *Carábiagosode***–**“People of the ‘carabia’.”

ayoreo: Ore jno nanique ga ore chẽcáre re, ga ore uniri tu*jachai, garai*.
english: Their land was*‘carabia’.* Their chiefs were:*Ijneráine,*and*Ocoi.*

ayoreo: – ore uniri tu*C**arábia*.
english: *Togarogosode*–  “People of the ‘togaro’.”

ayoreo: Ore asutedie u: Ijneraine, Ocoi.
english: Their land was*togaro*. *Oidábiadé – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988.*

ayoreo: – uniri tu*T**ogaro*.
english: *Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie.*

ayoreo: Chi cabayui jno Ayorei údi. Ga chi Ayorei chirote cabayui iji dajma dújna ga chi chiródiji dajma dújna. Ga chi chijnoque ga jé jno údi. Ga acotéraque chi chise gajmai dújna, mu cabayui e jno abai údi iji gajma dújna.
english: It’s said the tapir took off with an Ayoré man.  The Ayoré waited for the tapir by the*dújna tree*where he expected to get honey and he just waited there for him. And then the tapir came and carried him off.

ayoreo: Ga chi jno ga chorei iji ¡tiii! Ga chi chaguhei chejna ga chi chorei iji ¡tiii! Ga chi chaguhei chejna ga chi chojninga: “¡Ṍroro!” Jnusina cucha pibotique gu.
english: And his wife came but when she reached the*dújna tree*where her husband expected to find honey, he wasn’t there, because the tapir had already taken her husband off and he wasn’t at the*dújna tree*where he expected to find honey.

ayoreo: Ga cabayui chi chojninga: “¿Gotique baruode?” Ga chojninga: “Que yo cheme.”
english: And so it was that when the tapir left, he took the Ayoré man off with him. And, after a while the Ayorei got hungry because by this time they were far away. And he got hungrier and finally he said: “Oooooh!” He was thinking about food and that is why he groaned.

ayoreo: Mu cabayui chiraja ga chi chuchi. Ga chi ‘*ajidábia*’ tṍraji ga chi chipése. Ga chi subudé iji. Cutepéode chiese garani tu cabayúode ome jnani bajade.
english: And the tapir asked: “What did you say?”

ayoreo: Ga jnopo rĩ, ga chaguhei chejna iji ¡tiii! Ga isocai jé u uje chojninga: “¡Ṍroro!” uje jnusina cucha pibotique. Jé u uje cabayui chi chuchi ga*cutẽ́ri*tṍraji. Ga chipese ga e subudei iji. Ga chiese cutepéode garani u cabayui.
english: He said: “I didn’t say anything.”

ayoreo: Mu jnopo rĩ ga isocai jé u uje chaguhei chejna iji ¡tiii! Ga Ayorei chojninga: “¡Ṍroro!” uje jnusina cucha pibotique. Chi cabayui chojninga: “Baruode ¿írique?”
english: But the tapir knew and then, he pooped. And it’s said the ‘honey that comes from small yellow bee’s’ came out. And the Ayorei ate until he was full. Honey originated with the tapirs who provided it for the first men.

ayoreo: Enga Ayorei chojninga: “Que yo cheme.” Pógu daruode ome cabayui gu. Ga jé u uje jnopo rĩ ga chicáji ¡tiii! Cabayui chuchi ga chi ‘*orojonie*’ tṍraji ga chipese ga subudé iji. Ga jé jnopo rĩ ga áyipie doi acoté ga cho ¡ṍroro! Chaguhei chejna gu. Ga cabayui chojninga: “Baruode ¿írique?”
english: And the tapir took off again, and the Ayorei got hungry after a while. And the same thing happened, he said: “Oooooh!” when he remembered food. And then it was that the tapir pooped and the ‘honey of small black bees’ came out. And he sucked the honey till he was full.

ayoreo: Mu chojninga: “Que yo cheme.”
english: And it’s claimed that the origin of honey was the tapir.

ayoreo: Ga chi chuchi ga tṍra ‘*orójna*’ aja guesi ga jé chipese ga subudéji.
english: But he left again and the same things happened: the Ayorei got hungry way off there. And the Ayorei groaned and said: “¡Oooooh!” because he remembered food.

ayoreo: Jé noña ga cabayui oidi ayoré noñome cuchiso iji toniñeque.
english: They say the tapir said: “Your words, what were they?”

ayoreo: *Oidábiadé – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988.* *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Oidábiadé atatagode udojo nanique.*
english: And the Ayorei said: “I didn’t make a sound.” He hid his words from the tapir, that’s why. And then it was that the tapir took off and went much further. The tapir pooped, and it’s said another type of honey came out. This ‘honey came from black bees’ that makes it’s nest on the ground. And the Ayorei sucked honey till he was full.

ayoreo: Chi ayoré jnani bajade nanique ga chi ore chujé *Pade ore iñoñai.
english: **I will tell about what the Ayoreos did to an official of the *Pade:** They say the Ayoreos who lived long ago killed an official of the **Pade*.

ayoreo: Chi sumájningai jnué  gáyode uje ore chejna dajéode aja guesi iji gai. Chi Picanerane chi ca chijnime ore ãrírique. Posingórone u Picanerane ore iji erámi uje ore chosina dojode ayoréo aja erámi.
english: They say fury overcame them and they screamed at him. They said this about the*Picanerane,*they couldn’t be overcome*.*The*Picanerane*were pursuers; everywhere they went, they chased after their fellow*Ayoreos*.

ayoreo: Ga jé u uje cucherãque uñane sumájningai sué ore. Ga ore chejna dajéode aja guesi iji*Pade*ore iñoñai gai. Jé u uje sumájningai sué ore gai; ore chejna dajéode aja guesi, ga*¡ore cho jérere!*
english: And so it was that the other clansmen were overcome by fury. And it was they who worked themselves up to kill the **Pade’s official.*To become angry enough to kill they breathed deeply and expelled their breath with force, chanting ‘¡*jérere, jérere!*’

ayoreo: Jé basa que ore chijnime Picanerane ore ãrírique.
english: In spite of this, the other clans couldn’t catch up with the*Picanerane.*

ayoreo: Chi cucherane sumájningai sué ore gai ga ore chejna dajéode aja guesi iji darurui gai.
english: They say the clansmen would work themselves up to kill their victims.

ayoreo: Sumájningai sué ore gai gu.
english: That’s because fury would then control them.

ayoreo: Que Picanerane que ore chejna dajénique aja guetique iji darurui gai.
english: But not the Picanerane. They didn’t have to work themselves up to kill victims.

ayoreo: Ore sué sumájningai gai gu.
english: That’s because they could control fury.

ayoreo: *Pade – plural de*pai, ga ayoréode uruode ome*padre, sacerdote
english: **Pade –  Probably this is a term that the Ayoreos used to refer to Jesuit priests who were called padres.*

ayoreo: *Oidábiadé – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988.*
english: *Oidábiadé – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988.*

ayoreo: *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Oidábiadé atatagode udojo nanique.*
english: *Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie.*

ayoreo: Chi cucha bijnángone guiyangṍri tu*Tináiné*.
english: *Cucha Bijnángone Guiyangṍri*

ayoreo: Chi cucha bijnángone guiyangṍri tu*Tináiné*.
english: It is said the finder of lost things was Tináiné.

ayoreo: Ga chi ore chojninga nanique: ‘Beyoi*Tináiné*ome cucha bijnai.’
english: They would say: ‘Bring Tináiné to find the lost thing.’

ayoreo: Ga chi ore doi*Tináiné*, ga*Tináiné*chijna re aja jogadi, ore ijnanguiéni, ga chojninga: ‘Déji de uacajnanguiéni.’
english: And when they brought Tináiné, Tináiné would take himself right to where the lost thing was.

ayoreo: Ga chi ore doi*Tináiné*, ga*Tináiné*chijna re aja jogadi, ore ijnanguiéni, ga chojninga: ‘Déji de uacajnanguiéni.’
english: And he would say: ‘Here is your lost thing.’

ayoreo: Ga cucha bijnángone guiyangṍri u*Tináiné*.
english: And so, the finder of ‘lost things’ was Tináiné.

ayoreo: *Oidábiadé – Campo Loro, Paraguay nanique – 1988.*
english: *Oidábiadé – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988.*

ayoreo: *Marina de**Morarie churúsãre Oidábiadé atatagode udojo nanique.*
english: *Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie.*

ayoreo: Orobojnai:                  Abidie uña i tu Sabaide; Jnuruminórone ore ujatarójna.
english: Orobojnai:  One of his daughters was Sabaide; he was of the Jnuruminórone Clan, powerful in battle.

ayoreo: Cojnainené:                Cutamurajane ore ujatarójna. Dequejnangórone u ore.
english: Cojnáinené:  He was of the Cutamurajane Clan, powerful in battle. They were fierce warriors.

ayoreo: Yujátadé:                    Cutamurajane ore eduguéjna bajade uña.
english: *Yujátadé:  He was of the Cutamurajane Clan and was one of their first leaders.

ayoreo: Baráiyudé:                  Cutamurajai ga irábai u Yujátadé; jõrai tu.
english: Baráiyudé:  He was a Cutamurajai and lived at the same time as Yujátadé, and was his companion.

ayoreo: Ijnacuréguei:             Picanerai, jocãratáquechoquí.
english: Ijnacuréguei:  His name meant*One Palm*. He was a Picanerai, a valient fighter.

ayoreo: Garajade:                    Dosapec
english: Garajade:His name meant*Twins*. He was of the Dosapei Clan.

ayoreo: Jnañoróne:                 Dosapeque, dequejnasṍri u.
english: Jnañoroné:  He was of the Dosapei Clan, a fierce warrior.

ayoreo: Diguinórone Ũrui:     Cutamuraja, dequejnasṍre.
english: Diguinórone Ũrui:  His name meant*Victim of a Snake*. He was of the Cutamuraja Clan and a warrior.

ayoreo: Jané:                            Posõraja, dequejnasṍre.
english: Jané:  He was of the Posõrajane Clan and a warrior.

ayoreo: Chogaide:               Picanerai u. Jocãratáquechoquí.
english: Chogaide Picanerai:  He was a fierce fighter.

ayoreo: Juricai:                    Úringai paraque gajnei tu*Juricai*. Jé basa isijnaringuei gajnei tu*Chogaide*. (Jé cunguésõre Sáulo uje jocãrataque ga cha ga i ejoi u Pablo.)
english: Juricai:When his name was*Juricai*he was very good; before that he was called*Chogaide*and at that time he was really bad. (It was like it was with Saul who was a killer, but after he stopped killing,  he was called Paul.)

ayoreo: Dutagaidé:               Chiqueno; jocãratáquechoquí.
english: Dutagaidé:      His name meant*‘Black Ant*’. He was of the Chiquenoi Clan, and a fierce warrior.

ayoreo: Gatócoréone:           Dosapec, jocãratáquechoquí.
english: Gatócoréone:  He was a Dosapec, and a fierce warror.

ayoreo: Daijnámeque:          Picanerai, jocãratáquechoquí.
english: Daijnámeque:  His name meant ‘*Witchdoctor*’ and he was of the Picanerai Clan, and a fierce warrior.

ayoreo: *Oidábiadé – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988.*
english: *Oidábiadé – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988.*

ayoreo: *Marina de**Morarie churúsãre Oidábiadé atatagode udojo.*
english: *Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie.*

ayoreo: ***Jnanione****Chequedíe***Chiquenone.Chiquenoi.*C*hiquejnoro. Chiquenone u yoque.            Chiqueno yu.                         Chiquejnoro yu.
english: *Inoningayaque Yoque* ***Man Member****Woman Member*** ChiquenoiChiquejnoro Chiquenone u yoque.            Chiqueno yu.                 Chiquejnoro yu. *We are Chiquenone.**I am a Chiquenoi.**I am a Chiquenoro.*

ayoreo: Picanerane.Picanerai.                                  Picaneré. Picanerane u yoque.              Picaneraque yu.                     Picaneré yu.
english: Picanerai                         Picaneré Picanerane u yoque.              Picaneraque yu.             Picaneré yu. *We are Picanerane.**I am a Picanerai.**I am a Picaneré.*

ayoreo: Dosapéode.Dosapei.                                    Dosapé. Dosapéode u yoque.             Dosapeque yu.                        Dosapé yu.
english: Dosapei                                Dosapé Dosapéode u yoque.             Dosapeque yu.                     Dosapé yu. *We are Dosapéode.               I am a Dosapei.                      I am a Dosapé*

ayoreo: Juruminone.Jurumini.                                 Juruminé. Juruminone u yoque.            Jurumite yu.                           Juruminé yu.
english: **(pl.)**Jurumini                                Juruminé Juruminone u yoque.            Jurumite yu.                           Juruminé yu. *We are Juruminone.              I am a Jurumini.                      I am a Juruminé.*

ayoreo: Etacórone.Étacõri.                                        Étacõro. Etacórone u yoque.               Étacõre yu.                                Étacõro yu.
english: Étacõri                                      Étacõro Etacórone u yoque.               Étacõre yu.                               Étacõro yu *We are Etacórone.                 I am an Étacõri.                        I am an Étacõro.*

ayoreo: Posorajane.Posorajãi.                                     Posíjñoro. Posorajane u yoque.             Posorajã yu.                                Posíjñoro yu.
english: Posorajãi                                Posíjñoro Posorajane u yoque.             Posorajã yu.                           Posíjñoro yu. *We are Posorajane.               I am a Posorajãi.                    I am a Posíjñoro.*

ayoreo: Cutamurajane.Cutamurajãi.                          Cutamijñoro. Cutamurajane u yoque.         Cutamurajã yu.                      Cutamijñoro yu.
english: Cutamurajãi                           Cutamijñoro Cutamurajane u yoque.         Cutamurajã yu.                     Cutamijñoro yu. *We are Cutamurajane.**I am a Cutamurajãi.               I am a Cutamijñoro.*

ayoreo: *Oidabiadé – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988.*
english: *Oidábiadé – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988.*

ayoreo: *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Oidabiadé atatagode udojo.*
english: *Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie.*

ayoreo: Ayoréode ore jõra ñane jnese iji taningai. Ore chujé ayoré quedéjnane. Ore eduguéjna ejode u udojo: *Yacamanáquide*– ore eduguéjna cuchábi – Picanerãi – Tiegose
english: The Ayoreos were one nation, all together in the beginning, and at that time they fought other indigenous peoples with languages and cultures different than their own.  Here are the names of the Ayoré leaders who became leaders after the Ayoré had separated into groups.

ayoreo: *Cajõdáquide*– Étacõre – Tiegose
english: *(*Grandfather of Yacamai) – He was a very big chief of the*Picanerãi Clan*and a member of the*Tiegosode*(People of the River)

ayoreo: *Guebei*– Jnurumite – Tiegose
english: (Grandfather of Cajõi) –*Étacõre Clan*and a member of the*Tiegosode*(People of the River)

ayoreo: *Jnurumini*– Chiqueno – Tiegose
english: –*Jnurumini Clan*and a member of the*Tiegose (*People of the River)

ayoreo: *Chugúpẽre Ungoiné*– Picanerac – Ducodégose
english: –*Chiquenoi Clan*and a member of the*Tiegosode*(People of the River)

ayoreo: *Esoquedéjnainé*– Posorajã – Ducodegose
english: –*Picanerãi Clan*and a member of the*Ducodegosode (*People of the Graves)

ayoreo: *Namisedé*– Chiqueno – Ducodegose
english: *Posorajãi Clan*and a member of the*Ducodegosode (*People of the Graves)

ayoreo: *Beuidé*– Picaneraque – Tiegose
english: –*Chiquenoi Clan*and a member of the*Ducodegosode*(People of the Graves)

ayoreo: *Cojñoquené*– Dosapec – Tiegose
english: –*Picanerãi Clan*and a member of the*Tiegosode*(People of the River)

ayoreo: *Jnumoné*– Chiqueno – Tiegose
english: –*Dosapei Clan*and a member of the*Tiegosode (*People of the River)

ayoreo: *Jnoháiné*– Chiqueno – Tiegose
english: –*Chiquenoi Clan*and a member of the*Tiegosode (*People of the River)

ayoreo: *Uejai (Dichaca)*– Picaneraque – Tiegose
english: –*Chiquenoi Clan*and a member of the*Tiegosode (*People of the River)

ayoreo: *Idaidé*– Étacõre – Ducodegose
english: –*Picanerãi Clan*and a member of the*Tiegosode (*People of the River)

ayoreo: *Arangué*, Tiegoto, ga ore chirota ga ore chujé Totobiegosode ga ore cho jmite-jmite ome ñane. Ore eduguéjna*Jochadáquide*. Tagu jonane (cuguéode); jé ore chujé. Chiroque ore aja ga ore chujé.
english: –*Étacóri Clan*and a member of the*Ducodegosode (*People of the Graves)

ayoreo: *Oidábiadé – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988.*
english: There was a dispute involving*Arangué*and during the battle the*Tiegosode*killed some of the*Totobiegosode*and that’s when they separated from each other. The chief of the*Tiegosode*was*Jochadáquide*. Because*Arangué*and others of her*group*were eating bean seeds that had been saved for planting,*Jochadáquide*sent people to kill them.

ayoreo: *Marina de Morarie churúrsãre Oidábiadé atatagode udojo.*
english: *Oidábiadé – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988.*

ayoreo: Chi Cochocoigosode ore chicáji Jnupedogosode nanique iji diri uté nanique uje chuque misionérone iji. Jiei bajade uje e ore jno, mu uñane qué ore sase. Ijmai déji bajade ore ujai nanique enga jno to. Mu Caratábide chicáji dejnai bajaque ga déji uté uje chudute cuchárique. A misionérone u enga ore catecã́ri ñane uje ore chicáji uté yui. Todo ga chayo ga chicáji degúi ujetiga chingo darásade iji uté. Chojninga: “¡Cojñotígo e di é, ayóyome ore!” Enga urasade ore chayo jnese.
english: The men from the*Cochocóigosode*grouop were going off to the*Jnupedógosode*the day the men were killed. Many of them had already left and others were getting ready to follow.*Ijmai*was in the group who had already left.

ayoreo: **ore****díji ore irengúi.**
english: *Caratábidé*, who was also called*Ejóne*had gone out to his garden to hoe when he heard the missionary men talking as they approached. He ran back to camp and told the rest of the group to flee, and that “white people” were coming. Everyone left the camp.

ayoreo: A ore chimo uje ayoréode déji que, mu e tita ome ore. A misionérone chirote ore yui jeta ore chajníja darengúi, enga diyajmaque ore iji ta. Mu e ayoréode chichongara dojodie. Bajaque Soríané uje chicho ore mu chicho ore ejórique. Mu uñeque chicho udore uñai iji orotadé enga jé u uje misionérone ore chayome ore.
english: The missionaries finally arrived at the camp and were there for quite awhile before any of the*Cochocóigosode*had the courage to shoot arrows at them.*Soriane*was one of the first to shoot, but he missed. Eventually one of them was wounded in the shoulder, after which all the missionaries fled the camp.

ayoreo: Ayorérique chojninga: “A íjnoque ore pocadie jõ, uje yichongaco yocojodie mu que ore chicho yoque go. Jé u uje uté chaquesu pore jmanecárique, enga chojninga: “Bo yico, yajnángo ore enga a yo yogué iji pore jmanecai udé enga yujégo ore jne.”
english: One of the Ayoreos said, “They don’t have guns, because they didn’t fire upon us when we shot our arrows.”

ayoreo: Jé basa ga ayoréode ore chaparu ore gai, mu ore pogu ráguiji poridie ujade. Mu uje misionérone chudute ore enga ore chajire erámi enga ore chisa datójedie enga ore chachia datójedie iji dajmainie iji ore iquei.
english: He cut a tree branch then and said, “I’ll use this for a club. Let’s follow them and kill them.”

ayoreo: Mu ayoréode que ore chiraja jeta ore aringaquei u jéoga ore ininaquéi u. Jé u uje ore pésu dáyipiedie ujetiga ore chujé ore.
english: They didn’t follow on the open path the missionaries had taken, but spread out into the jungle and surrounded them. When the missionaries sensed someone was coming, they took off their hats and waved them at the Ayoreos.
The Ayoreos, however, didn’t know whether it was a gesture of friendship or not, and decided to go ahead with the killing.

ayoreo: A tie tuaté i tu Tucavaca ome cojñone ore nanique. Mu ore todo jeta udore urásarigo chucué ore enga jnutaque ore ome ore. Jecuté ore jnoji ore jogadi uté nanique iji erámi.
english: They buried the five missionaries on the bank of the river (probably the Tucavaca River), and then left that place, fearing retaliation.

ayoreo: Mu cojñone chise ore iji uté enga ore pocadíe cuse enga ore chujé ayorérique iji uté uje i tu*Tocoide*.
english: During rainy season the Ayoré́ settled down in one place to raise their rainy season crops.
