And so it was, that after some time had elapsed, the group returned to harvest their small garden plots and were found there by an expedition that had gone out in search of the missing missionaries.

ayoreo: (Uje*Ijmai*qué déji dojñane gai, mu uñeque chingome*Ijmai*uje chi*Tocoide*yuque cojñotique bajaque, enga cho jetiga*Ijmai*irasique re uje chudute ore ojñane udore.)
english: The expedition killed one of Ayoré group whose name was*Tocoidé.*

ayoreo: Chi uje cojñone jno, enga ayoréode chajniapója pijogadi uté rĩ uje ore gaidi u gu ujetiga ore chijóchame*Tocoide*.*Ijmai*chojniga: “Cojñone chicho*Tocoide*iji etarúdi, jé iji ica, arobé ore to rĩ. Chi*Tocoide*chirota dacoté uje todo gu ujetiga cojñone chise enga chujé uaté to.
english: *(Ijmai*was not aware that*Tocoidé*had shot one of the expedition first, and when this was told, he smiled as if he was pleased about it before going on with his story.)

ayoreo: Misionérone uje chejna yijmanai nanique, ore yungórone urásarigo ore asuté i tu*Iringoine*. Ore coráchade u asṍrenie, docoguedie ore nanique enga ore chijna udire uje ore chujé ore nanique.
english: The Ayoreos waited until the expedition had left to return and bury*Tocoidé*who he had been shot in the leg, stomach, and chest. He was defending his wife, fearing that the expedition might harm her.

ayoreo: Zapocogosi*Cuchacarataide*déji to uje*Ijmai*chatata udore ome yoque. Enga chojninga: “Que pisocai u go ome yoque jeta yujégo gosi uje íjnoque uté gosi coráchatique. ¿Gotique gu a? uje que udore uñeque chaquĩ́ra ore nanique. Ayoréode uyoque yangácoi paquiraquéode gu. A ore bai u uje ore chujé ore. A pijoque misionérone ore áyipie ueradedie uje ore chachía datójedie iji dajmainie, ga imoi u uje ajequeseque ore, mu qué ore chujé ore nanique.”
english: The following is a list that Ijmai gave us of the names of the men who killed the five missionaries, their clan names, group names, and where they first came out into civilization. He also told us if they had become believers or not.

ayoreo: Dupade anguresórone iji Bolivia, Paraguay ore, ore adebeque uje ore cháta raja ñane ome Dupade Uruode. Ga jé u nanique iji 2 de enero de 1985 uje*Ijmai*, jorane ore íjnongaja Campo Loro, Paraguay ujetiga ore tangári dojode Dupade anguresórone ore acajnaquéode. Enga jé u nanique uje ugúcoji uté to, Juan uyu, enga*Ijmai*chingome yoque ome cucha dacadode nanique uje chuque misionéro bajade udore uje ore chucué ayoréode iji montedie nanique iji taningai.
english: *Cuchacarataide*, from Zapoco, Bolivia, was present when*Ijmai*was giving us this information and commented the following: “When it was discovered that the men had no guns, someone should have counseled the killers to stop. Ayoreos generally listen to “*paquiraquéode*” (counseling to not do something),” he said. He felt it was an indication of guilt on their part, because they are usually careful to not kill*people who have good intentions.*

ayoreo: *Ijmai – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1985*
english: The group that our five men stumbled upon were the*Cochocoigosode.*

ayoreo: *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Ijmai atatagode udojo nanique.*
english: *Ijmai – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1985.*

ayoreo: Yirasique yu uje yugúsi uacajade iji de Campo Loro. Yacoté i tu Patricia, ga uaté pota jeta arajáyo uje chi unere uaquéode ome uaté.
english: I am very happy to be here with you today.  My wife’s name is Patricia, and she sends greetings to you.

ayoreo: Yijõrai Cadui e chingome uaque uje ayoréode chujé yai nanique iji 1943 uje qué disape yu nanique. Jecute que yujnusina yai gotique enga que yujnúsi to go. Yidai u Tobité nanique uje qué disi yu enga yirasiguipise yidai uté nanique. Ayoréode u uje ore chujé yai, mu que gusu yai, mu ore chuje yai uñai to uje i tu Roberto, jé aja udore ore jorane uje ore íode u David, Jorge, Eldon ore.
english: My friend Cadui has already told you that Ayoreos killed my father in 1943 when I was only a little boy.  I don’t remember my father at all, and I never think about him.  I lived in Tobité when I was a little boy and I really liked living there.  It was the Ayoré of Bolivia that killed my father.  They didn’t only kill my father Cecil, but they killed my uncle Robert, too.  Not only them, but they killed three other men as well named David, George, and Eldon.

ayoreo: Uje quene yicagóji Tobité nanique iji taningai, enga ité, yinanie, uyoque yujégo yogaja buéyode gáyode. Uje yirõco Tobité yui, mu yai yusṍrone ore chachai yoque. Uje ore chudute uje chi ore yui acoté chicáji Tobité enga ore pota jeta ore chachai uaté. Uje yĩríngo enga ore chojningame ité: “Yoco yocáyugu uje yipéco bajnusiétigade nanique. Ujetiga yirajágorase uje ueradei babai ore áyipiedie nanique enga, a ca yujégorase ore.”
english: The first time we went into Tobité‚ my mother, my sisters, and I had to ride oxen.  As we were arriving, we were met by my father’s killers. When they heard that the wife of one of their victims was coming into camp, they wanted to go out to meet her. When they met her, they said to my mother: “We are very sorry for the sorrow we have caused you. If we had known then what we know now, we would never have done it.”

ayoreo: Ité chajnai ore uruode ga chojninga: “Jé arec. Yimo uje e ajéquesé uaque gu.”
english: My mother answered them: “That is all right. I see that you are now peaceful.”

ayoreo: Jiei misionérone uje ore tibágui ayoréode uaquedáyode uje udore ore chiraja Dupade. Yipota jeta uacáyipiedie déji uje cuchade e chécãre re ome uaque enga ajéquesé uaque gu uje udore chise uaque nanique enga ore chacate uagome Dupade Uruode. Mu gose Dupade ijnoraquéi. Chojninga: *“Yababode uaque a, yoquijnoraquei jé ca u cuchade uje cachi garãte yoquibijnanone. Mu jé ũrepise yoquijnoraquei enga pijóguiji yocadode ome ñane.” (1 Juan 3:18)*
english: Many other missionaries have come to your villages who know God. Think how much has changed since they came to you. It’s all because of God’s love. First John 3:18 says:*“My little children, let us not love in word, neither in tongue; but in deed and in truth.”*

ayoreo: Jiei ore uje ore chojninga: “Yijnóra yujode ayoréo arĩa.” Mu cachi ore urúo. Mu Dupade uruode chojninga: “Yoquijnoraquei jé ca u cuchade uje cachi garãte yoquibijnanone.” Cucha ibiraque arĩa jeta yojníjo, ‘yijnóra ore,’ mu cucha amiri déji ome yoque ujetiga yipéco, enga u ujetiga yujuséjo yocujode ayoréo to. *“Mu jé ũrepise yoquijnoraquei enga pijóguiji yocadode ome ñane,” chojninga.* Quene yoquijnoraquei pijoque uje yoco ñane quique, jeoga yipéco udore gajnúone, enga jé u uje ore chojninga: a yijõrai uté chijnora dojoi ayoré uje cho quique gu.
english: There are many people who say, “I love my fellow men.” But you see in God’s Word that it says, “Your love should not just come out of your lips.”  It is easy to say, “I love you,” but that isn’t enough. The love that is real love is shown through our actions, and it is when we help others or counsel them. When we are doing things like that, others see that our love is real.

ayoreo: Que cachi Dupade uruode uje chijnóra yoque, mu yimóngo ijnoraquédatéi ome yoque uje chiroque dajnacari aja jnumi nanique ujetiga toji curesei gai ome yoque, ujetiga diga yanguretágo enga dai Dupade cho dabode iji yoque to.
english: That is how God loves us. God didn’t just say how much he loved us down here on earth, he showed it by sending his son to die on the cross for our sins so that he could make us a part of his family.

ayoreo: Yai, uté jorane ore, ore chicáji ore uniri Bolivia nanique. Que cachi ore urúo uje ore chijnóra ayoréode ore. Mu ore tẽra re ome ayoréode uje ore chucué ore nanique. Yirajago uje ũré ore ijnoraquei gu uje ore chucué ore nanique enga ore tẽra re ome ore.
english: My father and his companions went to the land of Bolivia. They didn’t just talk about how they loved the Ayoreos, but gave their very lives trying to reach them. That is because the love they had for the Ayoreos was real.

ayoreo: Dupade Uruode chatata uje tadipise Dupade ijnoraquei ome yoque.  Chojninga: *“Que pijnoraquei pijoque gu yoquijnoraquei ome Dupade. Mu pijoque gu Dupade ijnoraquei ome yoque. Uje chingo yoque dajnoraquédatéi uje chiroque dabi aja erámone nanique ujetiga cho yoquisijnaringuéone oré ome datoringai.” (1 Juan 4:10)*
english: God’s Word tells of his great love for us in 1 John 4:10:*“Herein is love, not that we loved God, but that he loved us and sent his son to be the propitiation for our sins.”*

ayoreo: Uje Dupade pésu cuchade, que pésu datígo bisideque.  Mu pésu gu dajnoraquei. Uruode chojninga: *“Yirajágo uje uacajnóraño Jesucrísto uje que amóño ome uaquedodie. Que amóño marínga, jé uacanguretáyo. Jecute uacarasiguipise uaque. Enga que uacarúrigo cus ome uacaninaquei uté uje uacarasiguipise uaque gu.” (1 Pedro 1:8)*
english: God doesn’t just do things, He does them with love. I Peter 1:8 says:*“Whom having not seen, you love; in whom, though now you see him not, yet believing, you rejoice with joy unspeakable and full of glory.”*There is none of us who has seen God face to face, and yet we love him.

ayoreo: Que yimóngo Dupade ome yoquedodie, mu yijnórango uté.
english: It is only a year ago that guerrilla fighters took me captive. I tried to talk to them about the love of God, but they said to me, “There is no God.”

ayoreo: Sequeré bajé u diríca uje chigocabéode iji Colombia ore chisa yu. Yicatecã́ri ore enga yingome ore uje Dupade chijnóra ore, mu ore chojningame yu diríca: “Que Dupárique cusipise go.”
english: But I said to them, “I have never seen God, but I’ve read in his Word that he loves me. He is the living God, and without him there would be nothing in existence.”

ayoreo: Mu yojningame ore: “Yingome uaque uje que yimo Dupade ome yedodie, mu yajire Dupade Uruode enga yirãrája gai. Enga uruode u uje chingome yu uje chijnóra yoque. Dupa uecai u uté. Ujetiga ca Dupárique cuse enga a ca erámi, gaté cuse to, uje uté u gu uje ipesute ore nanique.”
english: I said to them, “According to your reasoning, what you can’t see, doesn’t exist.  So I guess you don’t believe wind exists. For you don’t believe in anything that you can’t see.  Well, try as you will, you’ll never be able to see the wind, but only feel the effects of it.”

ayoreo: Yojninga: “Ome uaque enga ujetiga ca pimo cuchárique, enga íjnoque. Jecute a ca ẽtique cuse uje que pimo emi gu, ¿jec ũré yu a? A uacabéroyopíse uaquedodie iji jeta amoño emi ome uaquedodie, mu a ca amóño jne. Mu uacarajáyo uje déji uje uacamóño adode gu uje chubúchu cuchade.”
english: They couldn’t answer me.  But finally they said, “But we see what the wind causes.”

ayoreo: Ore cho paraque, uje que ore chiraja daru quitígome yu. Mu quigade enga ore chojninga: “Yimóngo adode ga yirajágo uje emi cuse.”
english: I said to them, “That is right, and we also see what God has done. Look at the trees, the tall trees.  The fact that you live and exist is also evidence of the God who created you.”

ayoreo: Yojninga: “Jé ũré uaque. Jé cho Dupade to. Yimóngo uté adode to ga jé u uje yirajágo uje Dupade cuse. Cajicho poridie to, pore umaripísedie ore. Uacaquecáyo, ¿jé ũré yu a? Jecute uje yimo uaquedodie enga yiraja uje uacapesusṍri Dupade eca to.” Dire gagájnione u uje ugúsi yisasṍrone ore gari ica. ¿Ama adúcho yisocáringome yúringai juseque ica ya?  Dejaque ica enga yi guesi, mu yisasṍrone ore mo jnese enga que ore chimo yu. Dejaque mu yise yachidi cuchabasuc. Yurugusiji yachidi aja dajei, mu jesi cuse ga que yimopise yajiritique. Mu yacája cuchabasui ajei, enga yijocase enga jĩraque cuchabasui déji gaté. Mu yajire jeta*gasolina*cuse, mu yimo uje garósi. Yojninga: “A*una hora*enga e íjnoque*gasolina*jõ.” Enga jéc ũré yu uje*hora chojmarai*enga íjnoque. Cuchabasui doi jnumi mu Dupade pésu ga yico jnumi enga que cuchárique déji yoquiquei. Uje dire enga yimo uje cuchabasui déji pore umarenie ujai, mu que cuchárique chosi ti iji yoc. Yijos eti uyu u ijocayasṍri, mu a Dupade u cuchabasui ijocayasṍripísi diríca jõ.
english: I was with these men four days. Do you know how I escaped from them? I escaped in the night, found the plane, pushed it to the airstrip, and with fog so thick I couldn’t see a thing, I took off.  After I was in the air I discovered I only had enough gasoline in the tank to fly for one hour. I ran out of gas and had to land while it was still night, but God found a place in the middle of the jungle for me to land; he was in control all the while and helped me to land without incident.  And here I am safe and sound!

ayoreo: U gu uje jiei ore uje ore catecã́ri Dupade ome yu uje ore chudute yujnomei uje chi chigocabéode ore chisa yu. Cojñone iji erámone jnese chiroque darusarane ome yu jeta ore chingome yu uje ore catecã́ripísi Dupade ome yu ica. Ore jnese chojninga: “Uje bagúsi basasṍrone ore josipiedie jmainie diríca enga que yibérogo yogome yoquicatecaraitigade ome ua.”
english: Many people were praying for me. I have received letters from all over the world telling me about their prayers. These letters say: “We constantly held you up in prayer while you were in the hands of the guerrillas.”

ayoreo: Yiraja uje iji Tobité ore catecã́ri Dupade ome yu to. Chiese ore catecã́ri misionerone iji dirode jnese enga ore chojningame ore: “¿Pablo déji rique a?” Enga uje ore chudute uje juseque yu, enga ore ninapúsu raque. Jé tiógara yoquicotaquéode ome Yocangureni Dupa. Yoquipesusṍri u gu. Cuchade jnese ipesusṍre uté. Yirajágo ijnoraquedatéi gu ipesudode cucha, cuchiyi tupúsu uje chiroque dajnacari Jesucristo aja jnumi ome yoque ujetiga cho daba yoquisijnaringuéone. Yacaranguipis ome Campo Loro gosode uaque uje chi uacacatecãchoi Dupade ome yu to ica.
english: The Ayoreos in Tobité were praying and asking: “Where is Paul?” And when they heard how I had escaped, they rejoiced. We have a wonderful God. He is the one who created us.  He made everything we see in this world. His love is seen in all that he made, but is seen most of all in the sending of his Son to earth to die for our sins.

ayoreo: *Pablo Dye – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1985*
english: I thank you so much for praying for me, also.

ayoreo: Chi chugúpẽredatéi chise siqueré diringai casica. Mu chise*corongóni arobé*iji siqueré diringai. Ga chise*mimiré*, chisapo*duchúbie*iji rĩ, ga jé chise*guejnai ujníerãi*iji, ga chisapo*cuguéode ujníerãi*, ga jé u uje chi chajo dejnai.
english: They say it was ‘Big Bird’ who found the rainy season long ago in the beginning of things. But with the rainy season ‘Big Bird’ discovered a new sprout of squash. And it also discovered sprouts of watermelons, sprouts of gourds, sprouts of corn, and sprouts of beans and then it was that Big Bird started clearing the land in order to plant these sprouts.

ayoreo: Ga jé u uje chi chacáji dajopidi. Jé u uje chiese cucha garani u chugúpẽredatéi uje chaca cuchade ujníerane iji dajopídi.
english: Then it was that he went to his field. And they claim that these things originated with Big Bird when he planted them in his field.

ayoreo: Jé u uje jnani bajade chimo cuchade ga jé u siqueré diringai uje cucha ajñunie suéji siqueré diringai. Yocayode ore chatata siqueré adode uje chiese pacapiéraque ca noña jnutique.
english: And so it was that the men of old saw these things and knew that it is the rainy season in which seed things come up. Our forefathers then told these stories about what happened in the rainy season so that the things they planted wouldn’t just stay in the ground, but would come up.

ayoreo: Putáningai gajnéone u siqueré diringai uje chiese ca pacapiéraque ca noñáji jnutique.
english: Things pertaining to spring come about because of rainy season, and because of the rainy season planting is not in vain.

ayoreo: *Oidábiadé – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988.*
english: *Oidabiade – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988.*

ayoreo: *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Oidábiadé atatagode udojo nanique.*
english: *Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie.*

ayoreo: Chi*Dachagaidé*chujé ayoréode uñai. Tõria. Tibáguirási ore idai uñai mu ore chise ñane iji dajeque pajei ga chãra re iji dusai, uje ore chuchi. Tibidi dei ayoré, mu que yimo írique. Chi ore chaji ti iji dajei, uñane. Chi chojninga: “Boji te. Yangácoi ñane ojñáringo.” Mu chi tibidi iji ti mu jĩrique chujé nanique. Sijnanque ga cachi yuc ore, ayoréode. Chi chijóchame dei, tagadeque. Chicháji pidode aja gai, odé, ga cho jnumone ujetiga ca cho ayorérique ódedie.
english: It’s said he killer. And he was known to steal. He was on his way to another village when he met up with someone on the road, and he pretended to have to move his bowels. He called to the Ayoré man he met along the way, already planning to kill him. ( I don’t know the Ayoré man’s name.) They were both on the road. And*Dachagaidé*said: “Come here. Let’s listen to each other’s talk.”

ayoreo: Uté uje ayoré chujé jé tibáguirási nero mu chóna ore yui uje*Dachagaidé*chujé. Chi acoté chiraja. E jiei dirode ga chiraja. Chi chajire guedé aja jnumone jeta di abai, mu que di. Ore chujé gu. Mu jnacarode qué ore déjibujai, dasoránique. Que yimo jeta guedosíode gadioc, jeta sequeré chojmára jeta ore chiraja. Diyajmácho gu. Chi ore daté po uje ore di ga ore chiraja uje*Dachagaidé*jõcaratáguebéi u uje chujé dai. Jnacarode ore di ijibujaiga ore chiraja uje*Dachagaidé*jõcaratáguebei u uje chujé dai.
english: But after calling to him, he quickly killed him.*Dachagaidé*was really evil and was always killing people.

ayoreo: Jnacarode ore di ijibujaidasorane ga ore chucué dai. Mu ore chopise dai casica uje ore abujadie yorícojna uje umare. Ore peropíse dai. Ore daté chojninga: “Aguitóyo. Yijnájoi uacai casé.*Dachagaidé*jõcaratáguebei a chujé.”
english: He quickly buried his victim, to hide what he had done. He put sticks over the top of the grave to make it look less like a grave.

ayoreo: Chi que ore mo iji dejai go, jnacarode. Ore jnusi dai uje ore jnusina gu. Ujnacari urẽjai chojningame ujnacari ugarómi: “Abágui ta ore idai uñai. Yibágui ta ore idáyode, yocai casé qui. A uñeque chujé,” chojninga.
english: The man that*Dachagaidé*had killed was on his way to visit his sister, but he never got there, because*Dachagaidé*killed him.

ayoreo: Chi ore jno. Uñai chicai ti. Uñai chicai te. Ore chajuque uñane dajei ome ore idáyode.
english: The Ayoré man’s wife eventually knew something must have happened. When many days went by and he didn’t return, she was sure something had happened. She would watch the sun every day until it went down, thinking surely he would show up, but he never came. That’s because he had been killed.

ayoreo: Mu*Dachagaidé*yui tu uje acotei cus.
english: But his sons kept searching, going on trips to try to find him. I don’t know if it was for three months or a year before they found out, because they kept it up for a long time. They say that their mother cried when they returned and told her that the killer*Dachagaidé*had probably killed their father. Their mother cried when they came back and told her that they had come to the conclusion that the assassin*Dachagaidé*had killed their father.

ayoreo: *(Manuélné*cho quique*:*Acotei ujnacarode. Jeta acotei mu jnani bajade ore chojningame jnacarode “yacotei.” Cho ujetiga ore abai ore.*Étacõre*ore acotéode*Dosapéode*. Que ore iguiose mu ore cho dacotéji ore uje que ore chuseque dacotei ore gu. Uje*Samáné*chojningame*Alíciade*“Yacotei.”*Étacõre Samáné*,*Dosapé Alíciadé*.*Chiquenoi*yu ga*Posorajane*yocacotéode.)
english: The sons came back from one of their trips and started looking for their father. They were both so much like their father who had been killed – they had long beards just like his.

ayoreo: Ujnacari urẽjai chise ta ore idai ujaminóri. E chucué to dacotei mu e chijno uje chirota dacotei. Ga chijno poridie jeta dajei uje ore chaquesu, chaquesu uje ore chucué cuchade.
english: Their mother said: “Hurry up. We must look for your father. Surely that big killer*Dachagaidé*has killed him.”

ayoreo: Mu e chacáji ti ore idai ejoi. Ore chase jnumi ome dai ode. Que ore jno dádi. “A yiji dirome jeta ca yise yocai casé ajnoquetígo,” chojninga. Chi obiréjna áyipie. Ore pésu dáyipie ome ñane. Chicai ti mu tõrai dajei. Chicai sona. Chicai uéchai, uéchai. Chimáni dajei iji ti. Chaquesu, chaquesu, chaquesu dajei. Chajire jnumone mu íjnoc.
english: The sons couldn’t sleep that night. They missed their father and kept thinking about him.

ayoreo: ¡Chi chise ode! Chajire mu jnup umare aja.
english: The oldest son said to the youngest: “You go to one of the villages. I’ll go to the other villages where our father was going when he left us. Surely someone has killed him,” that’s what he said to his younger brother.

ayoreo: “A yocai casé ode a déji sañec,” chi chojninga, “Ueradi udaque jnup umare aja. A chijóchame de gu.*Dachagaidé*ajéode u, a ca piquiya mu chise. Idógosi dajei,” chojninga, “A u.”
english: And so they left. One went in one direction and the other in another. They split up to go to all the towns.

ayoreo: Quĩrique chuga enga paráta enga jé u. Chejnapise ejode jnumi taja ajei. Iyoi isipídode. Jno. Que chijocase. E tibágui*Dachagaidé*.
english: But*Dachagaidé*’s victim had an**acotéi.*

ayoreo: Di uje guedé e jno. Chi ore chojninga: “¡E di yé!”
english: (Manuélné explained what an*acotei*was:*Acoteode*are young men. When there is an*acotei*, the men of old would say to him, “you are my wife.” And they were like husbands to these young men. The*male wives*of men of the Étacori Clan are chosen from the Dosapẽ  Clan. They must choose from young men who are not relatives, and they must treat them very well.*Samáné*says to*Aliciadé,*“you are my wife.”*Samané*is of the*Étacori Clan,*and*Alíciadé*is from the*Dosapé Clan*. I am from the*Chiquenoi Clan*, and my*male wives*are chosen from the*Posorajãi Clan.*)

ayoreo: Dajoguenie ore chojninga: “Boji te. Acai de e. Yangácoi bojñáringo.”
english: The oldest son reached a village that was in the middle of the other villages. He looked for his ‘*acotei’ (**male wife)*to help him search. He chopped down jungle growth to protect his*acotei*. They chopped and chopped and chopped, and searched as the were chopping. They were nearing the village. And they searched the area for his father’s grave. But they didn’t find anything.

ayoreo: Tibágui ore. Ore chãrei cuguei edodie mu que tagu go uje gusu ájningarãc uje chise dai cachodi. Ájningarãque ga que tagu ãraqueguedie. Jé jogate iji ejode uje jno. Que pota jeta tac. Chejna deréjnai. Que taque go. Gusu ájningarãc iji ajei. Chojningame dajogue: “¿Átaja yocai casé jeta chise de ica ya? Yucuei tu.”
english: The oldest son said, “I’ll leave tomorrow if I don’t find my father’s bones,” this is what the son said to his*acotei*.

ayoreo: Chojninga: “Chise de ica arĩa. Mu chicapo uáguiji ta ica.”
english: After they talked together about what they would do, their plan became clear to them. They’d go off in one direction, but then keep returning to the road. They would search on both sides of the road. And they kept doing this, following the road as far as it went, always chopping down the overgrown jungle on the road. The son looked all over the ground, but nothing.

ayoreo: Dajogue uña chojninga: “Yimo*Dachagaidé*. Chijína aja dajei ica. A chujé.”
english: And then, it’s said, he found the grave. He looked and there was a mound of dirt over it.

ayoreo: Chi ca chatata go,*Dachagaidé*. Que chatata dei go. Chi ore po, dajoguenie, dai ejode. Ijoguenie chi ore po ejode.
english: The oldest son said: “Probably*Dachagaidé*wanted us to think the grave was somewhere else.” He said, “This place is too neat with it’s mound. This is probably where he buried him, that’s why he worked so hard to camouflage it.*Dachagaidé*thought he’d found a place that wouldn’t be discovered. And it’s right here by the road,” he said, “Surely this is it.”

ayoreo: Mu chojninga: “¡Aya! ¡Aya! Ca abóyo.”
english: He gently began to dig and found the earth was still soft underneath. He took the dirt out of the hole and piled it at the side.  He dug down till he discovered where the blood had collected. And then he took off. He didn’t look any further. He went to find*Dachagaidé*.

ayoreo: Mu que chatata gotic. Chise cachodi mu que chatata go. Ájningarãque gu. Chi chojningame dajoguenie: “¿*Dachagaidé*iguíjnai déji rique? Qué yajire to,” chojninga.
english: He got there at sundown. They say the people called out: “Someone’s coming!”

ayoreo: “De uaiti mu ca abágui to. A chujé ua to,” chojninga.
english: His aunts said: “Come here. Come over here. We want to hear what you have to say.”

ayoreo: Chojninga: “Yibágui jne.” Ga tibágui. Chi guedé e jnosi.
english: He went to them. They had cooked beans, but he would not eat, because he was so angry from finding where his father had died. He was angry and would not eat the food they’d cooked. He stayed with them a little while, then left. He didn’t want to eat that whole day. He didn’t eat a thing. He was all torn up inside from anger. He asked his aunt: “Did you help my father when he arrived here a while back? I’m looking for him.”

ayoreo: Chi chojningame: “*Dachagaidé*, e yibiágui ua gu Dijnai pijnane. Chi ajína yu. Ajnoque guebéode. Ajnoque ijnoséode.”
english: She said: “He did come here a while back. But he said he was going back to you when he left.”

ayoreo: Chi*Dachagaidé*chisi ome dacoté ãraquegue ome, mu que tagu go. Ájningarãc. Chojninga: “Que yagu bacoté ãraqueguei go. Yaguinérupúsu dajei gu. Jé yáyipie jeta yĩriapo rĩ iji*Dijnai*.”
english: Another aunt said: “I saw*Dachagaidé*.  He went after him on the road. He probably killed him.*Dachagaidé*didn’t tell anybody anything. He didn’t mention his victim at all.”

ayoreo: *Dachagaidé*chojninga: “Que yijína ua. Yajna ua dirome uje guedé jno.”
english: But his aunts started crying*his father’s nearness.*They cried for his father.

ayoreo: Jnacari chojninga: “Aguinéru. Ta pijnani to.”
english: But the oldest son said: “Stop! Stop! Don’t cry.”

ayoreo: Chojninga: “Yicai dirome iji durasé.”
english: But he didn’t tell them anything. He had found the grave, but he didn’t tell anyone. That’s because he was very angry. He said to his aunts: “Where is*Dachagaidé*’s house? I want to see him.” That’s what he asked them.

ayoreo: Mu que chiraja dasocáringome to uje que pota doi. Jé moji to, mu que taque gotique. Diri uñai iji direjnímei, jé dejaque mu dajnoguenie ore chãrei cuguéode. Ore tṍra dãraquegué bajedie, mu que tagu go. Jnusi dayé ga que tagu. Qué durasape mu catecã́ri*Dachagaidé*ga chojninga: “Óji gaté. Yico,” chojninga.
english: They said, “It’s over there but don’t go over there. He’ll kill you, too,” they said.

ayoreo: Mu chi chojninga: “Ajnasegabi uáji te. Qué yaque,” chojninga.
english: He said: “I am going over there.” And he went to see him. The sun had just gone down.

ayoreo: Mu que taque to ujnacari. Mu e chimate dagúchade ga e ore jno.
english: When he got to his house he said to him: “*Dachagaidé*, I’ve come to you because*Dijnai*is calling for you. He wants you to come with me. He wants you to bring some iron and axes.”

ayoreo: Mu dajnoguenie e ore chiraja. Ore chojninga: “Ayáji quigade. Ca ayáji iquei.” Ore todopísa jeta chujé.
english: *Dachagaidé*gave him some of the food his wife had cooked, but he wouldn’t eat it. He was angry.

ayoreo: *Manuelné – Tobité, Bolivia – 7 de diciembre 1964.*
english: He said: “I can’t eat what your wife has prepared. I’m in a big hurry to get going. I’m thinking about getting back to*Dijnai*.”

ayoreo: *Joyce Davis Buchegger churúsãre Manuelné atatagode udojo nanique.*
english: *Dachagaidé*said: “I’m not going with you right now. I’ll follow you tomorrow when the sun goes down.”

ayoreo: Dibe chi chuchẽ́re oyídode gai. Chuchẽ́re gai uje ore pógu nanique. Jec ũré uásejna oyídode iji erámi uje chuchẽ́re gai. Yodi déji cucarani ajei. A cuchárique suru gai mu dibé chuchẽ́re gai ga e déji erámone jnése oyídode. Que pijoque jurugaisṍri. Cucha pijogarabia dibedie ome jnani bajade.
english: Fox, it’s said, let the fish go free. Truly there were many fish in the world, but they had all been hidden before Fox let them out. There was a mountain with much water inside and someone had shut it up so the water couldn’t get out, but Fox opened it up and when the water poured out upon the earth, all the fish came out with it. No one knows who shut it in. According to the men who lived long ago, the fox were wise and could reveal the things they discovered.

ayoreo: Ujode cuchiso ore chujérase dibé mu chayome ore. Ore chucuésosi iji cucoi choqui ójo. Ore tṍrasi aja erámi. Ore tṍrasi aja da te. Ore cho dajuyame gu mu ore chiquetásiapo rĩ.
english: When the other animals tried to kill Fox, it ran from them. They looked for dear Fox at the top of a large bottle tree. When they found Fox, they threw him down into the jungle below. They threw him far from the tree. They wanted to punish Fox and that is why they threw him down, but later, they brought dear Fox back to life again.

ayoreo: Uñeque chutĩ́raja jnumi ga utirane cho enuéone. ¡Cho quẽ́eeeera! ga ecásiapo rĩ iji jnumi, dibé. Que yiraja jeta poji nanique. A poji. Chiqueta dibé ome dajñone. Ore chãrángome ñane ajñoi jeta uñeque gajneque isocai to.
english: One of them spit on the ground, and his spit was like ropes. He cleared his throat in order to spit, and it sounded like this: ¡quẽ́eeera! And when he did this, Fox rose from the ground alive again. I don’t remember, but I think it was Lizard who brought Fox back to life. Yes, I’m sure of it, it was Lizard. He brought Fox back to life with his saliva. Those who have this kind of power forbid their saliva to others to prevent others from doing what they can do.

ayoreo: Jiei jnani bajade ore atuatigade, ore josipiedie. Cucha irajagónic atuatigade.
english: In many ways the men of old were crafty, deceitful, and abusive. Their wiles were clever.

ayoreo: *Manuélné – Tobité, Bolivia – 1960.*
english: *Manuelne – Tobité, Bolivia – 1965.*

ayoreo: *Joyce Davis Buchegger churúsãre Manuélné atatagode udojo nanique.*
english: *Transcribed by: Joyce Davis Buchegger.*

ayoreo: Chi abai neróraque dajai yuque nero abai uje i tu*Dachapiboraté*. Ga*Dachapiboraté*acoté dajai chojninga: “A yiji jne, yibágui ina ore jne.”
english: A man named **Dachapiboraté*was killed by his sister’s husband.

ayoreo: Chi guei ca tagu uté ore jutícabia to. Ga jno ga tibágui nero, mu chi chise nero abai ujuya gajnei gase ga chi chojninga: “Ujuya yoc iji yoquidai. Uje guérique que tagu yoc!”
english: *Dachapiboraté*’s wife’s brother said: “I’m going to go to see my sister and her family.”

ayoreo: Ga chise nero ga*Dachapiboraté*chojninga: “Bajaté u é.” Ga jé u uje chojninga: “Be dutué ome bajaté.” Mu chi chojninga: “Ca be dutué. Be doridie uje yipese babosíningo.”
english: They say the rain wouldn’t ‘bite’ their garden. So he left and went to his sister’s village, but it’s said his sister’s husband had a forked stick that is used for witchcraft called **gase*.

ayoreo: Ga yusṍri jno ga doi darásade ore, mu chi pógu re ga tṍraji daguíjnai uéchai. Ga chi abode chi chojninga: “Codé a, iapade u. Déji daguíjnai ajei.”
english: When he arrived he said: “We are not doing well in our town for the rain won’t ‘bite’ us.”

ayoreo: Mu ore codé chojningame disi: “¿Ama bayé ga pógu re ome yoque?”
english: And when he reached his sister’s place*Dachapiboraté*said: “Your brother’s here!” And then he said: “Bring some squash for your brother.”

ayoreo: Jé u uje chijnime dajocaningai. Chi chojninga: “A yujé daijnai jne uje tagu dajuticabia.”
english: But her brother said: “No, don’t bring squash, bring the fruit called**doridie*that you prepare and I will suck the mealy part that comes off it’s leaves.”

ayoreo: Chi ore doi ga chi chojninga: “A yibore ore ijnai ome jne, ga yisa iji gai jne.”
english: And then the killer left and went to see his countrymen. But it’s said, he hid himself and came out on the other side of his house.

ayoreo: Jé u uje chijna dorijnai, ga jé u uje chisa ga ore chujé iji jmainie. Chi ore jno mu chi ore dorási*Dachapiboraté*ipesudi erãpijóningai ajei. Ga ayoré áyipierãi chojninga: “Beyoi ãrei ga yimóngo jeta chejna yoque jne.”
english: His children saw him, and they said to their grandmother: “Grandma, it’s father. He’s inside the house.”

ayoreo: Ga*Dachapiboraté*chajna ore ga usereréjna chajna ore. Mu chise ga chajníji ipesudi erãpijoningai eruei.
english: But their grandmother asked the boy: “Is your father hiding from us?”

ayoreo: *Oidabiadé – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988.*
english: He was inside the house with his companions, planning how to kill*Dachapiboraté*. He said: “I’ll kill that witch doctor*Dachapiboraté*who eats from his garden.”

ayoreo: *Marina de**Morarie churúsãre Oidábiadé atatagode udojo.*
english: They say they talked and he told them how he would do it: “I’ll light a pipe for him, and grab him while he’s smoking.”

ayoreo: Chi disiode ore dayode baque. Ga ore abode chiposásu Gajnoque. Ga ore chipese datedie ajerabidie. Ga disiode ore chipese dayode gapudode iji. Ga chi Gajnoque Quedéjna chetaque ore ga chejna ore.
english: *Gajnoque Quedéjna*

ayoreo: Chejnápise ore ga uecai chojmára. Chi chayo ga chi choru chúrumei. Ga chojninga: “Udaque que déji tiéraque.” Ga chi moji chúrumei ujade. Chi dire ga jno.
english: It’s said the children’s fathers were hunting for honey. And their children were playing with the dirt of an Ant Hill. They were sucking their mother’s genitals. The boys were sucking their father’s genitals. And it’s said the Strange Ant Hill disapproved of them and finished them off.

ayoreo: Ga chise jõrábejnane ore idaidatéode. Chamacoco u. Chi cṍraji ore ajárojna. Ga chi ore nóna aja jnumi.
english: It killed all of them and only one was left alive.

ayoreo: Ore chojninga: “¿Cuchárique guejnaque báningo?” Ga chojninga: “Gajnoque Quedéjna chejna yayode ore.”
english: They say he ran off and climbed a **chúrume*i tree. (A small tree with thorns.) And he said: “This kind of tree (with thorns) is not found by the river.” They say he slept in the branches of the ‘*chúrumei’*tree all night long. At daylight he left.

ayoreo: Ore chojninga: “¿Bayode guejnato isocárigo cho rique? Chi chojninga: “Yayode guejnato a di jne,  ga bajé usereréjna aja iquei, úyujna cuchábe bajé aja iquei. Mu chi bajaque jnacode, chugúpẽre yote dequejnangórone aja iquei jne. A di jne.”
english: He ran till he reached the large village of the **Chamacoco tribe*who are friends of the Ayoré. It’s said he fell down when he got to their village square. And it’s said they all got down on the ground (where he’d fallen).

ayoreo: Ga chi ore pésu dajuyaquei iji poridie ga chaparu ore gai. Ga ore chijnoja iquei ome ore inamanie. Ga chejna yijmanai ore inamanie.
english: They asked him: “Has something happened to your parents?”

ayoreo: Ga ore chudute usererejna ga ore chojninga: “Ãcho inama.”
english: And he answered: “The Strange Ant Hill has finished our parents off and all of them are dead.”

ayoreo: Chi qué sué gai iji taningai.
english: They asked: “The one who finished off your parents, what did she do?”

ayoreo: Chi chojninga: “Yayode guejnato Gajnoque Quedéjna uaté ga e di.”
english: This is what he answered: “The one who finished off my parents it going to come, but long before she gets here you will hear the eerie sound of her approaching, and a great wind will arrive. Ducks and other water birds will also come first. All of this will happen first.”

ayoreo: Ga chi ore chachẽre ga ore chãru inamanie chojmarai. Ore chachẽrapo ga gare rĩ mu yodi qué sué gai ga que chajni. Ore chachẽrapo gadiogué ga que chajniapo rĩ. Ga gagánia ga yodi e chapu re iji jmainie. Udaque, cinco, ga chajnipise. Tiedie íjnonguiji erámone ga inoningayato u Gajnoque chagúi adode.
english: And, they say the people made a barricade of trees to surround them. And they dug trenches in front of them. And there were five barricades.

ayoreo: Chiese ore apose gatéode ga ca guérique tagu ayorérique.
english: And then they heard the eerie sound approaching and they said: “¡Set a barricade on fire!”

ayoreo: Ga que ore chatata.
english: Water kept coming, and wasn’t stopped.

ayoreo: *Oidábiadé – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988.*
english: And the boy said: “The Strange Ant Hill that finished off our parents has arrived.”

ayoreo: *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Oidábiadé atatagode udojo nanique.*
english: They began to defend themselves and lit the first barricade.

ayoreo: Chi yocai jnani bajátique chi baque. Chi chudute guei adode. Chi doi guei adode. Chi chudute adode mu chi todo ga chácãre darani tome. Jé todo guei basui adode gu.
english: They say that one of our forefathers was honey-hunting. He heard the rain. He started toward the rain. He heard it, but they say he was afraid, so he stayed where he was. That’s because he feared what Falling Rain might do.

ayoreo: Chi chimochẽre gatoi gai ga chi chorei damochecáya iji údi. Chi chojninga: “Que bayé udac.” Chorei damochecáya iji údi.
english: He shook his head and tucked his pony tail under. [Pony tail under is a sign of not being afraid.] And he said: “This is not a flood.” And he continued to tuck his pony tail under.

ayoreo: Jé u uje chise Guei Basui. Chi déji dachẽra pore údi. Chiese nóna dachẽra aja jnumi. Ga chi nóna aja degúi. Ga chi nóna aja dabatigade. Ga chi chijno cutérone ga ãré chejna. Chi chojninga: “¡*Jiiiii!*¡Yuyátique di iji yu iiii!”
english: And then he reached Falling Rain. Falling Rain was under the tree that he had struck with lightning. They say he came down to earth on a bolt of lightning.

ayoreo: Chi Guei Basui jno ga chijnásia chicorámi ababi. Chijócha mu uyádi suési gai.
english: And Falling Rain accompanied our forefather to the camp.

ayoreo: Mu chi jnani bajai chajire iji jnumi.
english: Later, he also went with him into the jungle for honey.  And they got thirsty. They say it was the sun that got to him and made him thirsty while he was chopping into tree. He said: “¡Heeeee! I’m thirsty ¡heeeee!”

ayoreo: Chijose dacajñui aja bajo ga irágu mu jotábia déji gai.
english: Falling Rain took off and brought back a “baby” water plant. He dug for it but his thirst overcame him too.

ayoreo: Chi jnani bajai chojninga: “¡Yijína cuchábi!”
english: But the man from long ago looked down from the tree and saw what he did.

ayoreo: Chi di ji degúi, ga chojningame dacoté: “A yijína cuchátique.” Chiraja uje jotábia dési ijoyade gai te.
english: To get enough water Falling Rain was wringing the hair that grows right by his ear into his gourd cup and it filled with water, but there was a river plant floating on top.

ayoreo: Mu chi jnani bajai cho paraque ga noñome Guei Basui iji degúi.
english: The man who lived long ago said: “I’m hanging out with a supernatural person!”

ayoreo: Chojningame darásade: “Yo paraque. Yijíñome iji degúi uje uacagachodi u jne.”
english: They arrived back in the camp, and he said to his wife: “Probably I’m accompanying something special.” He knew there was a tiny water plant on top of the water that Falling Rain had wrung from his hair, but he didn’t know where it could have come from.

ayoreo: Mu chi Guei Basui pésu daguíjnai. Mu chi disiode chimitingare. Ga ore chojninga: “E ajnorate ji. Ga chiese ajnorápiedie tṍraji uráidie.”
english: But the man made a mistake and let Falling Rain stay in the camp the next time he went out hunting.

ayoreo: Mu chi chimate daguíjnai ga chécãre dameserane aja ajei. Chécãre ore aja guiguíjnai ajei, ga jé u uje soca rome ore iji guesi. Tṍra dameserane aja guijnatajei; jé tṍra detégode, damitingarangórone aja guesi.
english: The man said to his companions: “I think I have made a big mistake. I left Falling Rain in the camp, and he might try to harm you, and that would be the end of you.”

ayoreo: Ga noña ga jmi que’. Ga chiese jnanione u jmíngone, ga chequedíe u jongogonie; chiese disiode, disidíe u paparanie, parátagabidie su guiáminie.
english: Back in the camp Falling Rain made himself a house. But the children started throwing things at him and taunting him. They said: “Oh, my, he’s got lightning! And they claim that lightning comes from his armpits.”

ayoreo: Tṍra ore ga yote ujósóño u. Ga ore poji yodi. Jé u uje cho jnare ga Guei Basui jno ga nóna aja gaté. Chejna ore. Yirajapídode eruei jé u.
english: When he finished building his house, Falling Rain moved his chosen ones into it. When he got them inside his house, he turned against those on the outside. Those he chose he shoved inside the house.

ayoreo: *Oidábiadé – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988.*
english: But he pushed out those he hated, those who threw things at him. And immediately they were changed into frogs. Men became frogs. Women became toads; boys and girls changed into various types of tiny frogs and toads, even the babies. Those he threw out became water dwellers. And they cried in the water.

ayoreo: *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Oidábiadé atatagode udojo nanique.*
english: And then there was heat lightning, and Falling Rain accompanied it back up into the sky. This was after he had finished them off. This is the end of the part of the story I know.

ayoreo: *Jnaguto*u uje tãre ome iji dati enga chiese ijárogome jnui ore.
english: The dragonfly (*J*naguto*) stamped its foot long ago, and when he did, he stepped on some*ají*and ground it into powder*,*and after that people ground the hot peppers into a powder.

ayoreo: Enga chi ayoré dacasutedie ore garani udé. Enga chi jnu ãri garanome ore úringane dacasuté ome ore nanique uje ore tagu.
english: Warriors come about from eating ground*ají*. And it’s said, the very first warriors originated long ago from eating these hot peppers.

ayoreo: Enga chi*Jnaguto*chimáta cucha ponáyone –cuchisode, ayoréode ore jnese- enga chi chojninga: “Beyoi yijaroi udé jnu ãri ga acho.”
english: *Jnaguto*gathered everything together –both animals and people. We’re told he called to the animals and people and he said to them: “Come, get what I’ve ground up, the*ají*, and eat the powder.”

ayoreo: Enga*Oroné*dayé*Jaboidé*chatata quigode ome yoque ga chósi te: “Ujode cuchape ore tagu. Ga chi ore tagu ga ujnuéngáyone u uje ore chijna dajéode aja guesode. Enga jé u uje ayoré dacasutedie uñanie chijna dajéode aja guesode nanique iji daruguringane, dajócaningáne (uje ore chugúji, enga ájningarãc ore).”
english: **Oroné’s*father, **Jaboidé,*told us all about this; he said: “Important men, like myself, eat ground*ají*to become warriors.”

ayoreo: Ga u udojo: Chiese ayoré asutedie ipesungóri tu udé, jnu ãri udé. Ujnuéngáyone chi ca uñane chijna dajéode aja guesode. Chi ca chijnime uñane arĩ́rique. Chi jé ujnéngáyone u uje udore ore tãraráji jmainie.
english: What happened to a person after he ate ground*ají*showed whether he would become a warrior or not. The ones who felt its sting and couldn’t suck their breath in and out after ingesting it, would not become warriors, for it was plain that they could easily be defeated.

ayoreo: Ga chi bajai tu*Ugobedai*uje choru poridie iji daté. Ore tagu jnu ãri iji taningai ga chinoningase ajei ga ajei gatíjna dosadode, ga pose bajaque ijaroi, mu que yirajapise quigode ome*Jaboidé*.
english: The ones who can be easily defeated are those who are not able to breath in and out after eating*ají*. Men like that, it’s said, won’t be victorious in battle. Those who can be defeated are those that tremble when they eat ground hot peppers.

ayoreo: Ga chi quigaminóri tu*Arori*iji datí uje basája poridie enga chi pose bajaque to. Yocáquiode chojninga: “Jé uacamóño*Arori*u uje cho dájninga mu que chiraja tijórique (que todo cuchárique).
english: And it’s said, the first one who ate hot peppers was **Ugóbedái,*a small lizard that climbs in the trees. In the beginning*Ugóbedái*ate ground hot peppers and was transformed by it and his insides became red.  He ate the ground peppers first, but I don’t know very well how the rest of the story goes that*Jaboidé*told about him.

ayoreo: Enga chi jé u uje ayoréode tagu to ga ujnuéngaisórone u uje ore chijna dajéode aja guesode. Jé uñane ga ore sué gai ga que ore chijna dajéode aja guesode to, que chijnime ore arĩ́rique. Udojo uje chi ayoré dacasutedie posode u nanique iji jnani bajade ore.
english: And it’s said, the next to eat ground hot peppers was**Arori,*the eagle that lights in the trees; he was one of the first to eat it, too, they say. Our grandfathers would say: “You know that it was*Arori*who first got angry and wasn’t afraid of anything.”

ayoreo: *(Oidábiadé*cho quique: Mu que ore chiraja nanique uje a Satanas utocaidode u nanique, uje ore chiroque yocáquiode ore aja dáringaringo pocaningai gaisóño.)
english: But some, even thought they didn’t breath in and out after eating*ají*, proved to be greater warriors than some who did suck their breath in and out, because when they went into battle their enemies couldn’t overcome them.

ayoreo: Uje yocáquiode ore chatatapo uñeque rĩ uje chi ayoré dacasutedie gajnenie jé u. Chopise ayoré dacasutedie poti jnu ãri adode rĩ. Uje chi ipesudé ayoré dacasutedie nanique jnu ãri.
english: (Oidábiadé said: I would like to make this comment, our forefathers did not yet know long ago about the influence of Satan’s demons, or that Satan is the author of killing.)

ayoreo: Uje pésu ore ájningane iji ore ajei uje chi ore chujé jnani bajade ore iñoñai, ome*Jaboidé*. Jé u uje dacasutedie cucherane chi ore ipesungóri tu ore iñoñai (*Poritai*). Chi dacasutedie ore ipesungóri udé, ome*Jaboidé*.
english: There are other things we’ve been told that pertain to Ayoré warriors.

ayoreo: Chi ore chujé ga chi Chiquenone ore chujé bajaque. Chi ore chijna dajéode aja guesi tiji gai. Ga chi ayoré dacasutedie ipesungórone u udojo, ome*Jaboidé*. Chiquenone chi ore chijna dajéode aja guesi ga ore cho je-je-je iji gai.
english: Once long ago, the Ayoreos ingested red hot peppers, and it made them so anger inside  that they ended up killing a very ugly official, according to*Jaboidé*.

ayoreo: Quigade ga Dosapéode ore chujé ga jé isoca ga ore chijna dajéode aja guesi.
english: (Malformed and ugly people are referred to as *P*oritai*in the Ayoré language. So, that is the name we will use throughout this story for the one that was killed by the Ayoreos.)

ayoreo: Quigade ga Posorajane ore chujé to, mu chi ca ore chijna dajéode aja guesi to.
english: *Poritai*, the very ugly official, was the maker of the warrior clans. He also made names for the clans (**cucherane).*He was an evil spirit referred to in Ayoré as*dicoré (walking spirit).*

ayoreo: Chi iñoñai ore sué iñoñai gai gu.
english: It was*Jaboidé*who told us that*Poritai*was the one who made warriors.

ayoreo: Quigade ga Picanerane ore chujé, mu jé ore cho, chi ca ore chijna dajéode aja guesi to.
english: It’s said that when they killed*Poritai*, it was the*Chiquenone Clan*who hit him first. They say, they sucked their breath in and out as they delivered their blows.

ayoreo: Quigade ga Étacorone ore chujé mu ore chijna dajéode aja guesi tiji gai.
english: When*Poritai*groaned and groaned as he was being beaten, his groans entered the men who were hitting him, and they became warriors. And  Ayoré warriors were made in this way, according to*Jaboidé*.

ayoreo: Enga chojninga,*Jaboidé*: “Jé uacamóño to uje posito (pijosito) ayoréode yoquiquicujáidie iji erámone. Uje ayoréode yijosínango yocurásade aja quedéjnane nanique ga quigade u udojo uje chi ayoré dacasuté pijositodie chi quicujaidie u udiji iji jnani bajade ore.”
english: Those who didn’t fear*Poritai*(who appeared as a phantom), and had the courage to hit him, they became **dacasuté*(brave warrior leaders).

ayoreo: Uje ore chosina dojode ayoré iji erámone nanique ga ore chayome ore uje ore chiraja gu uje sijnaque ayoréode.
english: The*Chiquenone Clan*, sucked their breath in and out, and chanted ¡*hay-hay-hay!*as they were hitting*Poritai*.

ayoreo: Ga*Jaboidé*chojninga: “Jé uacamóño Picanerane ore to uje ore chosina dojode ayoré iji erámi. Uje ore ujode ayoré chayóna erámi tome ore.
english: The*Dosapéode Clan*also hit him, and like the*Chiquenone Clan*, they sucked their breath in and out.

ayoreo: Juruminone chi jé cho ore. Juruminone ujatigui chi ore chujé mu jé cho. Que ore chijna dajéningai aja guetique. Enga chi pijositodie upúsu udiji iji ore quicujáidie. (Positodie jeoga Pijositodie). Uje que sujmáningárique chajire re ome ore nanique ga ore chosina dojode ayoré iji erámi.”
english: The*Posorajane**Clan*hit him, also, but we’re told they didn’t suck their breath in and out. It’s said, they were so much greater than the very ugly official, that there was no need to suck their breath in and out.

ayoreo: “Uacamóño to,” chojninga, “uje ayoré dacasutedie pijositodie u udiji ore.
english: The*Picanerane Clan*hit him, and like the*Posorajane Clan*, they didn’t suck their breath in and out.

ayoreo: *Posorajane, Picanerane, Juruminone*ore u uje que picasidé ore nanique ga chi u udojo,” ome*Jaboidé*nanique.”
english: The*Étacorone Clan*hit him, but they did suck their breath in and out as they did it.

ayoreo: Ayoré dacasutedie chi ore quigode u udojo uje ayoré dacasuté positodie u uje ore chujé ¡paque! iñoñai*Poritai*uté, mu que chijnime arĩ́rique uje que chijna dajéode aja guesi.
english: And*Jaboidé*said: “Now you’ve all heard that our forefathers were people who could push their enemies back.”

ayoreo: Chi ayoré dacasutedie u udojo, ome*Jaboidé*.
english: For a long time we Ayoreos have been know as people who have chased after other groups of Ayoreos through the jungle.  And it’s said that Ayoré warriors who can push their enemies back are true descendants of those long ago men who first became*dacasuté*(warrior leaders).

ayoreo: Jé u udojo eruei tu, enga que yiraja iqueminóningo.
english: They say that a warrior who stays calm during battle is superior to those who work themselves up into a frenzy.

ayoreo: *Oidábiadé – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988.*
english: Ayoreos who were pursued through the jungle by other groups of Ayoreos, knowing how fierce the Ayoré were, would run from their pursuers.

ayoreo: *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Oidábiadé atatagode udojo nanique.*
english: And*Jaboidé*said: “Now you know that the*Picanerane Clan*were those who often  chased their fellow Ayoreos through the jungles. And the*Juruminone Clan*were like the*Picanerane Clan*and pursued other groups of Ayoreos.

ayoreo: Jnanione ore chimáta ñane iji siqueredie ga jnani chojmarai chacacãre darásade.  Enga acacãrátigade u datatagode udojo ome siqueredie adode.
english: *Siqueré Adode*

ayoreo: Chi Dosapeque*caratai*.
english: The men would gather in the rainy season and, when they did, one of the men would bless the people. He would say, The blessing is the telling of this story:

ayoreo: Dosapeque*cúcujna*. Dosapeque*cucurui*. Dosapeque*tachei*.
english: *They say the jaguar is of the Dosapei clan.**The gopher is of the Dosapei clan. The smaller gopher is of the Dosapei clan. The *rabbit-like animal called ‘jochi’  is of the Dosapei clan.*

ayoreo: Ga chi siqueré díji ore, ga chi ore chudute siqueré usereréjna. Ore chayo ga*caratai*chi chojninga: “A yiyame gotique.”
english: *It’s said, ‘the rainy season’ arrived for them, and they heard the sound of something approaching. They started to run and the jaguar spoke and said: “I’m going to stand up to whatever it is that is coming toward us.”*

ayoreo: Ga*cucujna*chojninga: “A yijína yajábi ga yipatíorango ñane ome gotique.”
english: *The gopher said: “I’ll stay with my dear brother the jaguar and whatever that thing does will happen to us both.”*

ayoreo: Ga chi*cucurábi*patíorã datigate*caratai*. Ga*tachei*chojninga: “Yipatíorã yitigate*caratai*.”
english: *The little gopher stayed with his brother the jaguar for whatever would happen. And the rabbit-like animal said: “I will perish with my big brother the jaguar if it comes to that.”*

ayoreo: Ore nero u*cucujna*. Ga ore chayo ga bajé aja ore iquei. Ga chi siqueré di iji ore. Chi bajaque duchúbia aja ore iquei ga*caratai*chisa ga chijna ga chojninga: “¿Cucha pibose gotique u uné?”
english: *Their sister was a gopher. And she ran ahead of them. And ‘the rainy season’ caught up with them. The first thing to appear in front of them was squash and the jaguar took it and said: “What kind of food it this?”*

ayoreo: Chi jno enga*cucurábi*chachẽre dutué ga chijna ga chojninga: “Yagu daque jne.”
english: *They went on running from ‘the rainy season’ and the little gopher grabbed squash and took it and said: “I’m going to eat this.”*

ayoreo: *Cucujna*chachẽre mimiré ga chijna ga chojninga: “Yajné nac, a yagu jne.”
english: *The big gopher grabbed watermelon and took it and said: “This is mine, I’ll eat it.”*

ayoreo: *Tachei*chachẽre guejna ga chijna ga chojninga: “Yagu jne.”
english: *The rabbit-like animal grabbed corn and took it and said: “I’ll eat this.”*

ayoreo: *Caratai*chachẽre cuguéode ga chijna ga chojninga: “Yijna ga yagu jne.”
english: *The jaguar grabbed beans and took them and said: “I’ll take them and eatthem.”*

ayoreo: Ga Dosapeque ueque siqueré imaningórone ga jé ore edópasa.
english: It was just the Dosapéode clan members that took the things that ‘the rainy season’ produced, and that is why the rainy season crops are the ‘edopasade’ [clan possessions] of the Dosapei Clan.

ayoreo: Chi ore chiqueta ejodie, ga siqueré diriringai userejna aja jnumi, ga ore doi ga ore chécãraja edo, dutuedie.
english: It’s said they made a fire in the cooking pit, and the things that were brought by the sound of the rainy season were transferred into the hole,*squashes*and all the rest. And that is how they were cooked.

ayoreo: *Oidábiadé – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988.*
english: *Oidabiade – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988.*

ayoreo: *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Oidábiadé atatagode udojo nanique.*
english: *Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie.*

ayoreo: Jnani bajade ore chujé*Togábi*nanique uje i uñai u*Goródedé*. Ga chi ore chujé. Ga chi daijna ujopierãque  uté  nanique.  Ga chi*Ucatédé*(ore asuté), jõrai*Baráyudé*ga ore u uje ore chujé*Togábi.*
english: *Togábi*was killed by men who lived many years ago*.*(He was also called*Gorodédé.) Togábi*was a medicine man, but one that could curse people. They say it was*Ucatédé*(their chief) and his friend*Baráyudé*that killed*Togábi*.

ayoreo: Ore chujé  ga chi juticai tõraji ga chigase*Ucatédé*etarúdi ga chicha ijmaradatei aja etarúdi. (Ga chi i tu*Togábi*juticai.)  Ga jõrai tu*Baráyudé*ga uñai tṍraji ga chigase*Baráyudé*etarúdi.  Ga chi jé u uje chicha ijmaradatei aja etarúdi.
english: When they killed him, it’s said, a horse fly came out of him and bit*Ucatédé’s*leg and left a large boil on it. (It was really*Togábi*in the form of a horse fly.)  And another one came out and bit*Ucatédé’s*companion*Baráyudé*on the leg. And it left a large boil on his leg.

ayoreo: Uje e ore chujé*Togábi*uté enga ore chisa uro*Gorode*. Ga*Togábi*chojningame daro: “¡*Goro*á! Ayoréode chujé yu. ¡Ore chujé yu! Ga abidina yamorayatique iji gaté, gue tṍra.” Ga gue tṍrai di iji ore.
english: After killing*Togábi*they grabbed his daughter*Gorode*. He said to his daughter: “*Goro á*! Those men have killed me. They have killed me! So call down a storm upon them.” And a storm came upon them.

ayoreo: Ga jé u uje*Gorode*jnusina uruode ga chi tibidina ga chojninga: “¡Iapá! Aya, uje ore chisa yu diríca que.”
english: And then it was when she remembered what her father had said. She called with a purpose and said: “Father, that’s enough! They have grabbed me and have me in their power.”

ayoreo: Ga ore chojninga: “*Goro*á, be abidina bayé jeta urúo.”
english: And they said to her: “*Goro á*! Come now,  and call out with purpose to your father to see if he’ll make the rain stop.”

ayoreo: Ga tibidi ga chojninga: “¡Iapá, aya! ¡Ore chisa yu diríca é!” Ga chi jé u uje uruode udore enga cha guei, ga*Gorode*chiqueta darásade ome dayé amorañai guetṍra.
english: So she called and said: “Father, enough! They have taken me!” And, it’s said, after she said that, the rain stopped, and she saved her people from the storm that her father had thrown down.

ayoreo: *Oidábiadé – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988.*
english: *Oidabiade – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988.*

ayoreo: *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Oidábiadé atatagode udojo.*
english: *Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie.*

ayoreo: Chi disijate yacorenie oidi ayoré. Chi qué parátagape ga chijna dacode*ipipébi*ome yacorenie. Chi yacorenie guejnangóri enga yacorenie jno údi jne.
english: The Ayoré that the wild pigs took away was an orphan. It’s said that when he was still very small he took the scraper that his grandmother used for scraping **dajudíe*leaves and used it for hunting. It’s said, he became a wild pig hunter, and the wild pigs would one day take him away with them.

ayoreo: Chi chojninga: “Codé á, yijína ore to jne aja yacorenie, ga yajo yitique ape to jne.” Ga ore ababode qué parátasi ore uje ore chujési, yacorenie ababode. Ga dacode tagúsu yugábode ababío. Chi e arec, ga chujé yãcoré ape arégode. Chi dacode chi ore urásade chisi ome dutuedie ome, ga ore chojningame: “Arãjure udiji ome bájnami. Tagu jeta di.” Ga e ore tagu yugode yãcoré abío. Ga e arécho, ga ore chosõre to.
english: The little one said: “Grandmother, I want to go with the hunters to hunt pigs, and we’ll eat the little baby pig that I kill.” And the ones they killed that day were all little pigs. And he brought home some baby pigs that he had killed, and he and his grandmother ate his little victims. When he was bigger, he killed pigs that were bigger.

ayoreo: Ejoi ga chujé yãcoré icáguejnanie. Jé u uje e jnacare ga chejna yacorenie jochade. Ga jé u uje yãcoré guejnangõre. Ga jé u uje chejna jochai te mu yacorenie chacho disi ome raque ome dáringanie chuque gajiné. Mu  yacorenie niji iji quigade ga jé u uje jno údi uje yãcoré niji iji quigade ga jno údi.
english: It’s said that their countrymen would give squash to the grandmother and say to her: “Boil these for your grandson. He can eat them when he gets back from a hunt.” They did this because they wanted to eat some of the game the little boy brought back.  They would eat some of the young pigs he would kill, but even though the pigs were bigger now, they showed kindness to the boy.

ayoreo: Chiese yãcorenie chiraja ayoréode uyoque ga u oidi jnani bajaque ayoré ga chi chojninga: “Boyo iji ayoréode uyoque uje jocãratade udore gu. Ga boyo.”
english: Later on he killed ‘big-bellied pigs.’ And when he was a young man he would kill whole troops of wild pigs. And that’s when he became known as the great hunter of wild pigs. And it’s also when the wild pigs got fed up with him because of how many pigs he was killing.

ayoreo: Ga jé u uje yãcorenie chayo. Yãcorenie chayo uje chiraja ayoréode ore uyoc. Jé u uje chi yãcorenie oite ayoré chi chingo ayoréode yocadode aja yãcorenie, ga jé u uje yãcorenie chiraja yoque to iji de, ga chayome ore.
english: So the wild pigs that he had killed would rise again from the dead, and that’s when one of them grabbed the young man and took him away.

ayoreo: *Oidábiadé – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988.*
english: They claim that the wild pigs learned all about us Ayoreos, because the one they took off long ago told them about us. He said to them: “Leave us Ayoreos alone, for Ayoreos are all warlike. Leave and stay away from them.”

ayoreo: *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Oidábiadé atatagode udojo nanique.*
english: And that’s when the wild pigs started running away from the Ayoreos. They still run from us, because they know what we’re like. And that’s because of what the Ayoré that they took away so long ago told them. Wild pigs know our ways, and that is why, knowing all about us today, they still run away from us.

ayoreo: *Diti*chujé chugúpẽredatei. Ga chi chojninga: “Codé a, a yujé*Natingarai*te jne.” (Chugúpẽredatei chi i tu*Natingarai*.)
english: It is said that one of the first men killed “Big Bird.” It was **Diti*(formerly the giant sized ant) that killed Big Bird. And it’s said that he told his grandmother: “Grandmother, I’m going to kill **Natingarai*.” That was Big Bird’s name.

ayoreo: Chi chojninga: “A yujé jne.”
english: That’s what he said: “I’m going to kill him.”

ayoreo: Mu dacode chojninga: “¡Cucha chuque! Suépise dojode cucha gai ga chi ca chuque.”
english: But his grandmother said: “As if you were a killer! He’s stronger than his fellow creatures; it’s said he can’t be killed.”

ayoreo: Mu*Diti*chojninga: “Yirote iji jopidé gajño.”
english: But*Diti*said: “I’ll wait till he comes down to the lake to drink water.”

ayoreo: Chi e chirote, mu dacode chojninga: “¡Cucha chuque! Uajate.”
english: He went off to wait for Big Bird, but his grandmother only said: “As if you were a killer! He’s strong.”

ayoreo: Ebeguejnai ga bajágaja iquei. Ga chi chirote ga chijna dojiguéjnarique. Chijna dagaitéjna, ga chijna ome. Chi chichome dagaitéjna. Ga chojninga: “¡Uté, uté, e di, e di, e di!” Ga basája gajño ga e chichóji ga chujé. Chicho ga churugúsiji jnumi. Jé u uje chejna. Jé u uje chojninga: “Codé, ¡yujé*Natingarai*!”
english: A very strong wind went ahead of him. And it’s said he waited and took a huge bow with him. He took huge arrows with him. He said he’d shoot his huge target with them.

ayoreo: Ga codé chojninga: “¡Cucha daté u bei, mu jé bajé!”
english: He saw Big Bird and said: “There he is! There he is! He’s coming! He’s coming! He’s coming!”

ayoreo: Ga ore doi ga ore chasẽra chugúpẽredatéi ga jiei ore ga ore chasẽra jnese. Ga chi chóna abuei. Chijna iyoi. Chi gajneque iyojna. Ga jé noñája abuei ajei. Gati ajei.
english: And when he stopped at the lake,*Diti*shot his arrows and killed him. He shot him and drug him off. And that is how he finished him off.

ayoreo: Jé u uje chi datũra changuñéra iyoi aja dabai ga noñáji aoi.
english: And then he said: “Grandmother, I killed*Natingarai*.”

ayoreo: Jé u uje darujna changuñera dabai to ga noña ajei, aoi. Jé u uje ajniecai chujé asi aja dójo, ga jé noñáji ójo, ajniecai.
english: And his grandmother said: “Your victim was huge, and yet you managed to kill him!”

ayoreo: *Diti*yuc mu ore chasẽra ga ore chijna adode.
english: And they went and grabbed Big Bird, and it took many of them to pull him along.

ayoreo: Chi chugúpẽredatéi, ga jnani bajade ore chisa iyoi ga ore churúsãre daguíjnane gáyode ome iyoi. Ore gaidi tu gu uje ore churúsãre daguíjnane piagode ujetiga gatíjna.
english: Long ago there were no **abuei*cactus. So they took Big Bird’s blood. And from that victimized blood they made the*abuei*cactus. And the blood colored the inside of the cactus red.

ayoreo: Cutamurajane ore edopasa datũra, ajniecai, asi ore.
english: And then it was that the**datũra*cactus rubbed Big Bird’s blood on itself and that large cactus is red on the outside.

ayoreo: *Oidábiadé – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988.*
english: Then it was that the large cactus called **darujna*rubbed itself with Big Bird’s blood also, and this time, became red inside and outside on it’s peeling.

ayoreo: *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Oidábiadé atatagode udojo nanique.*
english: Then it was that the tree called*Ajniecai*threw the downy feathers of Big Bird upon its branches and they stayed there on the branches, and when it blooms it’s white like the down of Big Bird.

ayoreo:
**Adudui, Dajesui ore Adode:**

*Adudui*chirote nujna. Jé aca gu. Chacho cuchade. Chujé cuchade mu que chejna gotique. Mu chi chojninga: “Yipota jeta yujé cuchade jnese ome yigaite guebec.”

Chirote iji dejade, chirote iji dirode uje chi chãraque adie ome cuchade. Ore poté gu. Mu moi chujé gu uje chujé cuchade jnese.

*Adudui*que chujé to moi, mu chucué*Diti*.  Ucuegué iji erámi. “¿Jé potique jãaa? Que di to,*Yajógue*.”

*Adudui*catecã́ri*Diti*: “¿Jé*Yajógue*cho ringome jeta tagu cucha adie to?”

Chojninga: “Úngare. Que tagu cucha adie to. Posode u casecade iji jnumi.”

*Adudui*chojninga: “Jecute que chise yu to iji yirodé uje que tagu cucha adie.”

Chi*Adudui*chejna cuchade jnese. Chujé*caratade, caratedie*, jé u cuchade jnese. Chi chise dejai. Chujé*ántadie*,*erámoronie*ore. Chingo darásade: “Yejna cuchade iji yirodé.”

*(Chi a*yoréode tagu ore imeserane cucha nanique*:*Mu que ore tagu*caratade, caratedie*to. Ore poté u*yacorenie, yocade, ajaraméone*to. Jecute yico udé to. Jnani bajade ore tagu ore imeserane cuchade. Yico údi to disi ejo yoque ga yajo to*yacorenie, yocade, ajaraméone*to. Jnani bajade ore tagu*jóchiode*,*tachégode, pojíode*. Que ore tagu*ajaraméone*nanique. Ore posode u*pojíode*. Mu unere ome yoque*ajaraméone.*Que yajo*pojíode*to. A*pojíode*unere ome jnani bajade nanique.)

**Adudui chujé Dajusui:**

*Adudui*chujé*Dajusui*.*Adudui*chi chojningame darásade: “Beyoi uacatígo ome. Yetingare mu cachi deroco arione. Yagu mu quedéjna. Ai chójmare.”

Chaquesu daserani ome. Chojninga: “Yaquesu yaserani ga yuguhése jne. Yurásade ore tagu to jne.”

**Dajusui jno Adudui údi:**

*Anta (*español ome*Dajusui)*iyojna chiqueta raja*Adudui*gu.*Adudui*e chĩ́care. Chojninga: “Bo yúdi ga a ca yagu yibotíguiji erámi jne,”*Adudui*chojningame*Dajusui.*

Mu*Anta*chojninga: “Yisi ome ua jne babotígo.”

Chi jé chijnoque*Anta*arione mu jé chiqueta raja. Chijnónguiji damoi mu dire uje jno údi. Chi chojninga: “Yajniape to. Yijnóngape arágajñunie. Yurásade ore tagu to jne,*Dajusui*.”

Chi chijnoque mu*Dajusui*e chiqueta raja. Chi chojninga: “Bo, apúsaja erámi. Ajnoque yu.”

“A ca yagu yibotique jne,” chojninga,*Adudui.*

“Agu yibosode to jne,”*Dajusui*chojningame.

“A ca babotique cus,”*Adudui*chojningame*Dajusui.*

*Carne*(español ome arione*)*que iji gai, mu e chiqueta raja uje jno údi, ga e ayoré enga e catecã́ri. “Yibosode déji yajei.”

(Adi jé u uaque, Violeta,  uje ajucho uacabosode tiji*cajõ*ajei. Jé suru dabosode iji dajei.)

*Adudui*chacho iberode uje dachajna. Chi pojna erámi. Chi e chaguhei chejna. Chojninga: “¡Chaguhei dejna aaaa!”

*Dajusui*chojninga: “¿Jé ó rique?”

Chojninga,*Adudui*: “Que yĩrarare.” Jé pogu daruode uje chaguhei chejna.

Mu jé chiraja,*Dajusui*. Chi chojninga: “Cachi bãrarare ga ojninga ‘chaguhei chejna yu jõ aaaa.’”

*Dajusui*chojninga: “Yisi ome babotígo ome ua jne.” Jé chaca. (*Adi u uje yoquibosode tutúdiji yocajéode.*) Cho ‘puuuu’ mu*mimire*cuchábi nóna acai aja guesi. “Be, agu dac.” Chajire mu e*mimiré*cuchape. Chi timate ajei. Iyojnai chusaque gu ajei. “Agu. ¡Unere!”

*Adudui*tagu mu jec ũré unere. (*Bamo uje unere mimirenie.*)  Chi ica guejna. Cho guiyé ga chojninga: “Yica guejna jõ. Yiqueca jõ. Yijosa yiyaguhei jno jõ. Yijosa yibotique di jne,” chojninga. “Areque yu jõ. Subudé yu jõ. Yiqueca jõ,” chojningápo rĩ. E chacãre uté gai,*Dajusui*.

“Be yibai ga yijnoque ua,” chojninga. E jno údi. Jno údi ga jé adi dasocai que uje pojna erámi.

“Yacho erámi isagode jõ. Chaguhei chejna yu jõ.”

Mu jé isocai u dasocai.

*Dajusui*chojningame: “Yisi ua jne babotic. Bajné tu*zapallo*uje yijnime. I tu*dutué*ome ore.”

Jé adi isocai. Chojninga: “Que yĩrarare go.”

Mu chojninga: “Jé bãrarare. Bojninga, ‘chaguhei chejna yu.’ Yácare ga yisi babotígo ome ua.” Chácare. Chisi*dutué*ome. Chácare ga e taque.*Adudui*jnui*Anta,*ga*Adudui*pose*dutué*cucha.*Anta*que dique to.

(Posode garani tu Dajusui. Adudui jé urugúji erámi.)

Chi chojninga: “Be yibai ga yijnoque ua.”

Chi e chaguhei chejna iji tõo. E chijnime dabosode. Chojninga: “Chaguhei chejna yu jõ. Yijnime yibotique case jõ ga yagu jõ*dutué*case.”

Mu e*Anta*catecãrápoi rĩ. “¿O rique?”

*Adudui*chojninga: “Que yĩrarare.”

“Bãrarare. Bojninga, ‘Yijnime yibotique casé*dutué*jõ.’ Yijnime ujñáringome ua to jne ga agupo rĩ.”

Chi ijnimé*jóco*gajnerĩ ga chojninga: “¡Chi! Be, agu*purúa*. Dacódejna. Baca guejna iji.”

*Adudui*chojninga: “Yagu jne. Yica guejna ome jne. Cachi yajire yiboté casé mu e diapo rĩ. Toguéode pica guejnanie jõ.”

Chi cho guiyé.

“Be yibai ga yijnoque ua jne.” E jnopo údi rĩ. Jé isocai ga dasocai. Chojninga: “Yacho erámi ome yiriguitígadatéode jõ. Chaguhei chejna yu ome yiriguitígadatéode jõ.”

*Anta*chojninga: “¿O rique?”

“Que yĩrarare.”

“Bãrarare. Bojninga, ‘Chaguhei chejna yu ome yiriguitígadatéode.’ Mu*Anta*chijnime*guéjnajñunie*. Chijnimi bajo ga*guéjnajñúnie*irágu ajei. Nóna*ajíode*aja guesi. Chijároja.  Chojninga: “Uneripise. Agu.”

Tagu ga chojninga: “Jec ũré, unere gotígo jõ.” Chi ica guejna ome.  Chojninga: “Toguéode pica guejnanie jõ.” Chi guiyé.

“Be yibai ga yijnoque ua.” E doi gai ga e jnopo údi rĩ.

E dadi u dasocai. Chejnáreque ome chaguhei. Chojninga: “Yijnime yiboté casé jõ. Yagu jõ. Chaguhei urõso jõ.”

*Anta*chojninga: “¿O rique?”

“Que yĩrarare.”

“Bãrarare. Bojninga, ‘Chaguei urõso jõ.’ Mu agu babotígo jne.” Chisi ome*frejol*(Español ome*cuguéode)*ome. Chojninga: “Be, agu*cuguei edo*. Ajninguese gai urúi.

(Ore chojninga, asite gai urui. Ajñute gai urui. Urupis uje ore chijáro iji tacúdie*.*)

“A uneripis,”*Dajusui*chojninga. “Etingare jeta unere. Unere ga agu.”

*Adudui*tagu. E chajmára. Chojninga: “Unere jõ. Toguéode pica guejnanie jõ.”

(Deroco cuchade ga ore chojninga, ‘deroco, que yajmára gotique.’ Unere cuchade ga ore chajmára ga ore chojninga, ‘uneripise jõ.’ Chijnime yarecujadíe. Chequé bajedie ore urúo uje ore chajmára cuchade mu que déji disi ejode uyoque. Ore chojninga to nanique, ‘charore ti’ uje chusaque ore pusugúi.)

Chi jnopo údi rĩ. Adi jé u dasocai. Chojninga: “Chaguhei chejna yu jõ.”

“¿O rique?”

“Que yĩrarare.”

“Bãrarare. Bojninga, ‘Chaguhei chejna yu.’ Mu agu babotígo jne.” Chijnime*tocode*uje timate iji sequeredie to.*Dajusui*chojninga: “A yijnime*porade*ga agu.” Chijnime*datuadie, tocode, jnarujnanie, abuidie*(gati ajéode) enga irágu bajo.

(Que déji de to abuedie. Déji Guidaigosode ore achaminone. Ore posode u.)

*Anta*ijnimenie u. Chijna aja guesi sequeré adode. Cutéri adode rĩ. E dopo cutérone rĩ.

*Anta*chojninga: “Yécãre babotique jne.” Chécãre ga chisi*cutérone*ome. Mu chisi*esode*,*dajujña, durujna*ome to. Ijnimícho jé udore to. Sequeré adode jé u gu. “Yécãre babotíguiji ejoi tuné.”

*Adudui*chi chacho erámi. Adi jé u dasocai. Chojninga: “Chaguhei chejna yu jõ.”

*Anta*chojninga: “¿O rique?”

“Que yĩrarare.”

Mu*Anta*chojninga: “Bãrarare, bojninga, ‘chaguhei chejna yu.’ Mu yijnime*cutérone*ome ua.” Chi chaca ga chijnime cutérone. Chajni de agapi. Queru bajo. Chi chojninga: “Apese.”

E chipese ga ica guejna ome. Cho guiyé ga chojninga: “Toguéode pica guejnanie jõ.”

(E cutérone adode udojo u uje e sequeré adode e íjnoque. Cutepísode chugúja yoque, ijnimique bajai. Zapacógosode ore chojningame, ‘cute sijnángone.’ Unejac cute quedéjnai  -cute parai ome Zapacógosode. Que chugúja yoque. Chugúja yoque uje chigase yoque. Ájningare gu.)

Chi jno mu chaguhei chejna iji tõ. “Chaguhei chejna yu jõ.”

“¿O rique?”

“Que yĩrarare.”

“Bãrarare. Bojninga, ‘Chaguhei chejna yu.”*Anta*chaca ¡puuuu! ga chijnime*cute parai.*

*Adudui*chipese. Chujé dorotabidode.  Chojninga: “E yica guejna jõ.”

“ A ca yimécare ua jne.”

“A bamécare yu jne,” chi chojninga. “Babosode íjnoc.”

“ A badode ga chaguhei chejna ua iji toniñeque ga amurase yu ga aque jne. Atíraja bacajnogonie.”

(Chi chutíraja dacajnongónie ga ica guejna mu e doi údode.)

Chi chojninga: “Yachopise yaguhei jõ.”

“¿O rique?”

“Que yĩrarare.”

“Bãrarare. Bojninga, ‘Chaguhei chejna yu ga yachopise.”

*Anta*chaca mu e chijnime*ajidape*. Cojñone chojninga, ‘*señorita.’*

*Adudui*chipese ga ica guejna. Cho guiyé ga chojninga: “A ca yimécare ua jne uje yica guejna.”

Chojninga: “Arója bádode ga cha bádode.” Changuñera dádode ome dajmanai ga e chicai dádi.

Jnopo rĩ.*Anta*chijnime*jutai*(kind of honey).

(Que déji de go. Déji guesode. Cutéri ga chugúja yoque. Chopise abéjadie. A ajmárarase to, Violeta. A bojningarása uneripise jõ.)

Ejo chijnime*caritique*uje cojñone chojninga, ‘*oro*.’ Irágu bajo. Chojninga: “Be, apese*caritique*.” Chĩretapise daca uje posode chise ica eruei. Eruei chajni uchiéro. Chajniapo aguté tirógara rĩ.

(Zapacogosode uruode u ‘juriape’. Ore chojninga uje ore ica guejna.)

(Anta pésu íode uje ipesucho jnese cuchade, sequeré adode. “Yijnime ichatui, yijnime pirangórone, yijnime orojónie.” Orojo uje chaca jnumi. Chijnime cutepeque gajnaréngone jnese. Chijnimápo guiréjnai.)

*Adudui*chojninga: “Yachopise yujnui gotique jõ.”

*Anta*chojninga: “Amurase yu ga yiji. Yicai erámi.”

“¿Jé yibotique jne a? jeta yijíñome ua.”

“¿Jé babotique jne a? Apúsa babotígo jne. Etingare cuchade ga agu bameserane cuchade jne.”

“A yupúsa yibotígo to jne.” Ga tagu*erãpe gatoi, erãpe pijóningai, jnacarade*. “Unéño udojo. A yagu erãpe pijóninga jnacarade. Ajíñome yu.”

*Adudui*chojninga: “A yagu*cutérone*jne. Unéño.”

*Anta*chojninga: “¿E baguijnárique jne? Que babotique cus.”

“Yucué*pore garainie*ga yacája ga yagu.”

“Acué uje etócho uñane ga edo iji.”

E*cuchiso*. E jno ga noñáji erámi. E chucué daboso ga chaca garáinie ga tagu cutérone.

*Anta*tagu*erámi gatoi, poridie acarane*. Chetingare cuchade mu pogai iji cuchade mu chetingare*poridie acarane*ga que pogája. Doi yodi ga chetingare.

Chojninga: “Yajire jeta yipogai ome yodi. A ca yóji jeta yipogai ome.” Mu chóji yodi ga unéri ga chacãre yodi gai. Chojninga: “Yacaranguipis uje que yipogája to. A yóji jne.”

Mu ica guejnapise ome yodi. Yodi chajnerámi ica ga ica querújnapis. Tagúsu darujna ahábidie mu tutúdaja ajei. Tagu darujnanie aoi. Jecute ica queru,*ántadie.*

*Samané – Tobité, Bolivia – 1960.*

*Joyce Davis Buchegger churúsãre Samané atatagode udojo nanique.*
english:
*Adudui – Dajesui Ore*

***The story of Honey Badger and Tapir:***

*Adudui (*Honey Badger*)*watched over the*‘nujna’ (tree that produces red berries)*. He had planted it.*Adudui*was tired of chasing animals away from his tree. He hit them but didn’t kill them. But he said: “I’m so tired of chasing the animals away; I’d like to kill all of them with my iron weapon!”

He watched over the*‘nujna’*day and night, trying to keep the animals from eating the fruit. That is what the animals came for, to eat the fruit. But fighting off the animals was what he did day and night, and at last he grew very sleepy.

*Adudui*couldn’t stop being sleepy, but he went in search of*Diti*(big ant).*Diti*never bothered his tree but looked for his food in the woods. “I wonder what*Yajogue*(anteater) eats?”*Adudui*asked*Diti. “*I’ve noticed he hasn’t come around the ‘*nujna’*to eat its fruit.”

*Adudui*continued to talk with*Diti*: “How does*Yajogue*find things to eat? Does he eat fruit?”

*Diti*answered: “No. He doesn’t eat fruit. His food is*casecade*(ants) in the ground.

*Adudui*said: “Oh, that’s why he hasn’t come to where I’m watching over the tree, it’s because he doesn’t eat fruit.”

They say that*Adudui*finally did kill all of the animals that tried to take fruit from his tree. He killed*Caratade*(Jaguars),*Caratedie*(Lions) and all the rest. And when night came, he killed the animals that walk at night:*dajesúode/cabayúode*(tapirs),*erámoronie*(deer) and animals like that. He told his people, “I have killed the animals that got near what I was protecting.”

*(**Commentary of Samané**: But the Ayoreos didn’t eat Caratade (jaguars) or Caratedie (lions). The food they ate were yacorenie (wild pigs), yocade (turtles), ajaraméone (armadillos).  And so we hunt for these even now. The men who lived long ago ate what appealed to them. And we, their descendants, have continued to eat whatever they ate: wild pigs, turtles, and armadillos. The men who lived long ago also ate tachégode (the non-Ayoreos call them *jochi) and pojíode (large lizards/iguanas). We no longer eat lizards. But they must have appealed to the men who lived long ago, for they ate them.)*

**Adudui killed Dajusui long ago:**

*Adudui (*Honey Badger*)*killed*Dajusui (Tapir)*.*Adudui*said to his countrymen: “Do what you want with the tapir’s meat.  I tried it, but it didn’t taste good. I ate it, but it was strange and it was smelly.”

*Adudui*decided to smoke the meat so he made a platform for it. Telling about it, he said, “I cut some poles and made a platform to smoke the meat, and then perhaps, my countrymen could eat it.”

**Adudui****(Honey Badger) had a vision:**

It was the blood of*Dajusui*, the tapir, that he had hunted and killed that gave*Adudui*the vision.  And in his vision*Dajusui*took*Adudui*and carried him off into the jungles.

“If you take me off,”*Adudui*said, “I won’t find anything to eat in the woods.”

*Dajusui*said, “I’ll give you the food you need.”

It was while*Adudui*was carrying tapir’s meat to his people, that*Dajusui*appeared to him in a dream. He was carrying tapir’s meat in the dream, and when he awoke,*Dajusui*was still with him.

*Adudui*said, “I’ll go back to my countrymen. I’ll carry tapir’s meat to them. My countrymen will eat it.” And as he was carrying the meat,*Dajusúi*appeared to him.*Adudui*saw him in a vision.

*Dajusui*looked at*Adudui*and said, “Go with me and get whatever you want from the woods as you carry my meat to your countrymen.”

“I won’t have any food to eat if I go with you,”*Adudui*said.

*Dajusui*said, “Why, you’ll eat my food.”

“But you don’t have any food,”*Adudui*said.

*Adudui*still had the tapir meat he was carrying to his countrymen when*Dajusui*appeared to him, and then, suddenly,*Dajusui*became an Ayoré and he said to*Adudui*the honey badger, “I have food inside me.”

(**Samane’s commentary to Joyce Davis, who he called Violeta***: It is like you do when you put food in a box and close the box. That is what Tapir did with his food, he stored it inside of himself.)*

*Adudui*was very confused and didn’t know how to respond. And he grumbled,*scolding the whole wide world*. He was very hungry, you see, and in a bad humor. He said, “Oh, hunger is going to finish me off!”

*Dajusui*said, “What did you say?”

*Adudui*said, “I didn’t say anything.” He wanted to hide from the tapir that he was hungry.

But*Dajusui*said, “Yes, you did. You said, ‘Hunger is going to finish me off! ¡Jõ*aaaa!”*

And then*Dajusui*said, “I’m going to give you some food.” And he passed gas.*(Samané comments: It was like when the food inside of us causes gas and makes our stomachs swell.)*It was explosive and sounded like ‘POO!’  And then, a huge watermelon accompanied the gas as it came out of him. The watermelon was really ripe and red inside. (The blood of*Dajusui*had tinted it red.)  He showed it to*Adudui*and said, “Here, eat this. It’s delicious!”

*Adudui*tried it, and sure enough, it was delicious.*(Samané comments: You know, Violeta, how delicious watermelon is.)**Adudui*ate till he was full. And then he belched and said, “My belly’s full. I’m going to live. I believe my hunger left*¡jo!*I believe I’m going to have food when I need it,” he said. “I feel good! I’m full! I’m alive,” he said again. And then he thanked*Dajusui*.

“Crawl on my back and I’ll carry you,”*Dajusui*said. And he did, and they took off through the jungle. But soon*Adudui*was grumbling again.

“I’m tired of it all, just plain fed up with this world. And on top of it all, I’m hungry.”

But*Dajusui*knew what to do – just what he did before.

He said, “I’m going to give you something to eat.”  The next thing he gave him was ‘*dutué’*(a type of squash*).*

*Adudui*claimed he hadn’t said anything, but*Dajusui*told him, “Oh, yes you did. You said, ‘I’m hungry.’ So, I’ll stop right here and give you something to eat.” So he stopped and gave him squash.

*Adudui*stopped, too, and ate the squash. (Remember that*Adudui*was carrying the meat of the tapir everywhere they went, but, at the same time, here he was, in the vision, eating squash. In real life*Dajusui*wasn’t walking about for*Adudui*had already killed him and butchered him for meat.

*(**Samané comments:**This story is about the origin of the food that we eat in the Rainy Season. It all began with Adudui the Honey Badger when he walked like a man on earth.)*

*Dajusui*said, “Climb on my back and I’ll carry you.”

After they had gone a long distance*Adudui*got hungry again. He’d used up all the food he’d eaten before. He said, “I’m really hungry. I’ve used up all the squash I ate.”
