*Dajusui*questioned*Adudui*and said, “What did you say?”

*Adudui*answered, “I didn’t say anything.”

“Yes, you did,” said*Dajusui*. “You said, ‘It looks like I’ve used up all the squash I ate.’ So I’ll strain and push with all my might so you can eat again.”

He pushed the squash out of his body that is called ‘*purúa’*and he said, “Just look at that squash! Come and get it. It’s huge! Your belly will get full when you eat it!”

*Adudui*, the honey badger, said, “I’ll eat it. My stomach will really be full when I eat it. Why, it seems like I just see one kind of food and here comes another! I thought I’d never have a full stomach again.”

*Adudui*belched then and*Dajusui*said, “Crawl up on my back and I’ll carry you.” And he carried him off into the jungle.

*Adudui*continued to get hungry. He said, “I’m so tired of this long walk. It’s making me very hungry.”

*Dajusui*said, “What did you say?”

“Nothing,” answered*Adudui*.

“Oh, yes you did! You said, ‘I’m hungry because of this long trip.’”

But then*Dajusui*pushed out kernels of corn. He got a bowl and filled it with corn kernels. Hot peppers came out with the corn, and so he ground the hot peppers and the corn together and he said, “Now, this is really delicious. Eat it.”

*Adudui*ate it and said, “You’re right! Whatever this is, it’s delicious ¡jõ!” He ate till he was full and he said, “I thought I would never be full again!”

And then he belched.

*Dajusui*said, “Climb up on my back and I’ll carry you.”*Adudui*got up on his back, and they took off through the jungle.

The same thing happened again.*Adudui*got so hungry it hurt. He said, “I’ve used up all the food I’ve eaten. I have to eat something, because I’m so hungry I hurt.”

*Dajusui*said, “What did you say?”

“I didn’t say anything,”*Adudui*said.

“Yes, you did. You said, hunger hurts! But eat my food now.” He gave him beans (*frijoles).*He said, “Come, eat the*eyes*of the beans, but shell them first.”

*(**Samané comments**: They say ‘aside gai urui’ or ‘ajñute gai urui’ which means to take the beans out of the shells. Sometimes they use a mortar and pestle to shell the beans.)*

“Beans are really delicious,”*Dajusui*said to*Adudui*, “Try a few. If they taste good to you, eat the rest.”

*Adudui*ate and ate. He really liked them. He said, “They’re good. I thought I’d never be full again.”

*(**Samané comments**: When something doesn’t have a good flavor they say ‘deroco’ (it’s blah) or ‘que yajmára gotique’ which means ‘it’s flavorless and I don’t like it.’ When something tastes good they say ‘unere’ (delicious).  I just have to comment on the food when it’s well prepared. The old women used to talk about food all the time, and always commented on what they liked. We don’t do that much nowadays. When they used to like a certain dish, they would say, ‘now that food really warms my throat way down to here,’ (‘charore ti uje chusaque ore pusugúi).)*

*Dajusui*took*Adudui*off into the jungle again. And the same thing happened.*Adudui*said, “I’m hungry.”

“What did you say?” asked*Dajusui.*

“I didn’t say anything,” denied*Adudui.*

“You did too say something. You said, ‘I’m hungry.’  But now you will eat my food,” said*Dajusui*. He brought forth*tocode*(a type of cactus fruit which ripens in the rainy season). And he said, “I will push out all kinds of cactus fruits. Eat them.” And out came:*datuadie, tocode, jnarujnanie, abuedie (they are all red inside)*.*Dajusui*got a bowl and he filled it until it was full.

*(**Samané comments:**There isn’t any ‘abuedie’ fruit here. That type of cactus fruit grows up in the land of the Guidaigosode. They eat it up there.”)*

These were the foods that*Dajusui*pushed out of his body. And with that he had completed all of the foods of the rainy season.

Oh, yes, he also pushed out honey. And he said, “I’m going to make a change in your diet.” He changed it by introducing honey. But he pushed out other cactus fruits also that mature in the dry season, like ‘*dajuña’*and ‘*durujna’.*These are the things he brought forth for the rainy season and those he brought forth for the dry season. And he said, “I am giving you a variety of foods to eat.”

*Adudui*got nervous again because he was getting hungry and didn’t know what he could eat. And just as before he said, “I’m so hungry.”

*Dajusui*said, “What did you say?”

And he responded, “I didn’t say anything.”

But*Dajusui*said, “You did too say something, you said, ‘I’m so hungry.’ But I’ll push honey out for you to eat.” He passed gas and with the gas, honey came out, so much that he filled a bowl clear up to here, a big bowl. And said, “Suck the honey.”

He sucked it and got full from it. He belched and said, “I didn’t think I’d ever be full again!”

*(**Samané comments:**We depend upon honey (cutérone adode) when rainy season is over. But the first time we eat honey it is hard to digest and we say ‘cutepísode chugúja yoque’, meaning ‘the honey is angry with us and is pouting’. The people from Zapoco call it ‘cute sijnángone’, meaning bad honey. Other terms that the Zapoco people use for honey, with other meanings is ‘cute quedéjnai’ (strange honey) and ‘cute parai’ (tame honey because it doesn’t pout.) We say it is pouting because it stings our mouths. That’s because it’s angry.)*

After they took off again, the time came when*Adudui*began to complain and he said, “I’m hungry.”

“What did you say?” asked*Dajusui*.

“I didn’t say anything,”*Adudui*said.

“You did too. You said, ‘I’m hungry.’”  The tapir passed gas – and out came*cute parai*(tame honey).

*Adudui*began to suck it. It tasted so good he hit his shoulders, a sign that he was very pleased with things. He said, “Whew, I’m full.”

*Adudui*said*to Dajusui,*“I don’t think I can carry your meat to my countrymen because I’m too full.”

“You’ll be able to carry it with you,” he said. “In the future, when you don’t have anything to eat and you get hungry, you’ll remember me and think that I will always give you food. But, soon you’ll have to spit when you’re thirsty, because I won’t give you any other foods.”

*(**Samané comments:**They said that spitting when you’re thirsty will fill your stomach and the benefit spreads to your whole body.)*

And then*Adudui*said, “I’m really tired of being hungry.”

*Dajusui*asked, “What did you say?”

*Adudui*responded, “I didn’t say anything.”

*Dajusui*said, “Oh, yes, you did. You said, ‘I’m tired of being hungry.’”

Then*Dajusui*passed gas and with the gas out came the honey called ‘*ajidape’. (The non-Ayoreos call it ‘honey produced by the señorita bee’.)*

*Adudui*sucked honey and filled his stomach. He belched and said, “I can’t carry the meat any longer, my stomach’s too full.”

*Dajusui*said, “Rub yourself and the feeling of fullness will go away.” So he rubbed his belly with his hands and it went away.

Again Dajusui, the tapir, pushed out of his body a type of honey called ‘*jutai.’*

*(**Samané comments**: There isn’t any ‘jutai’ here. It’s in other places. It’s a kind of honey that pouts. It’s just like the ‘abéjadíe.’  Violeta (Joyce), you would like it. You would say, this is really good.)*

Afterwards*Dajusui*brought out ‘*caritaque’*(kind of honey) that the non-Ayoreos call*oro*(gold). He said, “Come, suck the ‘*caritaque’ honey!”*

*Adudui*got really full, so full that his food reached the edges of his stomach, clear down into the anus, and it pressed against his liver.

*(**Samané comments**: The people in Zapoco call it ‘juriape’ when their stomachs are overfull with honey.)*

*Dajusui*made names for everything that was pushed out of his body for the rainy season. He said, “I pushed out ‘*ichatui’ (*kind of honey)*,*and I also pushed out ‘*orojónie’*(kind of honey)*.”*

*(**Samané comments**: ‘Orojonie’ is a kind of honey that is found in the ground. Dajusui also pushed out all of the ugly types of honey. He also brought forth ‘guiréjnai’ (kind of honey).)*

*Adudui*, the honey badger, said, “I’m so tired of the meat I’m carrying, whatever its origin, tapir or not.” (He was referring to his victim the tapir’s meat, that he had hunted and killed before he had the vision.”

The tapir,*Dajusui*, said, “Well then, let me go now. I want to roam the jungle again.”

“Where will I get food if I let you go?” asked*Adudui*, the honey badger.

“Where will you get food? Just try all kinds of different things. Try things and then stick with eating what appeals to you.”

“That’s what I’ll do, I’ll start trying different foods.” And he started eating the*heads*of leaves from plants that grow in the jungle, other healthy plants, and grasses. “Why, these taste good. I will eat the things that grow like grass.”

*Adudui*now knew that he would be able to find food for himself and so he said to*Dajusui*, the tapir, “You can leave me now, I can find food for myself.”

And he added, “I’ll eat honey also. It’s good.”

*Dajusui*said, “But where will you take shelter? And where will you get food?”

*Adudui*answered, “I’ll look for hollows in trees for shelter and I’ll eat what’s around there in the jungle.”

“Make sure you go into the holes of strong trees,” counseled*Dajusui*.

*(**Samané comments**: Adudui was now an animal. He took off and lived in the woods. He looked for his own food and went into holes in trees and ate honey.)*

*Dajusui*also ate tree leaves and such that grew in the jungle. He tried things but some things made him throw up, so he only ate tree leaves that didn’t make him vomit. He went to a water hole and tried the water.

He said, “Let’s see if I vomit up the water. I won’t drink it if I throw it up.” But he drank it and it was good, so he thanked the water. He said, “I’m really thankful that I didn’t throw up water. I’ll drink it.”

But the water made his belly swell. The water increased the size of his belly and it became very large. He was big bellied from the water, but even more so, when he started eating ‘darujna’ fruit. He ate the skin of the cactus as well, but it made him swell up even more. But he kept eating the same diet and that is why the Tapir is so big, even today!

*jochi – a rabbit-like animal

*Samané – Tobité, Bolivia – 1965.*

*Transcribed by: Joyce Davis Buchegger.*

*Translated to English by: Maxine Morarie.*

ayoreo: *Corabé*jorã tu*Guiriamia*ga ore cho dajõra ome ñane. Ore chojningame ñane: “Yijõra.” Boquere ore ga ore canare dajmáinone jnacare. Chi ore ajmáini u*Caric Icha*, jnacare gatoi. Ore chuju datui aja guesi. Ore chajningarase gatererodie. Que sueapo rĩ. Ore canare gu. Ujo gapudie ore chojningame*Corabé*: “Aya bajmáinone. A topie baórique jne. Ore tujua pioi gueguerade.”
english: **Corabé (*White Butterfly) was friends with*Guiriámia*(type of bird) and they considered themselves best friends. They said to each other, “Friend.” They flirted and played around with the young men who liked them. It’s said that the one that really liked them was Caric Ichai, a very young guy. The girls would cut his hair and pull out his facial hair. It didn’t grow back, because they were always pulling it out, that’s why.

ayoreo: “A ca topie yu. Yésariji yodi jne ga ichapo yaoi rĩ,” chojninga.
english: Her girlfriends said to*Corabé:*“Quit playing around with those men who want you. Your skin will get dark. They rub coals from the fire on themselves.”

ayoreo: *Jongongo*(español: sapo) que churu re. Irasique jnacarode gu. Mu jnacarode ore**erutodie*pésu ugórodie iji gai. Chibica iji dabai uje dorit. Chajnéra dádi uje pésu, pésu iji gai, ugórodie.
english: “My skin won’t get dark. I’ll wash in water and my skin will get clean again,” she said.

ayoreo: *Guiramia*jõra tu*Corabé*.*Bujote*(español: pájaro) jõra tu*Jongongo*.*Caric Ichai*gajmáinenie jiei –*Guiriamia, Corabé, Bujote, Jongongo*ore.
english: Jongongo (frog) didn’t wash it off. She liked the young men, that’s why. But the girls that climbed on them made them get rash. They scratched themselves because it itched. And it just kept on itching and itching, the rash.

ayoreo: Jnacarode ore changureta jeta doi, mu que doi ore,*Corabé*. Cachi gajmai tu ore. Ore potapísa gu. Mu*Corabé*abáyabi carujnangue erusṍrami su jne.
english: Guiriámia went around with*Corabé*. Bujote (a bird) went around with Frog. Caric Ichai had all the girls wanting him –*Guiriámia(Bird), Corabé (White Butterfly), Bujote (Black Bird), Jongongo (Frog),*they all wanted him.

ayoreo: Chi chacho ore uje moi tome,*Corabé*. Chi jno. Cheru carujnangue. Ore chucué jeta ore doi. Ore chojninga: “Jé yacoté jeta yise jne. Jé yacoté jeta ca yiya jne.” Ore potapísa uje póroro gu. Ore chichaga dajéode taja jnese, jnacarode. Ore chojninga: “A yacotérase, a yacotérase.”
english: The young men hoped that they could get together with*Corabé*, but she wouldn’t go to them. She just flirted with them. They really wanted her for a fiancée. But*Corabé’s*little husband was going to be*Carujnangue Erusṍrami*(Lizard).  (*Carujnangue is the name of a tree with a slippery trunk, and Erusṍrami*is the term for ‘the small one who climbs’.

ayoreo: Ore pota jnese. Ore chojninga: “Boyo yico. Yico ga yarángo yóguiji yoquisorane jne.” Chi ore chutai jnese. Ore pota jõroque*Corabé*. Que toc jeta doi uñec. Cachi ajmai ore. Chi ore chacho udé niriji ga*Corabé*chojninga: “A yimope jne.”
english: But they say she got tired of the young men because she was sleepy. So she left. She climbed the Slippery Tree. They looked for her. They said, “She’ll be my wife if I find her first. She’ll be my wife, if I don’t stop trying.” They really wanted her because she was white. None of them could stop desiring her (placing their affections on her.) They’d say, “She’s almost mine! She’s almost mine!”

ayoreo: Chi jno. Chojninga: “A yiji. Yeru poría jne. Yipógu yu ome yajmáinone jne.”
english: All of them wanted her. They’d say, “Come on, let’s go.  Let’s act like we’re just going somewhere.” They all asked people where she was. They wanted to find her but in vain. There was no chance that she would go to any of them.  They were just the ones she flirted with.

ayoreo: Chiraja gu ga ore chucué jne. Jĩrique pógu re. Cheru carujnangue iji ti. Chi chingo raja dajõranie. Chojninga: “Uñec a chise yu jne, a yabai jne iji yiyangóri. A yeru carujnangue ga yimo iji gatei jne.”
english: Everyone was getting tired and*Corabé*said: “I’m going to go to sleep now.”

ayoreo: Chi ore chatata uruode, joranie uje jnacarode que ore chise gu. Ore chojninga: “Cajicho poridie gatéode uje chojninga, ‘A yimo iji poría gatei jne.’”
english: And then she left. She said: “I’m going now. I’m going to climb a tree. I’m going to hide from those guys I’ve been flirting with.”

ayoreo: Chi ore chucué mu jĩrique ore chise. Déji poria gatei. Iyangóri chojninga: “Dé daque, mu poria carujna aja jnumi.” Chi jĩrique cherurásu mu carujna aja jnumi, poria.
english: She knew the men were going to be looking for her. She quickly hid herself.  She climbed the tree with the slippery trunk that was way over there. She told her girlfriends what she was going to do. She said: “Whoever reaches me will be my husband, that is if he can find me. I’m going to climb the*carujnangue**tree*and sleep at the top of it.”

ayoreo: “ ¡Dé daque. Ga beyoi to mu déji poria iji gatei e!”
english: Her girlfriends told her words, because the young men couldn’t find her. They said to the young men:“Look for her up in the trees, because she said she was going to go to sleep at the top of a tree.”

ayoreo: Chi ore quejna yui. Ore chojninga: “¡Chi dé daque e! mu idája jnumi. Móji ti, poría ujai.” Jiei ore iji poria údi.
english: The men kept looking and quickly found her. She was at the top of the tree. One of men who found her said: “There she is, but the tree trunk is really slippery.” He quickly tried to climb up, but slipped back down because the tree was slippery.

ayoreo: Joranie ore chatata uruode que uje chojninga: “Yiyangóri yabai jne.” Ore chojninga: “Uñeque iya ga jé acoté jne.”
english: “There she is. You try to climb it, too,” he challenged the others, “but she’s at the top of the tree!”

ayoreo: Uñai chojninga: “Yipésu ¡pu! yiya jõ.”
english: They say all the men ran over to the tree. They said, “There she is! But she’s way up there. She’s sleeping in the branches.” Many of them were gathered beside the tree, underneath where she was.

ayoreo: Uñai chojninga: “¡Yichai yiquei jõ! A yacoté.”
english: “Her friends told us what she told them,” they said. “She says that whoever gets to her first will be her husband and she will be his wife.”

ayoreo: *Corabé*chojninga: “A gajnáreque yiyangónic u marínga, a jé yabai jne.” Chojningápo rĩ uje ore chise: “A yabai jne yiyangónic.”
english: One of the men said: “I will make an incantation and that will help me find her.”

ayoreo: Ore chajo údi.
english: Another said: “I’ll think about her, concentrate on her, and thoughts of her will always be before me! She’ll be my wife!”

ayoreo: Chojninga: “Echo yu. Yibai tu yiyangónique jne.”
english: *Corabé*said: “Even if the one who finds me is ugly, he’ll still be my husband.” After they found her, she said: “My husband will be the one who get’s to me first.”

ayoreo: Ore jnusi jórogome. “Yipésu ¡pu! ga yiya jõ.” Chi ore cheru mu cho caru aja jnumone, cho caru aja jnumone. Ore charatáyime ayoréode ome ñane. Ore chojninga: “Ca ese  gajnerĩ ¡pu jõ! Ca ese gajnerĩ ¡pu jõ!”
english: They cleared out the weeds under the tree.

ayoreo: Uñane ore chojningame daguiosode: “Ese gajnerĩ ¡pu jõ! Ese gajnerĩ ¡pu jõ!”
english: She said: “Climb up to me. My husband will be whoever gets to me first.”

ayoreo: *Corabé*chi chojninga: “Aguinécho. Ayoré yiyangónique, a yabai gosi gajnerĩ.” Chisi ome dajmanai ome ore. Chojninga: “¡Asa yijmanai, asa yijmanai!” mu cho caru, caru aja jnumone.
english: In vain they encouraged themselves. “I’ll chant when I’m climbing and then I’ll reach her,” they would say.

ayoreo: Uñane chojninga: “¡Pu o! Ca asa gajnerĩ. ¡Pu o! Ca asa gajnerĩ. O caru ua aja jnumi. O caru ua aja jnumi.”
english: They climbed but slipped right back down, and kept climbing up only to slip down again. They tried to climb higher than others. They would put hexes on each other and say: “You’re not going to reach her*¡pu jõ!*You’re not going to reach her*¡pu jõ!”*(They said*pu jõ*as a hex to keep others from getting to the top.)

ayoreo: Uñane chojninga: “¡Pu a! asa gajnerí. ¡Pu a! asa gajnerí.” Jiei ore iji carujnangue údi. Ore chajo údi ga guesa údi.
english: Others would encourage their relatives and try to put charms on them so they could reach the top. They would say: “You can do it*¡pu jõ!*You can do it*¡pu jõ!”*

ayoreo: Chi e iyangóri di. I tu ‘*Carujnángue Erusṍri*’. Chojninga: “Ajoso da ti. Yise gajnerĩ ga yacoté jne.”
english: *Corabé*would say to them: “Hurry up. The person who reaches me first will be my husband right now.” She reached her hand down to help them up. She would say: “Take my hand, take my hand!” But they would get just so far up and then slip down again; they would slip down to the ground.

ayoreo: Ore charatáyime acoté ome uje chise gaté uje chi gajnáreque gu, ayorei. Iyangóri u ‘*Carujnángue Erusṍri*’. Chi cheru caru, caru uje cata isagode gu. Que carujna isatique go.
english: Others would call out hexes and say: “*¡Pu o!*You’re not going to touch her.*¡Pu o!*You’re not going to touch her. ¡Slip back down! ¡Slip back down!”

ayoreo: Corabé chojninga: “¡Aguito!” Chi e chisa gapua jmanai. Chi e chimánaja dari. Chi ore tagu uñane uredie iji gaté uje chimate raja.
english: Some would say,*“¡Pu á!*grab her!*¡Pu á!*grab her!” There were lots of people underneath the slippery tree. But they told them to clear out from underneath the tree to make room for the climbers.

ayoreo: Ayoréode ore chetac uje gajnárec ayorei mu ueradé acoté.
english: And then the young man that would finally reach her arrived. His name was*Carujnángue Erusṍrami*. He said, “Get out of my way! I’ll reach her and she’ll be my wife.”

ayoreo: *Corabé*chojninga: “Jé yurúo que gu. A jé yabai yiyagónic. A disi marínga, jé yabai jne. Yapúsa yamanique jeta uñeque iya yu.” Chi gajnarénguipíse abai mu jé urúo que gu. Que chĩro darúo. Áyipie chojmára. Jecute que chãra dojo ayoréric.
english: They were jealous because the ugly person reached the top. The “finder” was*Slippery Tree Lizard*. It’s said he climbed right up that tree because he stuck to it, that’s why. He didn’t slip at all.

ayoreo: (A Dupade átapie nanique*Corabé*. Cojñone quic ujáidie u gu. Cojñoc ayoréode quic ujáidie*Jongongo*(Bolivianos) jecute ore *tabua bisidec, jnacarode. )
english: *Corabé*said: “Hurry up!” He reached up and grabbed the young girl’s hand. And he managed to put himself  right next to her. They “bit each other’s cheeks” (kissed), and joined together way up high.

ayoreo: (A Dupade átapie nanique*Corabé*. Cojñone quic ujáidie u gu. Cojñoc ayoréode quic ujáidie*Jongongo*(Bolivianos) jecute ore *tabua bisidec, jnacarode. )
english: The Ayoreos really disapproved of them because the young man was extremely ugly but his wife was very pretty.

ayoreo: Abai e na darei taja pijnani.*Corabé*chi chojninga: “A yijína ua aja jnumi jne, mu que yise baredie.” Obiréjna uje ca tṍra gajnerĩ go. A ca chise unerotiguéric. A ca tagurásu uredie.*Carujnangue Erusṍri*chácariji Corabé etarúdode gai.
english: *Corabé*said: “It happened just like I said, whoever reached me would be my husband. Even if it were a child that reached me first, he would be my husband. I gave everyone a chance to be my husband, whoever could reach me first.”

ayoreo: Mu jnacarode chetac. Ore chojninga: “¡Beyoi ga yujé ga!” Mu iguiosode narare gai ga que ore chujé. Ore chojningame*Corabé*abai: “A yico údi.”
english: Her husband was really ugly, but she kept her word. She wouldn’t erase her words. She didn’t change her mind. (She had one plan.) No one could say that she had deceived her people.

ayoreo: *(*Ore jno údi ga*Corabé*iguiosode nóna. Abai iguiosode nonapo rĩ. Ore qui tu uacanirone. Que iguínique cus*Corabé*. Jecute que uacaguínique cus. Yimóngo*Juana*(*Dye*) casica ga*Ejenai*chojninga: “A amanique déji sañeque ga aoi póroro to.*Corabé*quic ujáidie gu nanique. A cojñoque póroro irasique yoque uje ore quic ujáidie irasique yoque nanique. Ore quic ujáidie u*Corabé*nanique.” Chiraja nanique*Ejenai*uje póroro.*Queseidáquide, Esoquedéjnanái*chojninga: “Boyo yico. Yajo ore potígo to. Cachi canirac ore iji daquic ujáidie nanique.”)
english: *(**It was probably God blessing Corabé long ago. She was the source of all white people who became enemies of the Ayoreos. The Bolivians who are dark like Ayoreos descended from  the young women named Jongongo (frog), that is because the young men had sex with her without marrying her.)*

ayoreo: *Jotójnai*chugúja*Corabé*abai. Que ajénique tejnu ome go. Chi chojninga: “A yiji. Yico údi jne.”
english: *Carujnángue Erusṍrami*(Slippery Tree Lizard) put his tongue into*Corabe’s*mouth. They say that*Corabé*said: “I will go anywhere on earth with you, but I can’t find your cheeks to kiss them.” It’s plain to see that she didn’t throw her husband out for being ugly, and that she was *pleased with him, but she wouldn’t kiss him. The Lizard sat upon*Corabé’s*lap.

ayoreo: Mu todo*Corabé*abai yúode to uje iguiosode jiei.*Corabé*chosõre dabai uje ore chetágome. Uñane ore chojninga: “A yujé iji batigai jne.”
english: But all of the young men disapproved of Slippery Tree Lizard. They said: “Bring him here and we’ll kill him!”

ayoreo: Uñane ore chojninga: “Yujé ga yijna acoté aja yu.” Ore timíninga aja ajesúode, abai, jeta ore chujé. Mu chiraja ore, acoté, ga chojningame dabai: “¡Boyo yico!”
english: But Lizard’s relatives defended him and so they weren’t able to kill him. They said to*Corabé’s*husband: “We think it’s best if we leave here and take her with us!”

ayoreo: *Samane – Tobité, Bolivia – diciembre de 1960.*
english: *(*The lizard’s relatives took her off and*Corabé’s*relatives went with her. They went far away to your country (U.S.A.).*Corabé*was a beautiful white butterfly and had no dirt. That is why you missionaries don’t have any dirt either. When we first saw Jean Dye,*Ejenai*looked at how white she was and said: “She probably has a husband somewhere with white skin also. She must be a descendant of*Corabe*that lived with the*Ayoreos*long ago. That must be why these white people like us; it’s because their ancestor’s liked us long ago.”*Ejenai*knew this because he knew that*Corabé*had been white.*Quesei’s*grandfather,*Esoquedéjnai*said: “Let’s go to them. Let’s eat their food. They’re just being good to us, because they descended from us.”

ayoreo: *Joyce Davis Buchegger churúsãre Samané atatagode udojo nanique.*
english: *Jotójnai*pouted about*Corabé’s*husband. He was really upset. (Ayoré idiom: His insides were not cold at all.)  He said: “I’m going to get that husband of*Corabe’s*, and then we’ll take Corabé and them back with us.”

ayoreo: Jnani bajai baque nanique ga chise guei mu todopísa. Tibágui mu chajni, chajni yúode. Jose cucha ũrusopis iji. Chimochẽre dacãrai. Chóji gaté mu todopísa. Chicapo ta iji quigade rĩ. Chise dagúchade mu chucuepo rĩ. Chimósi ti iji purúdi. Mósiji purúdi. A basosi nanique.
english: A man from long ago was in the jungle, hunting honey, when suddenly he came upon a little boy and he was afraid of him. He would start toward the boy, and then back away. He looked different than a normal child, he thought, and that is when he started to think he might be dangerous.

ayoreo: Chichaga raja jnumi. Chojninga: “A yap ua jne. Ajína yu,” chojninga.
english: The man took hold of his pony tail and tied it up, because he was anxious to find out who and what this child was, but he still was afraid. He went back into the jungle to gather up his things, and when he’d found them, he went back to look for the child. He looked all over until he found him again.

ayoreo: Chi chojníngasia: “Yijína ua jne,” mu gajnárec uruábode. Iyajmánguiji jnumi gu, mu jé irasique re to. Chipocásusu dacarámi aja ñane ga que ore chirajási gotique.  Ore jose cucha bisidéguiji guei. Chisási dabade uje chirõre ore irengúi. Chidota re ome ore gu, ayoréode uje piogode déji. Dotãre ore irengúi gu. Areredatési*Guébi*ga que tõríasi piotique gu. Cási udui; déji acãrai, mu pósi. Chose yui. Dotãre ore irengúi gu uje piotígo déji. Mu isangóri chijnórasipísa. Chisasi jmanecábi.
english: He saw him asleep in the shadows of the trees. There was no doubt at all in his mind that he must have fallen there sometime back, and now he was resting on his little elbows on the ground. Having located the boy, the man knelt beside him and spoke to him. “Be my son, and go with me wherever I go,” he said.

ayoreo: Chojninga: “Yap ua. Que chugué ua.” Chimánosi aja degúi mu chijñecarósi aja dabai ome ore dotáningai.
english: The boy whose name was*Guébi*(Little rain) answered. But the hunter noticed that the little boy couldn’t talk clearly. “I will go with you,” he responded hesitantly. It was because he’d been on the ground asleep so long, but the man could tell that he was happy now. The boy tied his hair in a pony tail like an Ayoréi so that the people he feared couldn’t discover who he was.

ayoreo: Dire uñeque ga ore baque ga chi irasique dasasṍri. Chi chijno cutẽ́ri mu chi cajnoripísiji. Chirógaja chicṍri uje cajnóriji. Chi chimósi chicṍramí uje atíãp. Isasṍri chajire mu chojninga: “A ca chejna yuyádi jne.” Uje cajnoripise gu uje chijno cutẽ́ri iji ta. Que chajire dabi yui go mu e chijose chicṍri mu que chijose go. Chijose dacãrai. ¡Cho*sõrororo*! Yodi déji acãrai. Abi chirógaja jnumi, isasṍri.
english: All the people thought that*Guébi*was just another Ayoré boy. But when they were nearing the camp*Guébi*started crying.

ayoreo: Chojninga: “Be jnumi ga óji yijoyai. Iragupúsu bajo.”
english: He stayed back from the Ayoreos because of their fires. The camp was too warm for him; rain needs to be cold. That was why*Guébi*stayed far away from the fires.

ayoreo: Jos eti chicṍri, dayé. Chóji mu areredaté. Mu que chiraja. Jĩrique chipocásupo dacãrai gu. Jos eti chicṍri taja dajoyai. Jé abi gatoi tu udui. Mu cáma chiraja.
english: He also stayed away from the place where they had stored water, that was because*Guébi*had water in his hair; but still, he kept crying. Finally he got nearer to the people despite the campfires the Ayoreos had made, making it too warm for him. The man who had discovered him took hold of his little arm to comfort him.

ayoreo: “Cucha areredaté bajoyai. Abape que mu chicṍramí.”
english: “Don’t be afraid,” he said. “You are my little son and no one can hurt you.” And in this way he encouraged him to go into the camp; but he wasn’t comfortable because of the heat, for rain must be cool to feel right.

ayoreo: Mu*Guébi*que narárosi. Chiraja gu uje dacãrai u.
english: But the next day they went to hunt for honey, and little*Guébi*was very happy to be with the man who had discovered him.

ayoreo: Dire uñai ga bágasiapo rĩ. Dayé chojninga: “Yabi, ajína yu.”
english: The man climbed a tree and started chopping into the trunk of it to get the honey out, and it was hard work, so after a while he became thirsty. He sent his little companion to bring back the plant  called c*hicṍri*to quench his thirst. The little one brought a plant, but it was very small.

ayoreo: Chojninga: “Qué yijína ua jne.”
english: The man looked at it and said: “That little plant won’t give enough water to quench my thirst.” He said this because he was very, very thirsty from chopping the tree.

ayoreo: Mu adi jé u dadi uje cajnore uje chijno cutẽ́ri. E chiraja. Cáma chirajapise mu e chachase. “A yajiripise basocai gajnerĩ jeta ajose chicṍri.”
english: At first he didn’t know what the boy was doing. Confident that he was just squeezing water from the plant, he didn’t watch when he was twisting his hair to get water. It sounded like: ¡sooooo, sooooo! When he had enough, the boy invited the man to come down and drink. He said: “Come down, drink what I have squeezed; the gourd bowl is full of water.”

ayoreo: Chojninga: “Be chicṍri ga ajose. Yicajnóriji.”
english: His adoptive father thought the water had come from the plant, but when he drank it he found it to be very cold. He gave no thought to the idea that it had come from another source other than the*chicṍri*plant. That is because the boy had quickly tied up his hair after getting the water by twisting it. The man’s adopted son had gotten the water from his hair, but the father didn’t know that yet.

ayoreo: Adi jé u bajai tu. Doi chicṍri  mu abape yugábia. Chi chajni cutẽ́ri isangóri mu jĩrique edo uña, edo gapudi chajire yui uje e chachase diríca gu. Chijose dacãrai mu e chiraja. Todopísa dabi.
english: The father said: “The water you gave me was very cold, my son. The plant was very small, but what a lot of water came from it!”

ayoreo: Chojninga: “A cuchape yabi ¡jõ!”
english: But*Guébi*said nothing. He knew exactly where the water had come from and how he’d gotten such a lot of it, but he didn’t tell his father.

ayoreo: Uje chejñu dacãrai mu yodi tṍraji. Jotábia désiji gai.
english: The next day they went hunting again. The man said: “My son, come with me.”

ayoreo: Chóji dabi ijoyai mu areredaté. “Cuchape ua ya, yabí a? A apógu batígome yu.” Mu que narárosi ababi.
english: *Guébi*said: “I’ll go with you, Iapá (Father).”

ayoreo: Quigade mu e jnosi. Que yiraja gotique uje jnosi uje urása ayoréode. Diyajmánguiji ore gari.
english: The same thing happened. The father climbed the tree and was chopping into the trunk to get honey, and he told his son that he was thirsty. As he was cutting a hole in the tree he started to suspect that perhaps the water from the day before had not come from the plant. He wasn’t sure, but he said to himself: ‘This time, my son, I’m going to observe carefully to learn where you get the water.”

ayoreo: Ore bágapo rĩ uje jnosi gajnerĩ. Mu que jnusina dabi iji degúi gotique jeta chijína. Chojninga: “Yicadique yabi tuqué ¡jõ! A ujórique disírique chujé gajnerĩ. A pósi ga.  A jnósi ga.”
english: He called to his son: “Bring me a*chicṍri*plant and squeeze out the water. I’m thirsty.”

ayoreo: Mu ore tibidósi*Guébi*, jnani bajade abode. Ore chojninga: “Bo ji te á. Yuchígobi ñane. Yinganángope ñane.”
english: The same thing happened. The plant that he brought was small. When the boy got back to the tree, his father stole glances at his son down on the ground to discover where he would get the water. When he saw his son twisting his hair, he knew, and at that moment he began to fear his son.

ayoreo: Ore chuchíesi ñane uje ore canare ñane. Mu*Guébi*pésu bajac, daguíjnai. Chojninga: “Ayape jne. Jé yipésu yiguíjnai bajac.”
english: He said to himself: ‘My son is something supernatural! When he takes down his hair, water pours out of it – he’s full of water!’

ayoreo: Uásejna ãre gu. Que ore chiraja uje dayode doi gajnerĩ.*Guébi*que pota pajñamini mu ore potapísa, ayoréode abode. E pésusu daguíjnami ga uerat iguíjnai. Chimate ga e doi ore. Canare ore mu qué na dasangóri ugúchade taja iguíjnai bajaque. Mu uñeque chuchíesi de jmanéro ga chi quĩrique ájningarape to. Urõso uchiéjnai ji jmanéro. Chengame uráya mu quedéjna. Cho ajnorane. Gaidi tu jeta ore chudute, urasade.
english: He drank the water that his son held up to him, and again he found it very cold. He spoke to his son and asked: “Are you supernatural, my son? Are you hiding something from me?”

ayoreo: Chajúguiji ti iguíjnai piagode, urasade. Chingo aja ore gu uje ore doi jnumi. Iyajmánguipis uje base gotique nanique, mu que ore chiraja, jnani bajade. Ore cho guei iji. Que yiraja jeta Jesuse u nanique. Ore doi jnumi, urásade. Ore jnosi údi. Uásejnapise guei uje jnosi gajnerĩ. Ore chajire mu e ore dosi ore ababi iji jnumi. Ore gaidábi dababi iji jnumi.
english: But his little son didn’t say a word. Afterwards, the boy took off running. I don’t know why, because now he was an Ayoréi and had lived among them a long time.

ayoreo: *Guébi*chojninga: “Yapade a ore di jne,” mu que ore todo. Ore josipise ayorepísiji dayé. Dayé u guéode. Que ojningai u ayoré go, isasṍri. Chojninga: “Ca acayóji yiguíjnai. Sijnac uágome yu gu.”
english: Finally, the boy returned, and some time later the father went off to hunt with the other men. He didn’t think of taking the boy, however, and he left him in the camp.

ayoreo: Mu ore chojninga: “Que guérique cuse to,” disiode.
english: But then he started thinking about his son and he said: “Oh, no, I have forgotten my little son. The other children will probably mistreat him. Probably he is crying right now and will run off and hide again.”

ayoreo: Mu chi e beque ga uásejnapise. Guei abujade iji ore ejode. Cho dejade. Uásejnapise guei, mu*Guébi*que pota disi sijnángone ore ucode ome daguíjnai. Que pota chequé sijnángueraque, jnani sijnátique rĩ. Chetac ore. Pota ayoré parágode ome daguíjnai jeta ore chacája ome guei ome dayode.
english: Back in the camp the sons of the men who lived long ago invited*Guébi*to come out and play with them. They said: “Come here. Let’s kick each other and play together.”

ayoreo: E ore toi ayoré abode uje guei chinire poridie. Que pijoque jnutic. Mu ipesudi iguíjnai chicai gaté. Yodi que chacája go. Mu que pota jeta chujé isangṍri, acoté ore. Ore déji erámi mu e ore chicai iguíjnai yui. Isangóri abode dépoji iguíjnai ajei to. Que pota jéta chujé ore. Chimo dasangṍri ga jĩrique tṍra ore aja daguíjnai ajei. Irasique ore gu. Mu e jno.
english: And then, the children started kicking each other and playing. But*Guébi*began to make a house. He said to them: “I’ll play with you after I finish my house.”

ayoreo: Chojningame dasangṍri: “Arángome cucha sijnángone jne iji yiguíjnai.”
english: It was hot and he made the house for shade. The other children didn’t know that very soon*Guébi*’s heavenly parents were coming to get him.*Guébi*didn’t want to play, but the other children really wanted him to play.

ayoreo: Isangóri chãraque. Iyajmanguipise mu que ore tagu dabotic uje iyajmaque guei casodicai, mu ore borade déji ore gari iji ajei, yocade mu e garósi uje guedoside e gare. E pijoque poridie acarane. E ore changureta. Guedoside chejna yijmanai uje e íjnoque yodi iji erámone. Ore doi guesi iji iguíjnai. Ore chajuque dabai uje que piagórique cus. Ore tṍraji dasingapídi.
english: He worked on his house and it turned out very well. When it was finished, he went off to play with the children, but first he put his adoptive father’s things in his house.

ayoreo: *Samané – Tobité, Bolivia – 1970.*
english: But soon one of the children kicked*Guébi*’s arm and he got mad. It really hurt him when the boy kicked him.

ayoreo: *Joyce Davis Buchegger churúsãre Samané atatagode udojo.*
english: He raised one of his arms, and it was strange, for immediately a flash of lightning went out from his armpit. He did it so that his people up in the sky would hear it.

ayoreo:
*Ujetiga panguretigai ca nóna dajirigode enga cucha bisidécho Dupade Uruode ome uté gosi:*Enga ingome yoque to, ujetiga inoningáco, uje jé ũré*Dupade*ome daruode. Mu yoque gosi ujetiga ca chibéro iji dangoneparitigade ome*Dupade**Uruode*, enga jé u uje cho cucha bisidécho iji, ga que cuchárique tṍraji gotique ujetiga chajire jeoga chudute, ome yajéode.

Ujeta ca gosi changureta*Dupade*ome re, mu doi*Dupade**Uruode*, mu que chajire yúrique go, enga obiréjna uje cucha
bisidécho ome.

Ga chise gójni diri ujetiga*Dupade*di ga gosi jnusietigade jogadi tu,
goto jnusiétigade jogadi tu uje ore chise*Dupade**Uruode*bisideque nanique, enga que
panguretigai nóna dajirigode*Dupade*urúo.

**Jnacare ugucharãi quene áyipie déji Dupade Uruode úringai ũré uje chingo raja gajño pioi.**Jé uacadúcho, uje jecucha yingome uaque gu, uje chi jnacare ugucharãi chingo raja jnaropié te ga, jé jno ga chojningame yocai jnani bajai Abraham: “Ango yesade ore jeta ca ore chicai gajño pioi to,” chojninga.

Chingo raja Abraham gu, jeta
yoque gosi a adi tu ujetiga chijnóra darásarigo, ga chetaque jóroque desade
ome gajño pioi yui.

Chojningame Abraham: “Que yisapo
yicaniratiguei gotique iji. Arenie yuque yu iji,” chojninga. “Achopise
jnupuchade garapídode iji yu,” chojninga. “Achopise pioi ocaráidie iji,”
chojninga.

Mu yocayode jnani bajade uñai
chojninga: “Mu*Dupade**Uruode*déji jnumi,” chojninga, “ujetiga besade
ore chudute ga ore changureta*Dupade*.”

Mu quigade ga uté chojninga:
“Jé go ca aroque togode uñai,*Dupade*anguresórone uñeque, a chingo yesade ore iji jnumi enga ca ore chicai jnaropié
to,” chojninga.

Mu Abraham chojninga: “A yiroque
togode uñéngaja enga gosi di bojningane údi ome ore, mu dicoré,” chojninga. “Taque-taque
uje dicoré. Dicoré que chiraja cuchade. Ca besade ore changureta*Dupade*ome ore ojñáringo,” chojninga.

Ga yoque gosi ta adi tu jetiga
gosi chingo raja aníri, gajño pioi.

**Jé ũré Dupade ome ojningane uje chi idógosi uté uje e iroco yúode.**Mu jé uacamóño uje chi*Dupade**Uruode*chojninga: “A yiríringárique yúode ga pocaningane, unojo basája uñane quigade. A yiríringárique yúode jne enga chaguhéode déji erámi.”

Mu cáma yico chaguhéode gotique,*Dupade*ojningai. Mu jé déji punire
quedéjnane.

Jé cachi uñéngo ore tagu dabosode iji udore uje
chaguhéode chejna ore. Jé basa ga pocaningane ga ugúcoji jnese iji dirode
jnese.

Jé basa ga guédodie ga guédodie
jecucha basíji gatajnoquei, mu cama basipise go.

Ga Jesus chojninga: “Yãrapiedie unojo yui cáma yĩri
gotique. Yãrapiedie a cuse jne uje yiríringai idógosi gu. Guedé chécãre
dáringai guedé te ga, que qué guedé chisapo dáringai guedé gotique jne rĩ,”
chojninga.

“Guedé ta toi, ga topié erámi.
Jé basa ga guedoside ojodie que chuságapo jnumi gotique jne rĩ,” chojninga.
“Ga unojo u uje yiríringai idógosi,” chojninga Jesus. “Jeta yĩri iji jnumi ome
yanguresórone ga yiji ore údi.”

Ga unojo u jetiga anóningacho
uje chi*Dupade**Ujnacari*diapo jne rĩ.  Enga
que ajéninga daruode,*Dupade*. Mu que
di gu uje qué chirote ayoréode uyoque uñéngo jetiga yanguretágo.*(2 Pedro 3:7-10)*

**Jesus diringai a gajnui u yote quedéjna cuchábi uje israelgosode ore chaquesu nanique.**Uje israel gosode ore déji yote quedéjnai uéchai uñai nanique enga*Dupade*chojningame ore: “Cá boyo ji te á. Yaro yotequedejnai uje ajedaté mu yaróji,” chojninga.*Dupade*charo yotequedéjnai ga yotequedéjnai chetingare re, chetingare re iji unojo uéchade.*(Exodo 14:21 aja 22)*

Ga yote quedéjnai chetingare re,
chetingare re iji unojo uéchade. Mu uje Faraón, soldádode ore chise yote
quedéjnai uté mu ore chajuque yote quedéjnai uté cajnóningai to, mu ore
chuningame eti yodi chaco ore gáyode. Uje yodi chetingare re, chetingare re, mu
e*Dupade*juega doi ome enga jnopója
dajogadi casé rĩ.*(Exodo 14:26 aja 28)*

Ore uñane iji dirode udojo gajnerĩ ore chotigase Jesus diringai, enga jé adi u ome Faraón ore. Ore jos eti ca yotequedéjnai dopóji dajogadi, mu ore chuningame eti, yore ore gai enga chujé ore. Enga jé cho jetiga Jesus di. Cho jetiga ca di jne, cho jetiga*Dupade**Uruode*ca tocade jne ome dajnacari diringai, cho jetiga ca ũré*Dupade*ome ojningane cucha. Mu qué chirote jeta ayorérigo uñéngo changureta. Mu a chise diri jne ujetiga Jesus di jne. Enga otigayasórone a ore bijna, jé basa cho jetiga anguresórone ore chise tié uechai uñai uje ore angureni Jesus jno ore údi.

Unojo u uje Dupade que ajéninga
darúrique.

**Israélgosode ore jos eti ore jnoji Egípto enga jĩraque ore chise ore uniri ichárique jne.**Mu chaguhéode chejna ore iji dajei. Jecute ore chojninga: “Boyo, yicoi, yajnésacoja Egípto uje yajo yoquibotígo iji ta gu. Mu Moises qué jno ore údi aja punire ichárique, mu que ore chíse míraque. Jé basa ore chise erãpetaréode ga íjnoque cucha pibotique iji uté. Ga ore tagu arurégode, gatajnoquei jogonórone. Ore chipese cuchade uje tṍraja gatajnoquei. Enga que yotique unératique cuse ome ore.”

Ore chojningame Moises: “E
yicajnócoji. ¿Ga jé yójigo pírique jne a?”

Ga jé u uje Moises chutṍri*Dupade*jeta chátaja ore. Enga*Dupade*chingo pite quedéjnai aja Moises
ga Moises chisa ga tṍraja yote pepui uté ajei ga jé u uje nóña ga yote
unere.*(Exodo 15:22
aja 25 ore)*

Iji taningai nanique uje*Dupade*chiroque Moises ujetiga chijnime israélgosode iji Faraón
jmainie enga Moises todo. Mu*Dupade*nona
aja quedéjnane.*(Exodo 3:11 aja 12)*

Enga unojo u uje yirajágo uje
chi ca*Dupade*cho darupatáringa ome
uñeque. Uje chojningame Moises nanique uje chátaja ore jne, enga do gojni enga
chátaja Moises urasade ore ome cucha pibosode jeta ore tagu, jé aja yodi jeta
ore chóji.

Jé cho uje*Dupade*chingome yoque to uje a ujnacari di ome danguresórone uyoque
jne. Enga*Dupade*que cho
darupatáringa ome yoque. Jecute*Dupade*ujnacari a di jne ujetiga tiémpo u ome dayé.

Uje*Dupade*que ajéninga darúrique gu.

**Abraham catecã́ri Dupade ome daguiosode uje ore déji guidaidatei uje chi Dupade chejna jne.**Abraham catecã́ri*Dupade*enga chojninga: “¿Jeta jnani parágode déji ore idáyode udore enga jé ejnárase jne a? Ujetiga 50 u enga be ga amesẽre ore jne enga ca ejna ore idánique.” Enga Abraham chigotapísaja daruode ome*Dupade*ga chojninga: “Ujetiga 40 u, jeoga 30 u ore, jeoga 20 u, jeoga 10 u, mu ca ejna ore idáyode.”

Chi*Dupade*chojningame jnani bajai Abraham nanique uje a jé uacadúcho
aníri, to: “A yise parágode uje chejna yijmanane ore, enga a ca yejna ore
idáyode jne.” Mu*Dupade*que chise uje
10 u parágode iji guidaidatéode udore jnese. Mu*Dupade*chejnárase ore jnese iji ore idáyode ujetiga jé ca Abraham
catecã́ri ome ore.*Dupade*que chise parágode uje udore chejna yijmanane, mu chise
gagájni enga chiqueta gagájnione ore uje chejna guidáyode udore.

Gusu Abraham iguiose gagájnione
nanique uje paraque ore gu. A paraque ore gu uje udore ore dai uñai Abraham
cho ore quique ome*Dupade*adode. Mu
uje ore déji idaidatei guesi enga*Dupade*chiroque pioi aja jnumi enga chejnápise guidai gárode udore enga udore
jogosórone toi jnese.

Ga unojo u uje*Dupade*que ajéninga darúrique go.

Yocangarádejnai*Dupade*chojningame re: “A yingórasa
Abraham yipesutique cucha ome uje a yipésu iji ore idaidatéode udore jne. Uje
que cucha bisideque Abraham uté gu,” chojninga, “jecute a yingo yáyipiedie aja
jne uje gajneque yuruode gu.”

Adi jé u ome yoque to, uje*Dupade*que cho cucha bisideque iji
danguresórone uyoque, uje yocajneque uruode to gu, enga yijnórango*Dupade**Uruode*, enga que yoco cucha bisidéguiji.

Ga unojo u uje*Dupade*que ajéninga darúrique go.

**Dupade chingome yoque uje a tṍra Satanas, urasade ore aja gajño pioi jne.**Chi*Dupade*ujnacari diapo jne rĩ iji jnumi uné, enga jé u jne ujetiga*Dupade*tṍra Satanase, urasade ore aja gajño pioi, ga que ore chimopo pijoningai ome re rĩ. Ga jé unojo u uje*Dupade*chingome yoc ga ingongo ome yoque to. Ingongome purasade ore. Jé basa yingongo ore jne, enga chajuque gosi agutéraque ujeta qué chudute udore jeta ca changureta*Dupade*to. Que yanguretágo*Dupade*, mu uduco unojo ga yoque gosi chajéngome*Dupade*.

Uje a gosi chojninga: “¿Gotique
a yipesutigo uje chi paranája yu aja gajño pioi jne?”

Mu uje que ipéco*Dupade**Uruode*,*Dupade*ojningane atatagode, enga u uje Satanase urása yoc enga
uté qui jé u yoqui to.

“Gusu*Dupade**Uruode*ome yoc uje chingo yoquipesutígo ome yoc,” ore chojninga. “Jeta chingome yu ga a yipésu to uje que yipota*Dupade*ujuyaquei.”

Mu que gusu go ujetiga gosi pota
ga chojninga, “Que yipota*Dupade*ujuyaquei, ga a yangureta to.” Amiri u ujetiga changureta*Dupade ujnacari*enga cho daquetangóri iji uté.

Jé basa ga gosi pota jeta chiraja datígo ome*Dupade**Uruode*, ga a gosi chimesẽre datígo ome*Dupade*adode ga gosi chingome yu iji deréjnai udé enga a yo uté
gosi quique jne.

Gusu yacajnaquéode ome yoc.

**Mu yicatecã́ri Dupade jne:**“Yoque*Dupade*Dios, icatecã́ri ua iji deréjnai udé uje yacaranguipis ome ua,*Dupade*, uje jec ũrepise baruode. Jec ũrepise ua ome bojningane cucha. Icatecã́ri ua iji gajnerĩ uje yacaranguipis ome ua uje banguresórone que ore chacho ujetiga ore tangári baruode, yoque*Dupade*.

Yoque*Dupade*, apésu yoque gajnerĩ ga yoco jnani bajai David te ga,
jetiga ãro yoquisijnaringuéone. Ãro betétigade iji yocuruode. Ãro Satanase
píode iji yocuruode enga ichapise yoque gajnerĩ, yoque*Dupade*. Jeta barasique yoque aja yocajesúode erámio gajnerĩ, yoque*Dupade*.

*Dupade*, átaja yóguiji
yoquidai gajnerĩ, enga yijmacopise baruode aja yocáyipiedie enga yirajágo
udore quigode.

Yoque*Dupade*, icatecã́ri ua ujetiga jec ũré ga yanguretágo ua ga ãro
yoquisijnaringuéone uje yotigáco baruode, yoque*Dupade*.

Icatecã́ri ua ujeta asíome
yoque bosõraquei tome yoque, ga osóre yóguiji jnumi.

Yosõco uñane iji jnumi, yoque*Dupade*, ome baruode.

Yicatecã́ri ua ome cuchade
jnese gajnerĩ uje yirajágo uje bajnacari tu uje tẽra re ome dayoi iji jnumi
nanique ome yoc ujetiga churu yoquisijnaringuéone. Churu yocuru patáringane iji
yocajéode.

*Dupade*icatecã́ri ua
ujetiga átaja yoque gajnerĩ. A ayoréode uyóguiji erámi jnese ga yocuñéngo
chejna raja baruode, yoque*Dupade*. Átaja
banguresórone ore iji ne, ga yejnángo yóguiji baruode to, yoque*Dupade*. Icatecã́ri ua ome cuchade jnese
ujetiga átaja yóguiji erámi jnese gajnerĩ ome baruode batigade.

Átaja yu gajnerĩ. Ejnaretaque
yu enga ãro yoquejnaréngone iji yocúdode, yoque*Dupade*, ga asi ome pijóningai tome yoque, asíome betotiguei tome
yoc, yoque*Dupade*. Asíome pijoningai
ome yocurasade, yoque*Dupade*, enga
ijõre yoque ujetiga bajéo.

Átaja yoque jnese gajnerĩ ome baruode ga yoquirasique yoque gajnerĩ ujetiga yicoi guesi enga yocáyipiedie déji baruode gajnerĩ uje jé udúco. Yicatecã́ri ua ome Jesucrísto i, Amẽ.

*Ecarai – Tobité, Bolivia – 1960**Marina de Morarie churúsãre Ecarai atatagode udojo nanique*
english:
*Jé ũré Dupade ome daruode’*

**If faith does not accompany the reading of****Dupade’s****Word, it won’t****help a****person:**

I’m telling us, so that we’ll know, that what*Dupade*says is true.

But, this is what I think: when one of us doesn’t break from his disobedience,*Dupade*’s*words*mean nothing to him at all, and he gets nothing out of it when he reads or hears it.

When a person doesn’t apply*Dupade*’s*Words*to his own life, even if he attends church where*Dupade*’s*W**ords*are preached, he won’t pay attention to them, and it will be obvious that they mean nothing to him.

But the day is coming, as it was predicted, when that man will be sad, or that woman will be sad, because, for them,*Dupade’s**W**ords*had no importance, and faith did not accompany*Dupade*’s*W**ords*when they read them.

**A rich man only thought about Dupade’s true Words after he went to the ‘The place of the dead’:**

You’ve already heard this, for I have already told you about it, how a rich man who died and went to*Jnaropié,*the place of the dead, went to talk to our father Abraham, who lived long ago. He said to him: “Tell my brothers not to come to the*jnaropié*.”

He came to Abraham for help, just as any one of us would do that loved his brothers and didn’t want, in vain, for them to end up in the ‘place of the dead’.

He said to Abraham: “I can’t get comfortable here at all. I’m a victim of extreme heat,” he said. “I’m so tired of being where the worms are,” he said, “and I’m so tired of the flames.” This is what he said.

But Abraham who was one of our forefathers said: “*Dupade*’s*Words*are on earth; if your brothers hear them, they might believe in*Dupade*.”

But, after hearing this, he said to Abraham: “Why don’t you send one of the dead people that believe in*Dupade*to tell my brothers on earth about him? Then, my brothers won’t have to go to*Jnaropié*.”

But Abraham said: “I could send one of the dead ones to take your words to them, but they would be spirits (without bodies).” This is what Abraham said to him. “They could walk around but being spirits they wouldn’t be able to do anything else. Your brothers couldn’t believe in*Dupade*because of anything they could say.”

Any of us, were we to end up in the lake of fire, would be concerned about our relatives on earth.

**What****Dupade****tells about the coming of the O****ne we****’ve been****wait****ing for****is****true****”**

But you already know that*Dupade*’s*Words*say: “There will be wars just before my arrival, and other things happening, one right after the other. Around the time of my coming there will be famines on earth.”

But we’ve never had famines like those that*Dupade*tells about. But in some countries, they are already happening. Only a few have food to eat in places where hunger is finishing people off. In other places, wars are happening to us earth dwellers every day.

Stars have always fallen, but not like what’s going to happen soon. And Jesus said: “These signs of my coming will happen, but I won’t arrive yet. But they will happen when my arrival is getting near. The sun will completely change and will never return to how it was.” This is what Jesus said.

“The sun will die, and the earth will be dark. And the light from the moon will not ever reach the earth again.” This is what Jesus said. “These will happen when my arrival is very near, and when I return for my believers, I’ll take them away.”

And I tell you these things, so that you all will understand that*Dupade*’s*Son*is going to arrive again. And*Dupade*will keep his word. But he won’t come just yet because he is waiting for others of us Ayoréos to put our faith in him.*(2 Peter 3:1-10)*

**The coming of Jesus is like the river the israelites crossed on dry ground:**

Some people these days doubt that Jesus will ever come again, and they are like Pharaoh and his army. They thought that*the strange water*would never return to its place, coming back together again. But when they least expected it, the sea covered them and destroyed them. And that is how it will be when Jesus comes. It will seem like he won’t ever come, it will seem like*Dupade*’s*Words*weren’t true. But the day will come when Jesus will return. And the doubters will be lost. But not the believers – it will be as though they will reach the other bank of the river because they trusted Jesus to save them.

These things are because*Dupade*always keeps his word.

**The Israelites thought that****when they****le****ft****Egypt****they would immediately****reach their new land.**

Butthey suffered hunger on the road. And they said: “Come on, let’s return to Egypt for we had food to eat there.”

But Moses kept on taking them toward the new land, but they didn’t reach it right away. Instead, they came to a desert and there was no food there. And they sucked on bread that came down from the sky. They existed on what fell from the sky. And there was no potable water for them to drink.

They said to Moses: “We’re thirsty. What are we going to drink?”

And then, Moses asked*Dupade*to help them. And*Dupade*pointed out to him a strange stick, and he picked it up and threw it into the bitter water and it went down into the depth of the water, and the water became potable.*(Exodus 15:22-25)*

At the very beginning when*Dupade*called Moses to take the Israelites out of Pharaoh’s hands, Moses was afraid.*(Exodus 3:11-12)*

And the reason I’m telling you these things is so that you will know that*Dupade*does not lie to anyone. When he said to Moses that he would help him, he did what he said he would do, and he helped Moses and his people with food to eat and water to drink.

And what*Dupade*has told us about his*Son*coming back for his believers will happen.*Dupade*has not lied to us. Therefore,*Dupade*’s*Son*will come when his Father says it is time.

For*Dupade*always keeps his word.

**God was going to destroy a town and Abraham prayed for him not to destroy it:**

Abraham said to*Dupade:*“If there are righteous people in that city will you still destroy it? If there are 50 of them, then accept them and don’t destroy their city.” And Abraham went on repeating these*words*of his and said: “If there are 40, or 30, or 20, or even 10, then don’t destroy their towns.”

Then, after hearing his prayers,*Dupade*said to Abraham (but you have no doubt already heard this): “If I find 10 righteous people, then I won’t destroy the cities.” But*Dupade*did not find 10 righteous people in either of those cities. And*Dupade*would have destroyed everyone in those cities if Abraham had not interceded for them. Though*Dupade*didn’t find 10 righteous men, he did find four, and he took then out of the city, before he destroyed those cities.

Only Abraham’s four relatives were saved because they were righteous. They were righteous because their uncle Abraham had explained*Dupade*’s ways to them. But when they were safely outside the city, then*Dupade*sent fire down upon the city people and destroyed the two cities and all their inhabitants.

And I tell this because*Dupade*always keeps his word.

*Our Great Leader that we listen to*,*Dupade*, had said to himself: “I should tell Abraham what I am going to do to those cities. For he is worthy of my confidence, so, I am going to tell him my plans and give him my words.

It’s the same way with us, for*Dupade*does not consider us who believe in him to be unworthy, and we have his words, and we love*Dupade*’s*Words*and we would never consider them unimportant.

I say this because*Dupade*always keeps his word.

**Dupade****tells us that he is going to throw Satan and his followers into the****l****ake of****f****ire.**

*Dupade*’s*Son*is coming back to earth again, and then,*Dupade*will throw Satan and his followers into the lake of fire, from which they will never depart, never again recover their health. And this is what*Dupade*tells us and we tell it to each other. We tell it to all the people we know. And when we tell them,*it**splits the gall bladders*of those that do not believe in*Dupade*.

Let’s say that one of us hears about this and is ashamed before*Dupade*. And he says: “What have I done to deserve being thrown into the lake of fire?”

But when any of us disobeys*Dupade*’s*Words*and does not obey what*Dupade*tells him to do, then it is that he becomes one of Satan’s countrymen, and where Satan goes, that will also be his destination.

People might conclude: “Only what*Dupade*’s*Words*say will show us what to do about this terrible predicament. If I find out what it says to do, I’ll do it, for I don’t want*Dupade*to punish me.”

There’s something lacking in what he is saying, he must trust*Dupade*’s*Son*as his Savior.

If someone hearing this wants to know more about this, what the*Words*of*Dupade*are, and has decided to follow*Dupade*, he should come to me and I will explain it to him.

This is the end of my teaching.

**I****will****talk to****Dupade****:**

*Yoqui-**Dupade*Dios, I talk to you today because I am so thankful for the fact that whatever you say is true. You are truthful in what you say. I’m talking to you right now and so thankful to you that your believers are not growing weary of listening to your words.

*Yoqui-**Dupade*, work in our hearts so that we might be like David who lived long ago; forgive our sins as you did David’s sins. Forgive the things we say that you disapprove of. Forgive us right now for the way we let Satan have control of our words, that you might be pleased with us as we go about this earth.

*Dupade*, help us here in our village to get as many of your words into our minds as we can, and that we will understand them.

*Yoqui-**Dupade*, I’m talking to you and asking you that we’ll be sincere believers in you, and that you will forgive our sins of unbelief.

I ask you to give us your mercy and be very merciful to us. And that we might be merciful and kind to each other in obedience to your word.

I’m asking you for all these things because we know that your Son it was who shed his blood for us to cleanse us from our sins. To wash from out hearts the lies we’ve told.

*Dupade*, I ask you to help us right now. No doubt, there are some of us who follow you with all our hearts. Help all your believers that are here. May we honor your words,*Yoqui-Dupade.*I am talking to you about all these things and asking you to help us all over the world right now*wherever-your-words -fall.*

Help me, right now. I am sick and ask for you to erase the sickness in my body,*Yoqui-**Dupade*. And give us health and strength and, if it’s your will, help all our countrymen to be healthy.

Bless us with your words as we leave here, and may our minds be on what we have just heard.

I ask this in the name of Jesus Christ. Amen.

Jnaropié –Place of the dead

The strange waters – Sea, the Red Sea

Yoqui-Dupade – Our God

Where-your-word-falls – Place where the Word is taught, church

Our Great Leader that we listen to – Lord

To-split-someone’s-gall-bladder – To frighten someone

*Ecarai**–**Tobité, Bolivia**– 1970’s.*

*Transcribed and**T**ranslated to English by**:**Maxine Morarie**.*

ayoreo: *Dupade*, bagúsi guéode uéchai.*Dupade,*bamo yoque jnese, yoque*Dupade*. Uje ua u, uje bapesute erámi tuné,*Dupade*, bapesute ayoréode ore, enga baraja ayoréode ore yocajéode,*Dupade*.
english: *Dupade*, you dwell above the sky, you see all of us,*Dupade*, you are the one we believe in. It is you who created this earth, you who made the people in it, and you,*Dupade*, know what we are thinking.

ayoreo: Yoque*Dupade*, yicatecã́ri ua ujetiga átabája uyoque iji deréjnai tuné. Iji deréjnai unojo ujetiga yichago yoquenominone aja ua,*Dupade*. Yoque*Dupade*yirajágo uje pujopié déji ua ujetiga écãre ayoréode yocadode,*Dupade*, ujetiga yimóngo cucha ũréone iji baruode to.
english: *Dupade*, you are ours. We ask you to help us today to turn our eyes upon you. We know how powerful you are,*Dupade*, powerful enough to change us and to show us true things in your Words.

ayoreo: Yirajágo,*Dupade*, uje yocumajosṍri déji uje Satanas u, enga Satanas u*dicore*,*Dupade*. Satanas chetaque baruode. Que pota gotique,*Dupade*, ujetiga ayoréode yirajágo baruode, mu yirajágo uje Satanase sue yoquibai uje ujopié cuse uje sue yoquetotiguei gai,*Dupade*. Sue yocáyipie ayoré gai, mu yocacaranguipise,*Dupade*, uje yocangureni tu babi Jesuse,*Dupade*. Yocangureni tu babi Cristo,*Dupade*, enga babi Cristo chingome yoque uje uté déji yocari,*Dupade*. Enga uté pota ujetiga chátaja yoque,*Dupade*, ujetiga chisi ome pirajtigai tome yoque jeta yirajágo baruode. Jé upo rĩ,*Dupade*, uje chirote yoque, ujetiga aniri, uté chãraque yogome Satanase.
english: We know,*Dupade*, that we have an enemy who is*Satan*, and that*Satan*is a walking spirit.*Satan*hates your Word. He doesn’t want us to know your Words, and we know that*Satan*is more powerful than we are, and his strength is greater than ours. He is smarter than we humans are, but how thankful we are that we have your Son*Jesus*and he is*the one we listen to*, and he is much greater than our enemy*Satan*. We trust in your*Son Christ Jesus*and your Son tells us that he is with us. He tells us he wants to help us and give us understanding of your Words. He also takes care of us and protects us from*Satan*.

ayoreo: Yoque*Dupade*, yocacaranguipise uje ayoréode uñane changureta ua,*Dupade*– direquedéjnagosode, cochacoigosode ore – uje ore changureta babi Jesuse to. Yoque*Dupade*yirajágo uje sijnaque yoque icaite,*Dupade*. Que yirajágo baruode gotique icaite,*Dupade*.
english: Oh,*Dupade*, you are the one we trust. We are so grateful to you, that now we*Direquedéjnagosode*and*Cochacóigosode*know you because we believe in your*Son Jesus*. We know that we were fierce and evil people before,*Dupade*, but that was when we did not know your Words.

ayoreo:
Que yirajágo baruode gotique icaite,*Dupade*, que yirajágo baruode uje chise
direquedéjnaigosode, cochacoigosode ore, uyoque. Enga yacaranguipise,*Dupade*, iji dirode unojo uje ayoréode
uñane déji,*Dupade*, iji direquedéjnagosode,
cochacoigosode ore, uje baruode e chise ore,*Dupade*. Bapésu baruode iji banguretasórone ore,*Dupade*. Enga,*Dupade*, iraja to,*Dupade*,
uje adi tu uje sijnaque yoque icaite,*Dupade*,
iji erámone. Que yirajágo baruode gotique icaite,*Dupade*. Enga adi tu uje bécãre yocadode uje yanguretágo ua,*Dupade*.
english: We didn’t know your Words before they reached us D*irequedéjnaigosode*and*Cochacóigosode*. So we are so thankful that in these days these two groups of Ayoréos have heard your Word and have believed in you. Your Words have been working in our hearts. I know we used to be fierce people, living in the jungle. We didn’t know your Words then. But once we knew your Words, you changed us because we believed in You.

ayoreo: Angureta ua to,*Dupade*, jeta bojnigaite udire déji yoquiquei ujetiga guidaigosode ore uñéngo, yoquijorane guidaigosode uje yoquipotigádi tu ujetiga ore changureta ua to*Dupade*. Diga uñéngo ore chisi ome rágome ua to,*Dupade*, ujetiga amate baruode iji ore to,*Dupade*.
english: I have faith in you,*Dupade*, that one day you’re going to do what we asked concerning our friends, the*Guidaigosode.*And some of them will also believe in you. And some of them will give themselves to you and you will put your Words in them.

ayoreo:
*Dupade*,
adute yoquicatecãraitigade unojo,*Dupade*.
Jecucha icatecãcoi ua,*Dupade*, ome
guidaigosode ore,*Dupade*, uje yocujo
ayoré ore gu. Aqueta ore to,*Dupade*,
iji pisijnaringuéone,*Dupade*. Jec adi
u ujetiga ajna uñéngo to ome baruode,*Dupade*,
ujetiga uñéngo changureta babi Jesuse to,*Dupade*.
english: *Dupade*, hear these prayers of ours. We have been praying for the*Guidaigosode*for a long time, for they are our fellow Ayoréos. Save them, also,*Dupade*, from sin. In that way you can take some of them and, through your Words, bring them to faith in your*Son Jesus*.

ayoreo:
Asi ome pirajatigai tome yoque jnese,*Dupade*. Asi ome pirajatigai tome
yoquijorane guidaigoso ore urasade misionéro ore,*Dupade*, banguresórone ore,*Dupade*,
ujetiga ore chiraja bajegaidode cucha,*Dupade*,
ome ore jetiga ore chacate yoquijorane guidaigose ore ome baruode to,*Dupade*. Bei baruode ome yoquijorane guidaigoso
uje yoquipotigádi u ujetiga baruode chise erámi jnese ujetiga babi Jesuse
chimate daruode iji yoque enga uté diapo rĩ ujetiga jno danguretasóningo ore
údi,*Dupade*.
english: Give understanding to all of us,*Dupade*. And give understanding to our friends, the*Guidaigosode*and to the missionaries who are with them who believe in you also. Help them to know how to teach the*Guidaigosode*about your Word also. Take your Word to our friends the*Guidaigosode*, for we want the whole world to be reached with your Word so that we all may know what your*Son Jesus*has said about coming back for his believers and taking them with him to*Dupade*’s town.

ayoreo:
Ijíñongome yoquicatecãraitigade unojo jnese iji
deréjnai tuné iji bajmainie,*Dupade*. Ome
babi Jesuse i. Amen
english: We leave our prayers in your hands,*Dupade*, because of your*Son Jesus’s*name. Amen.

ayoreo: *Comai – Zapoco, Bolivia – 1960**Marina de Morarie churúsãre Comai icatecãraitigade udojo nanique*
english: *Comai – Zapoco, Bolivia – 1960.*

ayoreo: Ujetiga uacuñéngo changureta Dupade ga ca uacuñéngo chajéngome Dupade to. Uje Dupade jé que cho ayoréode uyoque go, jé que cho cojñone ugucharã́tique go, jé cãra re ome yoque. Uacuñéngo chejna raja Dupade to.
english: *Ca uacuñéngo chajéngome Dupade to***D** If any of you say you believe in*Dupade*, don’t be ashamed of him. For*Dupade*is not like us human beings at all, and he’s not like the rich/important people of the world, he is different than we are.

ayoreo:
Que yamo, mu a Dupade gajnui u camión pachidé dirome
ujetiga camión gachisṍri chãraque ga jé que toc jeta pibi aja Dupade.
english: Some of you have surrendered completely to*Dupade*. I don’t know, but let’s say to reach*Dupade*in the future one must enter the vehicle that takes you to him; if the owner won’t let you ride in it, you won’t be able to get into the vehicle that takes you to*Dupade*.

ayoreo:
Uje Dupade uruode chacapísaja
ore áyipiedie enga jé u uje ore chejna rángaja Dupade. Ujetiga Dupade di
dirome, cojñoque ugucharã́tique ta chojninga: ‘Yiplátadie déji daji, yijína ua
to jne.’
english: When*Dupade*’s words have truly gotten into people’s hearts, they are completely taken up with him. When*Dupade*arrives in the future, a rich person might say to him: “Here’s my money, I’m going with you.”

ayoreo:
Dupade uru quigode u udojo
ujetiga uacuñéngo changureta to. Uje a di camiṍraque jeoga cuchabasúrique, mu
gachisṍri chãraque ga je toque gotique, adi ta u jeta Dupade di. Adi upúsu
cucha unejai uje yipótago jóroga mu gajnengṍri chãraque ga jé que toque ome
ñoque.
english: *Dupade*’s words explain, however, that anyone who wants to go with Him must believe in Him! It might be a truck, or it could be a plane, but whatever it is, if the owner forbids it, you can’t ride in it, and it will be like that when*Dupade*arrives. It’s like that with other things that we, in vain, might want, if the owner forbids something, then it’s impossible for us to have it.

ayoreo:
Ta uacuñéngo chejna raja Dupade
to, Dupade anguretasṍrone uaque. Acatecãchoi Dupade, ca ajéchome. Ca ajéngome
Dupade jeta acatecã́ri.
english: Those of you who are completely taken up with*Dupade*are true believers. So, talk to*Dupade*and don’t be ashamed, don’t be bashful about praying.**Dupade includes young and old in his family:**As if*Dupade*is such a one who would say: Handsome young men and beautiful young women are who I want. Just the opposite! Old men, even the very old, will accompany*Dupade*if that person believes in Him.*Dupade*includes us with those he chooses when we believe in him.

ayoreo: Cho jeta cucha Dupade ga chojninga: ‘Ueradéjna jnacari, ueradé gapua, enga ore u yajegaisóningo.’ Jé basa ga choquíjnarique, a choquíjna marínga, jé chijína Dupade jeta gosi changureta. Dupade chapuse yoque aja ajegaisóningo jeta changureta.
english: I’m very thankful for every village in the world where*Dupade*’s words are taught, all those villages. I’m thankful for all those villages in the world.*Dupade*’s words are in Tobité, they are in Pailón, they’re in the place where don Len is living, they are in Zapocó, they are in Ayoré villages in the outskirts of Santa Cruz, and they are in other countries. They are in Paraguay also, beyond us here in Rincón, they are in Pajeidé’s town. I’m very thankful for all these villages where*Dupade*’s words are read and where both Cojñone and Ayoréos tell us about what*Dupade*’s words say. That’s all.

ayoreo: Yacaranguipise ome guidáyode iji erámone uje Dupade Uruode déji guidáyode jnese iji erámone. Yacaranguipise ome guidáyode iji erámone. Dupade uruode déji Tobité, déji Pailón rĩ, déji don Len idai, déji Zapocó rĩ, déji Santa Cruz uéchade rĩ, déji ducuniri uñane, déji Paraguay iji queyéchade, déji Pajeidé idai. Yacaranguipise ome udojo guidáyode uje Dupade Uruode e déji guidáyode ga cojñone, ayoréode chingo yoque Dupade Uruode aja yoque.
english: Dupade – GodDupade’s Words – God’s WordAyoréos – Ayoré peopleCojñone – Non-Ayoré people

ayoreo: *Chagaide – Rincón de Tigre – 1970**Janet Briggs churúsãre Chagaide uruode nanique*
english: *Chagaide – Rincón de Tigre, Bolivia – 1970**Transcribed by: Janet Briggs**Translated to English by: Maxine Morarie.*

ayoreo: Dupade chojnirame ore: “Adã, Eba, acho cuchade jnese tiji yejnai tuné. Enga arocho cuchade jnese iji.”
english: God said to them: “Adam and Eve, you may eat everything in this garden of mine. And you should take care of everything in it.”

ayoreo:
Dupade Uruode chatata uje iji
taningai icaite erámi taningane ga cuchisode parácho jnese icaite. Cuchisode
uñane chicai dasocade icaite iji isocade iji dirode unojo. Dupade Uruode
chatata uje Dupade chi pésu cuchiso ponáyone jnese. Chi parácho cuchisode jnese
icaite enga cuchisode chi ca asécho gotique icaite iji erámone taningane uje
qué Dupade déji Adã, Eba ore gari iji dejnai. Ugade chi parácho icaite.
Caratade chi parácho. Cuchisode chi ca asécho. Chi parácho jnese.
english: God’s Word says that in the beginning all the animals were tame. Some of the animals were just like they are today. The Bible tells us that God made all kinds of animals. And all of them were tame in the beginning and none of them were wild when Adam and Eve were still in the garden. Even the snakes were tame. The jaguars were tame. None of the animals were wild. All were tame.

ayoreo: Dupade chi chojnirame Adã, Eba ore: “Acho yejnai jnese jne, cuchade iji poridie jnese. Gusu yãrengué pore chojmára. A ca acho poria uaté adie gotique jne. Aníri, diri ujetiga acho tuté pore áraque, a atoyoi jne.”
english: God said to Adam and Eve: “Eat from my garden and from all the trees in it. There is only one tree from which I forbid you to eat. Don’t eat its fruit at all. Pay attention: the day that you eat the forbidden fruit, you will die.”

ayoreo:
Dupade
chi pota gu ujetiga chiraja Adã, Eba ore ujetiga ũré ujetiga angoneguite
guesaque ore ome, ujetiga ore chiraja úringai pujopierãque enga ore doi uruode
enga jé ca ore chijocase tuaté ádie.
english: God said this because he wanted to find out if Adam and Eve recognized that he was all-powerful and were going to be obedient to him and do what he said to do and not touch the fruit.

ayoreo: Imóngo iji Dupade Uruode uje cáma pisijnaringuérique déji Adã, Eba ore iji taningane. Dupade chi garéngone u pisijnaringuéone ome ore. Ujetiga jé ca ore pésu pisijnaringuérique ujetiga jé ca ore ajéngome Dupade.
english: We know from the Bible that Adam and Eve had not yet sinned in the beginning. God had forbidden sin to come into the world. He didn’t want them to sin, because if they did, they would be ashamed before him.

ayoreo:
Dupade chi potigádi tu jetiga
Adã, Eba ore doi uruode icaite ujetiga ore tangári. Ujetiga anire, ore changureta
uté. Enga Dupade chi urúo unojo ome Adã, Eba ore gu jetiga chiraja ujetiga
angoninguite guesac ore ome Dupade ujetiga ore doi Dupade Uruode.
english: God wanted Adam and Eve to obey him and to listen to him. And especially that they would believe in him. And that is why God said these things to Adam and Eve so that he could see if they would be obedient and do what he asked them to do.

ayoreo:
Jecute Dupade chi chojningame
Adã, Eba ore: “Ca acho poria tuaté adie. Dire ujetiga acho poria tuaté adie enga
a atoyoi jne.”
english: That is why God said to Adam and Eve: “Don’t eat the fruit of that tree. The day that you eat its fruit, you will die.”

ayoreo: A cáma pitoringárique déji Adã, Eba gotique iji dirode udore. Jecute Dupade chojnirame Adã, dapesute jnani bajade ore: “Ca acho poria tuaté adie uje yãrangué. A atoyoi jne jeta acho poria tuaté adie.”
english: Up till that time death was unknown to Adam and Eve. That is why God said to Adam, those first ones he had made: “Don’t eat the fruit from the tree that I have forbidden to you. You will die if you eat its fruit.”

ayoreo: Quigade ga iji Dupade Uruode unojo, ga Dupade Uruode chatata uje Dupade chi ca chimoque icaite uje chise jnacare bajade yocayode Adã, Eba ore iji dirode jnese. Dupade chi déji ore gari enga ore chimo Dupade ome dedodie.
english: After saying those things to them, God’s word tells us that God never missed visiting the first man and woman, our forefathers Adam and Eve. Every single day he would check on them. God would be in their presence and they would see God with their very own eyes.

ayoreo:
Mu Adã acoté Eba chajire poria
tuaté údi, uburúdi iji dirode uñeque. Enga anire. Eba qué chajire poria tuaté uburúdi
ga chajire gãrengué casé pore uburúdi ga chi chijire adie. Eti chi Eba chi
chudute darade enga chi chijire darade mu uga. Enga chi ugai chi narare. Enga
chi chojnirame Eba, ayoré bajé: “¿Gotique uje que agu poria tuaté adie á?”
english: But then, one day Adam’s wife Eve noticed how shady it was beneath that tree. And then this is what happened: When Eve was in the shade of the tree and enjoying it, she noticed its fruit. And then, she heard someone speak and she looked toward the voice and it was coming from a snake. It was speaking to her. And it said to Eve, the very first woman: “Why don’t you eat some of that tree’s fruit?”

ayoreo:
Mu chi Eba chi chojnira: “Que
yagu gotique.”
english: But Eve said: “I won’t eat it!”

ayoreo:
Enga ugai chi chojnira:
“¿Gotique gu a? uje que agu a.”
english: And the snake said: “Why won’t you eat it?”

ayoreo:
Enga chi chequé chojnira: “Que
yagu gu uje Dupade jé urúo. Dupade chatata diríca uje chi yajo poria tuaté adie
enga Dupade ãrengué enga chi itogoi jne. Chi pitoringai chacája yoque jne.”
english: And the woman said: “I won’t eat it because God said not to. God told us that if we ate the fruit of that tree, the one from which God forbade us to eat, that we would die. He said that death would enter us.”

ayoreo:
Enga ugai tuté chojnira: “Taque
Dupade. Taque Dupade uje chiese atoyoi jne. A acho poria tunaque adie maringa,
a ca atoyoi jne.”
english: And the snake said: “God is a liar, claiming that you will die. Even if you should eat this tree’s fruit, you wouldn’t die.”

ayoreo: Mu Dupade Uruode chatata uje dicore tu aníri, dicore chi pi aníri ugai tuté. Enga dicoré Satanas chi chacája ugai pijnani. Chacája ugai ujetiga chãra chequé tuaté. Enga ugai chi chojnira: “Dupade que pota. Taque Dupade uje chiese atoyoi jne. Que toc ujetiga atoyoi gotique iji,” chojnira dicoré. “Dupade chãraque poria adie ujetiga acho gu uje Dupade chiraja uje a achorásu poria adie enga a oyorásu Dupade, ujetiga arajáyo cucha arégode, cucha gajnaréngone ore enga anire a oyorásu Dupade. Jecute ujetiga uacacho enga a arajáyo cuchade jnese. Jecute Dupade charatáiyime pirajatigai tome uaque, ga anire, Dupade chãraque poria adie.”
english: But God’s Word tell us who the snake really was – he was the devil – he was in possession of the snake. The devil, Satan, was using the snake’s mouth and speaking through him. He used the snake to deceive that woman. And the snake said: “God just doesn’t want you to eat it. He’s lying about your dying if you eat it. There’s no way it will make you die,” the snake said. “God is forbidding you the fruit of that tree for he knows that if you would eat that tree’s fruit you would become just like Him, and you would know good things, and evil things just like God knows about all these things. So if you eat this fruit you will know about everything. Therefore, God forbids you to know what he knows, and that is why he is forbidding you to eat the fruit of that tree.”

ayoreo:
“Taque Dupade,” chi ugai chi
chojnira. Dicoré chi chojnira, chi chãra Eba. Enga jec u uje Eba, yocaté chequé
bajé chi doi dicoré uruode. Enga chi dicoré chojnira uje chi chãra:  “Agu. Jé quétingáre. Enga a amo cucha arégode
to iji.”
english: “God is just lying to you,” the snake said. But it was really the devil talking because he wanted to deceive Eve. And then it was that Eve, our mother, that very first woman, did what he told her to do. And this is what the devil said when he deceived her: “Eat it. Just try it. And you will know wonderful things.”

ayoreo: Eba chi doi aníri dicoré uruode ga chi chisa poria áraque enga jec u uje chi tagu. Enga Eba chi chisiapome dabai to rĩ. Enga Adã chi tagu to. Enga jec u uje chi ore jnusina uje e ore chimánaja dajei uje chi Dupade chetaque poria to ome ore ujetiga ore tagu. Chi ore jnusina uje Dupade chi anire, gãrengué diríca, enga chi jec u uje que ore chiraja daru quitígo ome Dupade gotique. Uje ore tagu gãrengué casé pore adie.
english: So Eve did exactly what he told her to do and she picked fruit from the tree and she took a bite from it. And then she gave some to her husband also. And Adam took a bite of it, too. And then it was that they remembered why God had not wanted them to eat from that tree. They remembered that God had forbidden it to them, and they didn’t know what they were going to say to God about what they had done. For now they had eaten what he had forbidden them to eat – the fruit from the tree.

ayoreo:
Uje Dupade chi chojnirame ore
diríca: “Ca acho jne. Acho cuchade jnese iji yejnai tuné, mu ca acho poria
tuaté adie jne.”
english: For this is what God had said to them: “Don’t eat from it. You can eat everything else in my garden, but don’t eat the fruit from that tree.”

ayoreo:
Mu Eba chi tagu poria adie. Enga
chisi ome dabai enga ore tagu jnese, enga jec u uje chi ore ajéngome Dupade
yui. Chi ore todo Dupade yui uje que ore doi Dupade Uruode gotique. Angoneguite
parique ore. Diri tuté u uje ore ajéngome Dupade yui, uje e que ore pésu Dupade
Uruode gotique. Que angoneguite guesac ore ome Dupade gotique, enga chi ore
chajéngome Dupade yui.
english: But now Eve had already eaten fruit from that tree. And she had given some to her husband, so both had eaten of it, and they were ashamed of what they’d done, and they didn’t want to meet up with God. They were afraid of what God would do now that they had disobeyed him. Their disobedience caused them to be ashamed. They had not obeyed God at all, so now they dreaded seeing him.

ayoreo: Que ore chiraja daru quitígome Dupade yui uje ore tagu Dupade gãrengué casé pore adie. Enga jec u uje Dupade Uruode chatata uje deréjnai chi uté uje dicore chãra Eba ore. Enga deréjnai chi uté u uje pajegátigade chacája ore. Pisijnaringuéone chacája erámone unojo. Pisijnaringuéone garanone u yocai jnani bajai udore ore uje yocãrapíode ore.
english: They didn’t know what to say when they met up with him since they had eaten the forbidden fruit. And that is what God’s word says about the day that the devil deceived Eve and her husband. And that very day shame entered them. And that was the day that sin entered this world. Sin came into the world through our forefathers from whom we have all descended.

ayoreo:
Enga pisijnaringuéone qué déji
erámone iji dirode unojo to, enga chiborátaja cuchade unojo ome ayoréode uyoque:
pajegátigade, pajé jũruachúringane, paringaquéode ore. Enga cuchade udore u
uje pésu re to nanique iji diri tuté uje yocai jnani bajade ore tagu Dupade
gãrangué pore ádie nanique. Enga jec u uje chi que ore chiraja dasocárique
nanique ome Dupade yui uje chi Dupade tibágui ore iji dirode jnese.
