And then he throws away the paper on which they were written. That paper is thrown away. You see how*Dupade*’s Word is helping me. The Holy Spirit is now in me.*Dupade*is telling me things from his Word. He has told me that there is nothing and no one good on earth. The only good one is*Dupade*. He really can see us when we talk to him. This is all I have to say about our sin, for*Dupade*helped me to find what his word says about it.  My knowledge is little, but I am grateful to*Dupade*for the things he has revealed to me.

When Satan was inside me he kept me from*Dupade*’s*W**ord**s*. I forgot it. Satan prevented me from finding anything in*Dupade*’s*Word*, and I couldn’t find what I looked for. And what I found, I didn’t understand. But I finally found what I was looking for. Though I almost gave up on finding it. But*Dupade*showed me those things I was looking for. He just placed those verses before my eyes.

And this is what it is about. If you want this, then do what I did. If you don’t want it, then don’t do it. Satan will always tell us bad things about*Dupade’s Words*. But*Dupade*opposes Satan. Satan says the word of*Dupade*isn’t important.

But*Dupade*says: “I have overcome the world. I am the only true*Dupade*.”

When*Dupade*and Satan give opposing views about themselves, Satan brags, but*Dupade*tells the truth. When they say what they can do, only*Dupade*tells the truth. But Satan never tells the truth, he is a big liar.

That is all I have to say.

Dupade – God

Dupade Ujopié –  God’s power, the Holy Spirit

Dupade’s Words –  God’s Word, Bible

Ujopié – Spirit, power

Yoqui-Dupade – Our God

Our Great Leader who we listen to – Our Lord

Jnaropié – Place of the dead

*Dijaide – Tobité, Bolivia – 1970’s.*

*Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie.*

ayoreo:
Chi cinco
misionérone ore chise Bolivia nanique iji taningapísode uje cáma ayorérique
yimóngo cojñotique gotic. Que yimóngo cojñone ore aórique. Mu eduguéjnane
bajade ore chudute ayoréode ore ujnomei enga chi ore déji erámi. Ga uje ore
chosõripis ore ome cuchade uje, anire, cojñone ore jocaningane ga chi ore
chojninga: “Ga yico, ga yico, ga, anire, yucuegoi ayoréode jne.” Mu que ore
áyipieraque déji uje ayoréode sijnanguipise ome ore, cojñone ore jnese iji
erámi. Mu chi ore jno ga ore chudute ayoréode ujnomei iji Santiago, Santiágo de
Chiquitos. Uje ore tṍraji ome, ayoréode ore tṍraji ome, anire,
dajocaningai. Uje ore chise cojñone udore ore uje ore chucué ayoréode ore enga
ore jno ga ore chise dedai. Mu chi cojñone ore, udore misionérone, ore chucué
ore. Misionerone bajaguipísode u ore iji erámi.
english: *Que yimóngo cojñotique aórique* We were told that five missionaries came to Bolivia long ago in the very beginning before the Ayoré had any contact with the non-Ayoré that we called*cojñone.*We hadn’t even seen their kind of skin. But we’re told that when those first missionaries heard about the people in the jungles of Bolivia, they had compassion on them because of how things were for them, and how the people were hunting them. And they said, “Let’s go, let’s go and look for the Ayoré.”

ayoreo:
Diyajmanguipíso
udojo uje uduco ore ujnoméone to. Uje que ayorérique yocáyipieraque déji
nanique ujetiga cojnotique paraque ome ayorérique uyoque. Mu yórango yógome uje
udúco ore ujnoméone to.
english: But they didn’t think about how fierce we were.  And so they went in search of the us. When they were in Santiago de Chiquitos they heard the news that there had been sightings of the Ayoré in the nearby jungles. Some people there told the missionaries that they had run into some of the Ayoré and that the Ayoré were afraid of them and must have thought they had wanted to kill them. After meeting up with those people, the missionaries decided to go looking for the Ayoré, but, we’re told they went back to their town first. Both groups, the people in Santiago and the missionaries decided to look for the Ayoré, but the missionaries went into the jungle first.

ayoreo:
Uje õsocacade u ore ome ayoréode uyoque ga
chi, chi misionérone ore jno ome, anire, dacuegode ayoréo. Ga chi ore chise ore
adode ayoréo. Ore chise ayoréode adode, ga que ore áyipieraque déji ujetiga ore
chujé ore jne. Ore áyipie gaidi upúsu Dupade ujetiga Dupade chiqueta ore iji,
anire, ore uniri uté.
english: For a long time we had been hearing that there were people looking for us. None of us Ayoré had any idea at that time that these were people that would be kind to us. That is why we were frightened when we heard that people were looking for us.

ayoreo:
Ore jno ga
ugucharãque ore. Que ore chojningame dacotedie, chi ca ore chojningame: “A qué
yiriapo jne dirome rĩ.” Ore áyipie gaidi u ujetiga ore chise dacuegode
ayoréode ore uyoque gu. Enga
ore chise ore jne jeta ore chingome ore ome Jesute to. Uje Ayoréode ore,
yirajágo iji dirode udojo uje eduguéjnane chubu erámone gu, anire, uje ore
chosõre yoque ome yoquisijnaringuéone iji udé yocuniri.
english: It was because of their wanting to be kind to us Ayoré that the missionaries kept going out to look for us. And they found signs of the Ayoré. And so they kept searching without giving much thought to the fact that the Ayoré might kill them.  They were trusting in*Dupade*and felt that he could save them – even in Ayoré territory.

ayoreo:
Ga, chi
udore, cinco misionérone ga ore chise ayoréode iji idaidatei. Chi ore
eduguéjnarique jé charanguirase ore,  mu
jé que piminingate uje chojmára, ga ujnájnane u udore uje que ore narare
misionéro ore gai to. Ga, chi chise amiri diri uje ore di jne, eduguéjnane ore,
ga chi ore eduguéjnane udore ayoré, chajningare, anire u, misionérone ore iji
ore garani, ujeta chicho ore ome dojódie. Ga uñeque chi chicho ore bajaque. Que
ore áyipiéraque déji ujetiga udore, anire*,
gríngone*, uje cinco misionérone ore chiqueta ore dirome, anire, ome cojñone
ore jocaningárique.
english: They left with a lot of stuff. They didn’t say to their wives, “We’ll be back soon.”  They were determined to keep looking for us Ayoré until they found us and could tell us about Jesus. And now, in these days, we know Him, and that is because missionaries have gone all over the world, and, here in our country, they’ve told us how our sins could be forgiven.

ayoreo:
Chi ore
ipotigádi u ujetiga ore eduguéjnai, anínique u, ayoré jeta chujé cojñotígo. Mu
que cojñone isocai tu gotic. Cojñone ore ipotigádi u ujetiga paraque
ayorérique. Chi jé u uje áyipie u ome enga ore chisí gai ome, aniri, dacotaquei
ome anínique u, uringárique uerate. Jeoga canirac. Jeoga, anire, osõcaca ome
dojórigo ayoré ujetiga chubúchu ore iji ore ejnarétigo. Mu que cojñone oráyipie
u ayoréode áyipie to go.
english: And then it was that these five missionary men found an Ayoré village. One of the Ayoré leaders wanted to protect them, but no one listened to him for he was only one, and there were many who didn’t speak up for the missionaries. Later, on the day the missionaries arrived at that village, some Ayoré warriors confronted them on their way back to the village. As the missionaries were leaving, the warriors circled around them to shoot them with their arrows. One of them was shot first and soon all the missionaries were killed. These warriors didn’t know that these gringos, the five missionaries, would have helped them to make peace with the*cojñone*who were hunting them.

ayoreo:
Jecute
yirajágo uje ayoréode yirajágopo rĩ uje que qué cuchárique bisideque iji
erámi. Ga yirajágo to uje gusu Jesus uje chosõre yógome, anire,
yoquejnaréngone. A pota ga chiqueta yoque. Je pota ga chujé yoque. Ga yirajágo
uje udore*cinco misionérone*toi iji ayoréode jmainie, mu Dupade e chiraja
ore cachodi uje cáma tocade.
english: It’s told that we Ayoré used to want chiefs that would kill our*cojñone*enemies. But many of the*cojñone*wanted to make peace with the Ayoré, but we did not know this.  The Ayoré chiefs also wanted to be praised for their generosity, for their compassion on the sick as they would blow on them to heal them. But the*cojñone*and the Ayoreos thought differently and this added to their misunderstandings.

ayoreo:
Chi udore ore
osõraquei u jóroque, aniri,*gríngone*ore,
ore ipotigádi u ujetiga yicagoi Dupade idai to jne. Mu que ayoréode yirajágo
gotic. Enga que yirãráco ore gai gotique nanique iji ore diringai. Ga chi ore
chujé ore. Ore chasẽra ore ugúchade. Mu jé areque, chi ore chijóchame ore.
english: **ayoréos****did not know that their intentions were good:** We know now that those missionaries felt sorry for us, but never got to show us how to go to heaven. But we Ayoré had no idea that was why they had come to find us. And that explains why no one defended them when they arrived, and why they were killed. After killing them, the Ayoré took their things. But fortunately, they buried the missionaries.

ayoreo:
Chi diri
uñane enga orujode cojñone iji Santiágo chi chudute ore ujnomei. Ga ore
eduguéjnai iji Santiago  chi, chojninga:
“Boyo yico. Yucuegoi, anírone u, eduguéjna casode. Ayoréode a chijna ore.”
english: Some time later, the people in Santiago heard that the missionary men were missing. And that is when a leader in Santiago, we’re told, said, “Let’s go. Let’s look for those missing missionaries. The Ayoré have probably taken them.” They searched for them but didn’t find them.  All they found were some of their things – paraphernalia from their cameras and other things. But by this they knew they had been killed and buried, for it was the custom of the Ayoré to bury their victims.

ayoreo:
Chi ore
chucué ore, mu que ore chise ore go. Ore chise, anírone, oidode*foto*carubode iji jnumone. Enga ore
chiraja gu udojo uje chi ore chejna ore, mu ore chijóchame ore. U ayorepísode
ore chijóchame degode ore uje ore pógu ore iji ore idai uté.
english: Little by little the news of the killing of the missionaries reached other groups of us Ayoré.

ayoreo:
Mu, chi chise
dirode udojo uje yirajágo uje ore chejna ore nanique.
english: Those five men had felt sorry for us. They felt sorry for us because of our sin, and the kind of lives we were living.  And because of them, other missionaries came, and we now know that there is someone, somewhere who loves us Ayoré –*Dupade*loves us. The kindness of*Dupade*is in some of the*cojñone*, also, and they are kind to us. Rich and poor people, of one mind, are reaching out to help us.

ayoreo:
Yocosõrasórone u
misionéro udore. Uje ore chosõre yogome yoquisijnaringuéone iji aníri
yocucuécaringuei to iji dirode udojo gajnerĩ. Ga jé
yirajágo to uje yocosõrasónique déji siñeque ga Dupade u uje chosõre yoque. Ga
jec u uje déji cojñone ore to uje uñengo chosõre yoque. Jeoga cojñoque atade u.
Jeoga cojñoque gajnareque maringa, jé chosõre yoque to. Uje jé jogadi jé u uje
chojmára gu ore áyipie qui. Uje jé ore chosõre yoque to.
english: And it was like that with Jesus, wasn’t it? He came to this earth because he pitied us earth dwellers and wanted to look out for us.  He wanted to show us his grace. I can truthfully say that we Ayoreos didn’t have many friends in this world at the time those first five missionaries came to us. And what I’ve told you about the ways of the Ayoré is also true. For very few of us Ayoré were even friendly with each other at that time.

ayoreo:
Ga jé gajnui
to gajnerĩ uje Jesuse chosõre yóguiji daríringárique ujeta áyipie déji yoque.
Ujeta chosõripise yoque. Enga jé ũrepise uyu uje que yocosõrasónique unájna
gotique iji erámi udojo. Ga, jé ũrepise yu ome, anire, udojo jnanione ayoréode
yocadode. Uje que anínique unájna gotic ujeta ore, yosõco ñane.
english: Only Jesus made the difference. His teachings made it plain to us that there is One who is kind and compassionate to all of us, Ayoreos and*cojñone*, we all have the same compassionate Lord.

ayoreo:
Mu gusu
Jesuse u. Acajnaquéode anírone quigode u uje yocosõcacai, yocosõcasṍri
chojmára.
english: It’s true what we’ve heard about Gutodaté who was taken captive by the*cojñone*. It was she, in the beginning, who first taught the missionaries our words. The Ayoreos and the missionaries couldn’t talk to each other for they didn’t understand each other. But we are so thankful that in these times we people, Ayoreos and*cojñone*, can communicate with each other everywhere. The missionaries know our language. Even among the humblest of the*cojñone*are those who have learned some of our words, and it’s so good that we Ayoré also now know their words (Spanish) and we can all communicate freely.

ayoreo: Ga jé ũré yu gajnerĩ uje udúcopo to rĩ uje chi u*Gutodaté*iji udojo iji taningane. Ga chi ore isagué nanique. Cho jóroque ore quic udore, anire, misionérone ome ayoréode uruode. Que toc ujetiga ore catecãri ñane. Uje que ore chiraja ñane urúrique gotique gu. Mu yocacaranguipise ome iji dirode udojo uje ayoréode, cojñone ore yirajágo ñane uruode iji erámi údode jnese. Ga udore misionérone ore chiraja yocuruode. Jeoga cojñoque gajnareque mu, chiraja yocuruode ga jé ueradipíse ujetiga yicatecãcoi uñane iji jnumi jnese.
english: And there’s a reason for this. And that reason is, if you want to learn another language you have to listen to it. And now, there are books that teach our language. It’s very good in these days how we are interacting with each other;*cojñone*and Ayoreos are becoming more and more united in these days.

ayoreo:
Uje yirajágo
gu uje quigode cuse. Quigode u uje, ujetiga yipótago puru uñeque ga yangácoi.
Ga jé u uje, aníri, yirajágo uje pacajnaquéode déji gu. Enga jé areguipise iji
dirode udojo gajnerĩ uje yirajágo uje yacágo aja ñane ajéode gu, cojñone,
ayoréode ore uyóguiji erámi jnese iji dirode udojo gajnerĩ.
english: It’s true that, even when we are miles apart in understanding each other,*Dupade*knows what is in our hearts. He knows all about our sins.  And he knows what we think about His Son, and whether our thoughts are good. For*Dupade*isn’t far off, he’s right here beside us wherever we are in this world. The end.

ayoreo: Ga jé ũrépise uyu uje cuchade a qué idája, mu Dupade e chiraja iji yocáyipiedie. Chiraja yoquisijnaringuéone. Jeoga chiraja ujeta jé aregue yocáyipierigui ome, ome Ujnacari. Uje que idája gotique gu. Déji yoquejode iji erámi jnese.   Eruei u. Dupade – Dios *Jomoné – Puesto Paz, Bolivia – 1960.**Faith Wyma churúsãre Jomoné atatagode udojo nanique.*
english: *J**omoné – Puesto Paz, Bolivia – 1970.* *Transcribed by: Faith Wyma**.* *Translated to English by: Maxine Morarie**.* Jomoné, eating palm heart. He grew up to be one of the

ayoreo:
*Dupade
anguresórone ore cho dajorane quique nanique:*

**Nacore (Rósadé) uruode u udojo:**Uacarajáyo Dupade Uruode. Yojningane u Bai, ore. Jnacare uaque.Ca oyo cocho cochógome Dupade yui.Ga uerate Josuédé (Dijaide) acajnaquéode. Ca bei jnumi ome Dupade adode jne.

Yoco yocasuté iji jnacarode
uñeque jne.

Dupade chãraque cuchade;
pijoque guetégode cucha, garéngone cucha. Jé ũrepise yu. Mu uacarajáyo uje
Dupade anguretasórone ajicho Dupade Uruode udojo iji darujna aone. Mu uacarajáyo
uje chãraque uje pacoté gárerigui. Chãraque pabai gárerigo, chequedíe
uaque. Gusu tojmára ome chequéraque. Gusu bacotéraque chojmára.

Garéngone udojo déji yu to
nanique. Mu que yudute nanique Dupade urúrique iji*móntedie*nanique. Ipésu udojo nanique. Jé yugúcoji eduguéjnane,
Dupade abode ore gari, ga yicoi áyipiedie.

Jé ũrepise. Ca yipéco Dupade
ujuredie. Yáyipiedie udaji ome uaque: Ucaré, aja Jómone, Urúidaté, Gatemitódacóde
basocade. Ga atatáyo uacate gajnaréngone. Ga íjnoque gajneri. Ayáyo ga asáyo
ore jmanáyone. Dupade áyipiedie jé u. Gusu yuruode.

**Dijaide uruode:**Ueradepise Rósadé áyipie. Payuquetotiguei u uje Dupade chisi ome yoque, páyuquetotiguei. Chiraja páyuquetotiguei.

**Rósadaté uruode:**Dejaque gadiógode udojo uje yipesudi udé. Moque unerotiguéode udojo udé iji dejade udojo. Uje yo yáyugu yu uje Satanas chirote yoque diríca ome Dupade Uruode uje e udúco Dupade Uruode to múñi. Jé yaquĩ́ra to diríca iji ore*viaje*casode ga unere erámi ome yu. Uje yabidie e ore di jnese. Ga jéode ti jé areguipise ore jnese. Jé a ca ãraque ore go. Pate gajnaréngone iji erámi. Ga yirasique uje yabidie ore chise yu jnese rĩ. Ga que yugúsiji anírique, to go. Puágueraque jé a e Satanas chetingare re iji dati ome yiguíjnai. Yácariji yijogadi diríca. Ga que yudute cuchárique to go. Jé a é Satanase chise yoque.

Yácariji yijogadi. Mu yimo
Eruidaté iji de yiguíjnai piagoi. Jeode ti taquiri. Ga jé tangári Dupade Uruode
to gajneri iji de yiguíjnai ajei. Yangári, mu e uruode déji Ucaré.

Chojninga: “Ucaré, jé yibágui
uaque,” chojninga. Chi, “Yacho uje uacapésu anire, Elena jurédie iji erámone.”

Ga Elena jé po. Que unere erámi
ome yu que. Mu jeodeti uje yabidie ca ore ipesutique cucha gajnareque déji
siñeque. Ga jé yirasé yu jne ome Dupade Uruode. Ujetiga ca Satanáserique déji
yu gajneri. Jé a déji yu.

Eruidaté chujé Ucaré gatoi. Ga
yachẽ́re Eruidaté. Ga yucuase te. Jé yucuase, yumuchase aja te. Mu e dépoji
Ucaré rĩ. Ga yisa pidi. Yisa pidi. Ga yujérase Eruidaté. Mu yajire. Mu
yoquihermáno casidé jõra.

Ga yojninga: “E jeodeti
unejángue u ua,” yojninga. “Jé a jé yoquijõra casidé. Yijõra u ua. Uje
yingongo que yuruode aja Dupade iji ti diríca iji iguíjnai ajei,” yojninga.

Chajẽ chague yibai gu Eruidaté.
Chague yibai Satanase, ga chirodipise yu iji yiguíjnai ajei. Jé yáyipie déji.
Jnani bajai a ore adode. ¡Jeta yudute cuchárique to! Ga anire, yudute mu chajẽ
Satanase chague yibai. Ga chiringare re iji yajei, ga que yiji go yajéode.

Ga jĩraque yojningame Eruidaté:
“Jeodeti unejángue ua que. Ga yujérase ua. Yujérase ua iji Ucaré gai.”

Que yimopo iji dejaque gadiógode
udojo rĩ iji ore viaje yui casé to rĩ ome jne. Ujetiga Satanase chajẽ
chirote yu gu.

Mu acatecãchoi Dupade ome yoque
to jne iji udé. Yocate gajnarei udé. Mu Dupade soca jé chĩro jne yocadode udojo.
Ga yoquirasépo ñane to jne rĩ. Jĩraque yachẽ́re diríca unojo.

Yojningame Erúidaté: “A yingóngo
Dupade jne rĩ yocuruode iji domíngo gajneri.”

Ga
Eruidaté chojninga: “¿A yicoi jnese jne rĩ a? Acatecãchoi to jne
Dupade ome yu iji udé. Yocate
gajnarei udé. E gusu yuruode jne.

**Dijaide uruode:**Ueradipise uaque uje uacangóño Dupade. Uje uacangóño uaquehermánone uacate gajnaréngone. Ga Dupade chojninga: “Angoño uaquehermánone bajaque. Ga quigade ga Dupade e chĩro uacate gajnaréngone. Yocacaranguipise ome udójo, uje Satanáse jiei iji erámi. Mu Dupade uruode chojninga ‘Uacanírique, ca jnoji uacáyipie. Arijínaño uaquejode iji erámi,’ chojninga Dupade.

Yicoi gajneri. Ga ca yajéngome gajneri
Dupade. Uje yoquihermánapise Eruidaté diríca uje déji de to diríca. Mu déji
yoquiquei uje yoco yoquihermána iji to. Mu que déji Dupade pijnani uje
chojninga: “Ca átayo cuchade aja ñane gajneri,” chojninga. “Ga átayoja Satanase
iji uacajei. Ca átaja yu aja uacajei,’ chojninga. “Ca anguretáyo yu. Ga ácajoji
guesi.” Que udore déji Dupade pijnani gotique.

**Josuedé chacãrája dacajnaquéode aja jnani bajai Josué isocade:**Jnani bajai Josué chi cho darásade quique nanique. Chi chojningame darásade: “Beyoi uacajegaitique iji erámi jne. Jeta anguretáyo Satanas, jeta anguretáyo Yangureni to. Beyoi uacáyipiedie.” Mu cho ore quique, chojninga: “Angári Yocangureni uje jé u yocayode jnani bajade ore iDupade.”

Ga jé ore catája Dupade urasade.
Chi ore angaráni u uje Josué chãraque cuchade ome ore uje chojninga: “Ca
anguretáyo Dupade gajneri, ga ca anguretáyo gajneri. Ca ajocáso Dupade gajneri,”
chojninga. “Puyaque Dupade. Anguretáyo Satanase, ga anguretáyo. Ca ẽ́care
Dupade i bisideque gajneri,” chojninga.

Mu yicoi gajneri uje qué
yugúcoji erámi tudé.*Tiempo*qué déji
ome Dupade ujetiga yanguretáyo.*Tiempo*déji.
Anguretáyo Dupade jne. Jé yoquihermáno ñane. Jé ca unejaque yógome ñane. E gusu.

**Bai uruode:**Jé ueradipise yoquihermána Rosadaté acajnaquéode. Uje que chudute cuchárique diríca, Dupade’s Uruode jé u gu. Ga nína darodie yui uje ore diringai u. Irasique darodie yui. Ga a gajnui u Dupade dirome ujetiga di. Ga queyirasique Dupade yui jetiga di dirome. Que yimóngo jetiga di gajneri, jeoga dirome u. Gajnui u uje Dupade irasique dabidie yui. Ga di dirome ga ore irasique Dai Dupade diringai yui. Jé uacamóño uje Dupade ipesute yoque. Ipesute queyibijnani ujetiga yirarã́co. Ipesute queyangoninguinone jetiga yangácoi cuchade.

Mu que yimo uje yajire Dupade
Uruode iji cojñone uruode que. Mu chamáñaje yajire*poquito**versículo*iji
anire, queyuruode jne. Quĩrique jé cho udore Rósadé ore ojningáiso. Ore
chojninga ujetiga ajnórañopise ñane. Ga oyo parácho iji jnumi ome ñane. Enga
angáchopísi ñane quitígo. Quitígo chojmára ome ñane rĩ. Ca angáchoi
cucháringome uaque. Ca oyo cucha daté iji uaque rĩ. Gusu jetiga ajnórañopise
ñane. Angáchopísi uacojode ayoré.

¿Socaseti irajataque gosi ome uaque jne a? Dupade ajéode u ujetiga queyáyipiedie chojmára iji jnumi iji de, iji jnumi jóroque.rep Jetiga yidotágo yógome cuchade. Mu chamáñaje chequedíe jé arajáyo anire to gu uje déji Santiago. Mu que yiraja versículo. Josuédé a chiraja. Mu que yiraja jogadi gu uje ga que yatatame uaque. Mu Dupade urúo. Chojninga: “Adotáyo uágaja ome cucha.”*(Santiago 4:1-6)*E gusu udojo yuru*poquito*.

**Dijaide uruode:**Uerate Bai versículodie uje chajire Dupade Uruode qué. Yocuruode. Uje chojninga: “Ca oyo cuchadaté iji uaque.” (Romanos 12:3)

Dupade chetaque.  Uje udúco Pablo uruode to uje chojninga: “Ca
oyo cuchadaté iji yu,” chi chojninga Pablo. “Que yipota jetiga ayorérique
chojninga, ‘Irajataque Pablo. Irajataque ga chiraja cuchade jnese’; que
yipota,” chi chojninga Pablo, Apostol Pablo.

Enga yocadi jé u gajneri. Ca
yoco cuchadaté iji yoque. Chamáñaje Dupade chetaque. Yicoi ga yajico uje que
yirajágo cuchárique.

**Carolínadaté uruode u, mu catecã́ri Dupade bajaque. Chojninga:**Yoque Dupade, yacaranguipis uje yugúcoi baguíjnai tudé, yoque Dupade. Josuédé chiroque baruode aja yoque. Yoque Dupade, yirajágo uje jé bamo yocate gajnaréngone. Mu que yimóngo, mu bamo uje daijna cuchábi u ua, yoque Dupade. Ãrope gajneri yoquipesudode cucha gajnaréngone, yoque Dupade.

Yirajágo uje Satanase ipesucho
yoque Dupade. Mu bajnué gai gajneri ujetiga ãro gajneri uje dajna cuchábi
ujopierãque ua, yoque Dupade. Yirajágo uje que yipijójoji cucha gajnaréngone
udé, yoque Dupade. Chácariji yocúdode. Mu jetiga bamate aja quigode gajneri,
yoque Dupade. Yacaranguipise gajneri ujetiga asi ome yu to gajneri,
pirajatigai, báyipie omenie, gajneri, yoque Dupade. Yanguretapise to gajneri,
yoque Dupade. Ga uchie jnumone ome badode iji yajei to, yoque Dupade.
Yacaranguipise gajneri ujetiga átaja yu to gajneri, yoque Dupade. Yicatecã́ri
ua ome bajnacari Jesucrísto i. Amẽ.

Margarita e di diríca. Ga
chacate yoque. Margarita chojninga: “Carolínadaté, ¿apota ya ujetiga jé
angári?”

Que yiraja to gu jnani bajade
íode. “Ta jé apota jnani bajai uje jnacari jno nanique,” chojninga.

Yojninga: “Margarita, cachi
yipota yá. Yipota jetiga yudute Dupade Uruode to,” yojninga. Jé gapu uyu. Ga
que yiraja darujna aoi mu yáyipie u. Yáyipie u uje yiraja udojo cuchade.”

Margarita chacate yóguiji udé
diríca. Jé a ore chijnája dati Satanase. Yĩro yõrájnui iji de. Mu que yũruáji
yõrájnui. Uje yajire, ga Margarita chingome yu uje chi ujnacari uné nanique.
Chingo yu. Chingo yu. Ga que yũruáji go. Mu e ore di. E ore di. Ga ore déji
yoque paca paca paque. Mu jé ore chijnája jne dojñane rĩ.

Jé ũrési suaqué yihermánamia
suaqué. Chojninga: “Que yijosa cuchárique déji siñeque.” Mu Eruidaté di. Ca
chácariji te. Ga, mu, gatíjna.

Eruidaté chojninga: “Yajéode jé
yingosía Jómone. Mu Ucaré chapite yúi ga,” chojninga Eruidaté.

Mu jĩraque yagu yui jõ. Mu je
ore tangári yu, yujodie chequé. Yojninga: “Eruidaté yá, ueradi u sereringai
ujetiga ango Jómone, ore gajnerí,” yojninga.

Mu
yujodie chequé ore chojningame: “Jé bo. Ga bei Ucaré, gajnareque ore adode.”

Enga e jno Eruidaté. Chojninga:
“Yiape.” E jno.

Mu e yiji to. Ga yijína. Mu Ironguené
chojninga: “Ironguenaté á, ca ararãre iji ti to jne,” chojninga.

“Cachi yajire ore,” yojninga, “Dupade
a jé ajéo gu jetiga yuchie jnumi. A yaquĩ́ra jne,” yojninga.

Yiji to diríca ga yijína ore to. Mu a jé chujé qué. Yajire udore, jé u uje yoquihermáno
ñane. Yajire ore ga. Mu yaquĩ́ra Eruidaté. “Eruidaté yá, bei guesi. Ga ajni
to.”

Mu yujodie chequei ore
chojninga: “Jé go, jé chaquĩ́ra. Je dóji ga chujé,” ore chojninga.

Mu yojninga: “Úngare. Dupade que
ajéo u go jetiga chujépise dojo ayoré,” yojninga. Carajnoque ore pojna yu uje
yaquĩ́ra udire ájninganenie.

Mu yojninga: “Jé yajéo ga que
yiya.” Jírape yangureta Dupade nanique. Mu jé yi. Ga yijína purupatáringai.
Ujetiga yojningame yujo ayoré, “¡Chi uati chujé ua jne á!”

Yacaranguipise uje e chĩro ore
ujetiga yiyapúsu udojo pate gajnaréngone. ¡Jeta yangári!

**Carolínadaté chatata dadode:**Yugúsiji uta ica yidai Urucu. Mu chajẽ táguedie di. Ga ore chojninga: “Ebedérodaté yá, ¿adute to a? Ujetiga chi ajócãre Goránaté ore iji udé jne.”

Yo yáyugu yu. Mu yabai narare
ome yu. Chojninga: “Jec ũré.”

Yojninga: “Ironguené á, ¿jé yo
ringome dara pijnimingore udore jne a?”

Mu
yabai chojningame yu: “Acatecã́ri Dupade ome ore, udire ore. Ca angári dara
purupataringane,” chojninga. “Mu jé yacaranguipis anire, yangácoi yocacadingórone
ore ga ca yangácoi purupatáringane.”

Yabai
chojningame yu, ga yiya.

Dejaque ica ga yo yáyugu yu. Mu
e yujé yu aja gai. Jiei yate gajnarénngone.

Yugúsiji Rincón ica, yidai
Rincón. Ga yicatecã́ri Dupade. Yise udojo Tobité. Uje cáma yangári
cucháringuiji to nanique. Yudute Oroné acaditigade. Oroné narare ome yu. Ga chojninga:
“Ebedérodaté, Dupade chetingare ayorédie ore jã.”

Yabai toi ica iji yidai Rincón.
Ebedéro chojninga: “Ité, aya bangári jne uaté gotóraque. E ore toi.
Chequésãre Chequeca casé.”

Mu yojninga: “Ebedéro, úngare.
Dupade chi etingarã yoque,” yojninga. “Yoquidai Tobité nanique ga Santiago (Jim
Ostewig) abi toi,” yojninga, “Mu jé yangureta jne ga jé yangureta jne,”
yojninga.

Que yurúo udore go jetiga yiya. Jecucha yudute uje yujodie chequei pésu yuruode. Mu
que Dupade ajéo go. Que Dupade ajepíso go. Jetiga jé yangarapoi purupatáringane
rĩ.

Yabai narare ome yu, ga
chojninga: “Ironguedaté ya, ca angári purupataringane. Ca angári.”

Ise ga purupatáringane pota
jetiga ore chijnime yu iji Dupade Uruode ya. Mu que yipota. Que Dupade ajéo go
jetiga yájni gai. Jetiga yiya ica mu yájni gai rĩ. Dupade Uruode u uje que jé yapáji
údode.

Que yipota jetiga yo
yurupatáringa go. Ujetiga yujé yiguiotique yihermánorique rĩ.

Gapu gatódape yu nanique ga yósi
jṍro to. Jeodeti ca páyipieraque déji siñeque nanique.
Jé páyipie omenie déji unojo Dupade iguíjnai udé.

Yo jṍro to nanique. Mu yabayode
toi. Ga yigana, yigana, yigana. Ga yabayode jiei. Yate gajnarécho. Ga que
yibasíji yúode. Yigana nanique. Ga yoriarása yujo gapu abai. Mu a yipésu
yáyipierigui ga yiya. Uje*móntedie*nanique ga que yiraja cuchade.

Jé ejode udojo ga que yipota
ujetiga yipésu Dupade ujuréraque go. Chajẽ chacája yajei gu Dupade Uruode. Jé
yóji Jesucristo iyoi to. Ga yacaranguipis uje a chĩro to jne yabai, yocate
gajnaréngone.

Yabayode toji yari iji dé.

Yacaranguipis uje Dupade a
chĩro jne yocate gajnaréngone Ejábiadacóde uyoc. Yiraja to uje chi Dupade
anguretatodie uyoque, jé yoquihermáno ñane iji jnumi tudé. Ga yiraja. Yiraja. Ga
que yangári yujórique ayoré yui go. Jetiga yipésu darai. Ga yipésu yujóraque
ayoré ajedotáningai. Yacaranguipis uje anire, yingongo udojo aja Dupade. E gusu yuruode.

**Dijaide uruode:**Jé ũrepise Ebedérodaté uruode uje pesapo ome yoque páyuquetotiguei tome yoque rĩ. Uje Dupade uruode qui jé u uje chojningame yoque.

Uje Jesute jé cho dacadigode
quique enga chi chojninga: “A ore pésu uacajuredie jne. Satanase pésu
uacajuredie jne. Ga Satanase a arane u uaque jne. A cojñone ore chojningame
uaque jne, ‘Urũso.’ Gajnareque ore uruode ome uaque jne. Que ore changureta yu
gu – mu anguretáyo yu gajneri,” chojninga.

**Pacádaté uruode, mu catecã́ri Dupade bajaque ga chojninga:**Yoque Dupade, yocacaranguipis uje e ugúcopóji baquei udé rĩ, yoque Dupade. Mu que cucha uña gotique uje Satanas ipesudode cucha u uje chacája yoque, yoque Dupade. Yocacaranguipis ome ua jetiga ãrope gajneri udojo cuchade iji deréjnane udojo. Que cuchárique déji siñec ujetiga chĩro cucha gajnarétique. Gusu ua uje bãro. Arajapísaja yoque gajneri, Rosadé, Rosadaté, ore uyoque gajneri. Yiyáyo gajneri uje yoco yocáyugu cuchade, cucha gajnaréngone. Gusu baruode jetiga déji yocajei. Mu yuchéco yocangoninguinone gai ome ua. Jeta ba-Espíritu Santo chacapísaja yoque gajneri. Ga jé ca yangácopísi cuchade udojo gajneri, yoque Dupade. Yicatecãcoi ua ome babi Jesucrísto i, amẽ.

Yirasique uje e ugúcopóji udé
rĩ baquei. Mu que cuchárique jnese go jetiga yatatágo. Gusu uje ore di iji
poridie diríca. Ga ore chojninga máranie gaisórone ore déji te. Yoquirasique.
Ga yoco jetiga e Dupade di iji de yocari iji udé jnumi. Ga yoquirasique ore yui
uje e ore di. Jeodeti ca cucha gajnarétique gu. Que yirote ore yui to go.
Jeodeti ca cuchárique déji gu.

Yojninga: “Yi jne. Yicai chaco
jne. Yiji yoquibosode jne. Que yipotápo cuchárique rĩ. Ga ¡jetiga déji yajei
pisijnaringuérique rĩ! Que yipota cucha uñeque. Yĩríngo mu e yipésu cuchade. Que
yicatecã́ri Dupade go ome cuchade. Gusu uje yitoji yajirigode gai. Jé
cuchárique a déji siñeque. Yacapísa yajei. Mu déji. Mu yacája. Mu cucha
gajnarétique que déji yajei go. Guedé jno. Ore chatata cuchade. Mu que yipota
cucha gajnarétique jetiga chacája yajei go. Que yipota pisijnaringuérique go. E
yudute ore uruode ome yangoninguinone.

Ore chojninga: “Jómone chi
Ucaré ore pota ñane.”

Mu que yipota jetiga cuchárique
chacája yajei. Que yipota Satanas atique go.

Yojninga: “Jé areque,
yicatecãcoi ome ñane gajneri,” yojninga.

Mu Eruidaté a ca áyipie déji
Dupade atique. Ga jĩraque áyipie déji Satanase. Ga que yaquĩ́ra Eruidaté go.
Jetiga yojninga, ‘Ayape. Ga yicobi Dupade adode bajaque.’

Ga
ejo Jómone ore to jne jetiga ore di. Yudute ore uruode, mu que chacája yajei go. Que ipotapísa cuchárique. Que yipota Satanase atique
go. Ga e yiji ga yicai ta. Ga yĩri ga Dapure chojninga: “¿Adute to a uje
Eruidaté chujé Ucaré a?”

Yojninga: “Yiraja. Jé yudute
to,” yojninga.

Yṍraji Elena. Yise yijogadi.
Yicatecã́ri Dupade. Que yáyipie que déji Satanas atique go ome yujóraque ayoré
jetiga yojninga, “Yibágabi. Ga yipojna jne.”

Gusu Dupade adode uje déji
yajei. Que yipota jetiga yipésu cucha gajnarei iji Rósadé iguíjnai ajei. Uje
yiraja gu uje a yiyáguego iji diríca uje e Jesucrísto chĩro yoque,
yoquisijnaringuéone. Ga que yipota Satanas ome Rósadé jeta Satanase chajuque
agute. Gusu Dupade jetiga Rósadé, Josuedé ore chacate yogome adode,
yoquihermáno Jómone to rĩ.

Mu a jé yatecãcoi Dupade ome ore
jne uje gajnáreque ore adode. Mu a jé yatecãcoi ome Dupade jne. ¿Socaseti
chijnápo to rĩ a? Que yipotapísa jetiga yipésu paje dotãringárique.

Yirasique uje Jesucrísto e
chĩropise yisijnaringuei. Ore
pésu dáguedie mu que yibágapoi ore to rĩ go.

Yipota jetiga yiraja darujna
aone. Ga yajíraja quigode. Jetiga yacate yujodie chequei to. Mu etoque ome yu
jetiga yiraja. Que yiraja gotique uje que yiraja go.

Ipota jetiga Ucaré ore
chatatarása dadode to, Jómone ore. Ca ore chajéngome. Que cuchárique déji go.
Ore hermánopíseráse uyoque to jne rĩ iji de diri uje Josuedé, Bai ore
chingo yoque. Ga e chĩro yoquisijnaringuéone.

Mu Eruidaté que chiraja to go. Ga
qui jé uté uje chajnerame Satanase isijnaringuei. Yipota jeta Eruidaté cho yu to.
Jetiga ca chudúdapo cuchárique rĩ. Yudute cuchade, mu yududipise ome udojo
yangoninguinone. Jé ca chacája go. Que yipota jetiga yojninga: ‘Qué yajirape
Elena ore iji te jne.’ Ga Satanase chise yu jne iji te. Mu que yipota go.

Ore chojninga: “Aquesuidé ore
di.”

Ga yajéode yosõre Aquesuidé ome
cuchárique. Cuchade uje jeodeti chirarese yu. Aquesuide di mu que chirarese yu
go. Yáyipie déji Dupade. Uje yabai paráta. Yabai pero uje ejnaretac.

Ga yihermáno di iji yu. Yajai ga
yirasique uje chise yu. Cho jetiga Dupade e di iji de. Uje yajai di. Mu yáyipie
mu jĩraque chachẽrapo Dupade rĩ. Ga que yibógo.

Yáyipie déji yabai uje pero.
Íjnoque jetiga yihermáno tagu yibosode. Que tagu go uje yabai pero. Que yiraja
yipesutique pose. Mu posode urásu Dupade Uruode ta yisi ome. Jeta yacate yajai
ome Dupade adode. Ga posode urásu.

Yacaranguipise uje yatatágopo
iji de rĩ yocate gajnaréngone, Satanase aringaquéode. A yujóraque chequé chise
yu iji yiguíjnai jne. Ga cháguejna ne ga chojninga: “Yigaidé u ua.”

Ga yojninga: “Ejẽ́. ¿Apota ga
apojna yu? Mu Dupade a chĩro bájningai jne.” Yajéode jé u udojo.

Yudute uñane diríca darade iji
dejai. Ga ore chojninga: “Atatayo ba bacaringanie agupierẽ iji te ore iquei.”

Úngare, que yipésu cucha
gajnarétique go. Que yipésu yujóraque chequé ajedotáningárique rĩ.

Goto chise yu iji yiguíjnai ga
goto pojna yu ga yojninga: “Yacaranguipis uje bapojna yu.”

Que yipota jetiga yo uaté Sára,
chequé bajei. Chi ore chojninga nanique ome chequé bajei Sara: “Chi ore chise
ome abi uje chi Dupade chisi ome uaté jne.” Ga cana ore gai. Chotigase ore.
Ajosipise ati taque ore, mu a jé ũré Dupade.

Ore chisi ome abi ome, ga jé
ũrepise ore. Enga Sára abi base nanique enga i u Isaac. Tṍra re uje e abi
bisideque. Ca bisideque, mu jé ũré Dupade. Ca yotigáco ome udojo chequé bajé
adode. Jé ũrepise.

Yadode jiei mu jé yatata, mu
Dupade chiraja ga chĩro. Que ipota go jetiga ájningarẽpise yu ome Dupade.
Gusu uje yatata cuchade. E gusu yuruode.

**Dijaide uruode:**Dupade uruode chojninga jetiga Dupade uruode qui jec u jne. Uacamóño yocaté Eba uje Satanase chãra. Ga chojninga: “Agu, taque Dupade.”

Satanase tṍraji ipatiguéode
ome yoque uje yanguretágo Dupade. Ga chapúsaja dabíode cucha ome yoque. Mu
yujnusinángo jetiga Satanase chapúsaja dabíode ome yoque.

“Yangureta Dupade. Que ipota
jetiga yipesute Satanas atígo,” oyósi te. Ajócãcho Satanase. E gusu.

**Jómone uruode:**Jé ũré chequedíe ome dacajnaquéode ome Dupade adode.

Mu Dupade uruode déji to uje
anire, que etoque ayoréode ore uyoque. Cojñone ore uyoque iji erámi jnese ga serẽrachuque
yoque gosi ome yocanguretigai. ¿Jec ũré yu a? (*1 Corintios 10:12)*

Ga anire: Ucaré ore canare iji
erámone. Mu que cho jetiga yiji. Jetiga yojninga, oré gaté yu. Mu a ore cho ore
gaté iji yu. Uje ore chutai yu iji degúode. Mu gusu Dupade uje chiraja anírone,
yocáyipiedie.

Dupade chiraja jnese iji
ayoréode uyoque. Chiraja anire, yoqueruei. Ta jec ũré. Ga ayoréraque jetiga
yipéjo cucha gajnarétic. Uje a yipéco ga yirajágo yoquipesutique gajnaréngone
jogadi u. Chiraja aníone, cucha ibirade uje que piraque yui. Mu e chiraja. Que
cucha ibirátique cuse ome Dupade gu. Uje chiraja cuchade jnese, anire, iji
yocáyipiedie.

Mu a yingongo udojo yocate gajnaréngone.
Jé uacamóño uje cucha anírone, puyaque to uje ayoréode uñane ore yiyágo.
Uacamóño chugúpẽre casicaite to uje puyé nanique, mu uje jnanione ore cha.

Ga chãraque udojo anírone,
cucha puyade udojo Dupade Uruode. Ga jé yijnángorásaja anírone, uruode udojo,
jetiga yiyágo ojningane cucha to uje puyácho, jé basa yipéco cuchade uñéngo
uje pota ome yoque. Jetiga ca yujnusinángo cucha gajnarétique rĩ.

Uje chi jec ũré ga anire,
Dupade di jne. Ujetiga jec ũré ga yanguretágo ga yirajágo yoquiqui. Jé basa
udore ore uje changureta Dupade, ga ore chiraja daqui. Ga cuchade udojo quigode
déji ujetiga jec ũré yanguretágo. Ga yicoi ga chi yicoi udore. Mu jé ca yicopoi
cucha gajnaréngone rĩ.

Ga yingongo yoquijõráringo uje
Dupade anguretasórone u ore. Ga yiyáguego iji ore iquei. Mu ¡cho jetiga aníri,
ujetiga íjnonguirase! Chajẽ qui jé uté cucha gajnaréngone ome anire, ujetiga
gosi chijnóraja date gajnaréngone.

Jé basa ga gosi changureta, ga
cha anírone, jnumi u gu, uje anire, pimoquereringai adode u gu. Uje chaquesu re
jnaque. Ga chapúsu. Jé basa chi ca gosi cha, ga jé chijnóraja. Ga que toque
jetiga anire, íjnoque ate gajnaréngone. Ga jé yadi u ome chequedíe.

*Dijaide, Nacore, Rosadaté, Bai, Carolínadaté, Pacádaté, Jomone  – Tobité, Bolivia – 27 de julio de 1975**Transcrito por: Marina de Morarie*
english:
**Don’t fear to come before Dupade**

*Ca oyo cocho cochógome Dupade yui Dijaide and others*

**Nacore’s words:**

You know what Dupade’s words say. I’m referring to Bai and all of you young men. Don’t fear to come before Dupade when we meet together.  Josuedé’s teachings are good. Don’t quit following Dupade.

We need to have other young men to lead us, as well.

Dupade forbids some things; things he hates and asks us not to do are obvious. I’m right about that. But you know about these things, too, for as believers, you have read these words of Dupade’s on the skin of the cactus (paper). You know that he forbids having two wives. He also forbids you women from having two husbands. Just one husband for the women. And just one wife for you men.

These same things were forbidden long ago to me. But I had not yet heard Dupade’s words in the jungle back then. I did those things long ago. But now we have leaders, and we are with Dupade’s other children, and are doing Dupade’s will.

And that’s the truth. Let’s not be a bother to Dupade. These are my thoughts for you, Ucaré, and for Jómone, Urúidaté, and Gatemitódacóde. Confess what you’ve done, your ugly ways.  And then they’ll be gone. Stop sinning and shake hands. Because that is what Dupade wills. And that is all I have to say.

**Dijaide’s words:**

*Rósade’s thoughts are very beautiful. Our wisdom comes from Dupade, he gives wisdom to us. He knows what is wise.

**Ró****sad****at****é’s words:**

This is what I did for three nights. I slept poorly. I was worried that Satan was on the watch for us even though we were hearing Dupade’s words and going to meetings. I wondered about those who were traveling and was looking forward to seeing my daughters, for all of my daughters were going to be arriving. And I was hoping all would go well for them. I wanted nothing to hinder them, the ugly things that can happen in this world. I was happy that my daughters were all coming to see me. I didn’t attend the woman’s gathering, and it was probably then that Satan was laying his plans against my house. I was staying at home. So, I didn’t hear anything at all.  Now, that’s the way Satan works.

I was in my house. And I saw that Eruidaté was at my house’s door. I thought she came to visit, and to hear Dupade’s words inside my house. I listened to her, but her words were about Ucaré. Everything seemed good to me. And I thought everything would be fine. Despite my preoccupations, I would never have asked my daughters not to come.

Eruidaté said: “Ucaré, I’ve come for you.” This is what she said and then she said: “I’m tired of you causing trouble for Elena everywhere you go in this world!”

Elena started to cry.

The earth was not good for me at that moment. I wondered what bad thing my daughters might have done? And I had been looking forward to the word’s of Dupade so that nothing of Satan could reach me. But here he was with me.

Eruidaté hit Ucaré in the head. So, I grabbed Eruidaté. I pushed her and turned her around. But she came at Ucaré again. So, I went for a stick. I picked it up. And I was about to hit Eruidaté. But I looked at her. I thought, she’s my brother’s friend!

I said: “I think you are someone else!” I said: “You are our longtime friend! You’re my friend! Isn’t that what we told Dupade the other day in his house?” that’s what I said to her.

Eruidaté was standing there before me. Satan was standing before me, looking for an opportunity to overcome me right here in my house. I started to think, we’re fighting like our forefathers used to fight. I thought, I’m acting as ignorant as they. And I heard myself, but Satan stood his ground in front of me, and was taking over my life, and I couldn’t do what I wanted to do.

Quickly I said to Eruidaté: “I think you’ve been acting like someone else. I almost hit you. I almost killed you over Ucaré.”

I hadn’t slept for three nights before my daughters came. Satan was there observing me, so he could attack when Eruidaté came.

But pray to Dupade for us. What we did was wrong, and maybe Dupade will erase these things we did. And we can like each other again. How quickly I reacted badly yesterday.

I said to Eruidaté: “Let’s confess to Dupade what we’ve done on Sunday.”

And Eruidaté said: “Shall we both do it?” Shall we do it together?”

All of you pray to Dupade for me right now. About the ugly things we did. That’s all I have to say.

**Dijaidé’s words:**

You’ve done the right thing by confessing to Dupade what you’ve done. And for confessing your sins to your brothers and sisters who believe in Dupade. For Dupade says: “Confess to your brothers in Christ first. And then Dupade will forgive your ugly ways. We are thankful you’ve done this, for Satan is busy all over the world.

Dupade words say we should we watchful and always remember Satan is at work, trying to deceive us.

Let’s never be ashamed to come before Dupade. Because Eruidaté, our true sister, was here with us not long ago. She is here before us now, and we consider her to be our sister. Let’s help her, for Dupade will never have words like these pass his lips: ‘Don’t help each other. Let Satan into your hearts. Don’t let me into your hearts. Don’t have faith in me. Go away.’ These things would never be on Dupade’s lips.

*******Josuédé****compares his teaching to****that****of****Joshua****who lived long ago****:**

One of the men that lived long ago named Joshua counseled his countrymen. He said to his countrymen: “Make up your minds in this land. If you will believe in Satan, or believe in the one I believe in. You must decide.”

He went on to counsel them and said: “*Listen to this great leader of ours*for he was the one our forefathers believed in, he was their Dupade.”

These words stuck to the believers who were Dupade’s countrymen. Joshua was their leader, and he told them what they should not do. He said to them: “If you don’t believe in Dupade, that’s alright, don’t believe in him. But don’t fool around with Dupade. Dupade is*puyac*. So, if you would rather follow Satan, follow him. Don’t even say Dupade’s name if you don’t believe in him.”

And let us make up our own minds while we’re still alive in this world. We have time. Time to put our faith in Dupade. Let’s be brothers and sisters. Let’s not be strangers. That’s all I have to say.

**Bai’s words:**

What our sister Rosadaté taught us is very good.

She hadn’t heard anything yet, because of staying home to read Dupade’s words. She was happy about the fact that her daughters were coming. She was excited to to see them. It was as though she was about to see Dupade. We welcome Dupade’s coming when we will see him arrive in the future. We don’t know if he will come today or in the future.  Just like Rosedaté being happy to see her daughters, Dupade will be happy to see his daughters when he comes. And when he arrives, his daughters will be happy to see their Father Dupade, also. You know that it was Dupade who made us – he made our mouths so we could talk. And he made our ears to we could hear things.

I only read Dupade’s words in the words of the Cojñone. I can only read very few verses in our own language.

It is a little like what Rósadé and them have said. They said we should love each other. And be kind to each other. And to really listen to each other. And try to agree and think alike. And not to listen to gossip about each other. Not to think we are better than others. And to only love each other sincerely. Ready to listen well to our fellow-Ayoréos.

Isn’t it possible that someone else might know more than you do? It’s Dupade’s will that we think alike and honor each other even though we are in this world. We should be patient with each other. But you women know what it says in the book of James. I’m not sure of where that verse is. ***Josuédé might know. I can’t remember where it’s found so I can’t tell you where it is. But it’s in Dupade words. It says: “Be patient.” (James 4:1-6)

These are just a few words of mine.

**Dijaide’s words:**

The verses that Bai read to us from Dupade’s words were very good. And he read them in our own language. When he said: “Don’t think too highly of yourselves.” (Romans 12:3)

Dupade hates that. We hear the Apostle Paul’s words about himself. He says: “Don’t think too highly of me. I don’t want anyone to say, ‘Paul is really smart. He knows everything.’ I don’t want you to say things like that about me.” This is what Paul the Apostle said about himself.

That is how we should be. We shouldn’t think we’re better than others. Dupade hates that kind of thinking. We should admit that we don’t know everything.

*******Carolinadaté’s words****; first she prays****:**

Dupade, you are the one we believe in. I’m very thankful that we are all together here in your house. Josuedé brings your words to us here. Dupade, we know that you see the ugly things we do. We don’t see them, but you do, because you are a great*Daijnai**.*Please forgive the ugly things we’ve done.

Dupade, we know that Satan influences us. But you are greater than he is, and you can erase sin, for you are a great and powerful*medicine man**,*you are Dupade the one we believe in. We know that we can’t always see evil ourselves. It’s all around us. But you will do something about it. I would be so thankful right now if you would give me knowledge, and your good thoughts. I have faith that you, Dupade, can do it. And I am taking steps to follow your ways with all my heart. I’d be so thankful if you help me right now. I pray to you in your Son Jesus Christ’s name. Amen.

Margarita came, and she taught us. Margarita said: “Carolínadaté, what do you want to hear?”

I didn’t know the names of the people who lived long ago. She asked: “Do you want the story about the man whose son left him?”

I said: “Margarita, of course I want to hear it. I want to hear Dupade’s words too. I’m still a young woman and I don’t know how to read but I think about it. I would like to know these things.”

Margarita taught us then. But others were using Satan’s ways. I was weaving a bag. But I wasn’t making it very well. I looked at Margarita and she was showing me the picture of the young man that left. She told me about it, and told me about it. And I didn’t understand much. About that time, they arrived. And they walked by us,*paca, paca, paque*. But they brought their gossip with them.

This dear little sister of mine was truthfully saying: “I thought something was going on over there.”

But then Eruidaté came. And she didn’t sit down. Her face was red.

Eruidaté said: “I have something to say to Jómone. But Ucaré took off alone,” she said.

But I quickly stood in her way. But my fellow women wouldn’t listen to me. I called to her, and said: “Eruidaté! It would be best if you talked to Jómone this evening,” I said.

But my fellow women said to Eruidaté: “Go on. Go to Ucaré. It’s ugly what they’re doing.”

And so Eruidaté left. She said: “I’ll go to her.” And she was gone.

But I started off too, to go with her. But Ironguené said to me: “Mother of Irongué, don’t say anything.”

“I’m just going to see what they do,” I said, “Dupade may want me to do something about it. I’m going to counsel her to stop what she’s doing.”

So, I left yesterday and went with them. But she was planning to hit Ucaré. I watched what was happening, bewildered because we were brothers and sisters. I watched them. But I counseled Eruidaté, saying: “Eruidaté, go outside, go back.”

But the other women said: “What? She’s counseling her to stop? Leave her alone, let her hit Ucaré,” they said.

But I said: “No! That’s not what Dupade wants at all, for her to hit her fellow-woman,” I said. They were ready to scold me for trying to counsel them to stop being angry.

But I said: “This is what I must do, I won’t stop.” I had become a believer right away a long time before. So, I took off. I went along with the those who were telling lies. I said to Ucaré: “They say she is going to hit you!”

I’m really grateful that Dupade erased them, and that I could stop sinning. As if I would listen to them!

Carolínadaté tells what happened to her:

I was in Urucu where I used to live, when some lying, trouble makers arrived. They called: “Ebedérodaté! Have you heard this? They’re saying that you’re going to make Goránaté leave.”

I felt bad. But my husband spoke to me and said: “It’s true.”

I said: “Ironguené! What should I do about these gossips who want to cause trouble?”

But my husband said to me: “Pray for them, these who trouble you. Don’t pay attention to their lies,” he said. “I’m thankful that we have chosen to listen to our teachers and not to lies.”

After what my husband said to me, I stopped worrying.

Then, one night I began to feel sorry for myself. I started remembering the many bad things I had done.

I used to live in Rincón – that was my town. And I prayed to Dupade. Then I went to Tobité. I was still rebellious. But I heard a message of Oroné’s. He spoke to me, and said: “Ebedérodaté! Dupade allows you women to have trials.”

My husband died there in Rincón. My daughter Ebedéro said to me. “Mother, stop listening to whoever she is. They’re dead now. She curses widows.

But I said: “Ebedéro, no. It’s Dupade who allows us to have trials.” This is what I told her. And I went on to say, “When Tobité was our town long ago, *Santiago’s son (Jim Ostewig’s son) died,” I said, “And I began to believe at that time, and will always believe.”

Despite the trial, I won’t stop believing. I’m always hearing about things that my fellow-women accuse me of saying. But Dupade isn’t directing them to say that about me. But it’s not Dupade’s desire, not his desire at all that I should again listen to lies.

My husband spoke to me and said: “Ironguedaté! Don’t listen to lies. Don’t pay attention to them.”

I think the lies have to do with pulling me away from Dupade’s words. But I don’t want that to happen. That’s not Dupade’s will at all for me to backslide. Whenever I would start to backslide, I would always repent and come back. Dupade’s word will never be a temporary thing for me.

I don’t want to lie at all. Or to hit someone I’m related to, a brother or sister.

When I was a young girl long ago I was never in a hurry. Possibly I never gave it a thought. But good thoughts are here in Dupade’s house.

I was carefree long ago. But then my husbands would die. And so, I would play around, and play and play. I had many husbands. I did many ugly things. I couldn’t stop. And all I did was play. I almost took another girl’s husband. But finally, I thought about it and stopped acting like that. That was when I was still in the jungles and didn’t know better.

But afterwards now I did not want to be a bother to Dupade. That’s because his word has entered my heart. I drink Jesus Christ’s blood. And I am so thankful that he has washed away my sins and those of my husband.

My husbands died here in my presence.

I am thankful that Dupade will forgive my sins and those of Ejábiadacóde, also. I know we are believers, that those of us who believe are sisters and brothers on this earth. I’m sure, very sure of this. And so, I don’t listen to what my fellow-Ayoré says. As if I would make something up about what another person says to make her angry! I am thankful that we believers can tell Dupade all about it.

This is all I have to say.

**Dijaide’s words****:**

Edebérodaté’s words that she has shared with us are true and full of wisdom. For they are from Dupade’s words.

Jesus counseled his disciples and he said to them: “People are going to make trouble for you. Satan is going to make trouble for you. He will deceive some of you. People will say you are crazy. The things they say about you will be ugly. But that’s because they don’t believe in Dupade, but you do.”

**Pac****á****daté****’s words****; she first prays to****Dupade****:**

Our leader Dupade, we are thankful that we have come before you again here in your house. But it’s not something else that Satan is doing when he comes into our midst. We are thankful that you can erase what he is trying to do these days. There is nothing else anywhere that can make wrong doings and sin go away. Only you can get rid of them. You know all about us right now, about Rósadé, Rósadaté, and all of us. We are going to stop being confused and worried about the ugly things that have happened. We only want your word to prevail among us. We are opening our ears to hear you. May your Holy Spirit enter all of us right now. And may we not listen to others, only to you. We speak to you in the name of your Son Jesus Christ. Amen.

I’m glad that we are gathered here in your presence. But we don’t want to be talking about anything but the matter at hand. Only about when the men returned from cutting down mahogany trees. We were as excited to see them as if it was Dupade returning to us here on earth! That’s how excited we were when the men came back. It seemed nothing could go wrong. Had we not been awaiting their arrival, nothing would have seemed right.

I said: “I’m leaving. I’m going to my garden. I’ll bring something back to eat. I won’t need anything. As if sinful happenings were on my mind! I didn’t want for anything. We got back and I started getting things ready. I didn’t pray to Dupade about anything. I was only concentrating on the things I had to do. Nothing else was on my mind. I was taken up with my own things. But there was something, I entered my thoughts. But it was still there and I kept pondering it. But nothing bad was on my mind.

It was late afternoon. They started talking about things. But I didn’t want bad things to enter my mind at all. I didn’t want anything to do with sin. I heard their words with my ears.

What said: “Jómone and Ucaré want each other.”

I still didn’t want anything to enter my mind. I didn’t want to hear about what Satan was doing.

I said:  “O.K., we’ll talk about it later.”

But I don’t think Eruidaté had her mind on Dupade. I think Satan was in control of her thoughts. And I didn’t say anything to Eruidaté at all. I didn’t even say, ‘Wait. First, let’s do what Dupade says to do.’

Jómone and the others didn’t arrive until later. I heard what they were saying, but it didn’t register in my mind. I didn’t want to have anything to do with it. I didn’t want anything to do with Satan. I finished what I was doing and went to do something else, and when I got back Dapuré said: “Have you heard that Eruidaté hit Ucaré?”

I said: “I know. I heard about it.”

I came from Elena’s house, and reached my own, and I  prayed to Dupade. I wasn’t thinking about Satan at all concerning my fellow woman, nor did I say, “I’ll go to her and bawl her out.”

I was only thinking about what Dupade wanted. I didn’t want to do anything ugly in Rósadé’s house. That’s because I knew that we had only recently stood up to accept Jesus and to be forgiven of our sins. And I didn’t want Satan to have his way and split Rósadé’s gall bladder. The only thing Rósadé, Josuedé, and our brother Jómone taught us was about Dupade’s ways.

But now we were praying for them, because of the ugly things that were done. And we kept praying to Dupade. Possibly he would take them back again. I did not want to make anyone angry.

I’m glad that Jesus Christ has forgiven my sins. They are taking sides, but I’m not lining up with either one.

I want to know how to read and to understand what I am reading. Then I I could teach my fellow-women. But it is hard for me. I don’t understand because I can’t read at all.

I wish that Ucaré and Jómone would confess what they’ve done, and not be ashamed before us. There’s no reason they should be ashamed for we are their brothers and sisters who also believe in Christ, just as Josuédé (Dijaide) and Bai have said.  And he has erase our sins.

But Eruidaté didn’t understand this. And that is why she carried on with Satan’s violence. I wanted Eruidaté to do as I do, and not be pay attention to the things people do. I hear what is said, but my ears are open to being understand. I don’t let it affect me personally. I don’t want to say, ‘I’m going over there and see what’s going on with Elena.’ And then have Satan influence me. That’s not what I want to happen.

They said: “Aquesudé and them are arriving!”

I felt sorry for Aquesuidé, and wondered what I could do for him. I thought probably I would be moved to tears when I saw him. Aquesuidé arrived but it didn’t make me sad to see. My thoughts were upon Dupade. My husband was weak. My husband couldn’t walk because he was sick.

When my brother came to me. I was happy when he reached me. I was as content as if Jesus himself had arrived. For it was my own brother that had arrived. But right away my mind grabbed onto Dupade again. We didn’t cry.

I was thinking about my husband who was crippled. My brother didn’t want to eat my food. He didn’t eat it, because my husband was sick. I didn’t know what to make to eat. But his food would be Dupade’s words if I gave them to him and taught him about what Dupade does. His food would be this – spiritual food.

I am thankful that we have gathered here to confess our  ugly ways, brought about by Satan’s deceptions. If a fellow woman came to my house and stood there and said: “I’ve come for you!”

I would say: “O.K. Do you want to bawl me out? Dupade could erase your anger.” This is what I would do.

I heard the voices of others in the night. They were saying: “You should confess before them what you’ve done wrong, tell who you have had intercourse with, your sex partners.”

I would say: “No, I haven’t done anything wrong at all. I have not made any of my fellow women angry.”

If another woman, whoever she might be, came to my house and bawled me out, I would say to her: “Thank you for bawling me out.”

I don’t want to be like that woman Sara that lived long ago. It’s said they spoke to Sara and said to her: “We’ve come to you to tell you that Dupade says he will give you a child.” Sara laughed at them. She doubted them. She thought they were lying about it, but Dupade was telling her the truth.

They gave her baby They had told her the truth. And Sara’s child was born, and his hame was Isaac. She was astounded and very old and thought her child would be illegitimate. But the father was her very old husband, and was not illegitimate. Dupade had told them the truth. We shouldn’t doubt what happened to that woman who lived long ago. It’s true.

I have done many things, but I always confessed them so that Dupade would forgive me. I never want to be angry with Dupade. I only want to be open with him and tell him about everything.

That’s all I have to say.

**Dijaide****’s words:**

Dupade’s words tell what they mean. You know how it was with our mother Eve that Satan deceived. He said to her: “Eat, Dupade is lying. You won’t die.”

Satan comes to us who believe in Dupade by twisting Dupade’s words. And he enters other things when he comes to speak to us. But let’s remember this that Satan comes to us in other forms.

Say this when you want to dismiss Satan: “I have faith in Dupade. I do not want to do what the things Satan wants me to do.” That is all I have to say.

**Jó****mone****’s words:**

These women have told the truth about what the things Dupade wants us to do.

But there’s something in Dupade’s words about how us humans are not strong. Ayoreos and*C**ojñone*all over the earth are alike in that we are all weak in our faith. Isn’t that true? (*1 Corinthians 10:12)*

This is what happened: Ucaré and them were playing around. But it isn’t as though I didn’t participate. I didn’t tell them I was ghost. But to them I may have seemed like a spirit. For they asked to be with me in their camp. Bu only Dupade knows what goes one in our hearts.

Dupade knows all about us humans. He even knows when our end will come. And that’s when it will come. And he knows the woman that we sin with. For when we are doing it, right then we know how wrong it is. He knows things, the difficult, unexpected things, he already knows when they will happen.  There is nothing difficult for Dupade, that’s why. That’s because he knows everything we think about.

But now we are will confess our ugly ways.

You know that there are things that were once*puyac*for us Ayoreos, that we have stopped and no longer observe. You know about the bird we once worshiped that she was*puyac*, but now we men have stopped doing the bird festival.

Dupade’s words address these things that were once puyac and forbids them. And that is why we should follow these words of Dupade’s, so that we can stop observing the things that were once puyac. On the other hand, he shows us other things he wants us to do. And when we do them, we no longer remember the things that were wrong.

For it’s true that Dupade is going to come. If it’s true and we believe in him, we know our future destination. As well as others that believe in Dupade, they, too will know their destinations. And these things are going to happen for those of us who truly believe. Let’s truly believe and do these things. But let’s not go back to being sinful.

We are telling our friends about this who are believers, standing here before them. Not as those who have no sin. For  we have sinned. It’s about what it means to do the wrong things and to being someone who loves and hangs onto his ugly ways.

On the other hand, if someone believes, he stops doing the things of this world – flirting and playing around. He’ll cut it out and stop it right away and is done with it. On the other hand, if the person doesn’t stop it, he’ll go right one doing what he loves to do.  Then it’s not possible to overcome sin. And that’s how I’ve been with women, weak.

Dupade – God

Leader, one we listen to – Lord

Great leader, one we listen to – Great Lord

Yoqui-Dupade – Our God

Dupade’s Words – God’s Word

Paca, paca, paque – Walking fast

Puyac – Taboo, holy

Skin of the cactus – book, paper

Daijnai – Medicine man, Shaman

*Santiago – Jim Ostewig. (Jimmy Ostewig who died at one years old, and was buried  in Tobité where Jim and Helen Ostewig were missionaries. Carolinadaté became a believer when she saw the faith the Ostewigs had; that they really believed that Jimmy would come out of his grave when Jesus came back.)

*Carolínadaté (mother of Carolina) who refers to herself with other names – depending on the name of the child she was known by at the time: Ebedérodaté (mother of Ebedero),  Ironguédaté (mother of Irongue)

*Josuédé – (father of Josué) Another name for Dijaidé.

*Rósadé – (Father of Rosa) Another name for Nacore.

*Dijaide and other**speak**ers –**Tobité, Bolivia – July 27,1975**.*

*Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie.*

ayoreo: Yirajai to uje Satanase chise yu, ga deroco Dupade Uruode yúode iji ujade. Mu yirajai u uje Espíritu Sánto chise yu, ga uneripise Dupade uruode yui ome yu. ¡Unere! Yipota ujeta yacate yujode ayoréo. Mu yacaranguipis ome Dupade uje Espíritu Sánto chise yu iji ujade, enga uneripise Dupade uruode ome yu.
english: What I know is this: when Satan is working in me, God’s word is not as interesting at those times. But I also know that when the Holy Spirit is active in my life, at those times, God’s word is very interesting. It’s so good that I can hardly wait to share it with my fellow Ayoreos. I’m so grateful for the times when God’s Holy Spirit makes God’s word appealing to me.

ayoreo: Udojo u ujetiga yo Dupade anguretaisṍri iji yu ga ajnusináño to gajine udojo uje yocãrai chi jec u yoque. Yãrai jé u yu.
english: This is what can happen, I might consider myself a believer in God, but, remember it is easy to fool ourselves. I could deceive myself about this.

ayoreo:
Dupade,
Jesuse chojningame yu dirome: “Bacate ore bisideque diríca. Cucha bisideque bacajnaquéode diríca. Que bei déji
darujna aoi to. Que batrabajándoringárique déji yu go. Íjnoque bacajnaquéringo
iji yu rĩ.”
english: If this were how things were with me, then the Lord Jesus would say to me in the future: “You taught people for nothing. Your messages had nothing of value in them. And your name is not found in the ‘book.’ I have not recorded any of the works you claim to have done for me, nor any of your teachings.”

ayoreo:
Ujetiga Jesus chojningame yu
dirome: “Mu bo, acai gajño pioi, bapotigádi casé. Bapotigádi jec u diríca uje
bangurenone u bapesute arégode jeta piquetaque ua ome udore. Jé basa ga que
bangureta Jesucrísto uyu gotique jeta yique ta ua.” Ga yijnaripísome yui.
Yitodo. Yitodo jõronga ujetiga jé ũré uje e chiroque yu aja gajño pioi.
english: If this happened, then Jesus would say to me: “So, now, you must go to the Lake of Fire. That was what you chose.” Hearing these words, I would be filled with dread. I would be afraid. But, afraid in vain, if it were true that he was sending me to the Lake of Fire.

ayoreo:
Chi yocangaráni tu dirome, Jesus
iji deréjnai uñeque. Ujetiga yi que déji to que, ga yujnúsi jórogui. Yujnúsi
jórogui Dupade ucuei casé. Que toque ujetiga qué*tiémporaque*déji jetiga qué yangureta.
english: One day Jesus will show himself as Lord. If my name is not there in the book, I will be sad, but in vain, it will not change anything. I’ll be sad that God looked for it, but it wasn’t there. There will be no time left for me to believe.

ayoreo:
Beyoi, yujode Jesus anguretaisórone uaque iji de gajiné, enga ca yarángo Jesus. Ca yarángo Jesuse, dayé ore gajiné. Yicoi ga yejnángo yógaja Dupade. Yejnángo yógaja. Ca yarángo Dupade dirome. Ca yocate gajnarétique déji yocáyipie. Ujetiga uaque gosi cáma icha
áyipie go, ga gosi qué pógu datique ome ayoréode uyoque, mu Dupade chiraja. Chise
diri dirome ujetiga chajire yoquíode ga íjnoque ujetiga qué chĩro uaque gosi
ate gajnaré casodé. Chamáñajé gosi que catecã́ri Dupade ome re gu ome date
gajnaré casodé.
english: Come now, my fellow believers in Jesus, let’s not deceive Jesus. Let’s not try to fool Jesus or his Father. Let’s give ourselves to God. Let’s give him everything we are and have. We won’t be able to fool God on that future day. Let’s not hide the sin that is in our hearts. Whoever does not have a clean heart might fool the rest of us, but God knows exactly what’s in our hearts. The day is coming when God will look for our names in the book to see if our sin has been forgiven, or if we have never truly asked God to forgive our sin.

ayoreo: Yocacaranguipise gajiné uje bisidi déji ome yocate gajnaréngone enga bisidi u Jesus iyoi. Yicoi ga yutõcoi Jesus jnese ujetiga chĩro yoquisijnaringuéone, enga ca yipójo yocate gajnarétique uñéngome uté: jé ca yãrájo yoque jne.
english: Oh, how grateful we should be that there is a remedy for sin. I think it was Peter who spoke to Jesus and said, “Lord, what should I do about the people who sin against me? Shall I ignore sin, or forgive them?”

ayoreo:
Que yiraja ujeta Pédro u uje catecã́ri
Jesus nanique ga chojninga: “Yocacadisṍri á, ¿jé yoco rique jne ome
yocujórique ayoréo ujetiga atígo gajnarécho a? Jeta yṍrango aja guesi, jeta
yi*perdonar*ome.”
english: Jesus explained it to Peter: ‘You all know that I have forgiven you many times. You should be like this also. Think the same way about this as I do.’ That’s what Jesus said to his disciples.

ayoreo:
Mu Jesuse chi chojninga: “Cachi
jiei á. Uacamóño to iji diríca uje qué yiji*perdonar*ome uaque diríca,” chojninga. “Enga uaque gajnui jé u ome uaque. Chorase
yáyipieraque gajiné,” chi Jesus chósi tome dacadigode. Chi íjnoque deréjnai iji
dirome, yijorane uaque, iji durase udaque. Mu beyoi ga anguretáyo Dupade jé iji
de diri. Ca yãrango yoc.
english: Time is running out, my friends, even this morning. Come now, and really believe in God today. Let’s don’t deceive ourselves.

ayoreo:
Gosi ujetiga pógu date gajnaréngone
diríca, mu cho Dupade anguretaisṍri iji re, ãrai jec u re. Que chãra Dupade
go. Ajnusináño to gajiné uje chi yocãrai jec u yoc. Yãrai tu yu mu yeruei tu
dirome iji gajño pioi.
english: If someone is hiding his sins, and has just been acting like a believer, he is deceiving himself. But there is no way he can deceive God. Remember now that it is easy to fool ourselves. If I go on fooling myself, though, I’ll end up in the Lake of Fire.

ayoreo:
Mu caniraque yocangarádejnai
Jesus enga pota chiqueta yoque, enga chojningárasome yoque: “Ujetiga arañiñome
cuchade iji jnumi, enga acho Satanas isagode, ga boyo ga abáchoi yu. Ga
yamuráse uacagúchade tiji uacabai. Uacagúchade u uacate gajnaréngone. Uje pénu.
Abáchoi yu ga yamurase uacagúchade iji uacabai.” Jesuse chósi tome yoque uje anire,
cho quique yogome yocate gajnaréngone.
english: “If you are tired of this world, if you’re tired of serving Satan, then come to Me. And I will take the load off your backs,” Jesus said. Our loads are the sins we go on trying to hide. They are very heavy. “Come to me, and I’ll lift them off you,” Jesus is saying to us, counseling us about our sin.*(Matthew 11:28,29)*

ayoreo: Mu íjnoque dirome panguretaretigai. Íjnoque panguretaretigai jeta Jesuse di. Mu gajño pioi déji enga chi gajnáreque. Gajnáreque dequejnaréngone uje chi jiei iji. Dequejnaréngone jiei iji ga jnupuchade chi chuchíja ore. Chójmare ai. Quedéjna ai. Pioi tagu ore, mu que chejna ore go. Ore po. Ore chigase ome damatarane. Ore jnare rĩ iji deréjnane, ga que ore chisapo guetique rĩ. Uásejna dequejnaréngone iji. Dequejnaréngone edo tu.
english: Soon it will be too late to have faith in Jesus. No more time for faith. The Lake of Fire is a terrible place. Terrible pain and suffering are there. So much pain with worms defecating on people. There will be an ugly odor there, a strange odor called sulfur. Fire burning people, but not killing them. They will cry. They will grind their teeth. They’ll long for escape, but in vain. The torment will continue, a kind that no one has ever experienced before – the very root of all suffering.

ayoreo: ¿Gotique gu a uje que yitodógo aniri, Dupade? Que yitodógo gu uje angoneparique yogome Dupade. Mu chi déji dirome ujetiga yitodógo Dupade, ga yitodógo dirome, mu e íjnoque tiempo. E íjnoque deréjnai ujeta qué yanguretágo. Ga jé areque gajneri ujetiga ca yijárogo yógaja ome Dupade uruode iji deréjnane unojo.
english: Why don’t we fear God? We don’t fear him because we have closed our ears from hearing him. But the day is coming when, at last, we will fear him, but it will be too late! Too late to put our faith in him to save us. It would be wise, right now, to stop hardening our hearts to his Word.

ayoreo:
Yacaditigade garósi, mu jé
chojmára yocangaráni. Chojmára yacaditigade gajnesṍri ome yoque iji deréjnane
unojo. Que uñeque déji siñeque jetiga chécãre Dupade uru quitique. Jé chojmára
yocangaráni, ga jé iji deréjnai tudé.
english: My teaching is a short one, but I want to say this: we all have the same Lord. The same teaching applies to all of us today. There is no one anywhere that can change anything God has said. We have the same Lord today.

ayoreo:
Ueradipise Dupade. Paraque. Arei.
Omi. Pajesereringane garani tu. Páyipiedie eruei tu.
english: Dupade is beautiful, gentle, good, kind. Peace originates with him. He has the final will about things.

ayoreo: Dupade chojninga: “Ayoréode uaque, que uacáyipie tu yáyipie go. Que uacáyipie yáyipie go. Uacáyipiedie catadape. Jé yáyipie chise jnumi eruéode,” chojninga Dupade ome yoque. Jecute ujopierãque ga áyipie sué yoquibai.*(Isaías 55:8)*
english: God says: “People, your thoughts are not my thoughts at all. Not my thoughts at all! Your thoughts are ‘short,’ but mine fill the whole world.”

ayoreo:
Mu uje que yanguretágo Dupade ga
yojnico, ‘Yujnué Dupade gai.’ Mu gajnáreque ujetiga yujnuéngo Dupade gai ome
yocajéode. Jeta yojnico, ‘Yujnué gai.’
english: This is what God says to us. Therefore we know He is all powerful and so much greater than we in the way he thinks.*(Isaiah 55:8,9)*

ayoreo:
Uacamóño uje páyipiedie eruei
tu. Que yocáyipieraque cuse.
english: But when we don’t believe in God we are saying, ‘I’m greater than God.’ But it’s a very ugly thing to think we’re greater than God, and to claim, “I know more that He does.”

ayoreo:
David gotique a u uje chi
chojninga: “Yucue jórogui yiquicho erãpe. Yucué, yucué ujetiga qué yacose yu
ome Dupade, ga yicai jnumi eruéode. Yicai yote quedéjna ajéode, mu Dupade
déji.”*(Salmo 139:1 aja 7 ore)*
english: You know, He is the all-knowing one, and we don’t know anything.

ayoreo:
Que eruénique cuse go ome
Dupade, jec u deréjna casé, ga jé upo dirome rĩ. Que chẽcare re. Que
choquíjna Dupade. Mu choquíjnane u yoque. Choquíjnane uyoque uje angoneparique
yógome Dupade gu.
english: David, of long ago who we’ve never seen, said this: ‘I looked in vain for a place in the world where I could hide from God, and I went to the ends of the earth. I went to the depths of the sea, but God was there.’ There is no end to God, he existed in the beginning and will exist in the future. He will never change. God doesn’t grow old, but we grow old. We grow old, because we disobey Him.*(Psalm 139:**1-12**)*

ayoreo: Enga gusu yacajnaquéode iji durase tudaque, mu yujnusinángo gajiné udé yacajnaqué garósiode ome yoque uje que piraque yuruode go. Que piraque yacajnaquéode go. Uje uacojo ayoré yu gu. Uacarajáyopise yacajnaquéode quigode ome Dupade áyipie quigode ome uaque. Ujetiga uacamóño Jesus uté to uje chojninga: “Ajnáño yu aja quedéjnane jetiga jec ũré uaque,” ome dacadigode. “Ajnóraño uacojode ayoré. Ca ajnóra ua. Ajnóra uacojode ayoré. Ujetiga gosi chutai uaque ujetiga bagúchade déji ga asíome. Mu ca acué oréraque,” chojninga Jesus ome yoque, “Ca acuei oréraque.”
english: There is nothing that excells God, nothing in the past, and nothing in the future. He never changes. He never grows old. We are the ones that age. It’s because of our disobedience, that we human’s reach a state of oldness.

ayoreo:
Yocacaranguipise gajiné uje yugúcoji
udojo. Jesuse uruode ome yoque u ujeta cho yoquiquique ome cuchade, ome cucha
omone yui. Ca yijárogo yoque aja ome Jesuse, Yocangarádejna, Yocaquirãsóri udé
arecujáidie ome yoque.
english: That’s all I have to teach this morning, but remember these few teachings of mine, for they are not difficult to understand. They’re simple, because a person just like yourselves has taught them to you. You’ve understood what I’ve taught you about what God’s thoughts are concerning you. You know about Jesus, and how he said to his disciples, ‘Follow me everywhere if you’re sincere about me. Love your neighbors, more than yourselves. If someone asks of you, and you have it, give it to him. And don’t expect anything in return.’ Jesus is saying this to us, ‘Don’t give with ulterior motives.’

ayoreo:
“Dajei tu yu,” chojninga. “Uaque
uñeque a changureta yu bajaque dirome enga dajei u yu ome Dupade idai, Yocai.
Que pibi yocai, yoquidai go ujetiga ca pangureta yu.” Chi ca yacája Dupade idai
jne ujetiga ca yanguretágo Dupade abi.*(San
Juan 14:6)*
english: We should be so grateful that we can be here, learning about what Jesus said and the counsel he gave, and all the good things he told that are yet to come. So, let’s not harden our hearts against what our Lord and Counselor has said.

ayoreo: Yocacaranguipise gajine iji durasé uje uacarajáyo yacajnaquei garósiode. Que qué piraque yacajnaquéode, mu que yimo jetiga gajnarécho ome gosi, mu Dupade uruode jec u. Uje chojninga: “Ujetiga gosi cáma changureta yu dirome ga, ujetiga chijároja dangoninguinone ome yu dirome ga, gosi qué uniri tu jnumi gu. Gosi qué uniri tu jnumi ga cáma uniri tu gaté go. Chamáñajé qué chirota jnumi jogosórone cucha, ga chejnáreque yocacajnaquéode. A ore chejnáreque uacacajnaquéode dirome mu jé u,” chojninga Jesucrísto.
english: “I’m the road,” Jesus said. “For any of you who put me first and are believing in me for the future, I will be the Road to Heaven. No one has entrance to the City that belongs to me and my Father, if he does not believe in Me.”

ayoreo:
Yicoi gajine ga a jé ca
yejnárejo yogome pacajnaquéode gajiné ujetiga jec ũré yoque. Uje ujetiga gosi
que changureta Dupade, ga ore chejnáreque re. Ore jos eti yipojnángo ore.
Chamáñajé Jesus uruode jé u gu. Ore jos eti yipojnángo ore iji jnumi tudé.
Chamáñajé cáma ore changureta, cáma ore chiraja danguretique gu. Ore chijnóra
jnumi jogosórone cucha. Uje ueracho ome ore.
english: We’re grateful this morning that my few teachings are not hard to understand. Though not hard, perhaps hard for some of you to hear; but they are still God’s words, not my own.

ayoreo:
Mu ome uhuac uje uacanguretáyo,
Dupade chojninga: “E uacarajáyo ujeta ca ejnarécho jnumi jogosórone ore urúrigo
ome uaque dirome. Cucha bisideque ore uruode udore ome uaque uje que ore
changureta yu, ga cucha omone u ome uaque dirome,” chojninga. “Jetiga gosode
pésu uaquejóporinguéi dirome enga, a yipésu ore ejoporinguéone to dirome iji
dirode eruei.”
english: He says, “If someone doesn’t believe in me and closes his ears to what I have to say, it’s because he still belongs to this world. And whoever is still of this world, that person does not yet belong to heaven. He is still defending the things of this world and finds our teaching offensive.”

ayoreo:
Ga jé areque gajine ujetiga jé
ca yipéco yocujode Dupade anguresṍre ejóporinguéone to uje Dupade chãraque
danguretaisṍrone gu. Chãjé puyaque ore gu. Puyaque Dupade rĩ. Ga chi e mu
ore cachodi tu aníri, Jesus diringai. “Angureta yu,” chojninga, “Anguretáyo yu
ujetiga jec ũré uaque, ga ujetiga uacojórique ayoré gajnáreque urúrigo ome ua,
ga ca achajnáñoi ore,” chojninga, “uje uacangureni tu yu gu. Anguretáyo yu. Jé
chicha gosi cachodi taja jnumi eruei dirome,” chojninga. “A yajéngome to dirome
ujetiga gosi ca cha date gajnaréngone.”
english: So, let’s not be like the ones who find his teaching unpleasant, let’s be sincere. For when people don’t really believe in God, they can’t tolerate the things of God. They think we’re bawling them out, but it’s Jesus’ words we’ve been telling them. They think we’re scolding them here on earth, because they don’t yet have faith, and they don’t yet have a Savior. They love the things of the world and find them appealing. But God says to you who believe: “Know that the people of earth will speak evil of you, but you don’t have to worry about that. They talk like that, because they don’t believe in me, but in the future, it will work out for your good.” That is what God’s word says. “Those who make trouble for you now, I will make trouble for them in the future when the end comes.”

ayoreo:
Mu beyoi uté gosi ujetiga
chetaque uaque, ga acatecãchoi Dupade ome. Ga jé ãro uté ate gajnarégone ome
uaque gajiné ujetiga yicoi yocangarádejnai Jesuse uru casodicaite ome yoque
ujetiga yicatecãcoi Yocangaráni ome. Enga chijna jne jeta di, enga, ¡socaseti
chijna dirome, ga yitojmárango iji jnumi á!”
english: It’s a good thing to not treat our fellow believers badly, for God protects his believers. They are holy, just as God is holy. But those others, when Jesus comes back again that will be the end of them.

ayoreo:
Gusu yacajnaquéode iji durase udaque to ome yoque. Enga yacaranguipise
ome uje yijorane ore cho paraque iji yacajnaquéode udé údi. Enga gusu
yacajnaquéode.
english: “Have faith in me,” God says, “Be true and faithful to me, and if someone speaks ill of you, don’t turn around and speak ill of him, just be still,” God says. “Just trust in me and believe in me. Their future is already planned, they will perish at the end of time.” And he says. “I will deny those who deny me and continue in sin.”

ayoreo: *Dijaide – Rincón de Tigre – 1975**Marina de Morarie churúsãre Dijaide uruode udojo*
english: Try to bring that one who hates you to the Lord and pray for him. And forgive him and let’s do what our Lord Jesus said for us to do. He said we should pray for our enemies and those that despitefully use us, for there’s always the possibility that they will believe, and the Lord will take them up also, and they will become one with us in Christ.
