ayoreo: Uacamóño uje ayoréode uyoque gare pisagode. A uaque gosi doi Dupade Uruode ome ayoréode ga isai tu ome uje chojmára. Enga uaque gosi sue Dupade anguresõre bajade gai. Uaque gosi sué eduguéjnane, Dupade Uruode jnungórone gai. Chise diri te ga a uaque gosi pésu dejnai. Enga e gare gosi isagode. Jecute jé ũrepíse uje a uaque gosi sue eduguénane ore gai uje uaque gosi u uje uaque gosi que chijnime re ome Dupade Uruode go. Enga chejna, chejna deréjnane. Chejna dirode uje chejna yijmanai. Chise gójni diri te ga changureta rome Dupade Uruode yui. Changureta rome Dupade úringarique irasique yui enga e chajire Dupade Uruode. Uje cha chajire Dupade Uruode, mu e chacate re iji dajei. Pésu dacajnaquéode ome darásade ore ga isagode e gare aja guesi.
english: I remember what Paul said about him: “Tito is not a Jew, so how is it that his faith is greater than ours who are Jews, and he knows more about*Dupade’s Words*.”

ayoreo: Enga ise ga Pablo ore ojningai tu, Pedro ore ojninga tu ome jnani bajai adode unojo nanique. A chise gójni udore uje ore chamáta ñane iji Dupade iguíjnane enga e pésu re ome Dupade Uruode. Pésu dacajnaquéode. Chacate dajei, que chirajaque Dupade atique jne. Chise dire omi te rĩ uje Lunes ga e désu dajéode. E pésu dejnai, enga isagode gáraja guesi. Enga diri tu uje que agutíyamátique cuse. Mu agutiyamáni jé u re. Enga uté u uje Pablo ore chojningame: “Chimo jóroque, uje que judío mu sue judíode yoquibai tome Dupade adode.”
english: And this is what I meant when I said that somewhere there is an Ayoré who is more faithful than the missionaries in how he lives, not doing anything ugly. He doesn’t get angry. He doesn’t steal. He would never think of spreading lies. And he would work harder than the missionaries who brought*Dupade’s Words*to us.*Dupade*might not provide food and clothing for him, but he would still serve*Dupade.*He would give his whole state of being to*Dupade*and*Dupade*would send him into all the world, and others of us*Ayoreos*would go with him. We would become those who give their all to carry*Dupade’s Words*to others. We would serve*Dupade*even more faithfully than the missionaries have served Him, and leave them speechless!

ayoreo: Pésu dejnai. Que yimo jeta pésu dejnatique, mu bamo uje ayoréode uyoque yocucuecaringuei garani tu yoquejnane. Chise gójni Domíngonie enga a pésu Dupade Uruode ome darásade. Enga a uaque gosi a doi Dupade Uruode unojo enga a uaque gosi sue Dupade Uruode jnungórone gosi gai.
english: Sometimes*Dupade*’s servants depend on*Dupade*to supply their needs. Others depend on the work of their own hands for their livelihood. Some live by faith, depending on their preaching of*Dupade’s Words.*This type of person surpasses us –Ayoreos, and Cojñone all of us in this world.

ayoreo: Que ayorérique Dupade Uruode jnungónique u uje di Dupade uruode ome yoque nanique. Que uté chiraja yocuruode iji taningai, mu diga chudute yocuruode enga chirate yocuruode nanique. Mu di bisideque ome uacuñane uje que amesecho Dupade uruode gu, enga que chiraja daru quitígo ome uaque. Jé uaque uñeque jetiga chojninga: “Que yangureta Dupade. Que yagutíyame Dupade isocárique to. Que yiji Dupade Uruode.”
english: You know that we Ayoré who serve*Dupade*have two jobs. One of us might preach*Dupade’s Words*to the*Ayoreos*, and that is one of his jobs. And he works harder than the first believers who preached*Dupade’s Words*. He works harder than the missionaries who brought*Dupade’s Words*to us. Because he also must work in his garden. And that is his second job. He has two jobs. Therefore, it’s true that he works harder than the missionaries, for he does not top preaching to work in his garden. And he does this day after day. He works for five days. When the sixth day comes around he starts preparing himself to preach*Dupade’s Words*the next day. He wants to please*Dupade*, and so he studies*Dupade’s Words*. And when he is through, he practices in his mind how he is going to teach what he has studied. And when his teaching is ready, he has completed two jobs.

ayoreo: Enga a uyoque gosi jé yocagutíyamáni u Dupade, ga que yirajágo yocuru quitígome uaque gosi go. Mu qué quitique cuse iji Dupade Uruode. Chojninga: “Gosi uje doi Dupade adode iroquéode enga ujetiga gosi iguidedie garani tu Dupade iroquéode.”
english: When it was time to gather in*Dupade*’s house, he would be ready to teach*Dupade’s Words*. His teaching was prepared ahead of time. He had meditated on it and would know what the scripture was about. But on Monday he was still thinking about it even though he was back at work in his garden, his second job. And for that job he had no one to help him. He must help himself.

ayoreo:
Mu uté yocayode jnani bajade uñai San Pablo ore chojninga: “Chamáñaje
ueca yu,” chojninga, “enga yucué yáyipie,” chojninga. “Yipésu yisagode to,”
chojninga, “yisa yejode to, enga yajiri tu yurásade, yijorane ore iji erámi uje
yoquiguidedie garani tu yijmaninie,” chojninga. “Que yedugáta Dupade gotique.
Que yedugáta Dupade anguresórone ujetiga yojningame ore ujeta ore chisi yu. Ore
chisi yu dagúchade mu yedugáta déji yijmainie,” chojninga.*(Hechos 18:2 aja 4)*
english: And that’s why Paul and they would say of Tito: “How is it that he is not a Jew, but he far surpasses us Jews in following*Dupade*!”

ayoreo: A uaque gosi a jé gajnui tu San Pablo ujetiga uaque gosi cheduape Dupade ome anínique dajnetique cucha. Gaidi tu uje Dupade irasique. Gaidi tu urasade jeta ore irasique rĩ enga cháta anírone dasagode cucha aja ore.*(1 Timoteo 5:17 aja 18)*
english: I don’t know if the man Paul talked about made a garden, but for us Ayoreos, our livelihood comes from our gardens. Then comes Sundays and he serves his countrymen by preaching*Dupade’s Words*. If any of you serve*Dupade*in this way, you surpass the missionary who brought*Dupade’s Words to us,*whoever he is.

ayoreo: Jecute yuruode qui u uje ise ga yoc uñeque déji jṍronguiji jeta gosi sue Dupade Uruode jnungórone ore yoquibai.
english: The one that brought*Dupade’s Words*to us long ago wasn’t an*Ayoré*. He didn’t know our language in the beginning, but when he arrived he heard our words and imitated us. But for some of us who refused*Dupade’s Words*, he came for nothing. He didn’t know what to say to those who didn’t believe, those who would say: “I don’t believe in*Dupade*. I don’t see the benefit in following*Dupade*. That’s why I don’t go to the meetings to learn about*Dupade’s Words*.”

ayoreo: *Ecarai – Tobité, Bolivia – 1960**Marina de Morarie churúsãre Ecarai acajnaquéode nanique*
english: But some of us have benefited greatly from following*Dupade*, and we still don’t know what to say to those that feel there is no benefit in it for them.  But*Dupade’s Words*talks about those that preach his word. It says: “Clothing and other things a person needs should be given to those that serve*Dupade*by preaching*His Words.”*

ayoreo: ¡Mu cho jeta yirajapise Dupade Uruode to iji darujna aone! Mu a yacate uaque garósirique jne.
english: **A****yoré****people****all over the world** *Yicatecã́ri ome ayoréode iji erámi jnese Ipeyai*

ayoreo:
Yugúsi Tobité ga
yirasique uje yicatecã́ri Dupade ome uaque iji ne Rincón. Yicatecã́ri ome
ayoréode iji erámi jnese. Jé u Zapocógosóde ore.
english: **Dupade****’s Word****:** As if I really knew all about*Dupade*’s written*Word*, so, I’ll just teach you a little bit.

ayoreo: A jé arajáyo to ujetiga uacacadisóningo ore chacate uágome iji deréjnane. Jeoga uñeque chacate uágome iji deréjnane uñeque. Jé uacamóño to uje anire cojñoque jmatai cuse. Chi cojñoque jmatai isocai u ayoréode eduguéjnarique ujetiga chetaque ayoré noñátique. Ga jé isocai u Dupade uruode enga jé upúsu.
english: When I’m in Tobité I like to pray for you here in Rincón. I pray for the Ayoré all over the world. And the people in Zapocó.

ayoreo:
Jé uacamóño to
uje don Len ore chicai uté que, Davidé ore, ga yicatecã́ri Dupade ome ore iji deréjnane
jnese. Domíngo ujai chojmára iji ti uje yatecã́ri ome ore quigade. Mu don Len
ore diapo rĩ ga Davidé chingo yu Dupade Uruode iji. Uje Pajei ore chi ore
chetaque Dupade uruode, mu jé gajnui u. Mu quigade ga don Len chi ore chacate
ore. Mu ore chojninga: “Que cuchárique cuse gotique.” Ore cho Dupade iji
iyequedie ore. Ore gaidi u uje ore chijnóra iyequedie ore.
english: **god****they wanted to worship was women:** You probably know about these persons if your teachers have taught you about them. Or if someone else has taught about them in the past. You see there was a poor man, but the same thing happened to him that happened to the deformed people that were hated by our leaders. That’s what*Dupade*’s*Word*tells about, and it’s true that this does happen.

ayoreo:
Mu quigade ga
uté Ajei chi chacate darásade ore ome Dupade Uruode ujade. Que chirajapise
Dupade Uruode mu chacate ore garósi, garósi, garósi. Ga jé isocai u.
english: You know that don Len and Ecarai went to visit others and I prayed for them after they left for six Sundays. And then, they came back and Davidé began telling me about*Dupade*’s*Word*. But Pajei and the others wouldn’t listen to*Dupade*’s*Words,*and that’s how it was with that rich man in the Bible. But afterwards don Len and Ecarai tried to teach Pajei and them. But they told Len and Ecarai: “There’s nothing to it.” They told them the only*Dupade*they wanted to worship was women. They just wanted to go after them and love women.

ayoreo: A jé aducho to uje uacacadisóningo chacate uágome uje cojñoque jmatai uje chi Dupade chacate uté, mu cojñoque atai ugucharãi uje iparátadie jiei. Isoca jé u yu iji deréjnane. Je yugúcharique cuse gotique. Ga jé gajnui u.
english: But later Ajei, they said, taught his people about*Dupade*’s*Word.*He didn’t know much about*Dupade*’s*Word,*but he taught them the little that he knew. And that’s all the teaching they had.

ayoreo:
Mu quigade ga
udojo ucode Pajei ore, chi ore cho etinguerẽ ome uté Davidé. Chojninga: “Que
cuchárique Dupade uruode gotique. Gusu iyequedie uje yijnóra ore.” Mu que ore
chiraja Dupade uruode gotique gu.
english: You have heard the story because your teachers have no doubt taught you about the poor man that*Dupade*taught about his*Word*and the rich man who had a lot of money. I am like the poor man. I don’t have anything. I’m like that poor man.

ayoreo: Chi jno uté, cojñoque jmatai. Tibágui eduguéjnai enga utaite iji quedéjnai. Chi ajéode u ujeta tagu potígo irérigui. Mu datéringa uje chetaque. Que áyipierigui déji Dupade uruode gotique gu.
english: Just as the rich man didn’t want to listen to*Dupade*’s*Word*, neither did Pajei and them. They tried to discourage Davidé and they said: “There’s nothing to*Dupade*’s*Word.*Now, women, that’s who I worship.”

ayoreo: Chi toi mu chise jnani bajade uñai,*queyayode*uñai. I tu Abram nanique. Chi chise iji pijogadi uté uje Dupade anguretasórone jogadi u.
english: But Pajei and the others just didn’t understand*Dupade*’s*Word*, and that’s why they acted like that.

ayoreo: Mu uté jnani atai uqué chi Dupade jnusipise uté jõ. Chi chojninga, uqué a: “Yujnusipise uje je arec adode que múñi.” Chi ajéode u gu jeta chisi ore iji dagúchade, mu chãrángome jmatai tuté. Chi chojninga: “Que yipota*yijico*uté yúrique gotique.” Uté aníri, jnani atai.”
english: And then the poor man came to that rich leader but stayed at a distance to make his request. He wanted to eat the food that the rich man had left over. But worse for him, because the rich man continued to refuse him. He never thought about what*Dupade*’s*Word*says about helping the poor.

ayoreo:
A jé arajáyo to
uje uacacadisórone iji ne ore irajatígo, mu quigade ga chi jnusi. Chicai
Abraham, mu chi jnopo rĩ ga chicáji gajño pioi.
english: But later the poor man died, and he met up with one of the first men, one of our ancestors whose name was Abram. He had reached the place where*Dupade’s*believers go.

ayoreo: Jé isocai upo rĩ uje chi don Len uje chise cojñotique ica iji dabié máquina ajei uje chi chetaque cuchade jnese. Chi ca don Len chiraja gotique gu. Chi chojninga: “Yaca jóroga aja udore Dupade anguresórone uacanírenie iglésiadie. Yacája udore arĩe. Yico ore anírenie mu que chajneráme yu dínique gotique go.”
english: But that rich man,*Dupade*would miss him for he didn’t go where his believers went.*Dupade*said: “I will miss him even though his ways were not good.”*Dupade*wanted him to help poor people and give his things to them, put he held them back from the poor man. So,*Dupade*said, “I don’t want to look after that rich man any longer.”

ayoreo: Ga gajnui jé u. Mu chi quigade ga uqué jno, jnani atai. Chi jnopo rĩ ga chicai uté.
english: And you know that your teachers with their knowledge have told you how later the rich man was sorry. He went to Abraham but had to go back to the*lake of fire.* That’s how it was with an encounter that don Len had with a man on the train. This man hated everything. Len didn’t know why this was so. The man said to him: “In vain I went to one of the churches where you believers in*Dupade*go. I went in, and we were with them, but it didn’t help me at all.”

ayoreo:
Yiraja íode mu
garósi jne yacajnaquéode ga que yatatapise gotique. Uje yiraja udojo. Jé gusu.
english: **went again to****Abraham, but he refused to do what the rich man asked him to do:**

ayoreo: Ipeyai – Rincón de Tigre, Bolivia – 1960Transcrito por Marina de Morarie.
english: This is what it was like with the rich man. He went to see Abraham twice but did not get what he went after. I know the names of some of the characters in this story but won’t mention them right now. This is what I understand. This is all. Dupade – God Dupade’s Word – God’s Word Cojñoi – Person not of the Ayoré tribe.

ayoreo:
**A jé adúcho to yatatagode uje Jnaníne a e chatata:**Yirasique uje uacatijáyome*Dupade Uruode*iji dé. A yise idai uñane uje que ore doi*Dupade Uruode*to iji ta guidai, enga a yingo uacajnomei ome ore dirome.

**Balaam, gachidi burro ore:***(Numeros 22:21-34)*Mu yingo uaque garósi uje chi anire, Balaam gachidé burro tocade ome dachisṍri nanique. Gachisṍri ájningarángome dachidi nanique. Mu gachidi chimo*Dupade*irótique gate unirísõre iji daquei enga chicai quedéjnai ome. Mu gachisṍri que chimo cuchárique uje áyipie déji dagaidode cucha. A jé uacadúcho to udojo*Dupade Uruode*garósiode uje ore chacate uagome gu.

**Yacajnaquéode u udojo to ome yurásarigo iji Tobité:**Yingome ore garósiode to ica,*Dupade Uruode*iji ta, mu que ore changureta yu to gotique. Ore chotigase. Ga jnanione uñane chojninga: “Chamo jóroque gotique uje ore chácari Tobité iji yiquei uté. Que cuchárique tṍraji yiquéode,” ore chojninga.

Mu yojningame ore: “Anguretáyo*Dupade*ga yanguretágo jnese. Uacamóño uje chi*Dupade*diapo jne rĩ,” yojninga.

Yingome ore aníri, uje chi “*Dupade*pésu jnumi iji taningai nanique
enga jé arajáyo to iji uacacadisórone ore pijnanone,” yojninga.

Mu chi yotigayasórone garipise.
Yotigayasórone jõra Ayoi. Ga Ichorai.

Yojninga: “Yipesupu ga ca uacuñeque
chejnárengapo iji ejo rĩ,” yojninga. Yapúsaja yuruode ome ore. Mu quigade ga
Betidé dosirase Ayoi. A jé arajáyo to.

Mu yicatecãrápoi*Dupade*iji ore gari rĩ. Yojninga: “Átaja uté gajnerĩ ujeta bajéo ga ecapo gajnerĩ, uje chotigasipise baruode.”

Mu eca. Mu quigade diríca ga yingo garósi*Dupade Uruode*aja ore mu chojninga: “A jec ũré*Dupade*cuse jõ. Uje cuchárique chujerase yu,” chojninga, “Ga yitecurásu.” Tecu iji jnumone.

**Jnani ditécai, Juan, Pedro ore:***(Hechos 3:1 aja 8)*Ga isocai u Juan, Pedro ore uje chi ditécai doi ore uruode nanique enga dique. A jé arajáyo to.

Juan, Pedro chi ore jno. Ore doi*Dupade Uruode*iji iglésia. A jé
arajáyo to. Mu chi ore chise jnani ditécai. Jé uacamóño to jnani ditécai. Chi
jé tecurasu. Chi ore chise enga chojninga: “¿Acayóji rique a?” Enga chi chutai
ore. Chi chojninga: “Asíome uacaparátarigui ome yu jne.”

Mu Pedro chojninga: “Cho jeta yiparátaraque gotique jõ.”

“Mu que uacaparátaraque cuse ga asíome yu uacabotígo ome yu,” chojninga.

Udore uje yingo uaque garósi, garósi. Jé umári yacajnaquéode ica mu a garósi jé yacajnaquéode iji de ome uaque. Yicatecã́ri*Dupade*ga, ome uqué uje tecurasu, Betidé, uje uacarajáyo to, Ayoi.

Mu Josédé e areque to iji ujade. E doi*Dupade Uruode*to. E irasique re iji ujade to rĩ.

Chi ore chojninga ucode, Juan, Pedro ore: “Yisi ome cucha ueradéjnai ome ua jne.” Jé uacamóño to uje jnani ditécai isocai u *Matéo iji guidai. Mu jé tupo rĩ. Chi ore chojninga: “Yisígome yocugúcha ueradéjnai ome ua gajnerĩ. Ga cucha ueradéjnai uté u*Jesucrísto i.*”

Enga ore ojningai u Jesus ujopié
uje tẽrája ore ujetiga ore chiqueta dojode ayoréo. Ga ore chisa jmanecai ga
chague uqué. A jé adúcho to jeta ore chacate uagome.

Garósi yacajnaquérigo mu yacaranguipísome*Dupade*uje a chátaja yoque gajnerĩ. Udore uje ingome uaque garósiode.

**Yingo yacajnaquéode udore ome ore ica rĩ iji uta Pailón:**Yingo ore ica rĩ iji uta Pailón mu Dugúidé chojninga: “A jecucha dadi jorónga ome ayoréode yocuñeque – chirajapise uruode to jóroque ja,” chojninga.

Mu uacajnuéño*queyibáyode*iji de Rincón. Que yicoi*Dupade Uruode*to go iji Tobité. Yicoi to, yacaco ore, mu gadiógosi ore iji ajei. Jé uaque ga jiei uaque. Gagájnisi ore, cinco su ore iji ujade. Jecucha ga yingome ore mu que ore doi to go. Ore chotigase.

Mu yimeseranie gare iji chequedie
ore iji guidai. Yimesera uaté anire, jé uacarajáyo to uje ore chojningame
nanique Timingue. Yimeserapise to ga yimesera Eruidaté to rĩ. Yimesera uaté to
– Dapuré uje dopísi to*Dupade Uruode*to. Catecã́ri ome uáguiji de gatuánguiji deréjnane to iji durasedie, dejade
ore.

**E gusu yacajnaquéode ome uaque:**Garósi. Jiei yacajnaquéode to iji ujade mu e gusu yacajnaquéode. Cachi iyajmaque yu iji gu.

Mu jecucha ajnusínaño*Jesucrísto i*, uje ueradéjnapise gu. Pujopié déji i to, ga jé cho Pablo urusarane.*(Filipenses 2:9-11)*

**Mateo u Ecarai,
Tomása ore abi uje pero iji dabatigai nanique enga que dique to gotique.*

*Ipeyai – Rincón de Tigre, Bolivia – 1970**Marina de Morarie churúsãre Ipeyai acajnaquéode*
english:
**Thrilled with****Dupade****’s****Word****s**

*Uacatijáyome Dupade Uruode iji dé*

**You have probably heard****this teaching****from Jnanín****é****.**

I’m happy that here in Rincón you are thrilled with*Dupade*’s*W**ord**s*. When I go to other villages, in places where they are not meeting for*Dupade*’s*Word**s*, I’m going to tell them about you.

**Balaam and his donkey:***(Numbers 22:21-3**4**)*

I’ll tell you a little about a donkey that burst out and talked to his owner. But in the beginning the donkey’s owner doubted his donkey and was angry with him. But the donkey could see*Dupade*’s angel in front of them and went in another direction. But the owner couldn’t see the angel. His mind was on the things he had to do. You’ve probably heard something about this when they’ve taught you.

**I also taught the people in Tobité about****this story****:**

I told them a little bit about Balaam and his donkey, but they wouldn’t believe it. They doubted my words. Some of the men said: “Why in the world would they get up before me here in Tobité and teach such a thing. Nothing would come from such a teaching like this.”

But I told them: “Have faith in*Dupade*and then we will all believe. You know that*Dupade*is going to come back again.”

I told them they should believe*Dupade*’s*Word**s*because in the beginning*Dupade*made the earth, just as they’d heard from the mouths of their other teachers.

There were two who were most vocal in doubting me. One of them was Ayoi. The other was Ichorai.

I said, “May neither of you suffer for your unbelief later on.”

I said other things to them. But afterward they attacked Bétidé whose other name is Ayoi. You probably know all about this.

Then I prayed with them again.  I said, “Help him, if it’s your will, to live, because he really doubts your*W**ord**s.*”

And he did begin to live. Later, I was telling them a little more about*Dupade*’s*Word**s*and he said, “It’s probably true that there is a*Dupade*, because I could have been killed,” he said, “I’m having to crawl.” He couldn’t walk and had to crawl on the ground.

**Peter,****John,****and the lame man****:***(Acts 3:1-8)*

It was like that when a lame man obeyed John and Peter’s command and walked. You probably know that story. They were going to a meeting in the temple, but you probably already knew that. And they came upon a lame man, who, as you know, couldn’t walk at all, and he could barely crawl. And when they reached him, he said, “Where are you going?” And then he started begging. He said, “Give me some of your money.”

But Peter said: “As if I had any money!”

“But if you don’t have money, then just give me something to eat,” he said.

(I’m just telling you parts of the story. But when I taught it before, my teaching was longer, but I’ll shortened my teaching for you that are here.)

Just as Peter prayed for the lame man, I prayed for Bétidé who you may know as Ayoi, the one who had to crawl also.

Bétidé is generally okay now. He goes to hear*Dupade’s Words*. And for the most part he is happy.

It says that John and Peter said to the lame man, “I’m going to give you something very beautiful.”

You probably know all about **Mateo*in our village who was born crippled. It was the same with that lame man – he was just like Mateo. They said to him, “We’re going to give you something beautiful that we do have. And that beautiful thing is the name of Jesus Christ.”

They were referring to the power of Jesus that could heal people. Jesus had given them his power, so they could heal their fellow people with it. And they took hold of his arm and helped him to stand, and you know what happened next because you’ve heard the story before from those who teach*Dupade*’s*Word**s**.*

This teaching of mine is short, but I’m so thankful that*Dupade*helps us right now. He helps those of you to whom I’m teaching this story.

**I****told this story in Pailón****:**

I told this story in Pailón but Dugúidé said, “That’s always the way it is with some of us Ayoreos, they think they really know how to preach, but they speak in vain.”

**You have surpassed us all:**

You believers here in Rincón have surpassed us all. We’re not following the*Word of**Dupade*in Tobité right now. We continue to meet, and we teach, but only three will show up. But here the place is full of people. There, only four or five at the most come at times. I’m always inviting people to come, but no one responds. They’re full of doubts.

But there are two women in our village that come to hear me. One of them is the one who you used to known as Timingue long ago. She’s faithful to come hear me, and so does Eruidaté. Dapuré is the other one who never fails to show up to hear*Dupade’s Words*. She frequently prays for you here in Rincón at the morning and evening meetings.

**This is all my teaching for you****:**

I know it’s a short message. Sometimes I have longer messages, but this is all my teaching for now. I didn’t want to take too long, that’s why.

But always remember that the name of Jesus is very beautiful. And remember that there is power in his name.*(Philippians 2:9-11)*

*Mateo was Ecarai and Tomasa’s son who was born crippled long ago and he never walked at all.

Dupade – God

Dupade’s Words – Bible, God’s Word

*Ipeyai**–**Rincón de Tigre**-1970*

*Transcribed and translated to English by Maxine Morarie.*

ayoreo:
**Ga gare nanique uje Jesus chisi ome arurégode ome cojñonguemúone**:Yijorane uaque á, uacamóño uje jec ũré*Dupade**Uruode*, mu dicoré Satanás choréi yógaja quedéjnane enga que yicopoi*Dupade**Uruode*jnese gotique rĩ. Enga uneroque*Dupade**Uruode*ome yoque uje que yico yocúringárique arégome*Dupade*gu.  Cucha gajnaréngone déji yocari enga deroco*Dupade**Uruode*ome yoque. Mu yoquigosi uje aregue áyipie tenga*Dupade*unéningai quérã ome. Jecute iji ne a yingo*Dupade**Uruode*ome uaque iji San Marcos 8.

**Cojñone chi cuatro mil ore uje ore tangáre Jesus acajnaquéode nanique:**Enga yijorane uaque á, uacarajáyo*Dupade**Uruode*iji uné. Ibirácho ome, piraguipise ome yoque, iji uné*Dupade**Uruode*jogadi. Enga uacarajáyo uje*Dupade*Abi Jesucristo chi chijna cojñonguemui aja yotequedéjnai cuchátique pijnanai nanique uje cojñone chi*cuatro mil*ore.

Enga
uacarajáyo uje*Dupade**Abi Jesucristo*uté acadigode ujno cojñoc
ore díningo chi gadioc ore ome ore uje ore déji te uje ore tangári Jesus
acajnaquéode. Enga chi que ore tagu dabotique gotique. Que ore tagu dabotique
uje ore chejna raja dacadisṍri acajnaquéode.

**Jesus chosõripise cojñonguemui**:Enga quigade enga, iji ujno enga, chi Jesus chojnirame dacadigode ore: “Yosõripise cojñonguemui uje e ore guejnane dire gadioque iji yocari. Enga a e íjnonguipíse ore potígo. Enga que togome yu ujetiga yiroque ore aja ore iguíjnane. Chamãñaje yiraja uje que ore tagu dabotique gu enga uñéngo a ore chorase sere ome dayaguhéode iji dajei.”

Chi idajapise
uñane ore iguíjnane. Jecute que Jesus chiroque ore aja doi uje socaseti enga
chaguhei uásejna iji dajere tome uté gosi enga topiéjna ajirigode iji siñeque,
anínique daguíjnarique yui guesi.

Jesus chiraja
cuchade jnese. Chiraja uje*Dai*chátarásaja gajnerĩ. Enga jec u uje chojnirame, chojnirame dacadigode ore: “¿A
ca arurétique déji uacuñeque a?”

**Déji San Juan uje chi 5.000 ore, cojñonguemúrique uñeque nanique:**Enga uacarajáyo uje disírique déji uté uje qué parátaque uné, a cho Jorge uné, a cho Rubén uñeque. Disi ta chejna raja*Dupade**Abi Jesucrísto*acajnaquéode to, enga que qué jnusina dajuránone anícho gotique yui arurécho. Que jnusina yui. Que jnusina gotique ujetiga tagu uje chejna raja dacadisṍri acajnaquéode. Enga jec u uje arurégode jé qué déji enga a chejna yijmanai arurégode. Enga jec u uje ore chingo dacadisṍri. Ore chingo dacadisṍri tenga ore chojnira: “Disi posode arurécho déji enga chejna yijmanai. Que chimo ujeta oyídode gare.”

**Jesus chibéro disi arurégode aja jnumi ome cojñonguemui uté:**Enga jec u uje  chi ore chisiome arurégode ome Jesus uje chejna yijmanai. Enga Jesus chi chibéro aja jnumi tome ore. Enga chi chacãre*Dai*gai. Tenga, jé u uje Jesus chojnirame dacadigode ore: “Aquíraño cojñone ore enga ore chácari ji jnumi.”

Enga chi ore
chaquĩ́ra cojñone ujetiga ore chácariji jnumi jnese, cojñonguemui. Enga jec u
uje chi Jesus chacãre*Dai*gai tome
arurégode enga*Dupade*chátaja
dajnacari. Enga quigade enga arurégode quérã jnese. Enga ore tagu.

Ipésu yajéode
enga yipecorásu arurétique iji Zapocó enga a ca tiógararása iji ujeta yajo
arureque cíncorigo. Ta qué uneréjnarase yui tome yoque. Unéningane que jnorase
ome yoque. Cinco arurégode mu, cinco mil cojñone, enga ore chiretáguiji dacadie
jnese.

**Jesus acadigode ore chamáta ore iquebéode iji arurégode nanique:**Enga yijorane uaque á, ome cuchade ujnojo iji San Marcos 8 enga chi Jesus acadigode ore chamáta ore iquebéode iji arurégode. ¿Arurégode piedie cho rique? ¿Cho rique, yijorane uaque á? ¿Cho rique piedie arurégode?

Gare iji yirídai.
Jec u. Yijorane uaque á, ome arurégode piedie ujnaji chacate yongome*Dupade**Abi*acadigode uje, uje acadigode gare ore iji yirídai to. Enga
arurégode que chona acadigode ore uje 12 ore, uje a ore cho dajnéngu piedie
enga jé piedie jé chopise ore to uje 12 u.

**Que ore chicadígui Jesus ipesudi ome ore enga ore chucué Jesus quigode jnese**:Yijorane uaque á, uje Jesus e chimate anirone ujno arurégode uje*Dai*chõre arurégode ome ore, enga jé u uje a Jesus acadigode ore tagu to. Enga quigade enga Jesus qué catecã́ri*Dupade*iji siñeque enga cojñone ore jno to. Enga acadigode chi ore chiramísaraja yotequedéjnai tuté enga cojñonguemui que chicadígui Jesus ipesudi ome ore gotique. Ore chucué Jesus quigode jnese. Chi ore chicáji sona ujeta Jesus déji, déji yotequedéjna cuchábi uéchai uñai.

Enga chi
quigade enga Jesus chise yotequedéjna cuchábi uéchai uñai to. Mu cojñone ore
chudute Jesus ujnomei uje déji ta, enga ore chisapo Jesus rĩ. Jesus chojnira:
“Iraja uaque,” chi chojnira. “Uacacueyoi yu gu uje icatecã́ri*Yai*diríca enga chátaja yu enga chõre,
chõre arurégode ome uaque enga uacacho,” chi chojnira ome ore.

**Arureque uecai u Jesuse:**Jesus chojningame ore: “Arureque uecai u yu. Arurei uté uje chisi ome pucuecaringué eyúgojmai ome ore jé u uyu. Ujetiga gosi tibágui yu ome arurei uté enga a ca chaguhénique chejnápo uté gosi rĩ. Ujetiga gosi changureta yu ga a ca gosi cajnorapóji jne rĩ.”

E mu
cojñonguemui chi ore chojnirame Jesus: “Pacadisṍri á, be arurei tuté gotique
enga yajo.”

Jec u uje
Jesus chingopísa ome ore enga chojnira: “Arurei tu yu,” chojnira. “*Dupade*chiroque yu ujeta anguretáyo yu,”
chi chojnira.

Jec u uje iji
deréjnai uje cojñone ore chetaque Jesus uruode iji enga chi ore jno.

Chi cutejma
gáguedaté íjnongaja erámone. Enga chi íjnoque ore iji Jesus gari, aníri,
cojñonguemui, uje ore chetaque Jesus uruode gu uje cho arureque eca iji re
nanique.

**Cojñone ore chetaque Jesus urode uje cho arureque uecai iji re ujetiga ore tagu to**:Yijorane uaque á, iji deréjnai tuné ¿gotique gu a uje cojñone ore chetaque Jesus uruode ome arurei? ¿Gotique gu a uje cojñone ore chetaque Jesus urode uje chojnira  ‘*Dupade*chiroque arurei taja uaque ujetiga acho.’

Mu ore
chetaque uruode. ¿Gotique gu a? ¿Gotique gu a uje cojñone ore chetaque Jesus
uruode ome arurei?

Yijorane
uaque á, cojñone ore chetaque Jesus uruode ome arurei te gu uje ore ajegaidi
tupúsu arurei tiji deréjnai tuné. Ipésu yajéode to enga yocajegaidipise arurei
ome yoque iji deréjnai tuné, yocajegaidi tupúsu arurei iji deréjnai tome yoquibai.
Yocajegaidi tupúsu. Mu ome yajéode, enga que yipótago arureque uecai gotique.

**Yoquica potapísa arurei tenga yicoi yoquica áyipiedie:**Yijorane uaque á, yijoranie uaque á, yoquigaidode u yoquica áyipiedie. Que yicoi arureque uecai ome yocáyipiedie gotique. Yoquica potapísa arurei tenga qué yicoi yoquica áyipiedie. Mu yirajágo uje cucha bisideque yoquica ajegaidi cucha. Mu cucha arei tome yocáyipie, aníri ajegaidi cucha u uje Jesus déji yocáyipie te gu.

Jecute
yijorane uaque á, ome cuchade ujno, e yirajágo uje Jesus acadigode uje cutejma
gaguedaté jnese, ore chetaque uruode ome arurei uje Jesus chingopísa, uje
chojnira: “Arurei tu yu ujetiga acho.*Dupade*chiroque yu aja jnumi ujeta anguretáyo yu,” chi chojnira.

Mu cojñone
jnese ore jno. E ore jno jnese. Ore jno.

**Cojñone ore jno jnese, mu acadigode ore chácari Jesus gari jnese:**Enga yijorane uaque á, ome cuchade ujno, jnacarode ucode uje gare ore iji yirídai te que, enga ore déji Jesus gari jnese. Enga Jesus catecã́ri ore enga chojnira: “¿Jé oyo rique jne a ujetiga apótayo doi to a?”

Mu uñai
chajnai enga chojnira: “Yocangaráni á, que togome yoque ujetiga yico to. Uje
que pacadisṍri uñeque déji ujetiga uruode cho baruode. Ua u gu uje baruode
chisi ome pucuecaringué eyúgojmai ome yoque,” chojnirame Jesus. “Bacadigode
uyoque e yanguretágo baruode enga yirajágo uje yoquijnáti Crísto u ua. Enga*Dupa uecai Abi*upo ua rĩ.”

**Júdas chetaque arureque uecai to**:Enga Jesus, Jesus chajnai ore enga chojnira: “Yajegaidode jec u jnaníringo jeta gare iji yirídai. E mu uñai déji uacari uje dicoré cáma chĩra to.”

¿Gosi te,
yijoranie uaque a? Júdas u ojnongai enga uacarajáyo Júdas déji to iji ne. Jesus
acadique to.  Mu, yijorane uaque á, ome
cuchade ujno, que yipota jeta yoco Júdas. 
Jesus e chingo cuchade ome iji deréjnai tuné. Mu Júdas áyipie uajadé
ome. Mu Júdas e jno. Chacate cojñone ome Jesus adode to,*Dupade**Uruode*to, mu que
cha dajé gajnarétigai gotique, Júdas. Que cha gotique.

Quigade enga
uje cucha gajnaréngone suepise Júdas gai, chisiome Jesus ome sijnángone ore
jnese. Júdas ipotigádi u arurégode ome daca, enga cho cucha bisidéguiji Jesus
guerani pucuecaringué eyúgojma.

**Yoquica pota arurégode iji dirode jnese, jecuté yicoi yoquica áyipiedie udire**:Jecute, yijorane uaque á, ome cuchade ujno, e uacarajáyopise jnese. Imóngo uje Jesus acadigode ore, cojñonguemui, anire, ore ajegaidi upúsu arurégode ome daca. Que ore ajegaite arureque uecai gotique ome dáyipie.

Enga,
yijorane uaque á, yijoranie uaque á, ¿jec uacajegaitígo cucha ujno to a? Ujeta
arureque, aníri tu, arureque tome yoquica, ¿uacajegaitígo a iji deréjnai tuné?

Yijorane
uaque á, ome cuchade ujno, a yicopísi yoquica ajegaidode cucha enga a que yicoi
Jesus to jẽ. Enga jé yicoi, yicoi yoquica áyipiedie te gu. Yoquica pota
arurégode iji dirode jnese enga chojnira: “Asíome yu. Ipota arurétique.” Enga
icoi, icoi. Que yicoi yocáyipiedie ajegaidode cucha gotique.

Yocáyipiedie
enga ajegaidipíso Jesus adode ujetiga yipéco te gu arureque uecai áyipiedie,
ujeta yanguretágo Jesus. Uje Jesus jé déji yocáyipiedie.

Que yipota
jeta yoco Júdas. Uje Júdas e chiraja dasijnaringuéone uje ũré*Dupade**Uruode*. Yijorane uaque á, ¿jec uacajéningo cho rique?

**Jorgedé chãcara dacajnaquéode aja daguidedie uje pacatai**:Ujeta ibaque enga yijocharása ajaramétique gai enga yiguidedie pacatárase. Yiguidedie pacatárase enga icháji yiguidedie ji ti enga que yingo Jórgedaté gotique. Que yojnira: “Jórgedaté á, yiguidedie déji ujnaji. Pacatája. Jabón déji ne enga aru jẽ.”

¿Uacajéningo
cho rique jeta Jórgedaté, ja chijocasirase? ¿Ja chijocasirase? Úngare.

Yijorane
uaque á, yijoranie uaque á. Jórgedaté que chijocase yiguidedie gotique, jé quigade
enga dijidája iji ti. Quigade enga dire uñai enga jec ũré e yõra yiguidedie
uje, uje e dijite jnese.

**Júdas chiraja cucha gajnaréngone ome dáyipie te mu, que chingome Dupade**:Enga, yijorane uaque á, ome cuchade ujno, e imóngo ome Júdas uté, uje Jesus chingome uje chojnira: “Uñai déji uaque uje cáma dicoré cama chĩra.”

Enga Júdas
chiraja cucha gajnaréngone ome dáyipie te mu, que chingome*Dupade*. Enga*Dupade*que
churu gotique. Jé cho yu enga que yingorása yiguidé ome Jórgedaté. Que yojnira:
“Yiguidedie déji ujnaji enga aru jẽ.” Enga que churu gotique. Mu ujeta yingo
jabóninguiji ajirigode enga ojnirarása: “Jórgedaté á, yiguidé déji ne enga aru
jẽ.” Jórgedaté churu enga ichai.

**Uacarajáyopise arureque uecai to:**Enga cho ome, jé ocopise ome Júdas ome cuchade ujno. Jecute yijorane uaque á, ome cuchade ujno, uacarajáyopise arureque uecai to. Que beyoi uacaca, aníri, uacaca ajegaidode cucha arurei tome. Ujeta iquérango iji yoquica ajegaidode cucha enga a que yicoi Jesus gotique to jẽ. Que yicoi gotique to jẽ. Chãje*Dupade*chingome yoque gu uje arureque uecai tu Jesus. Jesus chojninga: “Arurei tu yu,” chi chojnira. “Pucuecaringuei tupo yu rĩ. Dajei tu yu,” chojnira Jesus.

Jecute,
yijorane uaque á, ome cuchade ujno, e uacarajáyo cuchade ujno. Uje que cho
jeta, qué gajné jeta*Dupade*anguresórone ore chacate ore ome*Dupade**Uruode*. Enga uacarajáyo. Que yipota
jeta yoco Jesus acadigode ore uje porójna ore, cojñonguemui ore, mu ore
ajegaidi tupúsu daca ajegaidode cucha.

Uñane u uje,
uje chojmára ore iji yirídai, ore potapísa arureque uecai. Enga chirẽta ore.
Enga Jesus, aníri, Jesus acadique ũréone u ore. Enga ore chopise ujopierane
ome aníri, Jesus, uje ujopie déji ore gu.

Jecute e
uacarajáyo ujno ore, aníri, Jesus acadigode ore jnese. Júdas ta chiraja cucha
gajnarétigo iji dáyipie mu que chingome*Dupade*gotique. Enga*Dupade*que pésu
cuchárique ome gotique.

**A é gome ome uaque uje yãcara yacajnaquéode aja ajaramétique:**E gome gajnui tome uaque ome ujeta yijocharase ajaramétique enga yiguidedie pacatárase. Enga que yingome Jórgedaté gotique ujeta yojnira: “Yiguidedie déji ne. Aru jẽ.” Enga que yingome enga Jórgedaté que pésu cuchárique ome gotique, enga quigade enga uje dijite enga jec u úringanie ducaranei. Jec u. Enga ujeta yiraja cuchárique, ujeta Jórgedaté pésu cuchárique enga ojnira: “Aru jẽ,” ojnira. Enga churu enga ichapo rĩ.

**Irajágo cucha gajnaréngone iji yocáyipie jnese**:Enga cho ome yoque, yijorane uaque á, yijoranie uaque á, iji deréjnai tuné. Irajágo cucha gajnaréngone iji yocáyipie jnese. Irajágo cucha gajnaréngone iji yocáyipiedie ujeta yingóngo cuchade ome*Dupade*enga*Dupade*churu yoque enga ichapo yoque rĩ.

Yijorane
uaque á, yijoranie uaque á, ome cuchade ujno, que toque ujetiga yico
yocúringárique areque gotique ome*Dupade*.
Que toguipise gotique. Cucha gajnaréngone basaja yocáyipiedie jnese iji, iji
deréjnane jnese. Mu yijorane uaque á, ome cuchade ujno, acatecã́choi*Dupade*ome uáguiji dirode jnese ujeta*Dupade*churu uaque jẽ iji Ujnacari
iyoi. Chajẽ*Dupade**Ujnacari Jesus*chojne ome dayoi ujetiga
bisidi cuse ome yocate gajnaréngone, yocáyipie gajnarénguenie ore.*(1 Juan 1:7 aja 9)*

**Ujnusina Tetáguedé:**Ujnusina, ujnusina Tetáguedé ucasode uje, uje ojnirame Tetáguedé: “Tetáguedé á, angureta*Dupade*to,” ojnira.

Mu Tetáguedé
jĩraque chajnai yuruode enga chojnira: “Yiraba yá,” chojnira, “jec areque uje
que yangureta*Dupade*to enga o
parágui usodi,” chojnira. “Uje*Dupade*anguresórone ore, ore pojna dojode ayoréo aja quedéjnane,” chi chojnira. “E
gusu yuruode.”

Chãje aguhétago cuchade aja*Dupade*anguresórone ore gai. Que yajico
yocáyipie gotique tome cucha gajnaréngone gu. Aguhétago cucha gajnaréngone,
anírone,*Dupade*anguresórone ore gai
te gu.*(San Juan 13:34 aja 35)*

Jecute imóngo
iji uné uje, iji deréjnai tuné, uje cojñone ore chetaguipise Jesus uruode ome
re, uje chojnira: “Arurei tu yu.*Dupade*chiroque arurei tome uaque ujetiga acho. Mu uaquetácho.” Jecute ore jnopise.

**Pumajo yoc ome dicoré, uje pota gu ujetiga yiyágo Dupade**:Jecute, yijorane uaque á, ome cuchade ujno, e uacarajáyo. Acueyopísi arureque uecai to. Uásejna yóguiji erámone uje ipótago yocucuecaringueque arei. Mu que yucuegoi cucha gajnaréngone iji yocáyipie gotique ujetiga yatatago ome*Dupade*,  enga que toguípise yocúringárique areque gotique jẽ, uje cucha gajnaréngone jogate yocáyipie.

**Dajere chiberopise yoque nanique:**Jé cho uqué case uje ijoi jnumi diríca uje icagóji Zapoco yui diríca enga dajere chiberopise yoque. Cho ome, cho ome yocanguretigai iji deréjnai tuné, uje gajnareque yocanguretigai tiji, iji deréjnai tuné. Jmátaque yoque ome cuchade. Jmátaque yoque ome yoquiplátadie. Jmátaque yoque ome yoquiguidedie. Pumajo yoc ome dicoré, uje pota gu ujetiga yiyágo*Dupade*.

Jec ũrepise
Pablo uruode ome cuchade ujno uje chatata dajnéone pujuyaquéo. Mu que gusu
Pablo, mu Jesuse chejnáreque re to. Jesus dóji jnumone diríca enga íjnoque, ome
anírone, dariguitigade ome cojñone uyoque ujetiga chacate yogome*Dai*, anírone, iroquéode ujetiga yipéco.
Enga íjnoque, a ca iguíjnarique cusapo rĩ. A chaguhéode chejnapo rĩ.

Enga jé cho ome*Dupade*anguresórone uáguiji deréjnai
tuné, ejnángo cuchade aja yoque. Cojñone ugucharãque, mu que iyieque uguchade.
Mu que ore chiraja*Dupade*enga*Dupade*guerãte ugúchade ome ore. Que ore
chosõre dojórique ayoré ome anírone daguchade. Ore nína raque. Jeque*Dupade*anguresórone jmatac ore.*Dupade*anguresórone ore íjnocapo ore
rĩ. Que ore tirógaja anírone, cucha urõsoringuéone ome dabai.*(1 Pedro 4:12 aja 13)*

Jecute,
yijorane uaque á, ome cuchade ujno, e uacarajáyo jnese ome cuchade ujno uje que
cho jeta qué gajné ujeta*Dupade*anguresórone ore chacate yóngome*Dupade**Uruode*gu. Mu ujetiga apota ararãcho
aja Jesus ipesudode gai uje chisi ome arurégode ome cojnonguemúone, arureque
uecai ore, enga obiréjna jogadi iji*Dupade**Uruode*uje déji San Marcos 8, aja 9,
aja 10 ujno, jé iji San Juan 6 to rĩ.

*Jorgedé – Zapoco, Bolivia – 13 de octubre de 1974**Janet Briggs churúsãre Jorgedé acajnaquéode udojo nanique*
english:
**Dupade****’s****Word is true**

*Jec ũré Dupade Uruode*

**Twice Jesus gave bread to great crowds of people long ago:**

My friends, you know that*Dupade’**s Word*is true, but the*walking spirit,*Satan, scatters us so that not all of us are coming together to hear it. And we find his*W**ord*uninteresting because we are out of fellowship with*Dupade.*And when we are involved in sin we find hisWord*deroco**(uninteresting).*But when one of us thinks the right thoughts,*Dupade*becomes increasingly more important to him. Therefore, I want to tell you about what*Dupade’s Word*says in Mark 8 about feeding a great group of people.

**The ‘cojñone’ were 4,000 who listened to Jesus’ teaching:**

My friends, probably you are already acquainted with this passage in*Dupade*’s*Word*. It’s a hard one. You know that*Dupade*’s*Son*Jesus Christ took the crowd of 4,000 to the shores of a large lake long ago.

And you know that*Dupade*’s*Son*Jesus Christ, his disciples, and the people had been together three days to hear the teachings of Jesus. But they had nothing to eat at all. They were so taken up with what their teacher taught them that they didn’t take time out to eat.

**Jesus felt sorry for the great crowd of people:**

And afterwards Jesus said to his disciples: “I feel sorry for these people; they have already been with me three days and have had nothing to eat. If I send them home hungry, they will collapse on the way.”

Some of their houses were very far away. And that’s why Jesus didn’t dismiss the crowd in case someone would be overcome with hunger and get dizzy and faint before he reached his house.

Jesus knew all things. He knew that his Father would help them. But he wanted his disciples to know it, too, and that’s when he asked his disciples, “How many loaves of bread do you have?”

**John tells about another large group of people****;****there were 5,000 in this group:**

In the book of John, it tells how it happened with the crowd of 5,000. And you know on that occasion there was a little boy with food who was not much older than*Jorge*or*Ruben*. This little boy had been so caught up in what*Dupade*’s*Son*Jesus Christ was teaching that he must have forgotten all about his lunch – the bread he’d brought along. He didn’t think about it. He was so busy listening to his teacher that he forgot all about his food. But he had five breads. He gave them to the disciples and they showed them to their teacher Jesus and said: “This little boy has five breads in his lunch and says there are two fish as well.”

**Jesus broke the little boy’s bread****into pieces****for the crowd to eat:**

And then it was that they gave the five breads to Jesus. And Jesus, it’s said, started breaking them in pieces. And then it was that he told his disciples to tell the people to sit down.

They told the people, and the whole crowd sat down. And then it was that Jesus thanked his*Father*for the bread and*Dupade*helped his*Son*. And afterwards the bread began to increase, and they ate it.

When I think about what it would be like to make some bread for all of us here in Zapocó, I don’t think five breads would be enough. It would really be delicious and we’d all want some of it. There were five breads, 5,000 people, and they ate until their bellies were full.

**The disciples gather****ed****up the bread that was left over:**

My friends, where it tells about these things in Mark 8 it says that Jesus’ disciples gathered up the bread that was left over. How many containers full of left overs were there? How many, my friends? ¿How many containers of bread?

There were 12. That’s how many. My friends, how many disciples did*Dupade*’s*Son*Jesus have? There were 12 disciples and 12 containers full of bread. After the crowd had eaten, the disciples had plenty to eat themselves – a container for each of them!

**The people did not forget what Jesus had done:**

My friends, when Jesus had finished feeding the crowd the bread that his*Father*had increased, probably he and his disciples ate. Afterwards Jesus went off alone to pray and the people went away. And the disciples started off across the lake in their boat. But the people who had been there didn’t forget what Jesus had done at all. They kept track of everywhere Jesus went, so they could go wherever he was. Jesus was still on the other side of the lake.

But after awhile he also went over to the other side of the lake. And when the people heard the news that Jesus was there, and they went to him again. Jesus said to them, “I know you. You are looking for me because I prayed to my*Father*and asked him to help me to increase the bread to feed you.” This is what he told them.

**Jesus is the living bread:**

And then Jesus said, “I am the*bread of life.*That is the bread that gives people everlasting life and I am the one who provides that bread. He who comes to me to get that bread will never go hungry again. And if that person believes in me he will never be thirsty again.”

But the crowd of people said to Jesus, “Teacher, bring that bread, whatever it is, and we’ll eat it.”

Then Jesus told them plainly, “I am that bread.” And then he said, “*Dupade*sent me to this earth so that you might believe in me.” This is how he spoke to them.

It’s the same way today, though, when people don’t like what Jesus says, they go away.

The whole group of them took off in different directions. No one stayed with Jesus, none of the crowd, because they hated Jesus’ words when he told them he was the living bread.

**The****people****hated Jesus****’s words when he****told them to eat****the living****bread:**

My friends who are here today, why do you think the people hated Jesus’ words about the*bread**?*Why did they hate it when he said, ‘*Dupade*sent*bread*for you to eat’?

They hated his words. Why was that? What was it that made the people hate what Jesus had to say about*bread*?

My friends, the people hated Jesus words about bread, because they were looking for a different kind of bread; the bread they ate daily. I have decided that we, too, are most of the time just seeking our daily*bread*as well, bread for our stomachs. That’s all we desire. I don’t think we are all that interested in*living bread*either.

**O****ur stomachs want bread and we do what our stomachs want:**

Men and women, my friends, what we go after is what our bellies want. We are not interested in getting*li**ving bread*for our souls at all. Whenever our belly tells us it wants bread, we go get it. But we know that what our stomachs want is a very ordinary thing. But what is good for our souls, what our souls crave, is for Jesus to dwell in our hearts.

Therefore, my friends, concerning all this, we know that the crowd of people that called themselves Jesus’ disciples didn’t like the words about*bread*that Jesus told them all about. He said: ‘I am the bread you should eat.*Dupade*sent me to this earth so that you might believe in me.” This is what he told them.

But all the people left him. They all took off. They were gone.

**The multitude took off, but****all****the disciples stayed with Jesus:**

And, my friends, about all this, the twelve young men remained there with Jesus. And Jesus asked them: “What do you say? Do you want to leave too?”

But one of them answered him and said: “Lord, no way can we leave. There is no teacher anywhere that has words like yours. For your words give us eternal life.” This is what they said to Jesus. “We who are your disciples know that you are the Christ we’ve been waiting for. You are the*Son**of**Dupade*who is living in heaven.”

**Judas****hated the living bread also:**

And Jesus answered them and said: “I have chosen you twelve men? And yet there’s one of you that*the walking spirit*has not let go of?”

Who is that one? Judas was who he spoke about, and you know there are other Judas’s right now.

He was a disciple of Jesus. But you, friends of mine, concerning all this, I don’t want us to be like Judas. Jesus spoke to him on that day, but Judas heart was resistant against him. And Judas took off. He had taught people about Jesus, he taught them about*Dupade’s Word*also, but he hadn’t stopped his ugly ways at all.

It was later that the ugliness overcame him, and he gave Jesus up to the evil men who were seeking to kill him. Judas wanted bread for his belly and was disinterested in what Jesus could give him which was eternal life.

**Our bellies wan****t****the kind of bread we eat each day:**

Therefore, my friends, all of you, about this, you all know what happened. We see that the crowd Jesus taught who called themselves his believers were only looking for bread for their bellies. They weren’t seeking*living bread*for their souls at all.

And my friends, what it is you are seeking. Is it bread for your belly? Is that what you want most today?

My friends, about this, if we are only going after the desires of our bellies, then we are not following Jesus. And so, we go after and go after the will of our bellies. Our belly is demanding, and it wants bread every day and it says, “Get me some. I want some bread.” And we go get it, and we go get it, and we go get it. We don’t go after the things our souls desire at all.

Our souls really want us to go after Jesus, to do his will because it is the will of Jesus who we have faith in, the*living bread*. That’s because Jesus is in our hearts.

I don’t want us to be like Judas. That’s because Judas was quite aware of his sin according to*Dupade**’s**true**Word*. My friends, what are you seeking?

**J****ó****rgedé like****ne****d his teaching to****his****dirty cloth****es****:**

Let’s say I went out to hunt honey and came across an armadillo, and in the process of digging it out, my clothes got all muddy. Do you think I would just put them aside and not tell*J**ó**rgedaté*anything about them? Wouldn’t I say, “*J**ó**rgedaté*, my clothes are over there. They are all muddy. Here’s some soap for you to wash them.”

What do you think? If I hadn’t pointed them out to her, would she have picked them up and washed them? No, I don’t think so. She probably wouldn’t have seen them.

My friends, men, and women, if*J**ó**rgedaté*didn’t pick up my clothes, afterwards they would get all smelly and full of mildew right there where I left them. Afterwards, on another day, we’d surely have to throw my clothes away because they’d be ruined.

**Judas knew the sin in his****heart,****but he didn’t tell****Dupade****about it:**

And, friends, we can see that this is what happened to Judas when Jesus said plainly, “There’s one of you that the*walking spirit*hasn’t let go of.”

Yes, Judas knew that what he was planning in his heart was evil, but he didn’t tell*Dupade*anything about it. Just like me – if I hadn’t told*J**ó**r**g**edaté*about my clothes; if I hadn’t said, ‘My clothes are over there, wash them,’ she would not have washed them at all. But if I had shown her the soap and said, ‘*J**ó**rgedaté*, there’s my clothes, wash them,” then she would have washed them clean.

**You know very well about the living bread:**

That’s how it is, we’re just like Judas. So, friends of mine, to sum it up, you know all about the*living bread.*So, don’t let your belly tell you what to do – demanding you to bring it bread. If we keep on serving our belly, then its demands will grow and grow, then after a while we won’t go after Jesus’ ways. We won’t have anything more to do with him. For*Dupade*tells us that Jesus is*the**real**bread*that gives us life. Jesus said, “I am the bread that gives life. I am the road that leads to heaven.”

Therefore, friends of mine, about this, you are informed. It’s not like you’re just now hearing about it for the first time.*Dupade*’s believers have been teaching*Dupade*’s*Word*all along, and you know it. I don’t want us to be like the crowd of people that Jesus taught who said they were believers but were preoccupied with getting bread for their bellies.

Some of them, though, the eleven, wanted the*living bread*. And they got it and it filled them up. And they were Jesus’ true disciples. And because they were full of Jesus, they were powerful like Jesus is powerful.

And you know all about Jesus’ disciples. Judas, however, had evil in his heart that he didn’t confess to*Dupade*, so*Dupade*didn’t do anything about it.

**That’s probably enough of my talking about armadillos:**

I’ve given enough examples to you about digging out armadillos and muddy clothing and about not telling*J**ó**rgedaté*to wash them. And not telling her: “Here’s my clothes. Wash them.” And because I didn’t tell*J**ó**rgedaté*about them, she didn’t do anything about them, and afterwards my clothing was ruined, and we threw them out. Just like that. And if I were wise I would have told*J**ó**rgedaté*about them and would have said, “Wash them,” then she would have, and they’d have been all clean and useful again.

**We are all aware of the sin in our hearts:**

And it’s like that with us, friends, men and women, these days. We all know when we have sin in our hearts. And we know that if we tell*Dupade*about our sins, he does something about them, he forgives them and washes us clean.

Men and women, friends, concerning these things, we can’t make ourselves right with*Dupade*on our own. That’s impossible. Sin comes into our minds every day. But, my friends, about this, pray to*Dupade*every day, and ask him to wash you clean in*His Son’s*blood. For*Dupade*’s Son Jesus spilled his blood so that there would be a remedy for our sin and evil thoughts.*(**I John 1:7**–**9**)*

**I remember Tetáguedé:**

Oh, how well I remember Tetáguedé when he was alive, and I would say to him: “Tetáguedé, believe in*Dupade*,” I’d say.

But Tetáguedé would quickly respond and say: “Uncle,” he’d say, “It’s fine for me not to believe in*Dupade*, because you’re mistaken about him,” he’d say. “For the believers are always bawling each other out,” he’d say. “That’s all I want to say on the subject,” he’d say.

For we believers do blame each other and accuse each other. That’s because we don’t examine our own hearts to see the sin in them. So, we accuse other believers of wrong doing.*(John 13:35)*

And so, we can see it’s the same today as with the great crowd of people. People don’t want to accept for themselves the words of Jesus who said: ‘I am*the**bread*that*Dupade*sent to you to eat. But you won’t eat it.’ That’s when the crowd of people left him.

**The ‘walking spirit’****is our enemy, he wants us to forsake****Dupade****:**

Therefore, my friends, about these things, you have already heard them. So, diligently look for that*living bread*. There’s lots of us here on earth looking for a good life. But we are not looking for the sin in our own hearts, so we can confess it to*Dupade.*And because of the sin in our hearts, we cannot have a good life.

**The road we****travel on****is long and hard for us:**

It’s like what happened to me when we were on the road to Zapoco, we were worn out from the journey. It’s that way with our faith these days, our faith grows weak. We’re poor and in need. We lack money. We lack clothing. The*walking**spirit*troubles us to make us give up on*Dupade*.

It’s true what Paul wrote about himself and the things he went through. But not only Paul, Jesus suffered also. While on earth Jesus got tired traveling to different places to teach people about his*Father*and the things that we should do. He would be tired, but didn’t have a house to sleep in. He was often hungry but had nothing to eat.

And it’s like that for you who believe in*Dupade*today, we struggle to get things for yourselves. And the people who have a lot of things, never seem to have enough. But they don’t know*Dupade*and so they don’t realize that it is*Dupade*who blessed them with the things they do have. They don’t share what they have with their fellow men. They happily go on their way. But*Dupade*’s believers are often poor. They are often tired. They often have more pain than their bodies can stand.*(**I Peter 4:12-13**)*

Therefore, my friends, about these things, this isn’t the first time you have heard these things, for*Dupade*’s believers have taught*Dupade*’s*Word*to us. But if you want to read about what Jesus did to feed the crowd of people, and about the*living bread,*my words can be found in Mark, chapters 8, 9 and 10, and in John 6 also.

Dupade – God

Dupade’s Word – God’s Word

Walking spirit – Devil

Cojñone – People who are not of the Ayoré tribe

Deroco – Flavorless, uninteresting

Jorge and Ruben – Little boys in Zapoco at the time Jórgedé gave this message

Jórgedé – Father of Jorge

Jórgedaté – Mother of Jorge

Things break us – Things are hard for us

*J**ó**rgedé – Zapoco, Bolivia – 10-13-1974.*

*Transcribed by: Janet Briggs**.*

*Translated to English by: Maxine Morarie.*

ayoreo:
**Cojñone chirajapise Dupade Uruode enga ore chingo yoque:**Dupade Uruode déji darujna aode jeta chacate cojñone, ga cojñone chirajapise Dupade Uruode iji darujna aode, ga ore chirajapise enga ore chingo yoque. Uacaducho to San Pablo acajnaqué casode ome yoque uje chojnira, chatata uje Jesus iroi Pedro chi chojninga, “Yique u yoquijõrai Pablo acajnaquei.”*(2 Peter 3:15-17)*

**Ayoréode uyoc uñane yocáyipie chejna raja yoquipotiguéode cucha uñane.**Chi Ecarai chudute cojñoi uruode diríca uje chojninga: “Yiraja uje Dupade déji gaté. Enga ipesute cuchade jnese mu a yangureta to jeta choquíjna yu. Cáma choquíjna yu gu,” chi chojninga.

Mu jé adi u ayoréode uyoc uñane uje yirajágo uje Dupade déji gaté, mu yirajágo uje Dupade cuse, mu yocáyipie chejna raja cucha uñane. Yoquijnorétigaiomecucha uñane. Ga que yajico Dupade yúrique gotique.

Ga adi u cojñoi uñai uje chiraja to uje ijõre, ga chiraja uje Dupade déji gaté ga chimo yogome dajopié, mu uté chijnóra cucha uñane. Ga jé adi u ayoréode uyoc uñane gajnerĩ uje yocáyipie chejna raja yoquipotiguéodecucha uñane. Ga que yicoi Dupade ajegaidode cucha gotígo. Iroquéode quigode.

**Dupade churúsãre daruode ome Moises nanique.**Jecucha ga yingo uaque uje Dupade churúsãre daruode ome Moises nanique. Churúsãre daruode ome Moises ga churúsãre jeta etégode cucha déji. Churúsãre ome Moises ujetiga yirajágo to Dupade Uruode quigode – ipotigadode cucha, etégode cucha. Dupade gaidi u ujetiga ca yopo Dupade iji cucha uñeque rĩ. Jeta ca yopo Dupade iji ayoré gajnúrique rĩ. Jeta ca*yatecãcoi*ayoré gajnúrique. Dupade chetaque cucha uñane. Jé Dupade chojninga: “Ca barúrigo gajnárecho ome yu. Ca adojnóngaja yu. Ca adojnóngaja yi,” chojninga.

“Anire, angaripísi baté, bayé
ore,” chojninga Dupade ome darusarane ome Moises. Churúsarame cucarãte uejade.

Yudúco udojo mu que yicoi go. “Ca
asa bejóriguiji diri gare iji ti,” Dupade chojninga uje churúsãre ome Moises.
Chojninga: “Ca ajé bojórique ayoré, cojñotique uñeque.” Dupade etégode u udore.
“Angaripísi bayé, baté ore.” Jé u uje a ca yangácoi yocayode, yocatedie ore, ga a jé ca yangácopoi Dupade to
gotique rĩ, uje yocaiu
Dupade gu. Ga a ca yangacorási Dupade adode.”

“Ca apota gapu unejángueraque,
cheque unejángueraque.” Ga gosi pota jeta changureta Dupade, ga ca goto
ajeigaite. Ca potapise dojóraque chequé, dojórique jnani, jnacare acotéraguiji
dejórique. A gosi pota gapu unejángue ore mu e cucha gajnáreque ome ujórique
ayoré. Gajnáreque cuchade.

“Ca arapatárique ua,” chojninga,
“Ca apota bejominónique ayoré ugúcharigo, ca oria cucha unejángone.”

Dupade uruode quigode u unojo mu
yoco cucha bisidéguiji Dupade Uruode uje que yanguretágo to go. Jé basája yocáyipiedie chejna raja cuchade
uñane, Dupade etégode cucha. Yoquipotiguéodeunéño cuchade. Yocajéode gaidode
u cucha udore, ga deroco Dupade uruode ome yoque.

Dupade Uruode jogadi udé gajnerĩ ujetiga yicoi Dupade iroquéode ga*yatecãcoi*Dupade iji deréjnane. (*(Romanos 8:1-4)*Yimóngo ome Pablo urusãrane unojo uje que togome yoque ujetiga yipéco Moises urusarane Dupade áyipiei, mu Jesuscristo ujopié déji yoque uje yanguretágo uté, enga e que ibirachome yoque rĩ ujetiga yicoi Moises urusarane udore.

**Oroné chacãra dacajnaquéode aja jnanírique uje ujnacarode gare:**Uje jnani uje ujnacarode gare ga ujnacare ungaroi chutai dayé ugúchade.  Chutai dayé iplátadie. Chojningame dayé, udé cojñoi: “Yapai, jé go ca asi yu baplátadie uécharique ga yi ga yicai punire quedéjnarique uñeque to.”

Dayé chisi. Dayé chisi daplátadie ome ga jno. Jno ga chicai punire uñai mu chise dagaidi punire uñai mu iyajmaque ga chojninga: “Qué ujuya yoquibosode ica mu yijíñongome jnumone ica. Jé gotique a jé yi ga yajni yapade gai to. Yapade uqué, ucode ore gai to.” Jnacari udé adode u.

Cho yoque, cojñone udore ore uyoque, uje yanguretágo Dupade mu yiyágo. Cho jetiga cojñoi uté dopísi ga dopísi páyipiedie quigode ome Dupade. Uje a yangureta Dupade diríca mu yiya to. Yiji ome yáyipie ga que yajirapo yapa, usodé yúode rĩ. Yijíñome te. Ga e yiji. Ga íjnoque Dupade ueratigai iji yu. Ga adi uyoque que. A adi disiode jne. Yiyágo Dupade. Enga yijíñongome iji te.

Ga gajnui tu jnacari uté uje chijíñone dayé iji te. Ga chise. A gosi áyipieraque, áyipieraque ga chajniapója Dupade Uruode gai rĩ. Chajniapója Dupade uru casodica ga gajnui a u cojñoi uje cho dáyugu re iji erámone ga chajni dayé gai. Ga uyocuñéngo adi u iji gajnerĩ ujetiga yujnusínango Dupade uje ujopierãi, enga yajníngo gu uru casodica gai to iji Dupade. Iyágo yocate gajnaréngone. Iyágo yocate gajnaréngone ga yajníngo Dupade gai enga yanguretágopo to rĩ.

Cojñoi di iji dayé. Udé cojñoi di iji dayé. Dayé chimo iji uta ga quejnáji yui. Quejnáji dajnacari yui. Ga chisa uechai iji re, chisa jare ga tagu uredie. Tagu dajnacari uredie. Mu ujnacari chojninga: ‘Yapa, yadode gajnarécho jõ. Yadode gajnarécho. Sijnaque yu ome ua. Ome Dupade, uhuac. Ca opo bajnacari yu jne rĩ. Ga o baróguiji yu ga…’ Ga dayé irasique. Dayé irasique ga irasique.

Jé adi upúsu yoque que. A yiyárase Dupade Uruode ga yiya Dupade Uruode mu cho jeta cha Dupade, ga dayé chojninga: “Baya yu diríca jõ. Cánguretapo yu to jne rĩ.” Uñeque cha jne Dupade Uruode, mu uñeque jé changuretapo rĩ iji dire uñeque, jé changuretapo rĩ. Adi u ujnacari uje dayé que chetaque go.

Ga jé adi u Dupade uje a yiya Dupade Uruode ica, mu yajniapo Dupade uruode rĩ, ga irasigápo yu rĩ. Adi u uté cojñoi jnani uje irasique dajnacari. Chojningame darógode: ‘Beyoi, aquejnáño ome iguidé ueradérigui ga ajnáchoja. Ajnácho jmanepiéragaja jne rĩ. Ajnácho guidárigo aja rĩ,’ chojningame darógode. ‘Boyo ga acueyoi aquiirájna urojnáraque ga yujégo ga yajo jne. Ipéjo domíngo gajnetique ome yujnacari uje yujnacari iyajmaque uje jno nanique ga cho togode. Cho jetiga yujnacari toque mu, ecapo rĩ ga diapo iji yu rĩ.’

**Cucha gose Dupade, mu chijnóra ayoré gajnaréngone uyoque, enga pota yoque.**Enga irasique dajnacare ungarómi, mu ujnacare urẽja casica chetaque dangaroi. Ga ujnacare urẽjai déji garai. Di, mu tangári dayé edai, mu jé ore doi dadode iji gajñaminone. Ore chujé deguedie, mu dayé iroi chachai, ga chojningame dayé iroi: “¿Jé gotique u cuchade udore iji guidai?”

Mu chojninga: “Cachi bajõrai casica diapo rĩ. Diapo rĩ, ga ore doi ga ore terame.”

Ga gajnui tu ome yoque gajnerĩ ga ore irasique. Mu chetaque dangaroi. Que chiraja cuchárique. Urásu uje jé chetaque uqué casica. Mu dayé que chetaque to go.

Adi jé u Dupade gajnerĩ uje que chetaque yoque go. Dupade potarása jetiga yanguretágo jnese – jnanione, chequedie, oruyoque. Jé uacamóño to don Pablo acajnaquei que, uje chojninga: “Ca arocho uacáringárique choquíjna. Jĩrique anguretáyo Dupade to iji ne.”

A gosi chojnírarasa: ‘A
jĩrique yangureta Dupade jeta e jnani yu. Jeta e choquíjna yu, jeoga yangureta
sequere ejo to. ¿Mu socase yitoji yui a? ¿Soca yitoji yui ga yitoi
yisijnaringuéone údi ga yicai gajño pioi a?

Jé ueradi u iji de
yucuecaringuei. Ga jeta uñeque changureta enga ucuecaringuei déji. Yocuñeque
jnani chi changureta iji sequere ejo. Que pimo ejórique. Que pimo jeta yitogóji
yui. Jé pimo jeta yico sequere ejórique to. Ajningacho Dupade Uruode to udojo
quigode.

Cucha gose Dupade, mu chijnóra ayoré gajnaréngone uyoque. Pota yoque. Que yimo jeta je yiqueca to. Soca yitoi dirome ga, que yujórique ayoré chiraja Dupade uruode quigode to. A gosi basája jeta jĩrique chingo dojode ayoré to jeta piqueta ore to ome Dupade. Que pamo jeta yitogoi, que yirajágo dínique jeta yitogoi. Gusu Dupade uje chiraja cuchade, uje chirajapise cuchade.

**Picatecãraitigade ome Dupade:**Dupade, átaja yoque jne. Orone ore chingo yoque baruode to. Yoquirasiguipise ñane. Yoque Dupade, don Len ore. Jeta don Len ore jno dirome ga, yoque Dupade, ga ca ore ate gajnarétique ome máquina aja jnumi dirome aja ore idai yui. Átaja Chagaide ore iji Rincón ujetiga ujnacari ore jnusina ua ome yoque to. Yoque Dupade, uje barodipise yóguiji erámi jnese, yoque Dupade. Átaja Iguijnamúiné ore. Uje jnanione jé adi u ujeta ore chetaque dacotedie oruñanie. Mu Dupade, acate ore ajéode, yoque Dupade. Átaja Davidé ore iji ga ore jnusina ua ome yoque to. Jeta Pajei ore chise dacotedie jeta ca pésu date gajnarétique ome ore, enga chequedíe ore jnusina ua ome dabayode to. Yoque Dupade, ome don Fernando to rĩ. Don Fernando ate gajnarétigo upome guidai jogosórone, Yoque Dupade. Átaja ore iji data ujeta ore cha date gajnaréngone ome ayoredie.

Yicatecã́ri ua ome chequedíe uñanie to uje ore chajéngopísome baruode ga arote ore ga ore cha dajegátigade enga ore catecã́ri ua ome yoque to. Yicatecã́ri ua ome Davidé ore. Eruidé ore ga ore jé ũrepise uje que ore chotigase barúrigo to. Yicatecã́ri ua ome Ipeyai ore urasade cojñoque ga jeta que uñeque chimo dequejnarétique gajnerĩ, yoque Dupade. Badute yoquicatecãraitigade ome ua to. Ca otigase yoque. Jé ũrepise yoque, yoque Dupade.

*Oroné – Rincón de Tigre, Bolivia – 1970**Marina de Morarie churúsãre Oroné acajnaquéode udojo*
english:
**Paul’s teaching is hard to understand**

*Yique u yoquijõrai Pablo acajnaquei  Oroné*

**Dupade****’s Word in written form****was given****to teach people***:*

From that book, people have learned about*Dupade*, and then they have taught us. When don Pablo taught us, he told us about what Peter said about the Apostle Paul’s teaching. He said: “What Paul teaches is hard to understand.”*(2 Peter 3:16-17)*

**Some****of us****think only of getting what we want****:**

Ecarai told about a man that said: ‘I know that there is a*Dupade*in heaven. And that He made everything, but I will believe in him later when I’m an old man; and I’m not an old man yet.’

Some of us*Ayoreos*are like that: we know there’s a*Dupade*in heaven, we know he exists, but our minds are on other things. We love those things more than*Dupade*, so we don’t pay attention to*Dupade*.

Another person says he knows what’s true, he knows*Dupade*exists, and he knows*Dupade*sees everything from heaven by his power, but he, too, loves other things more than*Dupade*. The things we crave and want to have preoccupy us so much that we don’t do the things that*Dupade*wants us to do, but instead, we do what he says not to do.

**Dupade wrote****his words****for Moses:**

I am always telling you about the commandments that*Dupade*wrote for Moses long ago on tablets of stone. He wrote these so that Moses could tell his people about the things he wanted them to do, and the things he did not want them to do.

*Dupade*does not want us to not consider other things as though they were*Dupade*. Not make idols nor images of men. He told us not to pray to idols.*Dupade*hates the other things men believe in. He says: “Don’t say anything ugly about me. Don’t make fun of me or belittle me. Don’t make light of my name.”

“Obey your mother and father.” These are what*Dupade*wrote and gave to Moses; they were written upon tablets of stone.

We know about these commandments today, but we don’t obey them. Here are some of the other commandments: “Don’t work on the seventh day. Don’t kill your fellow human beings.”*Dupade*really disapproves of doing these things.

“Carefully obey what your father and mother tell you to do,” he wrote. If we do not obey our fathers and mothers, we probably will not obey*Dupade*either, for*Dupade*is our Father. And if we are disobedient to Him, then we can’t follow Him.

“Don’t desire young women, or the wives of other men.” If a man wants to believe in*Dupade*, he mustn’t sleep with the women who come to him. And he should not lust after other women, and women should not lust after other men and other people’s mates, we shouldn’t lust after them. To do those things is ugly.

“Don’t be a liar,”*Dupade*says. “Don’t covet what other people have. Don’t steal from others.”

These are the words of*Dupade*, but when we do not believe in*Dupade*, we make light of them and don’t try to obey them. Instead of thinking about these things, we are preoccupied with doing other things, the very things that*Dupade*hates. We think about how to fulfill our lusts, doing the things that bring us pleasure. We seek after those things, and*Dupade*’s*Word*does not interest us.

And even if we try to obey*Dupade*’s*Word*, we are unable to do so without the help of Jesus and the Holy Spirit. Paul tells us about this in the book of Romans. He says:*(Romans 8:1-4)*

We see from what Paul wrote that we cannot obey*Dupade*’s commandments on our own, but only by the power of the Lord Jesus. When we believe in him, it is not hard for us to obey*Dupade*.

**Oroné illustrates his teaching with a man who had two sons****:**

There was a man with two sons, and the younger one asked for his portion of his father’s things. And he asked for money. He said: “Father, why don’t you give me some of your money, so I can go away and live in another land?”

His father gave it to him and his son left. He went to another land and after a long time his money was gone, and he was hungry. So, he said: “I had lots to eat in the past, so much that I would throw the left overs on the ground. Why don’t I go back to my Father and the others I left to come here?” That’s what the young man did.

It’s just like us, all of us*Ayoreos*and*cojñone**;*we started out believing in*Dupade*, and then we left him, just like the prodigal son. We believed in*Dupade*but then stopped believing. We started doing our own thing and had nothing more to do with our heavenly Father. We abandoned him and went our own way. And then, the beauty of the Lord was no longer in us. And that is how we are. We’re just like that young man. We leave*Dupade*, and abandon him, and end up with nothing.

Just like that young man who left his father and ended up in a strange land, we end up the same way when we abandon our heavenly Father. But when the young man came to his senses he knew what to do. And if we find ourselves like him, miserable in a strange land, we need to come to our senses and return to our Father. And some of us need to remember how powerful*Dupade*is and we need to turn back and follow*Dupade*’s*W**ord*which will lead us back to*Dupade*. We need to repent of our ugly ways. Leave them behind, and return to*Dupade*, and trust in him to help us.

That’s how that young man felt about his father, and he went back to him. And his father saw him coming and ran to meet him. He just took off running toward him. He hugged him and kissed him. He kissed his son. But his son said: ‘My father, I’ve done ugly things. My ways have been sinful. I’ve been bad to you and I’ve been bad to*Dupade*. So, you mustn’t call me a son any longer. Consider me a servant.’ His father was so happy he wouldn’t let him say anything more.

And we’re exactly like him, thinking we are very bad. We also may have stopped reading*Dupade*’s*Word*, and it seemed like we even stopped believing in*Dupade*. But*Dupade*is like that young man’s father who said to his son: ‘You may have left me back then, but I’m asking you to once again believe in me.’ Whoever ignores*Dupade*’s*Word,*may turn back, and believe again, and again walk in faith with*Dupade*. Just like that young man’s father,*Dupade*will not refuse you – he’ll take you back.

*Dupade*is like that, I may turn from him, but when I come back and honor his*Word*, it will make him very happy. Just like the young man’s father was happy to have his son back again. He told his servants: ‘Hurry up, bring the good clothes, and put them on him. Put a ring on his finger. Put shoes on his feet.’ And then he told them: ‘Now go and find our fattest cow and kill it and prepare the meat. We’ll make the kind of feast we eat on Sundays for my son who was gone so long it was as though he was dead. Yes, it was as though he were dead, but is now alive and with us again.

**Dupade is so good, but he loves us sinners and wants us:**

The father loved his youngest son, but his older son hated him. The older son was in the field when his brother returned. As he was going home he heard his father and others celebrating. He heard music being played and he asked his father’s servant who had come out to meet him: ‘What is going on back in town?’

The servant said: ‘Why, your brother has returned. Since he came back they have been celebrating his return.’

Some of us are like those that rejoiced to see the young man returning. But the older son hated his younger brother. He had the wrong attitude for he hated him. But his father didn’t hate him.

And that is how*Dupade*is today, he doesn’t hate us. He wants everyone to believe in him– men, women, all of us. Remember that don Pablo taught us about this and said: “Don’t wait till you’re old, but quickly turn to*Dupade*and believe in Him.”

Someone might say: ‘Oh, I will quickly believe when I am a grown man or older, or maybe even next year.’ But he should ask himself: ‘But what if I die before then? There’s a possibility I could die before then. What if I die in my sins and go to the lake of fire?’

It’s good that I am alive today. And good if someone believes while he’s still alive. Some people really think they will believe next year. But who knows anything about next year? We can’t know whether we’ll die before the coming year. We don’t know if we’ll reach next year. Better to pay attention to what*Dupade*’s*Word*says right now.

*Dupade*is an awesome*Dupade*, but he loves us common people. He wants us. I don’t know how long I’ll live. I could die tomorrow while some of my fellow*Ayoreos*have no knowledge of*Dupade*’s*Word*. We need to tell our fellow*Ayoreos*about Jesus so that*Dupade*can save them also. I don’t know when I’ll die, none of us knows the day he will die. Only*Dupade*knows, for he knows all things.

**Closing Prayer:**

*Dupade*, help us. Oroné has told us about your*Word*and we’re all happy about it.As don Len and they leave, protect them on the train trip to their village. Help Chagaide and his son, and the others in Rincón to remember us in prayer.*Yoqui-**Dupade*, you are always watching over us everywhere on earth. Help Iguijnamúiné and his people. Help the men who are having problems with their wives. But*Dupade*, speak to their hearts. Help Davidé and his people to pray for us also. Help Pajei and the others to return to their wives and to not wrong them, and that the women will look to you to help them with their husbands.*Yoqui-**Dupade*, I pray for don Fernando also. Don Fernando has mistreated the people in his town.*Yoqui-**Dupade*, help those who are mistreating their wives to stop their ugly ways. I pray to you for some of the women who are embarrassed to participate in meetings; watch over them and help them to stop being bashful and to pray for us also. I pray for Davidé, Eruidé and them, for it’s true that they never doubt your*Word.*I pray for the health of Ipeyai and his people,*Yoqui-**Dupade*. You hear us when we pray because we do not doubt you. Because we do not doubt you, do not doubt us. We are truthful with you,*Yoqui-**Dupade*. I pray again for the men in Rincón and ask that they will also pray for us. Amen.

Dupade – God

Yoqui-Dupade – Our God

Dupade’s Word – God’s Word

Ayoreos – People of the Ayoré tribe.

Cojñone – People not of the Ayoré tribe.

*Oroné – Rincón de Tigre, Bolivia – 1970.*

*Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie.*

ayoreo:
**Yapade chojninga: A jĩrique Oroné toi jne:**Uacadúcho to yate bajade iji taningai ujeta ga jĩrique yangureta Dupade. Jĩraque yipota yúringai Dupade angurete to. Que yajirape yapade ore áyipie quitígome, ore isocade to, ore isadodéringane gajnéone, ore tódode gajnecho.

Mu yapade chojninga: “A uté
jĩrique toi jne ujetiga ca doi yocadode to.”

**Que yi cojñone adode uñane to:**Mu que yangari gotique, que yangári yapade gotique. Jé yajo yu aja Dupade. Yacaranguipise ome Dupade uje yṍraji monte ga jĩraque yṍra yu ome eduguejnane ga que qué yi cojñone adode uñane to. Mu ayoréode uñane jé ore chirate cojñone aja ore jódedie, jé ore chóji yote pioi. Yacaranguipise uje que yimo udojo gotique iji yoquidai, mu yudute puru ũréone ome misionéro udore uje ore chojninga: “Jesus di jne. Dupade chiroque dabi aja jnumone jne uje ore chujé nanique. Ga chi diapo jne rĩ,” ore chojninga nanique, “ga jno yocúdi aja gaté.”

**Ore chojningame yu: Dupade a chiqueta ua jne:**Chi uté diapo rĩ ome ore ga udojo u uje jĩrique yangureta Dupade to ome ore ojñane, mu cho jetiga cojñoque yu ga que yiraja ore acajnaquéode quigode. Mu yipota jóroga Dupade úringai pangurené. Jĩraque yicatecã́ri Dupade ome yu. Jé u uje eduguéjnane ore catecã́ri yu ome Dupade gatuaque enga ore chojninga, ‘A chiqueta ua jne.’

Gajnáreque yatique ome yu, mu jé
yipésu; yipésu cucha gajnaréngone. Que yiya gotique. Yipésu cucha gajnaréngone.
Yipésu iji taningai. Yagu yipesudode cucha gajnaréngone; yeteque yu ome Dupade.

Mu iji udojo ga sequeredie a
déji nueve, mu quene yujúsipise yio. Quene yujusipise yio ome cuchade gu, quene
yangaripísi misionérone uje e ore di. Uje yipésu cucha gajnaréngone mu e
yiraja.

**Chise diri uje yucué Dupade adode to:**Yacaranguipísome Dupade uje e yiraja cojñone uruode garósirique to, enga quene yirajapise ore acaditigade. Ore chojninga: “A gosi chimesa dapesudode cucha gajnaréngaja dacatecãraitigade ome dangurené Dupade, ga Dupade que chátaja uté gosi go.”

Enga jé u uje yipésu yajéode – yipésu yajéode mu quene
yibasapísaja Dupade úringa pangurené to iji sequeredie uje a déji nueve iji
yiquigade. Enga quene yucué Dupade adode. Yanguretapise Dupade, yacate cojñone
ore to. Mu yucuépo yisocáringome ga yojninga: “Dupade pésu yu ga pésu
yibijnani. Enga pésu yibijnani ujeta yacate Dupade uruode ome yujode ayoré
ore.” Gusu yu iji yiyaridode, yajesúode erámio. Mu jé yucué yáyipie ome Dupade.
Mu yacaranguipis ome Dupade uje íjnoque yujórique ayoré jeta chirajapise
cuchade iji yidai, ga cojñone ore que gosi cho yiquique mu. Qué yucué, que
yiraja yisocáringome Dupade gotóraque. Cáma yujusipise yio ga yicatecã́ri
Dupade. Yicatecã́ri Dupade icaite, ga idógosi jeta que idájapise gotique.

**Que iyajmanguipise gotique uje yicatecã́ri Dupade ica iji yejnai:**Gusu yu iji yejnai tiji erámi, mu ujnusiétaque yu aja yejnai. Yo yágugu yurásade ore uje íjnoque ayoré jeta chiraja cuchade jeta chatata Dupade adode ome ore uyoque, mu yáyipie déji Dupade enga yicatecã́ri Dupade iji yejnai.

Yojninga: “Dupade á, acué yacadisóño,
acué yacadisónique; chãje gosi chacate yu ome Dupade adode ujetiga chacate yu
ga yiraja cuchárigo to ga yatatapo adode to ome yurásade ore gaté.”

**Quigade ga ore urusarane di ome yu:**Quigade mu, enga jé garósi, garósi dirode iji yicatecãraitigade mu ore urusarane e di. Tṍraji Tumi Chucua. Ore urusarane diji yocurasade eduguéjna ga chojninga: “Euguéjnane déji ta Béni ga ore acadigode ayoré quedéjnane. Ujetiga apótayo ga arocho Oroné aja de to.” Enga ore chingo yu ga e yáyipie déji yicatecãraitigade. Yáyipie déji yicatecãraitigade casé ga jé upúsu.

Mu yicatecarápoi Dupade rĩ ga
yojninga: “Yicatecãraitigade a u uje a yajneque baroquéode. A déji jnupe
eruénique marínga, isega jé yi. Jeoga escuela déji jnup eruei, mu jé yipota
jeta yiraja badode garósirique to jeta yiraja cuchade, jeta yiraja darujna
aode.”

**Timotéo chatata Dupade uruode iji de:**Yajei upúsu. Idája diríca, mu yacaranguipise uje Dupade qué chiroque yu aja uté ga jé yiji. Jé yacaranguipise ome uaque, yijorane uaque uje uacadotáyo Dupade Uruode iji de. Yacaranguipise uje Timotéo chatata Dupade uruode iji de ga jé yiraja ujade to ome yurásade. Chãje Dupade a ajeo u ica gu ome yu iji taningane. Yacaranguipise ome cuchade uje Dupade chátaja yu ga tangáripísi yicatecãraitigade.

Uacuñengo, ayoréode uáguiji
uaquedai, uacuñéngo chucué dasoca to ome Dupade. Uacamóño uje Dupade u uje
chátaja yoque. Cuchade uñane qué déji mu garósi deréjnane enga e yiya. E gusu.

*Oroné – Puesto Paz, Bolivia – 1976**Pablo Wyma churúsãre Oroné uruode nanique*
english:
**Q****uickly****I****believe in Dupade**

*Jĩrique yangureta Dupade Oroné*
